<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Define Telecardiology. List any four clinical applications.**

**Model Answer:**

Telecardiology is the application of telecommunication and information technologies to provide remote cardiovascular diagnosis, monitoring, and treatment.

**Four clinical applications:**
1. Remote real-time 12-lead ECG monitoring.
2. Ambulatory Holter monitoring with off-line cloud analysis.
3. In-ambulance STEMI detection and hospital pre-notification.
4. Remote follow-up of implanted pacemakers and ICDs (trans-telephonic monitoring).

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark × 4 = 2 marks for applications = 3 marks total)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]**
**State the standard ECG paper speed, calibration, and frequency range used in telecardiology.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|-----------|----------------|
| Paper speed | **25 mm/s** |
| Calibration | **1 mV = 10 mm vertical deflection** |
| Frequency range | **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** |
| Sampling rate (clinical) | **500 Hz** |
| ADC resolution | **12 bits** |

*(1 mark each × 3 = 3 marks total)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Forgetting to convert **mm to seconds** (e.g., 5 mm ≠ 5 s) costs **2 marks**.
> - Mixing up **small box (0.04 s)** and **large box (0.20 s)** is a **3-mark deduction**.
> - Writing formulas without stating **assumptions** (e.g., standard 25 mm/s speed) is penalized **1 mark**.
> - Skipping the **unit** in the final answer is a **½ mark deduction per occurrence**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 1 Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the complete block diagram of a digital ECG acquisition system used in telecardiology. Describe the function of each block in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 12-lead ECG is sampled at 500 Hz with 12-bit resolution per channel. Calculate:
   (i) The raw data rate in kbps.
   (ii) The size of a 10-second ECG record in kilobytes (KB).
   (iii) The transmission time over a 256 kbps GSM channel. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Block Diagram of Digital ECG Acquisition System (7 marks):**

The digital ECG system consists of the following blocks:

1. **Electrodes and Lead Wires (1 mark):** Ag/AgCl electrodes placed on limbs and chest capture ionic currents generated by cardiac depolarization. The standard 12-lead configuration uses 10 electrodes to derive 12 independent views.

2. **Patient Cable and Protection Circuit (½ mark):** Routes the signal safely, with defibrillator protection resistors and isolation barrier to prevent patient electrocution.

3. **Instrumentation Amplifier (1 mark):** Provides high CMRR (>100 dB) and amplifies the millivolt-level ECG (typically ±5 mV) to volt levels (gain ≈ 1000×).

4. **Bandpass Filter (1 mark):** High-pass at 0.05 Hz removes baseline wander; low-pass at 150 Hz removes high-frequency muscle artifact and electromagnetic interference.

5. **Notch Filter (½ mark):** Centered at **50 Hz** (India) or **60 Hz** (US) to remove power-line interference.

6. **Sample-and-Hold + ADC (1 mark):** Holds analog value constant during conversion. 12-bit ADC with sampling rate ≥ 500 Hz produces a serial digital bit stream.

7. **Microcontroller / DSP (1 mark):** Performs QRS detection (Pan-Tompkins algorithm), heart rate computation, and compression (e.g., wavelet or Huffman).

8. **Display / Communication Interface (1 mark):** LCD for local viewing, plus UART/USB/Bluetooth/4G module for remote transmission to cardiologist workstation.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $f_s = 500$ Hz, $n = 12$ bits, $c = 12$ channels, duration = 10 s, channel capacity = 256 kbps.

**(i) Raw Data Rate (3 marks):**

$$R = f_s \times n \times c$$

$$R = 500 \times 12 \times 12$$

$$R = 72{,}000 \text{ bits/s} = 72 \text{ kbps}$$

**(ii) File Size for 10 seconds (2 marks):**

$$\text{Size} = R \times T = 72{,}000 \times 10 = 720{,}000 \text{ bits}$$

$$\text{Size} = \frac{720{,}000}{8 \times 1024} \approx 87.89 \text{ KB}$$

**(iii) Transmission Time over 256 kbps (2 marks):**

$$T_{tx} = \frac{720{,}000 \text{ bits}}{256{,}000 \text{ bits/s}} = 2.8125 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{T_{tx} \approx 2.81 \text{ seconds}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a Holter monitoring system and its role in telecardiology. List the advantages of Holter-based telecardiology over in-clinic ECG. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ECG strip is recorded at standard speed (25 mm/s). Two consecutive R-peaks are separated by **18 small boxes**. A second strip shows R-R separation of **3.4 large boxes**.
   (i) Calculate the heart rate in each case.
   (ii) A 5:1 lossless compression is applied. Calculate the compressed size of a 24-hour, 2-channel Holter recording originally sampled at 250 Hz with 12-bit resolution. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Holter Monitoring in Telecardiology (7 marks):**

A **Holter monitor** is a portable, battery-operated device that continuously records the ECG (typically 24–48 hours) using 5–7 leads attached to the patient's chest.

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
1. Electrodes capture ECG continuously.
2. Analog signal is amplified, filtered (0.05–150 Hz), and digitized.
3. Data is stored in internal flash memory or directly streamed to a smartphone via Bluetooth.
4. After the recording period, data is uploaded to a cloud server.
5. Software performs automated arrhythmia detection (PVC, AF, pauses).
6. Cardiologist reviews flagged events remotely and generates a report.

**Role in Telecardiology:** Holter bridges the gap between short in-clinic ECG (10 s) and long-term cardiac surveillance, especially for **paroxysmal arrhythmias** that are missed in routine ECG.

**Advantages over In-Clinic ECG (3 marks):**

| Feature | Holter + Telecardiology | In-Clinic ECG |
|---------|--------------------------|---------------|
| Duration | 24–48 hours continuous | ~10 seconds |
| Arrhythmia detection | Paroxysmal events caught | Only persistent abnormalities |
| Patient mobility | Ambulatory | Stationary |
| Geographic reach | Rural / remote patients | Hospital-only |
| Real-time alert | Possible with GSM module | Not feasible |
| Diagnostic yield | 5–10× higher for intermittent arrhythmias | Lower |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Strip 1: $n_{small} = 18$
- Strip 2: $n_{large} = 3.4$
- Holter: $T = 24$ h, $c = 2$ channels, $f_s = 250$ Hz, $n = 12$ bits, $CR = 5{:}1$.

**(i) Heart Rate (3 marks):**

**Strip 1 (small-box method):**

$$HR_1 = \frac{1500}{18} = 83.33 \text{ bpm}$$

**Strip 2 (large-box method):**

$$HR_2 = \frac{300}{3.4} = 88.24 \text{ bpm}$$

**(ii) Compressed Holter Size (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total samples per channel in 24 hours:**

$$N = 250 \times 24 \times 3600 = 2.16 \times 10^7 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 2 — Original raw data size:**

$$D_{raw} = N \times n \times c = 2.16 \times 10^7 \times 12 \times 2$$

$$D_{raw} = 5.184 \times 10^8 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to MB:**

$$D_{raw} = \frac{5.184 \times 10^8}{8 \times 10^6} = 64.8 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 4 — Apply 5:1 compression:**

$$D_{compressed} = \frac{64.8}{5} = 12.96 \text{ MB}$$

$$\boxed{D_{compressed} \approx 12.96 \text{ MB}}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Telecardiology**

- [x] **Telecardiology = Telemedicine + Cardiology** — focus on ECG acquisition, transmission, and remote interpretation.
- [x] Standard ECG paper speed = **25 mm/s**; calibration = **10 mm/mV**.
- [x] Small box = 1 mm = 0.04 s; Large box = 5 mm = 0.20 s.
- [x] Heart Rate formulas: $HR = 1500 / n_{small} = 300 / n_{large} = 60 / RR_{sec}$.
- [x] ECG bandwidth: **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz**; clinical sampling rate: **500–1000 Hz**.
- [x] Nyquist rate = $2 f_{max}$ — always add a safety margin in clinical systems.
- [x] Standard ADC = **12 bits** → 4096 quantization levels.
- [x] Data rate formula: $R = f_s \times n \times c$ (in bits/s).
- [x] Quantization SNR: $SNR_q = 6.02n + 1.76$ dB.
- [x] Compression Ratio = Original / Compressed; reduces transmission time by same factor.
- [x] **Holter monitor** = ambulatory 24–48 h ECG recorder, key to capturing paroxysmal arrhythmias.
- [x] **STEMI network** = in-ambulance ECG + cloud triage + cath-lab activation.
- [x] Encryption standards: **AES-256** and **TLS** mandatory for patient data (DISHA / HIPAA compliance).
- [x] Common pitfall: Forgetting **mm→s conversion** in ECG strip problems.
- [x] Common pitfall: Using Nyquist rate (300 Hz) instead of **clinical sampling rate (500 Hz)** in ECG systems.
- [x] Real-world benchmarks: 12-lead ECG @ 500 Hz × 12 bits = **72 kbps**; 24-h Holter ≈ **64.8 MB raw**.
- [x] 5 high-yield formulas to memorize: HR (small/large box), Nyquist, data rate, SNR_q, compression ratio.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Teleoncology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT861 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Teleoncology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Teleoncology** is defined as the application of telecommunications and information technologies to deliver oncological (cancer-related) healthcare services remotely. It encompasses the **diagnosis, treatment planning, follow-up care, survivorship support, and palliative care** of cancer patients across geographically distributed locations using synchronous (real-time) and asynchronous (store-and-forward) modalities.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEEBT861**, Teleoncology is positioned as a sub-discipline of Telemedicine that integrates clinical oncology, biomedical engineering, and digital health informatics. It is formally recognized by bodies such as the **American Telemedicine Association (ATA)** and the **European Society for Medical Oncology (ESMO)** as a critical enabler of **equitable cancer care**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy: The "Virtual Tumor Board"**
> Imagine a patient in a remote village in Wayanad, Kerala, who discovers a suspicious breast lump. Traditionally, she would need to travel **hundreds of kilometers to a Regional Cancer Centre** in Trivandrum or Kochi for a multidisciplinary opinion — a journey that is expensive, time-consuming, and emotionally draining. **Teleoncology acts like a "digital bridge"** — instead of moving the patient, it moves the *expertise*. Through high-definition video conferencing, digital pathology slides, and DICOM-compliant imaging, a panel of oncologists, radiologists, and pathologists at a tertiary cancer centre can collectively review her case, recommend a treatment plan, and even supervise chemotherapy administration — all without her leaving her hometown. It is the **"hospital without walls"** for cancer care.

### 1.3 Key Terminology in Teleoncology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Multidisciplinary Team (MDT) Meeting:** A virtual tumor board where surgeons, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, pathologists, and radiologists converge online to discuss complex cases.
> - **Store-and-Forward (SAF):** Transmission of prerecorded clinical data (e.g., histopathology images, MRI scans) for later expert review.
> - **Real-Time Synchronous Consultation:** Live, interactive consultation using video, audio, and shared screen tools.
> - **Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM):** Continuous tracking of vitals, chemotherapy side-effects, and recovery metrics via wearables.
> - **PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System):** A medical imaging technology that provides economical storage, retrieval, management, distribution, and presentation of images.
> - **HIS/RIS Integration:** Hospital Information System and Radiology Information System interoperability for unified electronic health records (EHR).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Teleoncology

- **Bandwidth requirement:** Minimum **1 Mbps** for SD video, **4–6 Mbps** for HD video tumor boards, and **> 50 Mbps** for real-time multi-modality imaging review.
- **Diagnostic concordance rate:** Typically reported in the range of **88%–95%** between telepathology and conventional microscopy.
- **Mean turnaround time (TAT):** Reduced from **7–10 days** to **24–48 hours** for remote second opinions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Framework of Teleoncology

The teleoncology system is structured around **four primary functional pillars**:

1. **Clinical Data Acquisition Layer**
   - Digital pathology scanners (whole-slide imaging, WSI)
   - DICOM-compliant imaging modalities (CT, MRI, PET-CT, mammography)
   - Wearable biosensors for chemotherapy patients
   - Patient-reported outcome (PRO) questionnaires

2. **Transmission and Communication Layer**
   - Secure Virtual Private Networks (VPN)
   - HL7 / FHIR-compliant data exchange protocols
   - Cloud-based PACS archives
   - End-to-end encrypted (E2EE) video conferencing

3. **Specialist Decision-Making Layer**
   - Remote oncologists and tumor boards
   - AI-assisted computer-aided detection (CADe) and computer-aided diagnosis (CADx)
   - Clinical decision support systems (CDSS)

4. **Patient Delivery and Follow-up Layer**
   - Local peripheral chemotherapy infusion centers
   - Tele-nursing for symptom management
   - Mobile health (mHealth) applications for survivorship

### 2.2 Modes of Teleoncology Service Delivery

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification Table**

| Mode | Description | Typical Latency | Use Case |
|------|-------------|------------------|----------|
| Synchronous (Real-Time) | Live video + audio interaction | < 200 ms | Virtual tumor boards, patient consultation |
| Asynchronous (Store-and-Forward) | Pre-captured data uploaded for later review | Hours to 24 h | Telepathology, teleradiology |
| Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM) | Continuous streaming of vitals/symptoms | Real-time to 15 min | Chemotherapy toxicity surveillance |
| mHealth (Mobile Health) | Smartphone-based PRO and education | Asynchronous | Survivorship, palliative care |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are essential for solving numerical and analytical questions on Teleoncology in KTU 2024 ESE.

**1. Bandwidth Estimation for Video Teleoncology:**

$$BW_{req} = (R_{video} \times bpp_{video}) + (R_{audio} \times bpp_{audio}) + OH_{protocol}$$

Where:
- $BW_{req}$ = Total required bandwidth (in bits per second)
- $R_{video}$ = Video frame rate (fps)
- $bpp_{video}$ = Bits per pixel of video stream
- $R_{audio}$ = Audio sampling rate (samples/second)
- $bpp_{audio}$ = Bits per audio sample
- $OH_{protocol}$ = Protocol overhead (typically 15%–20% of payload)

**2. Effective Diagnostic Concordance:**

$$C_{eff} = \left( \frac{N_{agree}}{N_{total}} \right) \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $C_{eff}$ = Effective concordance rate (%)
- $N_{agree}$ = Number of cases where tele-diagnosis matches gold-standard diagnosis
- $N_{total}$ = Total number of cases reviewed

**3. Cost-Benefit Ratio for Rural Teleoncology Deployment:**

$$CBR = \frac{C_{travel} + C_{lost\;wage} - C_{tele}}{C_{in-person}}$$

Where $C_{travel}$ is travel cost, $C_{lost\;wage}$ is opportunity cost, $C_{tele}$ is teleoncology session cost, and $C_{in-person}$ is conventional in-person consultation cost.

**4. Mean Turnaround Time (TAT) Reduction:**

$$\Delta TAT = TAT_{conventional} - TAT_{teleoncology}$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern cancer care delivery, teleoncology serves as a **force multiplier** in:
- **Resource-constrained settings:** Enables tertiary cancer expertise to reach Tier-2 and Tier-3 cities.
- **Disaster and pandemic response:** Maintained continuity of cancer care during COVID-19 lockdowns.
- **Clinical trials:** Facilitates decentralized (remote) clinical trial participation.
- **Continuing Medical Education (CME):** Specialist oncologists can train peripheral clinicians virtually.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example: Bandwidth Calculation for a Virtual Tumor Board

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A tertiary cancer hospital in Kerala is conducting a live virtual tumor board with a peripheral center. The setup uses a 1080p video stream (1920 × 1080 pixels) at 30 fps, with 24-bit color depth. The audio is sampled at 44.1 kHz with 16-bit samples (stereo). Calculate the raw bandwidth requirement, and the effective bandwidth including a 20% protocol overhead.

**Step 1: Calculate raw video bandwidth.**

The number of bits per video frame is given by:

$$bpp_{frame} = W \times H \times D$$

Where $W$ = 1920 pixels, $H$ = 1080 pixels, $D$ = 24 bits/pixel.

Substituting the values:

$$bpp_{frame} = 1920 \times 1080 \times 24$$

$$bpp_{frame} = 49,766,400 \text{ bits/frame}$$

**Step 2: Multiply by the frame rate to get video bitrate.**

$$R_{video} = bpp_{frame} \times fps$$

$$R_{video} = 49,766,400 \times 30$$

$$R_{video} = 1,492,992,000 \text{ bits/second}$$

$$R_{video} \approx 1.493 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Step 3: Calculate raw audio bandwidth.**

For stereo audio, the total sampling rate is $2 \times 44{,}100 = 88{,}200$ samples/second.

$$R_{audio} = 88{,}200 \times 16$$

$$R_{audio} = 1,411,200 \text{ bits/second}$$

$$R_{audio} \approx 1.411 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 4: Sum the raw payload bandwidth.**

$$BW_{raw} = R_{video} + R_{audio}$$

$$BW_{raw} = 1{,}492{,}992{,}000 + 1{,}411{,}200$$

$$BW_{raw} = 1{,}494{,}403{,}200 \text{ bits/second}$$

**Step 5: Apply 20% protocol overhead.**

$$BW_{eff} = BW_{raw} \times (1 + 0.20)$$

$$BW_{eff} = 1{,}494{,}403{,}200 \times 1.20$$

$$BW_{eff} = 1{,}793{,}283{,}840 \text{ bits/second}$$

$$BW_{eff} \approx 1.79 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Step 6: Interpret the result.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The result ($\approx 1.79$ Gbps) shows that uncompressed HD tumor board sessions require dedicated high-capacity fiber links. In practice, **H.264/H.265 video compression** reduces this to **4–6 Mbps** with negligible perceptual quality loss — which is why **H.265 (HEVC)** is the KTU-recommended codec for teleoncology.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Teleoncology Subspecialties

> [!NOTE]
> **Subspecialty Functional Matrix**

| Subspecialty | Data Modality | Bandwidth | Diagnostic Concordance | KTU Weightage |
|--------------|---------------|-----------|------------------------|---------------|
| Telepathology | Whole-slide images (WSI, 1–3 GB/slide) | 50–100 Mbps | 92%–96% | High |
| Teleradiology (Oncology) | DICOM CT/MRI/PET (50–500 MB/study) | 10–50 Mbps | 94%–98% | High |
| Telechemotherapy Monitoring | Wearable sensor streams | < 1 Mbps | 88%–92% (symptom concordance) | Medium |
| Virtual Tumor Board | Multi-stream HD video + imaging | 4–6 Mbps (compressed) | 96%–99% (decision match) | High |
| Survivorship Tele-follow-up | PRO questionnaires, video | 1–2 Mbps | 90%–95% (patient satisfaction) | Medium |

### 3.3 Cost-Benefit Analytical Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Calculation:** A breast cancer patient in Kannur requires a second opinion from an oncologist at a Regional Cancer Centre 250 km away. Conventional cost: ₹4,500 (travel) + ₹1,500 (lost wage) + ₹2,000 (in-person consultation) = ₹8,000. Teleoncology session cost: ₹1,500.

$$CBR = \frac{4{,}500 + 1{,}500 - 1{,}500}{8{,}000}$$

$$CBR = \frac{4{,}500}{8{,}000} = 0.5625$$

$$CBR \approx 56.25\%$$

**Interpretation:** The teleoncology pathway saves approximately **56% of total cost** per consultation, validating its economic viability for rural Kerala.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Teleoncology System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following block-level architecture diagram illustrates the end-to-end data and decision flow in a teleoncology ecosystem, mapping interactions between the **peripheral spoke center**, the **tertiary hub**, and the **supporting technology stack**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Peripheral Spoke Hospital] --> B[Patient Registration Module]
    B --> C[Clinical Data Acquisition]
    C --> C1[Digital Pathology Scanner WSI]
    C --> C2[DICOM Imaging CT MRI PET]
    C --> C3[Wearable RPM Devices]
    C --> C4[Patient Reported Outcomes]
    C1 --> D[Secure Transmission Layer]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Cloud PACS and EHR Archive]
    E --> F[Tertiary Cancer Hub]
    F --> F1[Virtual Tumor Board MDT]
    F --> F2[AI Assisted CADe and CADx]
    F --> F3[Oncology Specialist Review]
    F1 --> G[Integrated Treatment Plan]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Decision Output]
    H --> H1[Chemotherapy Protocol]
    H --> H2[Surgical Recommendation]
    H --> H3[Radiotherapy Planning]
    H --> H4[Palliative Care Plan]
    H1 --> I[Local Delivery at Spoke]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    I --> J[Follow Up and Survivorship]
    J --> K[Continuous RPM and PRO]
    K --> F
```

### 4.2 Workflow Sequence: Synchronous Teleoncology Consultation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Pat as Patient at Spoke
    participant Spoke as Spoke Clinician
    participant Net as Secure Network
    participant Hub as Tertiary Oncologist
    participant MDT as Tumor Board
    Pat->>Spoke: Clinical complaint
    Spoke->>Spoke: Acquire WSI and imaging
    Spoke->>Net: Upload to PACS via VPN
    Net->>Hub: Encrypted data transfer
    Hub->>MDT: Schedule virtual MDT
    MDT->>Spoke: Live video consultation
    Spoke->>Pat: Convey treatment plan
    Pat->>Spoke: Consent and follow up
    Spoke->>Hub: Periodic outcome updates
```

### 4.3 Subsystem Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AcquisitionLayer[Acquisition]
        A1[WSI Scanner]
        A2[CT MRI Scanner]
        A3[Wearable Sensor]
    end
    subgraph TransmissionLayer[Transmission]
        T1[VPN Tunnel]
        T2[HL7 FHIR Gateway]
        T3[Cloud PACS]
    end
    subgraph DecisionLayer[Decision]
        D1[Oncologist]
        D2[AI Engine]
        D3[Pathologist]
    end
    subgraph DeliveryLayer[Delivery]
        DL1[Chemo Infusion]
        DL2[Radiotherapy]
        DL3[Survivorship App]
    end
    A1 --> T1
    A2 --> T2
    A3 --> T3
    T1 --> D1
    T2 --> D2
    T3 --> D3
    D1 --> DL1
    D2 --> DL2
    D3 --> DL3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define Teleoncology. List any four clinical scenarios where it is most effectively deployed.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Teleoncology is the remote delivery of oncological care — encompassing diagnosis, treatment, multidisciplinary consultation, and follow-up — using telecommunications and information technology, eliminating the need for patient travel.

**Four clinical scenarios (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Virtual Tumor Boards (MDT meetings)** for complex cancer case reviews between spoke and hub centers.
2. **Telepathology** for second-opinion review of histopathology slides from rural hospitals.
3. **Teleradiology** for remote interpretation of CT, MRI, and PET-CT oncology imaging.
4. **Tele-chemotherapy supervision** where peripheral nurses administer chemotherapy under remote oncologist guidance.
5. *(Any valid 4th point — e.g., survivorship care, palliative teleconsultation)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Distinguish between synchronous and asynchronous modes of teleoncology with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | Aspect | Synchronous | Asynchronous |
> |--------|-------------|--------------|
> | Timing | Real-time, live interaction | Store-and-forward, time-shifted |
> | Latency | < 200 ms | Hours to 24 h |
> | Example | Live virtual tumor board | Telepathology slide review |
>
> *(1 Mark for definition of each, 0.5 Mark for example, 0.5 Mark for differentiation point.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently lose marks by **listing components without explaining their functional role** in the architecture. Always pair each component with a *function*.
> - Do **not confuse Telepathology with Teleradiology** — they handle different data modalities (WSI vs. DICOM).
> - Avoid generic answers; **anchor responses to oncology-specific use cases** (tumor boards, chemotherapy, survivorship).
> - For 14-mark questions, KTU examiners expect a **diagram** — failing to draw one typically costs **2–3 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a teleoncology system**, clearly describing the role of the **acquisition, transmission, decision, and delivery layers**.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

1. **Introduction (1 Mark):** Teleoncology architecture is a four-layered, hub-and-spoke model where a tertiary cancer centre (hub) provides specialist expertise to peripheral hospitals (spokes) via secure digital infrastructure.

2. **Acquisition Layer (1.5 Marks):** This includes:
   - **Whole-slide imaging (WSI) scanners** for digitizing histopathology slides at 40× magnification.
   - **DICOM-compliant imaging modalities** (CT, MRI, PET-CT) producing multi-megabyte studies.
   - **Wearable RPM devices** for monitoring vitals, nausea, fatigue in chemotherapy patients.
   - **Patient-Reported Outcome (PRO) tools** (smartphone questionnaires) for symptom tracking.

3. **Transmission Layer (1.5 Marks):** This handles secure, interoperable data movement:
   - **VPN tunnels** with AES-256 encryption.
   - **HL7 / FHIR gateways** for EHR interoperability.
   - **Cloud-based PACS archives** for on-demand image retrieval.
   - **H.265 (HEVC) video codecs** for compressed tumor-board streams at 4–6 Mbps.

4. **Decision Layer (1.5 Marks):** At the tertiary hub:
   - **Oncologists, pathologists, radiologists** form the MDT.
   - **AI-based CADe/CADx engines** flag suspicious lesions.
   - **Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS)** recommend evidence-based protocols.

5. **Delivery Layer (1 Mark):** The treatment plan is implemented locally:
   - Peripheral chemotherapy infusion.
   - Tele-supervised radiotherapy planning.
   - Survivorship mobile applications.

6. **Diagrammatic Representation (0.5 Mark):** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **clinical applications of teleoncology** in cancer care delivery, with emphasis on **virtual tumor boards, telepathology, and remote chemotherapy monitoring**.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

1. **Virtual Tumor Boards (2 Marks):** MDT members convene via HD video conferencing to discuss complex oncology cases. Real-time DICOM image sharing and WSI viewing enable collaborative decision-making. Studies report **96%–99% decision concordance** with in-person boards. They reduce TAT from 7–10 days to 24–48 hours.

2. **Telepathology (2 Marks):** WSI scanners (e.g., Aperio, Hamamatsu NanoZoomer) digitize slides at gigapixel resolution. Pathologists remotely navigate slides via browser-based viewers. Diagnostic concordance with conventional microscopy is **92%–96%**. Enables subspecialty consultation (e.g., hematopathology, molecular pathology) for rare cancers.

3. **Remote Chemotherapy Monitoring (2 Marks):** Patients receiving chemotherapy at spoke centers are monitored via:
   - **Wearable biosensors** tracking heart rate, temperature, SpO₂.
   - **Tele-nursing** for symptom triage (nausea, febrile neutropenia).
   - **AI alert systems** that escalate adverse events to the hub oncologist.
   This reduces **emergency department visits by 30%–40%** and improves quality of life.

4. **Survivorship and Palliative Care (1 Mark):** Smartphone-based PROs and teleconsultations support long-term survivors with psychosocial needs and enable home-based palliative care for terminal patients.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the role of **telepathology and teleradiology** in cancer diagnosis, highlighting the data modalities, bandwidth requirements, and diagnostic accuracy.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

1. **Telepathology (3.5 Marks):**
   - **Data Modality:** Whole-slide images (WSI) at 40× magnification, file sizes 1–3 GB per slide.
   - **Workflow:** Glass slides → WSI scanner → Cloud PACS → Remote pathologist review via web viewer.
   - **Bandwidth:** 50–100 Mbps recommended for smooth navigation.
   - **Diagnostic Concordance:** 92%–96% with conventional light microscopy.
   - **Subspecialty Utility:** Enables molecular pathology, frozen-section review during remote surgery, and second-opinion services.

2. **Teleradiology (3.5 Marks):**
   - **Data Modality:** DICOM-compliant CT, MRI, PET-CT, and mammography studies (50–500 MB per study).
   - **Workflow:** Scanner → DICOM router → PACS → Remote radiologist workstation.
   - **Bandwidth:** 10–50 Mbps sufficient for diagnostic-grade image transfer.
   - **Diagnostic Concordance:** 94%–98%, particularly for solid tumors (lung, breast, colorectal).
   - **AI Integration:** CADe/CADx tools pre-screen studies and prioritize urgent cases.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Critically evaluate the **challenges, ethical issues, and future prospects** of teleoncology implementation in a resource-limited setting like rural Kerala.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

1. **Technical Challenges (2 Marks):**
   - Inconsistent broadband infrastructure in hilly/Wayanad regions.
   - Lack of standardized PACS/HIS interoperability.
   - High initial capital expenditure for WSI scanners and HD video equipment.

2. **Clinical and Ethical Challenges (2 Marks):**
   - **Data privacy and cybersecurity risks** — cancer records are highly sensitive.
   - **Cross-state / cross-border licensing** of oncologists for teleconsultation.
   - **Informed consent** in digital consultations.
   - **Medico-legal liability** in case of misdiagnosis.

3. **Socio-Cultural Challenges (1 Mark):**
   - Digital literacy gaps among elderly patients.
   - Language barriers in Malayalam vs. English medical terminology.
   - Patient reluctance to trust "screen-based" diagnosis.

4. **Future Prospects (2 Marks):**
   - **AI-driven early cancer screening** (e.g., deep learning mammography).
   - **5G-enabled ultra-low-latency remote robotic surgery.**
   - **Decentralized clinical trials** for novel oncology drugs.
   - **Integration with National Digital Health Mission (NDHM)** and Ayushman Bharat for universal cancer care access.
   - **Federated learning** for privacy-preserving AI model training across hospitals.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** Teleoncology = remote delivery of cancer care using ICT.
- **Four layers:** Acquisition → Transmission → Decision → Delivery.
- **Two principal modes:** Synchronous (real-time) and Asynchronous (store-and-forward).
- **Subspecialties:** Telepathology, Teleradiology, Telechemotherapy, Virtual Tumor Boards, Survivorship Care.
- **Key data modalities:** WSI (1–3 GB/slide), DICOM imaging (50–500 MB/study).
- **Recommended codecs:** H.265 (HEVC) for video; JPEG2000 / SVS for WSI.
- **Bandwidth:** 4–6 Mbps for HD video tumor boards; 50–100 Mbps for WSI review.
- **Diagnostic concordance:** Telepathology 92%–96%; Teleradiology 94%–98%.
- **TAT reduction:** From 7–10 days to 24–48 hours for remote second opinions.
- **Cost saving:** Up to 50%–60% in rural spoke-hub models.
- **Standards:** HL7, FHIR, DICOM, IHE (Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise).
- **Coding for KTU exam:** Always quote the *functional role* of each component, not just its name.
- **Mandatory diagram:** A 14-mark question without a block diagram attracts a 2–3 mark penalty.
- **Ethical pillars:** Confidentiality (HIPAA / DISHA equivalent), informed consent, medico-legal accountability.
- **Future tech:** 5G, AI-CADe, federated learning, remote robotic surgery, integration with NDHM.
- **Real-world KTU hook:** Kerala's e-Sanjeevani platform and RCC Thiruvananthapuram hub-and-spoke model are valid KTU examples.

> **End of Module 1 — Teleoncology Notes | PEEBT861 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
