# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Data Rate of a POTS Channel via Shannon-Hartley

**Problem Statement:** A residential POTS line is measured to have a usable bandwidth of 3.1 kHz and a signal-to-noise ratio of 36 dB. Calculate (a) the theoretical maximum channel capacity, and (b) the percentage efficiency compared to a 56 kbps V.92 modem.

**Given:**
- $B = 3.1$ kHz $= 3100$ Hz
- $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 36$ dB
- Modem practical rate $R_{\text{modem}} = 56{,}000$ bps

### Step 1: Convert SNR from dB to Linear Scale

The decibel definition for power ratio is:

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)$$

Rearranging to solve for the linear ratio:

$$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} / 10}$$

Substituting the value:

$$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{36/10} = 10^{3.6}$$

Now we evaluate the exponent:

$$10^{3.6} = 10^3 \cdot 10^{0.6} = 1000 \cdot 3.9811 = 3981.1$$

Thus:

$$\frac{S}{N} \approx 3981$$

### Step 2: Apply the Shannon-Hartley Theorem

The channel capacity formula is:

$$C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

Substituting:

$$C = 3100 \cdot \log_2(1 + 3981) = 3100 \cdot \log_2(3982)$$

### Step 3: Convert $\log_2(3982)$ to a numerical value

Using the change-of-base identity $\log_2(x) = \frac{\ln(x)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{\log_{10}(x)}{\log_{10}(2)}$:

$$\log_2(3982) = \frac{\log_{10}(3982)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{3.6000}{0.30103} = 11.958$$

Therefore:

$$C = 3100 \cdot 11.958 = 37{,}070 \text{ bps}$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 37.07 \text{ kbps}}$$

### Step 4: Compare with V.92 Modem Performance

Modem efficiency is defined as the ratio of practical rate to theoretical capacity:

$$\eta_{\text{modem}} = \frac{R_{\text{modem}}}{C} = \frac{56{,}000}{37{,}070}$$

$$\eta_{\text{modem}} = 1.510 = 151.0\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The "impossible" efficiency >100% is explained by the fact that V.90/V.92 modems use the **digital PCM backbone** of the PSTN. The downstream server is connected to a **digital 64 kbps PCM channel (G.711)** at the CO, so the analog-to-digital quantization noise is avoided. Shannon's theorem applies to a *fully analog* channel; the modem achieves apparent super-Shannon performance by exploiting the *digital* termination of the network.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Transmission Time of a Clinical ECG over POTS

**Problem Statement:** A 12-lead ECG recorder stores a 10-second full-disclosure strip as a 240 kB uncompressed file. The patient transmits it from home via a V.34 modem (33.6 kbps) over POTS to a cardiologist. (a) Calculate the raw transmission time. (b) The file is compressed by a lossless algorithm to 40 kB. Calculate the new transmission time. (c) If the line is 6 km long with 1.1 dB/km attenuation at 1 kHz, what is the received signal attenuation in dB?

**Given:**
- File size $S_1 = 240$ kB $= 240 \cdot 1024 \cdot 8 = 1{,}966{,}080$ bits
- Modem rate $R = 33.6$ kbps $= 33{,}600$ bps
- Compressed size $S_2 = 40$ kB $= 40 \cdot 1024 \cdot 8 = 327{,}680$ bits
- Cable length $L = 6$ km
- Attenuation coefficient $\alpha = 1.1$ dB/km @ 1 kHz

### Part (a): Raw Transmission Time

The fundamental relationship is:

$$T = \frac{\text{File Size in bits}}{\text{Data Rate in bps}}$$

Substituting:

$$T_1 = \frac{1{,}966{,}080}{33{,}600}$$

Performing the long division:

$$T_1 = 58.51 \text{ seconds}$$

$$\boxed{T_1 \approx 58.5 \text{ seconds}}$$

### Part (b): Compressed Transmission Time

Compression ratio is defined as:

$$r_c = \frac{S_2}{S_1} = \frac{40}{240} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 16.67\%$$

The compressed transmission time is:

$$T_2 = \frac{327{,}680}{33{,}600}$$

$$T_2 = 9.752 \text{ seconds}$$

$$\boxed{T_2 \approx 9.75 \text{ seconds}}$$

### Part (c): Line Attenuation (Frequency-Independent Approximation)

The total attenuation in dB is the product of the attenuation coefficient (in dB/km) and the cable length (in km):

$$A_{\text{total}} = \alpha \cdot L$$

Substituting:

$$A_{\text{total}} = 1.1 \text{ dB/km} \cdot 6 \text{ km}$$

$$A_{\text{total}} = 6.6 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{A_{\text{total}} = 6.6 \text{ dB}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 6.6 dB attenuation is well within the POTS attenuation budget (typically 8–10 dB maximum at 1 kHz before load coils are required). At higher voiceband frequencies (e.g., 3 kHz), the attenuation is approximately $\sqrt{3}$ times higher, i.e., ~11.4 dB. This is why **frequency-dependent equalization** is required in modern modems to compensate for the "tilt" of the local loop spectrum.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Number of Voice Channels in a T1/E1 Digital Trunk

**Problem Statement:** Using the G.711 standard (8-bit PCM at 8 kHz sample rate), determine (a) the bit rate of a single voice channel, (b) the total bit rate of a T1 system (24 channels + 1 framing bit per frame), and (c) confirm the E1 standard (32 time slots × 8 bits × 8 kHz).

### Part (a): Single PCM Voice Channel

$$R_{\text{DS0}} = f_s \cdot n = 8000 \cdot 8 = 64{,}000 \text{ bps}$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{DS0}} = 64 \text{ kbps (one DS0 channel)}}$$

### Part (b): T1 System Calculation

A T1 frame has:
- 24 voice channels
- 1 framing bit per frame
- Frame duration = 125 µs (i.e., 8000 frames/sec)

Bits per frame:

$$N_{\text{bits}} = 24 \cdot 8 + 1 = 193 \text{ bits/frame}$$

Total T1 bit rate:

$$R_{T1} = N_{\text{bits}} \cdot f_s = 193 \cdot 8000$$

$$R_{T1} = 1{,}544{,}000 \text{ bps} = 1.544 \text{ Mbps}$$

$$\boxed{R_{T1} = 1.544 \text{ Mbps}}$$

### Part (c): E1 System Calculation

An E1 frame has:
- 32 time slots of 8 bits each
- 30 user channels (TS1–TS15, TS17–TS31)
- 1 signaling channel (TS16)
- 1 framing channel (TS0)

Bits per E1 frame:

$$N_{\text{bits}} = 32 \cdot 8 = 256 \text{ bits/frame}$$

Total E1 bit rate:

$$R_{E1} = 256 \cdot 8000 = 2{,}048{,}000 \text{ bps} = 2.048 \text{ Mbps}$$

$$\boxed{R_{E1} = 2.048 \text{ Mbps}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **E1 vs T1 — Examinable Distinction:** E1 is the **CEPT/ITU-T standard** used in Europe, India (and Kerala/India follows E1 for PSTN), most of Asia, and Africa. T1 is the **AT&T/Bell System standard** used in North America and Japan. E1 carries 30 voice channels vs. T1's 24, but E1 uses **dedicated channel-associated signaling (CAS)** in TS16, whereas T1 uses **bit-robbing** for in-band signaling.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — POTS Limitations vs. Modern Alternatives

This matrix is **directly examinable** in 14-mark KTU questions comparing communication media:

| Parameter | POTS | ISDN (BRI) | DSL (ADSL) | 4G LTE | 5G |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Max data rate | **56 kbps** | **128 kbps** (2B+D) | **24 Mbps** down / 1.4 Mbps up | **100 Mbps** (theoretical) | **10 Gbps** (theoretical) |
| Channel type | Analog + digital core | Fully digital (2B+D) | Digital, discrete multitone | Digital OFDMA | Digital, mmWave/Massive MIMO |
| Switching | Circuit | Circuit | Packet (always-on) | Packet | Packet / network slicing |
| Typical latency | 50–150 ms | 25–50 ms | 15–30 ms | 20–50 ms | <5 ms (URLLC) |
| Mobility support | ❌ (fixed line) | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Video consultation | ❌ | Marginal (≤ 128 kbps) | ✅ (SD) | ✅ (HD) | ✅ (4K/8K) |
| CT/MRI transfer | Hours | ~30 min | ~1 min | ~10 sec | <1 sec |
| Infrastructure cost | Very low (legacy) | Moderate | Moderate | High (spectrum) | Very high |
| Ubiquity in India | ✅ Highest | Limited | High in cities | High | Growing |
| Cybersecurity | Weak (analog tap possible) | Strong (digital) | Strong (digital) | Strong | Very strong |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End POTS Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete POTS infrastructure** from the patient's telemedicine terminal to the remote specialist's hospital workstation, with all major network elements and signal transformations labeled.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PREM_A["Patient Premises (Home / Clinic)"]
        direction TB
        node1["Medical Sensor / ECG / Spirometer / BP Cuff"]
        node2["Patient-Side Modem (V.34 / V.92)"]
        node3["Telephone Handset (POTS Set)"]
        node1 -- "Analog sensor data" --> node2
        node3 -- "Voice (300-3400 Hz)" --> node2
    end

    subgraph LOCAL_A["Local Loop (Access Network)"]
        direction TB
        node4["Twisted-Pair Copper Cable 24-26 AWG"]
        node5["Attenuation ~1.1 dB/km at 1 kHz"]
        node6["Loading Coils (if L > 5.5 km)"]
        node4 --> node5
        node5 --> node6
    end

    subgraph CO_A["Central Office (Class 5 Switch)"]
        direction TB
        node7["Main Distribution Frame MDF"]
        node8["BORSCHT Line Card"]
        node9["PCM Codec G.711 8 kHz Sampling"]
        node10["Time Division Multiplexer TDM"]
        node7 --> node8
        node8 --> node9
        node9 --> node10
    end

    subgraph TRUNK_A["Trunk Network (Inter-Office)"]
        direction TB
        node11["Tandem Switch Class 4"]
        node12["Digital Trunk E1 2.048 Mbps or T1 1.544 Mbps"]
        node13["SS7 Signaling Channel Out-of-Band"]
        node11 --> node12
        node12 --> node13
    end

    subgraph HOSP_A["Specialist Hospital (Remote Site)"]
        direction TB
        node14["Hospital PBX or Direct Exchange Line"]
        node15["Specialist-Side Modem or Fax Machine"]
        node16["Diagnostic Workstation with EHR Software"]
        node14 --> node15
        node15 --> node16
    end

    node2 -- "Analog voiceband signal 3.1 kHz" --> node4
    node6 -- "Filtered, attenuated signal" --> node7
    node10 -- "64 kbps DS0 PCM channel" --> node11
    node13 -- "E1 or T1 bearer to destination" --> node14
    node16 -- "Clinical interpretation and diagnosis" --> node1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flow moves from **left (patient)** to **right (specialist)**. Notice that the **analog-to-digital boundary** is located at the **Central Office** (the G.711 codec). This is why the channel is "mixed" — the local loop is analog, the trunk network is digital, and the destination local loop is analog again. The **modem must fight against quantization noise and codec distortions** that the analog channel imposes twice in the path.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    trunkBus -- "Inbound from TDM" --> fnT
```

## 4.3 POTS Channel Bandwidth Allocation (Spectral Block Diagram)

The following diagram shows the **frequency spectrum allocation** of a POTS channel and the **adjacent channels** in a TDM/FDM hierarchy — this is the basis for understanding why **xDSL coexists with POTS** on the same twisted pair.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPEC_A["POTS Local-Loop Spectrum 0 Hz to 1.1 MHz"]
        direction LR
        spA["0 to 25 Hz - DC and 20 Hz Ringing Power"]
        spB["200 to 500 Hz - Dial Pulse Signaling"]
        spC["300 to 3400 Hz - Voiceband Speech 3.1 kHz"]
        spD["3400 to 4000 Hz - Voiceband Guard Band"]
        spE["4000 to 26000 Hz - Unused Silence Gap"]
        spF["26 kHz to 138 kHz - ADSL Upstream"]
        spG["138 kHz to 552 kHz - ADSL Downstream"]
        spH["552 kHz to 1.1 MHz - VDSL2 Upstream and Downstream"]
    end

    spA --- spB --- spC --- spD --- spE --- spF --- spG --- spH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The POTS band and the ADSL band are **separated by a guard region (4–26 kHz)**. This is why a **low-pass splitter (LPF)** can be installed at the subscriber premises to **isolate** the POTS service from the DSL service on the **same physical twisted pair**. The POTS signal occupies only the bottom 4 kHz; everything above 26 kHz is available for high-speed digital subscriber line (DSL) transmission.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define POTS. State any four limitations of POTS for modern telemedicine applications.**

**Model Answer:**

**POTS Definition:** Plain Old Telephone System (POTS), also known as the Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN), is the traditional analog circuit-switched telecommunication network that uses twisted-pair copper cables in the local loop and a hierarchical switching architecture to provide voice and low-speed data services. It is band-limited to **300 Hz – 3400 Hz** for voice transmission.

**Four Limitations for Modern Telemedicine:**

1. **Bandwidth Limitation:** Maximum practical data rate is **56 kbps** (V.92 modem). Insufficient for real-time video consultation, high-resolution medical imaging (CT, MRI, full-resolution X-ray), and high-fidelity multi-channel biosignal streaming.

2. **Circuit-Switched Architecture:** A dedicated physical channel must be reserved end-to-end for the entire call duration, even for bursty, packet-based medical data. This is inefficient and economically wasteful for store-and-forward transfers.

3. **Latency and Jitter:** End-to-end propagation through tandem switches, A/D–D/A conversions, and echo cancellers introduces **50–150 ms latency** with non-trivial jitter, which compromises real-time physiological signal monitoring (e.g., remote auscultation, Doppler ultrasound).

4. **No Native Mobility:** POTS is a fixed-line technology tied to a physical address. It cannot serve patients in transit, ambulances, or rural settings without pre-installed copper infrastructure.

5. *(Additional, optional)* **Security Vulnerabilities:** The analog local loop is susceptible to **line tapping, eavesdropping, and inductive cross-talk**, posing risks to patient data confidentiality under regulations like the IT Act (India) and HIPAA (US).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the BORSCHT functions performed at a POTS Central Office.**

**Model Answer:**

The acronym **BORSCHT** represents the seven essential functions that every Central Office (CO) line card must perform to interface between the digital switching fabric of the PSTN and the analog subscriber line:

1. **B — Battery Feeding:** The CO provides a **–48 V DC** power supply over the twisted pair to energize the subscriber's telephone instrument (microphone biasing, signaling detection).

2. **O — Over-Voltage Protection:** Protects the CO electronics from lightning strikes, AC mains cross, and other transients using **varistors (MOVs) and gas discharge tubes**.

3. **R — Ringing:** A **90 V AC signal at 20 Hz** is superimposed on the line (with DC offset) to ring the subscriber's bell, alerting the user to an incoming call.

4. **S — Signaling:** Detection of **on-hook / off-hook transitions**, **dial pulses** (rotary), and **DTMF tones** (touch-tone) to determine call origination and destination address.

5. **C — Coding:** **A/D and D/A conversion** using the **G.711 PCM codec** (8-bit samples at 8 kHz, yielding 64 kbps per voice channel) for digital trunk transmission.

6. **H — Hybrid:** **2-wire to 4-wire conversion** to separate the transmit and receive directions on the bidirectional local loop, with **echo cancellation** to prevent the far-end speaker's voice from being heard as an echo.

7. **T — Testing:** Remote **loopback, BERT (Bit Error Rate Testing), and line diagnostics** so that a technician at the CO can isolate faults (open, short, leakage to ground) without dispatch.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part A):** Many students list only **4 or 5** of the 7 BORSCHT functions and lose 1–2 marks. **Memorize all seven** in correct order. The order is canonical — it is the *exact* sequence in which these functions appear in the line card's signal path. A common mistake is to swap "Coding" and "Hybrid," which is technically incorrect because coding (A/D) must occur on the 4-wire side of the hybrid.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Bank]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the **complete POTS infrastructure** from a patient's telemedicine terminal to a remote specialist hospital, identifying all major network elements and signal transformations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A POTS voiceband channel has a measured signal-to-noise ratio of **30 dB** and a usable bandwidth of **3.1 kHz**. Calculate:
   (i) The **theoretical maximum channel capacity** in kbps using the Shannon-Hartley theorem.
   (ii) The **practical transmission time** for a **360 kB ECG file** if a V.34 modem (33.6 kbps) is used, assuming no compression.
   (iii) Comment on the **clinical feasibility** of transmitting a **full-resolution chest X-ray (10 MB)** over the same modem link. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) — POTS Infrastructure (7 marks)

The POTS infrastructure consists of **five functional segments** traversed in sequence by a telemedicine signal:

1. **Customer Premises Equipment (CPE):** The patient's home or clinic contains a **medical sensor** (e.g., ECG machine, electronic stethoscope, BP monitor) connected to a **modem** (V.34/V.92 standard). A standard **POTS telephone handset** is bridged on the same line for voice. The modem converts the digital biosignal into an analog audio waveform confined to the 300–3400 Hz voiceband.

2. **Local Loop (Access Network):** A **twisted-pair copper cable** (24–26 AWG) of length 1–6 km connects the CPE to the Central Office. The signal suffers **attenuation** (≈ 1.1 dB/km at 1 kHz) and **crosstalk** (NEXT, FEXT). For lines longer than 5.5 km, **loading coils** are inserted at 1.8 km intervals to flatten the frequency response.

3. **Central Office (Class 5 End Office):** Houses the **Main Distribution Frame (MDF)** for physical termination, **BORSCHT line cards** for line interface, **PCM codecs (G.711)** for A/D conversion at 8 kHz × 8 bits = 64 kbps per voice channel, and a **TDM multiplexer** to aggregate multiple voice channels onto a single digital trunk (E1 or T1).

4. **Trunk Network (Inter-Office Links):** A **hierarchical** system of **Tandem (Class 4)** and higher-order switches interconnected by **digital trunks** (E1 = 2.048 Mbps, 30 channels; T1 = 1.544 Mbps, 24 channels). **SS7 (Signaling System No. 7)** provides out-of-band call setup, teardown, and routing.

5. **Specialist Hospital:** Terminates on a **PBX (Private Branch Exchange)** or a direct **DEL (Direct Exchange Line)** connected to a **specialist-side modem** (or fax machine) feeding the **diagnostic workstation** running the hospital's **EHR (Electronic Health Record) software**.

*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for a visual rendering of this architecture.)*

##### Part (b) — Numerical Computations (7 marks)

**Given:** $B = 3.1$ kHz, $\text{SNR} = 30$ dB, file size $= 360$ kB, modem rate $R = 33.6$ kbps, X-ray size $= 10$ MB.

###### (i) Shannon Channel Capacity (3 marks)

Step 1: Convert SNR from dB to linear:

$$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000$$

Step 2: Apply Shannon-Hartley:

$$C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

$$C = 3100 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000) = 3100 \cdot \log_2(1001)$$

Evaluating the logarithm:

$$\log_2(1001) = \frac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{3.0004}{0.30103} = 9.967$$

Therefore:

$$C = 3100 \cdot 9.967 = 30{,}898 \text{ bps}$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 30.9 \text{ kbps}}$$

###### (ii) Transmission Time for 360 kB ECG (2 marks)

Convert file size to bits:

$$\text{File Size} = 360 \text{ kB} \cdot 1024 \cdot 8 = 2{,}949{,}120 \text{ bits}$$

Transmission time:

$$T = \frac{2{,}949{,}120}{33{,}600} = 87.77 \text{ seconds}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 87.8 \text{ seconds} \approx 1 \text{ min } 28 \text{ sec}}$$

**Clinical Feasibility:** A transmission time of ~88 seconds is **acceptable** for store-and-forward ECG. The cardiologist can review the trace within 2 minutes of the patient pressing "send" — clinically useful for a follow-up consultation.

###### (iii) Feasibility of 10 MB X-ray over V.34 Modem (2 marks)

Convert X-ray size to bits:

$$\text{File Size} = 10 \text{ MB} \cdot 1024 \cdot 1024 \cdot 8 = 83{,}886{,}080 \text{ bits}$$

Transmission time:

$$T = \frac{83{,}886{,}080}{33{,}600} = 2496.6 \text{ seconds}$$

$$T \approx 41.6 \text{ minutes}$$

**Clinical Feasibility:** A 41-minute transmission time is **clinically unacceptable** for urgent or emergent radiology. A full-resolution X-ray requires **broadband infrastructure** (DSL, fiber, 4G/5G) for timely transfer. Over POTS, only **heavily compressed low-resolution thumbnails** (e.g., 256×256, 8-bit, <100 kB) can be transferred within clinically reasonable time windows.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks (Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Shannon-Hartley theorem** as applied to the POTS voiceband channel. Derive the theoretical maximum data rate for a POTS line with **40 dB SNR** and **3.1 kHz bandwidth**, and compare it with the practical V.92 modem rate of 56 kbps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of POTS in telemedicine** with at least **four specific clinical applications**, clearly stating the bandwidth requirement of each application and justifying whether POTS can support it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) — Shannon-Hartley Analysis (7 marks)

**Shannon-Hartley Theorem Statement (3 marks):**

The Shannon-Hartley theorem, formulated by **Claude Shannon (1948)**, states that the **maximum error-free data rate** (channel capacity) that can be transmitted over a band-limited communication channel in the presence of **additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN)** is given by:

$$C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{[bps]}$$

Where:
- $C$ = channel capacity in bits per second
- $B$ = channel bandwidth in Hertz
- $S$ = average signal power
- $N$ = average noise power (within the channel bandwidth)
- $S/N$ = signal-to-noise power ratio (dimensionless)

**Key Engineering Insights:**
- Capacity grows **linearly** with bandwidth $B$.
- Capacity grows only **logarithmically** with SNR.
- The theorem is an **upper bound** — it is unattainable in practice but can be approached arbitrarily closely using sophisticated coding (e.g., Turbo codes, LDPC codes, TCM).
- It assumes a **channel with AWGN**, which is a reasonable model for the POTS voiceband after filtering and equalization.

**Numerical Derivation (4 marks):**

Given: $B = 3.1$ kHz, SNR = 40 dB.

Step 1: Convert SNR to linear:

$$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{40/10} = 10^4 = 10{,}000$$

Step 2: Substitute into Shannon-Hartley:

$$C = 3100 \cdot \log_2(1 + 10{,}000) = 3100 \cdot \log_2(10{,}001)$$

Step 3: Evaluate $\log_2(10{,}001)$:

$$\log_2(10{,}001) = \frac{\log_{10}(10{,}001)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{4.00004}{0.30103} = 13.288$$

Step 4: Compute capacity:

$$C = 3100 \cdot 13.288 = 41{,}193 \text{ bps}$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 41.2 \text{ kbps}}$$

**Comparison with V.92 Modem (additional credit, 1 mark):**

The V.92 modem achieves **56 kbps** in the downstream direction, which **exceeds** the Shannon limit of 41.2 kbps computed above. This apparent paradox is resolved by recognizing that:
- Shannon's theorem applies to a **fully analog channel** with AWGN.
- In V.90/V.92, the **modem server (e.g., ISP) is connected digitally** to the CO via a 64 kbps PCM (G.711) channel.
- Therefore, the channel from server to subscriber has **only one A/D conversion** (at the CO) and is not bound by the analog local loop's SNR.
- The 64 kbps is reduced to 56 kbps because of **robbed-bit signaling, framing, and codec padding** in the PSTN.
- The **upstream** direction (subscriber to CO) is still analog and is bounded by Shannon's theorem to ~33.6 kbps (V.34).

##### Part (b) — POTS in Telemedicine: Clinical Applications (7 marks)

**Application 1: Synchronous Voice Teleconsultation** *(2 marks)*
- **Bandwidth requirement:** 64 kbps (PCM G.711) or 8 kbps (low-bit-rate vocoder like G.729)
- **POTS suitability:** ✅ **Excellent — natively supported.** POTS was designed for voice; the 3.1 kHz voiceband is more than sufficient. End-to-end latency <150 ms (per ITU-T G.114) ensures natural conversation. Used universally for triage, follow-up, and psychiatric teleconsultation.

**Application 2: 12-Lead ECG Store-and-Forward** *(2 marks)*
- **Bandwidth requirement:** ~200–300 kbps for real-time streaming; ~30 kB per 10-sec strip for store-and-forward
- **POTS suitability:** ✅ **Feasible.** At 33.6 kbps (V.34 modem), a 240 kB ECG file transfers in ~58 seconds. Compression (e.g., wavelet) reduces this to <10 seconds. Suitable for cardiology referrals in rural primary health centers (PHCs) lacking broadband.

**Application 3: Asynchronous Teleradiology — Low Resolution** *(1.5 marks)*
- **Bandwidth requirement:** 100 kB – 1 MB for a low-resolution 512×512 grayscale chest X-ray
- **POTS suitability:** ⚠ **Marginal.** A 512×512 × 8-bit = 256 kB X-ray takes ~60 seconds at 33.6 kbps. Diagnostic accuracy is **compromised** by the loss of fine detail. Acceptable for **screening triage** (e.g., TB detection in mass screening) but not for definitive diagnosis.

**Application 4: Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM) of Chronic Disease** *(1.5 marks)*
- **Bandwidth requirement:** <1 kbps for periodic uploads of BP, glucose, SpO₂, weight
- **POTS suitability:** ✅ **Excellent.** Data volumes are trivial; an "always-on" copper pair can transmit hourly vitals using a simple modem or **acoustic coupler**. Particularly useful in elderly care and congestive heart failure (CHF) home monitoring, where broadband internet is unavailable.

**Conclusion:** POTS serves as a **critical fallback and primary medium in low-resource settings**. For modern high-bandwidth telemedicine (real-time video, full-resolution radiology), broadband media (DSL, 4G/5G) are essential. POTS therefore remains a **complementary** rather than **primary** infrastructure in 2024 and beyond.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B 14-mark):** When answering "Role of POTS in Telemedicine" questions, students frequently lose marks by:
> 1. **Listing applications without stating bandwidth requirements** — the examiner allocates marks specifically for the *numerical* justification.
> 2. **Failing to distinguish between voice (synchronous) and data (asynchronous) applications** — these have fundamentally different network requirements.
> 3. **Ignoring the comparator** — the question is implicitly asking you to *justify* POTS's role against modern alternatives. A balanced answer that says *"POTS is sufficient for X but inadequate for Y"* scores higher than a one-sided "POTS is great" or "POTS is obsolete" answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print or memorize this checklist before every POTS-related exam revision:**

- ✅ **POTS = Plain Old Telephone System = Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN)** — they refer to the same infrastructure. POTS emphasizes the *subscriber-facing* legacy nature; PSTN emphasizes the *network-switching* architecture.
- ✅ **Voiceband = 300 Hz – 3400 Hz, Bandwidth B ≈ 3.1 kHz, Channel spacing = 4 kHz.** This is **the single most important numerical fact** in the entire topic.
- ✅ **Shannon Capacity formula:** $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)$. Always state the unit of $C$ as **bits per second (bps)**, never "bits."
- ✅ **Modem practical ceiling: 56 kbps (V.92 downstream) / 48 kbps (V.92 upstream) / 33.6 kbps (V.34 symmetric).** Modem data rates are *practical* engineering achievements, not Shannon limits.
- ✅ **Central Office battery = –48 V DC; Ringing voltage = 90 V AC @ 20 Hz; Nominal line impedance = 600 Ω.** These three values are **standard KTU numerical facts.**
- ✅ **BORSCHT = Battery, Over-voltage, Ringing, Signaling, Coding, Hybrid, Testing.** Memorize in this exact order. All seven are functions of the **CO line card**, not the subscriber's telephone.
- ✅ **E1 = 2.048 Mbps, 30 user channels; T1 = 1.544 Mbps, 24 channels.** India uses **E1**.
- ✅ **PCM (G.711) = 8-bit samples × 8 kHz = 64 kbps per voice channel (DS0).** This is the basic building block of the digital PSTN backbone.
- ✅ **DTMF uses 8 frequencies: 4 low (697, 770, 852, 941 Hz) and 4 high (1209, 1336, 1477, 1633 Hz) → 16 unique key codes.**
- ✅ **Twisted-pair attenuation ≈ 1.1 dB/km at 1 kHz; grows with $\sqrt{f}$ (frequency-dependent).**
- ✅ **POTS local loop is analog; PSTN trunk is digital; transition happens at the CO via G.711 codec.**
- ✅ **In telemedicine, POTS supports: voice triage, ECG/EEG/spirometry store-and-forward (small files), fax, RPM. POTS does NOT support: real-time video, full-resolution teleradiology, CT/MRI, real-time ultrasound.**
- ✅ **For exam, always frame POTS as the *baseline technology* — its limitations motivate the need for ISDN, DSL, and broadband in telemedicine evolution.**
- ✅ **Latency bound (ITU-T G.114): one-way mouth-to-ear delay ≤ 150 ms for acceptable voice quality.**
- ✅ **ADSL coexists with POTS on the same twisted pair using a low-pass splitter** — POTS occupies 0–4 kHz, ADSL occupies 26 kHz – 1.1 MHz.
- ✅ **Local loop maximum length before load coils: ~5.5 km (~18,000 ft).** Beyond this, voice quality degrades severely.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ISDN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT861 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** ISDN

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Integrated Services Digital Network (ISDN)** is a set of communication standards defined by the **ITU-T (International Telecommunication Union – Telecommunication Standardization Sector)** under the **I-series recommendations**, designed to provide a unified, end-to-end **digital transmission medium** for the simultaneous carriage of **voice, video, data, and signaling** information over the traditional copper-wire circuits of the Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN).

In the context of **Telemedicine (PEEBT861)**, ISDN represents one of the earliest **circuit-switched digital backbone technologies** that enabled reliable, low-latency, real-time transmission of medical imagery, video consultations, and remote patient monitoring data between geographically separated healthcare facilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** ISDN is grouped under "Telecommunication Infrastructure for Telemedicine." Students must clearly distinguish between **Narrowband ISDN (N-ISDN)** and **Broadband ISDN (B-ISDN)**, and understand the role of the **B, D, and H channels** in supporting medical data flows.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the traditional telephone line as a **single narrow, one-lane country road** that can only carry one slow-moving vehicle (a voice call) at a time. To modernize it, the telecom engineers decided to **widen the road, pave it perfectly smooth, and add multiple dedicated lanes with strict traffic rules**:

- **Two main lanes (B channels)** carry the "payload" — the actual medical video, voice, or file data.
- **One small service lane (D channel)** carries only the "traffic police signals" — dial-up, call setup, and teardown instructions, kept separate so it never gets jammed.
- The "smooth digital pavement" means the analog noise and distortion of old copper lines is eliminated, giving **clearer medical images and crisper voice for telehealth consultations**.

So, **ISDN = Digital road + Multiple lanes (B/D channels) + Strict signaling rules (SS7/DSS1)**, all running on the same old copper pair — a perfect metaphor for telemedicine's need for **digital clarity + simultaneous multi-service carriage**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics (must memorize for KTU):**
> - **B (Bearer) Channel = 64 kbps**
> - **D (Delta/Delta-channel) Channel = 16 kbps (BRI)** or **64 kbps (PRI)**
> - **BRI Total = 144 kbps (2B + D)** plus 48 kbps framing overhead = **192 kbps physical**
> - **PRI (E1, Europe/India) = 2.048 Mbps (30B + D)**
> - **PRI (T1, USA/Japan) = 1.544 Mbps (23B + D)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Evolution and Classification of ISDN

ISDN evolved through a clearly traceable generational hierarchy. For KTU, students must remember the **three-tier classification**:

1. **Narrowband ISDN (N-ISDN)** — Based on **64 kbps channelization**, uses copper pairs, up to PRI (2.048 Mbps). Standardized in the 1980s.
2. **Broadband ISDN (B-ISDN)** — Based on **ATM (Asynchronous Transfer Mode)** with fiber optics, supports rates from **155 Mbps to 622 Mbps** and beyond. Standardized in the 1990s (ITU-T I.121, I.150, I.361).
3. **IP-based successor (technically not ISDN)** — VoIP and MPLS have largely replaced ISDN, but the **conceptual channelized QoS guarantee** lives on.

### 2.2 The ISDN Channel Structure — The "Why" and "How"

| Channel Type | Bandwidth | Purpose | Why it exists in Telemedicine |
|---|---|---|---|
| **B (Bearer)** | **64 kbps** | User data: voice, video, file transfer | One B for live voice, second B for medical image or motion video |
| **D (Delta)** | **16 kbps / 64 kbps** | Out-of-band signaling (call setup, caller ID) | Keeps clinical voice channel always free of setup clicks |
| **H0** | **384 kbps** (6B) | High-speed data, fast facsimile | Bulk transfer of radiological images |
| **H10** | **1472 kbps** | High-quality audio/video | Telesurgery video (rare, experimental) |
| **H11** | **1536 kbps** | Same as above (T1 framing) | Used in North American PRI |
| **H12** | **1920 kbps** | Same as above (E1 framing) | Used in European/Indian PRI |

### 2.3 ISDN Interfaces — The Access Points

A student must know the **five reference points** (R, S, T, U, V) and **two main interfaces**:

- **BRI (Basic Rate Interface) = 2B + D** → For **residential / small clinic** use.
- **PRI (Primary Rate Interface) = 30B + D (E1)** or **23B + D (T1)** → For **hospitals / large telemedicine centers**.

### 2.4 ISDN Reference Model (ITU-T I.320)

The ISDN architecture is divided into **three planes** and **functional groupings**:

1. **User Plane (U-plane)** — Transfers user information (voice, video, medical files).
2. **Control Plane (C-plane)** — Handles signaling using **DSS1 (Digital Subscriber Signaling No. 1)** or **SS7 (Signaling System No. 7)**.
3. **Management Plane (M-plane)** — Manages connections, performance, and fault handling.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **must-memorize** cheat sheet for ISDN numerics. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve Markdown table integrity.

| Quantity | Formula | Value / Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| B-Channel Data Rate | Fixed by PCM standard | **64 kbps** | Derived from 8-bit × 8 kHz PCM sampling |
| BRI Payload | $2B + D$ | $2(64) + 16 = 144$ **kbps** | Pure data only |
| BRI Physical Line Rate | $2B + D + \text{Framing}$ | $144 + 48 = 192$ **kbps** | 48 kbps = 2B + D + sync bits |
| PRI (E1) Data | $30B + D$ | $30(64) + 64 = 1984$ **kbps** | Pure payload |
| PRI (E1) Line Rate | $30B + D + \text{Framing}$ | $1984 + 64 = 2048$ **kbps = 2.048 Mbps** | Standard E1 |
| PRI (T1) Data | $23B + D$ | $23(64) + 64 = 1536$ **kbps** | Pure payload |
| PRI (T1) Line Rate | $23B + D + \text{Framing}$ | $1536 + 8 = 1544$ **kbps = 1.544 Mbps** | Standard T1 |
| H0 Channels | $6B$ | $6(64) = 384$ **kbps** | High-speed |
| H10 | $23B$ | $23(64) = 1472$ **kbps** | Fits in T1 |
| H11 | $24B$ | $24(64) = 1536$ **kbps** | T1 native |
| H12 | $30B$ | $30(64) = 1920$ **kbps** | Fits in E1 |
| PCM Sampling Rate | Nyquist for 4 kHz voice | **8 kHz** | 8000 samples/sec |
| PCM Bits/Sample | Companding standard | **8 bits** | $\mu$-law / A-law |
| B-Channel Bit Rate | $f_s \times n$ | $8000 \times 8 = 64{,}000$ **bps** | Foundation of all ISDN |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Telemedicine Engineering

- **Teleradiology (1990s–2000s):** A single B-channel carried control/voice; the second B carried compressed DICOM images. A **384 kbps H0** could transmit a chest X-ray in seconds.
- **Live Teleconsultation:** ISDN BRI's **128 kbps aggregate payload** (2 × 64) became the de facto standard for **H.320 video conferencing codecs** used between district hospitals and medical colleges.
- **PACS Connectivity:** PRI lines linked Picture Archiving and Communication Systems across hospital campuses, providing deterministic, low-jitter links for image retrieval.
- **Home Telemonitoring:** BRI's always-on D-channel enabled low-bandwidth vital-sign telemetry without disturbing the B-channels.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Origin of the 64 kbps B-Channel

The **64 kbps** number is not arbitrary — it falls directly out of **PCM (Pulse Code Modulation)** theory for digitizing a 4 kHz voice band.

**Step 1: Voice Bandwidth**
Human speech intelligibility requires frequencies from approximately **300 Hz to 3400 Hz**, a band of roughly **4 kHz**.

**Step 2: Apply the Nyquist Sampling Theorem**
The minimum sampling rate must be **twice the highest frequency** in the signal:

$$f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max}$$

Substituting the voice channel's 4 kHz:

$$f_s \geq 2 \times 4000 \text{ Hz} = 8000 \text{ Hz}$$

So we sample voice at **8 kHz (8000 samples per second)**.

**Step 3: Quantize Each Sample to 8 Bits**
Standard PCM uses **8 bits per sample** (256 quantization levels using A-law in Europe/India, μ-law in North America).

**Step 4: Multiply to Get Bit Rate**

$$R_B = f_s \times n = 8000 \text{ samples/s} \times 8 \text{ bits/sample}$$

$$R_B = 64{,}000 \text{ bits per second} = 64 \text{ kbps}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 64 kbps figure is the **atomic unit of ISDN** — every B-channel, every PRI, and every H-channel is an **integer multiple of 64 kbps**. KTU examiners love to ask: "Why 64 kbps and not 56 kbps?" The answer is precisely the derivation above.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: BRI Line Rate (192 kbps)

**Step 1: Compute pure payload.**

$$R_{\text{payload, BRI}} = 2B + D = 2(64) + 16 = 144 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 2: Add framing overhead.**
The BRI uses **4 kbps per B-channel for framing/synchronization** plus **extra D-channel bits**. The total overhead is **48 kbps** per the ITU-T I.430 standard.

**Step 3: Total physical line rate.**

$$R_{\text{BRI, line}} = 144 \text{ kbps} + 48 \text{ kbps} = 192 \text{ kbps}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: PRI (E1) Line Rate (2.048 Mbps)

**Step 1: Pure data payload for E1 (European/Indian) PRI.**

$$R_{\text{payload, E1}} = 30B + D = 30(64) + 64 = 1920 + 64 = 1984 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 2: Add framing.** E1 uses **32 time slots of 8 bits each, sampled at 8 kHz**. Of the 32 slots, 30 carry B-channels, 1 carries the D-channel, and 1 is used for **synchronization/framing**.

**Step 3: Compute total.**

$$R_{\text{E1}} = 32 \times 8 \text{ bits} \times 8000 \text{ samples/s}$$

$$R_{\text{E1}} = 256 \times 8000 = 2{,}048{,}000 \text{ bps} = 2.048 \text{ Mbps}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: PRI (T1) Line Rate (1.544 Mbps)

**Step 1: T1 uses 24 time slots** (23B + 1D-signaling — note: T1 historically used **bit-robbing** for signaling, but ISDN PRI T1 dedicates one full D-channel).

**Step 2: Each frame = 24 × 8 = 192 bits, plus 1 framing bit = 193 bits per frame**, sampled at 8 kHz.

**Step 3:**

$$R_{\text{T1}} = 193 \times 8000 = 1{,}544{,}000 \text{ bps} = 1.544 \text{ Mbps}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparing N-ISDN and B-ISDN

| Parameter | N-ISDN | B-ISDN |
|---|---|---|
| Standardization era | 1980s | Late 1980s – 1990s |
| Channelization | **64 kbps** TDM | **53-byte** ATM cells |
| Transmission medium | Copper (existing PSTN) | **Fiber optics** (SONET/SDH) |
| Data rate | Up to **2.048 Mbps** (PRI) | **155 Mbps / 622 Mbps** and beyond |
| Switching | **Circuit switching** | **Fast packet / ATM cell switching** |
| User data unit | 8-bit PCM sample | 48-byte payload + 5-byte header |
| QoS support | Limited, fixed | Strong (CBR, VBR, ABR, UBR) |
| Recommendation | ITU-T I.430, I.431 | ITU-T I.121, I.150, I.361, I.363 |
| Telemedicine use | Teleradiology, H.320 video | High-resolution telesurgery video |
| Status | Largely **legacy / sunset** | Replaced by Ethernet/IP/MPLS |

### 3.6 Capacity Planning for a Telemedicine Centre (Example Walkthrough)

> **Problem:** A district telemedicine centre needs to support **5 simultaneous teleconsultation sessions**, each requiring **2B (128 kbps) for H.320 video + 1 B reserved for medical data**. How many PRI lines are required?

**Step 1:** Each session consumes 3 B-channels = $3 \times 64 = 192$ **kbps**.

**Step 2:** Five sessions = $5 \times 3 = 15$ **B-channels**.

**Step 3:** Add 1 D-channel for the PRI: $15B + D$ → fits comfortably inside a **single E1 PRI (30B + D)**.

**Step 4:** Headroom = $30 - 15 = 15$ free B-channels for future growth (e.g., teleradiology, EMR sync).

**Conclusion:** **One E1 PRI line suffices**, with 50% spare capacity. This is the kind of planning question KTU examiners use to test applied understanding.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ISDN Channelization Block Diagram (BRI & PRI)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BRI["BRI 2B PLUS D 192 kbps"]
        B1["B1 64 kbps VOICE"]
        B2["B2 64 kbps VIDEO OR DATA"]
        D1["D 16 kbps SIGNALING"]
    end

    subgraph PRIE1["PRI E1 30B PLUS D 2.048 Mbps"]
        BE1["B1 to B30 64 kbps EACH 30 SLOTS"]
        DE1["D 64 kbps TS16 SIGNALING"]
        FE1["TS0 64 kbps FRAMING"]
    end

    subgraph PRIT1["PRI T1 23B PLUS D 1.544 Mbps"]
        BT1["B1 to B23 64 kbps EACH 23 SLOTS"]
        DT1["D 64 kbps SIGNALING"]
        FT1["F 8 kbps FRAMING"]
    end

    CLINIC["DISTRICT CLINIC H.320 CODEC"] --> B1
    CLINIC --> B2
    CLINIC --> D1

    HOSP["TERTIARY HOSPITAL PRI LINE"] --> BE1
    HOSP --> DE1
    HOSP --> FE1

    USH["US CLINIC T1"] --> BT1
    USH --> DT1
    USH --> FT1
```

### 4.2 ISDN Protocol Reference Model (ITU-T I.320)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PLANE_U["U PLANE USER INFORMATION"]
        U1["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL 2B1Q OR 4B3T LINE CODE"]
        U2["LAYER 2 LAPD ON D CHANNEL"]
        U3["LAYER 3 USER DATA VOICE VIDEO FILES"]
    end

    subgraph PLANE_C["C PLANE CONTROL SIGNALING"]
        C1["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL D CHANNEL"]
        C2["LAYER 2 LAPD Q.921"]
        C3["LAYER 3 DSS1 Q.931 OR SS7"]
    end

    subgraph PLANE_M["M PLANE MANAGEMENT"]
        M1["CONNECTION MANAGEMENT"]
        M2["PERFORMANCE MONITORING"]
        M3["FAULT AND ERROR HANDLING"]
    end

    PLANE_U --> PLANE_C
    PLANE_C --> PLANE_M
```

### 4.3 ISDN Reference Points (R, S, T, U, V)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TE1["TE1 ISDN TERMINAL EG CODEC"] --- R
    R["R REFERENCE POINT"] --- TA
    TA["TA TERMINAL ADAPTER FOR NON ISDN DEVICE"] --- S
    S["S REFERENCE POINT 4 WIRE BUS"] --- NT2
    NT2["NT2 NETWORK TERMINATION 2 PBX OR ROUTER"] --- T
    T["T REFERENCE POINT"] --- NT1
    NT1["NT1 NETWORK TERMINATION 1"] --- U
    U["U REFERENCE POINT 2 WIRE COPPER LOOP"] --- LT
    LT["LT LINE TERMINATOR EXCHANGE SIDE"] --- V
    V["V REFERENCE POINT"]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: ISDN-Based Telemedicine Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PATIENT["PATIENT AT REMOTE CLINIC"] --> CAM["MEDICAL CAMERA ECG STETHOSCOPE"]
    CAM --> CODEC["H.320 VIDEO CODEC ISDN BRI"]
    CODEC -->|2B PLUS D 144 kbps| NTU1["NT1 AT CLINIC"]
    NTU1 -->|COPPER PAIR| LE["LOCAL EXCHANGE LE"]
    LE --> PSTN["PSTN ISDN BACKBONE"]
    PSTN --> PRIE1["PRI E1 30B PLUS D 2.048 Mbps AT HOSPITAL"]
    PRIE1 --> NTU2["NT1 AT HOSPITAL"]
    NTU2 --> PBX["HOSPITAL PBX NT2"]
    PBX --> SPEC["SPECIALIST WORKSTATION H.320 DECODER"]
    SPEC --> PACS["PACS DICOM SERVER"]
    SPEC --> EMR["HOSPITAL EMR SYSTEM"]
```

### 4.5 N-ISDN vs B-ISDN Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph N["N ISDN CIRCUIT SWITCHED 64 KBPS TDM"]
        N1["COPPER PAIR 2 WIRE"]
        N2["NT1 ISDN MODEM"]
        N3["CHANNELIZED 2B PLUS D OR 30B PLUS D"]
        N4["EXCHANGE CIRCUIT SWITCH MATRIX"]
    end

    subgraph B["B ISDN ATM CELL BASED FIBER"]
        B1["FIBER OPTIC OR SONET SDH"]
        B2["ATM ADAPTATION LAYER AAL 1 2 3 4 5"]
        B3["53 BYTE CELLS 48 BYTE PAYLOAD PLUS 5 BYTE HEADER"]
        B4["ATM SWITCH WITH VPI VCI ROUTING"]
    end

    N --> B
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define ISDN. List the channel types used in BRI with their data rates.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **ISDN (Integrated Services Digital Network)** is a set of ITU-T standards (I-series) that provide end-to-end digital connectivity for the simultaneous transmission of voice, video, and data over the PSTN.

**Channel types in BRI (Basic Rate Interface, 2B + D):**
- **2 × B (Bearer) channels**, each **64 kbps**, used for user data (voice, video, medical files).
- **1 × D (Delta) channel**, **16 kbps**, used for out-of-band signaling (call setup, teardown).

**Total payload = 2(64) + 16 = 144 kbps**; with 48 kbps framing overhead, the physical line rate is **192 kbps**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Differentiate between B-ISDN and N-ISDN.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | N-ISDN | B-ISDN |
|---|---|---|
| Data rate | Up to 2.048 Mbps (PRI) | 155 Mbps – 622 Mbps+ |
| Switching | Circuit switching | ATM cell switching |
| Medium | Copper | Fiber |
| Channel unit | 64 kbps (B) channel | 53-byte ATM cell |
| Telemedicine role | H.320 video, teleradiology | High-res telesurgery video |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of ISDN with a neat block diagram showing the B, D, and H channels. Discuss the role of each channel in telemedicine applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hospital plans to set up an ISDN PRI (E1) link connecting its telemedicine centre to a regional medical college. Calculate the number of B-channels available for medical data, the total data payload, and the physical line rate. If 12 B-channels are reserved for live H.320 teleconsultation and 6 B-channels for bulk PACS image transfer, how many B-channels remain free? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ISDN Architecture (7 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** ISDN provides end-to-end digital transmission integrating voice, data, and video over PSTN using standardized channel structures (ITU-T I-series).
2. **Channel structure (2 marks):** 
   - **B channel – 64 kbps:** carries user information; in telemedicine, one B for voice, one for video/data, supporting H.320 codecs.
   - **D channel – 16 kbps (BRI) or 64 kbps (PRI):** carries out-of-band signaling using LAPD (Q.921) and DSS1 (Q.931), keeping the clinical B-channels interference-free.
   - **H channels (H0 = 384 kbps, H12 = 1920 kbps):** used for bulk transfer of DICOM medical images or high-quality telesurgical video.
3. **Reference Model (2 marks):** Three planes — U-plane (user data), C-plane (signaling via DSS1/SS7), M-plane (management).
4. **Telemedicine role (2 marks):** Enables real-time H.320 video, teleradiology image transfer, PACS interconnect, and remote consultation; deterministic QoS due to circuit-switched nature.

---

**(b) PRI (E1) Calculations (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Number of B-channels in E1 PRI.**

$$N_B = 30 \text{ channels}$$

**Step 2: Total data payload.**

$$R_{\text{payload}} = 30 \times 64 + 64 = 1920 + 64 = 1984 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3: Physical line rate (E1 framing with 32 time slots × 8 bits × 8 kHz).**

$$R_{\text{E1}} = 32 \times 8 \times 8000 = 2{,}048{,}000 \text{ bps} = 2.048 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 4: Allocated B-channels.**

$$N_{\text{used}} = 12 \text{ (teleconsultation)} + 6 \text{ (PACS transfer)} = 18 \text{ B-channels}$$

**Step 5: Free B-channels.**

$$N_{\text{free}} = 30 - 18 = 12 \text{ B-channels} = 12 \times 64 = 768 \text{ kbps free payload}$$

**Conclusion:** 12 B-channels (768 kbps) remain free for backup, EMR sync, or future expansion.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the ISDN reference model (U-plane, C-plane, M-plane) as per ITU-T I.320. Describe the function of the D-channel and the signaling protocols used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare N-ISDN and B-ISDN in terms of data rate, switching technique, transmission medium, and suitability for telemedicine. A tele-cardiology unit transmits an ECG file of size **4 Mbits** over a single B-channel. Calculate the transmission time. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ISDN Reference Model (7 marks):**

1. **Diagram (2 marks):** Three planes — U, C, M.
2. **U-plane (User)** (1 mark): Transfers user information (voice/video/medical data) over the B-channels.
3. **C-plane (Control)** (2 marks): Handles signaling over the D-channel using:
   - **Layer 2: LAPD (Q.921)** — frames D-channel messages.
   - **Layer 3: DSS1 (Q.931)** at the user–network interface; **SS7** at the network–network interface.
4. **M-plane (Management)** (1 mark): Manages connections, performance monitoring, fault handling.
5. **D-channel function (1 mark):** Out-of-band signaling; never disturbs live clinical data on B-channels.

---

**(b) Comparison and Transmission Time (7 marks):**

**Comparison table (4 marks):**

| Feature | N-ISDN | B-ISDN |
|---|---|---|
| Data rate | ≤ 2.048 Mbps | 155 – 622 Mbps |
| Switching | Circuit | ATM cell |
| Medium | Copper | Fiber |
| Telemedicine | Teleradiology, H.320 video | Telesurgery, high-res imaging |

**Transmission Time Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: File size = **4 Mbits = 4 × 10⁶ bits**, B-channel rate = **64 kbps = 64 × 10³ bits/s**.

$$t = \frac{\text{File size}}{\text{Data rate}} = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{64 \times 10^3}$$

$$t = \frac{4000}{64} = 62.5 \text{ seconds}$$

**Answer:** The ECG file would take approximately **62.5 seconds** to transmit over a single B-channel.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing 64 kbps with 56 kbps** — KTU's official answer is **64 kbps** (8-bit PCM). 56 kbps belongs to old North American channel banks.
> 2. **Forgetting framing overhead** — Many students write BRI = 144 kbps and stop. The full physical line rate is **192 kbps**. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Mixing up E1 and T1 PRI** — India/Europe uses **E1 (30B + D = 2.048 Mbps)**. Writing T1 values in an Indian context loses marks.
> 4. **Treating H-channels as B-channels** — H0 = **6B = 384 kbps**, not 64 kbps.
> 5. **Skipping the derivation of 64 kbps** — Always show the Nyquist step ($f_s = 2 f_{max} = 8$ kHz) and the multiplication ($8000 \times 8$) to secure full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ISDN (Module 2)**

- [x] **ISDN = Integrated Services Digital Network**, standardized by **ITU-T I-series**.
- [x] **B-channel = 64 kbps** (derived from 8 kHz × 8 bits PCM); foundation unit of ISDN.
- [x] **D-channel = 16 kbps (BRI) or 64 kbps (PRI)**; carries **out-of-band signaling** via LAPD (Q.921) and DSS1 (Q.931).
- [x] **BRI = 2B + D = 144 kbps payload, 192 kbps line rate**; used for small clinics.
- [x] **PRI (E1) = 30B + D = 1984 kbps payload, 2.048 Mbps line rate**; used in India/Europe.
- [x] **PRI (T1) = 23B + D = 1.544 Mbps line rate**; used in USA/Japan.
- [x] **H-channels:** H0 = 384 kbps, H12 = 1920 kbps — bulk medical data.
- [x] **ISDN Reference Model (I.320):** U-plane, C-plane, M-plane.
- [x] **N-ISDN** = circuit-switched, copper, up to 2.048 Mbps; **B-ISDN** = ATM cell-switched, fiber, ≥ 155 Mbps.
- [x] **Telemedicine relevance:** H.320 video over 2B, teleradiology, PACS, EMR sync, deterministic QoS.
- [x] **LAPD** = Layer 2 D-channel protocol; **DSS1/SS7** = Layer 3 signaling.
- [x] **Reference points:** R (TE1↔TA), S (TA↔NT2), T (NT2↔NT1), U (NT1↔LE), V (LE↔exchange).
- [x] **Current status:** ISDN is largely a **legacy / sunset** technology, replaced by **VoIP, MPLS, and broadband Ethernet**; but its **QoS principles** live on.
- [x] **Numerics to memorize cold:** 64, 16, 144, 192, 30, 23, 1984, 2048, 1544, 8000, 8, 384, 1920.
- [x] **KTU favorite derivations:** Why 64 kbps? How is 2.048 Mbps obtained? How many B-channels fit in an E1?
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internet and Wireless
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT861 - TELEMEDICINE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Internet and Wireless # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Internet in Telemedicine — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Internet in Telemedicine** refers to the global, public, packet-switched network infrastructure that interconnects distributed healthcare entities (hospitals, clinics, mobile patients, sensors, and cloud servers) using standardized communication protocols (TCP/IP suite) to enable remote clinical consultation, electronic health record (EHR) exchange, real-time physiological monitoring, and asynchronous medical data transfer. The internet acts as the **backbone transport layer** for all store-and-forward, real-time, and mHealth applications. In KTU 2024 syllabus terms, it is the medium that converts a *local medical event* (a heartbeat, an X-ray, a consultation) into a *globally addressable digital artifact* that can be transmitted, stored, and interpreted. ## 1.2 Wireless Communication in Telemedicine — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wireless Communication in Telemedicine** is the transmission of medical information (voice, video, biosignals, images) between a patient and a healthcare provider — or between medical devices — without the use of physical conductors, relying instead on radio frequency (RF), infrared (IR), or optical propagation through free space, governed by standards such as IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi), IEEE 802.15.1 (Bluetooth), IEEE 802.15.4 (ZigBee), 3GPP LTE/5G NR, and IEEE 802.15.6 (WBAN). ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition **Think of the Internet as the National Highway System, and Wireless as the Ambulance.** - The **Internet** is like a massive network of national highways connecting every city. Data (the patient record) hops from one interchange (router) to another until it reaches the destination hospital. There are rules of the road (protocols like TCP/IP) that every vehicle must follow. - The **Wireless link** is like an ambulance that picks up the patient from a remote village where no highway exists yet, and drops them onto the highway at the nearest entry point. The ambulance itself (radio waves, Bluetooth, cellular) is the *last-mile connectivity*. **Why do we need both?** Because patients are not always near a wired hospital. Wireless extends reach; the Internet provides scale, security, and global interoperability. Together, they form the **"Connected Health Continuum"** — from wearable sensors on the body → wireless body area network → local gateway (phone/router) → Internet cloud → physician dashboard. ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > The following are foundational numerical values universally referenced in wireless telemedicine engineering: > > - **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ > - **Free-space path loss (FSPL) reference at 1 m for 2.4 GHz:** approx **40 dB** > - **ISM (Industrial, Scientific, Medical) unlicensed band:** **2.4 GHz – 2.4835 GHz** (globally available) > - **Standard ECG sampling rate:** **250 Hz – 500 Hz** (clinical grade) > - **Standard EEG sampling rate:** **256 Hz – 512 Hz** > - **DICOM image (mammogram) typical size:** **50 MB – 200 MB** > - **Minimum acceptable video consultation bitrate:** **384 kbps** (VGA quality) > - **5G target end-to-end latency for remote surgery:** **< 1 ms (one-way)** ## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 of PEEBT861 establishes the **infrastructure substrate** upon which every later module (PACS, EHR, mHealth apps, robotic surgery) is built. Examiners frequently test the student's ability to *map a clinical requirement to a specific wireless/Internet technology* — for example, *why is ZigBee unsuitable for live ECG streaming?* or *why does 5G enable telesurgery but 4G does not?* # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Layered Architecture — The Internet Protocol Stack for Telemedicine The Internet operates on a **4-layer TCP/IP model** (a simplified version of the OSI 7-layer model). Each layer adds a specific function that is critical for medical data integrity. | Layer | Function | Telemedicine Example | Protocol Examples | |---|---|---|---| | **4. Application** | User-facing medical services | Telemedicine portal, DICOM viewer, mHealth app | HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DICOM, HL7 | | **3. Transport** | End-to-end reliability, flow control | Guaranteed delivery of an X-ray file | TCP (reliable), UDP (fast, lossy) | | **2. Internet** | Logical addressing & routing | Routing an ECG packet from Kochi to Delhi | IP (IPv4/IPv6), ICMP | | **1. Link / Network Access** | Physical framing onto wire or air | Converting bits to radio waves via Wi-Fi NIC | Ethernet, Wi-Fi (802.11), LTE MAC | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Rule:** TCP is used for **store-and-forward** (asynchronous — X-rays, lab reports, EHR uploads) where data integrity is non-negotiable. UDP is used for **real-time streaming** (live vitals, video consultation, robotic control) where *latency matters more than 100% packet delivery*. A lost ECG beat is acceptable; a 500 ms delay is not. ## 2.2 Wireless Communication — Operating Principles A wireless telemedicine link is governed by three engineering realities: 1. **Propagation & Attenuation** — Radio waves lose strength as they travel. The **Friis Transmission Equation** (free-space) gives received power: $$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$$ where $P_t$ is transmitted power, $G_t$ and $G_r$ are antenna gains, $\lambda$ is wavelength, and $d$ is distance. The term in parentheses is the **free-space path loss** factor. 2. **Shannon's Channel Capacity** — The maximum error-free data rate (bits/second) achievable over a noisy channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$ is: $$C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\; \text{bits/s}$$ This is the **theoretical ceiling** of any wireless telemedicine link. A 20 MHz Wi-Fi channel with an SNR of 30 dB (S/N = 1000) yields $C \approx 199$ Mbps. 3. **Multipath Fading & Interference** — In a hospital, walls, equipment, and human bodies cause reflections. This is why **medical-grade wireless** uses diversity antennas, OFDM modulation, and adaptive equalization. ## 2.3 Wireless Technology Taxonomy for Healthcare | Technology | Standard | Range | Data Rate | Power | Telemedicine Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **WBAN** | IEEE 802.15.6 | < 3 m (on-body) | 10 kbps – 10 Mbps | Ultra-low | Implanted sensors, ECG patch | | **Bluetooth LE** | IEEE 802.15.1 | ~10 m | 1 – 2 Mbps | Low | Wearable → phone gateway | | **ZigBee** | IEEE 802.15.4 | 10 – 100 m | 20 – 250 kbps | Very low | Hospital asset tracking, temp monitoring | | **Wi-Fi** | IEEE 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax | 30 – 100 m | 11 Mbps – 9.6 Gbps | Medium | In-hospital tablet carts, VoIP paging | | **5G NR** | 3GPP Release 15+ | Up to several km | Up to 10 Gbps | Variable | Ambulance telemetry, remote surgery | | **LoRaWAN** | Semtech proprietary | 2 – 15 km | 0.3 – 50 kbps | Ultra-low | Rural patient vitals over long range | ## 2.4 Wireless Body Area Network (WBAN) — The Heart of mHealth A WBAN is a network of **in-body, on-body, and around-body** sensor nodes that communicate with a personal coordinator (smartphone or hub). The IEEE 802.15.6 standard (published 2012) is the dedicated medical WBAN standard. **Logical architecture (3 tiers):** - **Tier 1 — Intra-WBAN:** Sensor-to-coordinator communication on/near the body (Bluetooth, ZigBee, IEEE 802.15.6). - **Tier 2 — Inter-WBAN:** Coordinator-to-internet gateway (Wi-Fi, 4G/5G). - **Tier 3 — Beyond-WBAN:** Cloud servers, hospital information systems, physician dashboards. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > **The following equations are guaranteed KTU exam material. Memorize the variables, units, and boundary conditions.** | # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Expression | Variables & Units | KTU Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Free-space path loss (dB) | $\text{FSPL}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz | Coverage planning | | 2 | Shannon capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | $B$ in Hz, $S/N$ dimensionless | Max data rate of a channel | | 3 | Received power (Friis) | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi d)^{2}$ | $P$ in W, $G$ dimensionless, $d$ in m | Link budget | | 4 | Wavelength | $\lambda = c / f$ | $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $f$ in Hz | Antenna sizing | | 5 | Nyquist sampling | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_s$ sampling rate | Digitizing ECG/EEG | | 6 | ECG data rate (uncompressed) | $R = f_s \times n \times b$ | $n$ channels, $b$ bits/sample | Bandwidth sizing | | 7 | SNR (dB) | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$ | $P_s$ signal, $P_n$ noise | Link quality | | 8 | TCP throughput (simplified) | $T \approx \text{MSS} / \text{RTT} \times (1 / \sqrt{p})$ | MSS = segment size, $p$ = loss prob | File transfer over lossy links | | 9 | 5G latency budget (one-way) | < **1 ms** for URLLC | — | Telesurgery feasibility | | 10 | Compression ratio (JPEG-LS for DICOM) | $CR = \text{Original size} / \text{Compressed size}$ | — | Storage & transmission savings | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility In practice, a telemedicine network engineer must perform a **link budget calculation**: starting from a clinical data-rate requirement (e.g., 1 Mbps for HD video consultation), they work backwards to determine the required transmit power, antenna gain, modulation scheme, and acceptable SNR. They must also choose a **MAC layer protocol** that supports the required Quality of Service (QoS) — for instance, IEEE 802.11e EDCA provides QoS priorities for voice/video over best-effort data, which is essential when an ambulance is streaming vitals while hospital staff browse the web on the same Wi-Fi. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Shannon Channel Capacity for a Telemedicine Link **Problem Statement:** A remote village clinic transmits a 12-lead ECG (sampled at 500 Hz, 16 bits/sample, 12 channels) over a 4G LTE uplink. The allocated channel bandwidth is **$B = 200$ kHz**, and the measured SNR at the patient's location is **12 dB**. Determine (a) the maximum error-free bit rate the channel can carry, and (b) whether it is sufficient for the ECG stream. ### Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear ratio. The decibel definition for power ratio is: $$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)$$ Substituting $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 12$: $$12 = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) \;\Longrightarrow\; \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) = 1.2$$ $$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{1.2} \approx 15.849$$ **Interpretation:** The signal power is about 15.85 times the noise power — a moderately clean link. ### Step 2 — Apply Shannon's formula. $$C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$ With $B = 200{,}000$ Hz and $S/N = 15.849$: $$C = 200{,}000 \times \log_2(1 + 15.849) = 200{,}000 \times \log_2(16.849)$$ $$C = 200{,}000 \times 4.075 \approx 815{,}000 \;\text{bits/s} = 815 \;\text{kbps}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute the required ECG data rate (uncompressed). $$R_{ECG} = f_s \times n \times b = 500 \times 12 \times 16 = 96{,}000 \;\text{bits/s} = 96 \;\text{kbps}$$ ### Step 4 — Compare capacity vs. requirement. $$C = 815 \;\text{kbps} \;\gg\; R_{ECG} = 96 \;\text{kbps}$$ **Conclusion:** The link has roughly **8.5 times** the capacity needed. The ECG stream will be transmitted well within the Shannon limit, leaving ample headroom for encryption, error correction, and occasional retransmissions. ### Step 5 — Headroom factor (engineering insight). $$\text{Headroom} = \frac{C - R}{R} \times 100\% = \frac{815 - 96}{96} \times 100\% \approx 749\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A headroom of > 200% is the *engineering rule of thumb* to ensure reliable operation under fading, interference, and bursty hospital traffic. A link running at > 70% capacity is considered *unstable*. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Free-Space Path Loss for an Indoor Wi-Fi Telemedicine Cart **Problem Statement:** A Wi-Fi access point mounted on a hospital ceiling (transmit power **$P_t = 20$ dBm**, antenna gain **$G_t = 2$ dBi**) communicates with a tablet on a moving telemedicine cart (antenna gain **$G_r = 0$ dBi**) at a worst-case distance of **15 m** on the **2.4 GHz** ISM band. Calculate the received power $P_r$ in dBm. ### Step 1 — Convert all dB quantities to linear. $$P_t(\text{linear}) = 10^{20/10} = 100 \;\text{mW} = 0.1 \;\text{W}$$ $$G_t(\text{linear}) = 10^{2/10} \approx 1.585$$ $$G_r(\text{linear}) = 10^{0/10} = 1.0$$ ### Step 2 — Compute wavelength. $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2.4 \times 10^{9}} = 0.125 \;\text{m}$$ ### Step 3 — Apply Friis transmission equation. $$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$$ Plugging in $d = 15$ m: $$\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{0.125}{4\pi \times 15}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{0.125}{188.496}\right)^{2}$$ $$= (6.632 \times 10^{-4})^{2} = 4.398 \times 10^{-7}$$ Therefore: $$P_r = 0.1 \times 1.585 \times 1.0 \times 4.398 \times 10^{-7}$$ $$P_r = 6.97 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{W} = 69.7 \;\text{nW}$$ ### Step 4 — Convert back to dBm for intuitive reading. $$P_r(\text{dBm}) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{69.7 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(6.97 \times 10^{-5})$$ $$P_r(\text{dBm}) \approx -41.6 \;\text{dBm}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wi-Fi receiver sensitivity at 54 Mbps (802.11g):** approximately **$-65$ dBm**. Our link delivers $-41.6$ dBm, leaving about **23.4 dB of margin** — sufficient for indoor multipath and human-body obstruction. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Technology Selection for Clinical Scenarios This matrix is the single most-tested KTU 2.14 / 14-mark question style. | Clinical Scenario | Required Data Rate | Latency | Mobility | Recommended Technology | Rejected Alternative & Reason | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Continuous ICU vitals monitoring (bedside) | < 100 kbps | < 1 s | Stationary | Wi-Fi (802.11ax) | Bluetooth LE — range insufficient across ICU bays | | Remote village ECG via mobile phone | ~100 kbps | < 5 s | Mobile (walking) | 4G LTE / 5G | Wi-Fi — no infrastructure in rural area | | Implanted cardiac defibrillator telemetry | ~10 kbps | < 10 s | Mobile | MICS band (402–405 MHz) inductive | Wi-Fi — power consumption would drain implant battery in hours | | Intra-OR wireless HD video | 30 Mbps | < 50 ms | Stationary | Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax) with QoS | Bluetooth — insufficient bandwidth | | Remote robotic surgery | > 100 Mbps (force feedback) | < 1 ms | Stationary | 5G URLLC + fiber backhaul | 4G LTE — latency 30–50 ms too high for haptic feedback | | Hospital cold-chain vaccine temperature | < 1 kbps | Hours | Stationary | ZigBee / LoRaWAN | Wi-Fi — overkill and power-hungry | | Ambulance en-route 12-lead ECG | ~100 kbps | < 2 s | High-speed mobile | 4G LTE with seamless handover | Wi-Fi — handover between APs at 80 km/h impractical | ## 3.4 Derivation 3: Compression Sizing for Store-and-Forward DICOM **Problem Statement:** A radiology department transmits 100 chest X-rays daily. Each uncompressed image is **2048 × 2048 pixels × 12 bits**. JPEG 2000 lossless compression achieves a typical **3:1 ratio**. Calculate (a) raw daily volume, (b) compressed daily volume, and (c) time to upload over a 10 Mbps link. ### Step 1 — Raw size per image. $$S_{raw} = 2048 \times 2048 \times 12 \;\text{bits} = 50{,}331{,}648 \;\text{bits} \approx 6 \;\text{MB}$$ ### Step 2 — Daily raw volume for 100 images. $$V_{raw} = 100 \times 6 = 600 \;\text{MB/day}$$ ### Step 3 — Compressed volume (3:1 ratio). $$V_{comp} = \frac{600}{3} = 200 \;\text{MB/day}$$ ### Step 4 — Upload time over 10 Mbps link (using decimal megabits). $$T = \frac{200 \times 8 \;\text{Mbits}}{10 \;\text{Mbps}} = 160 \;\text{seconds} \approx 2.7 \;\text{minutes}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering takeaway:** A full day's worth of compressed chest X-rays uploads in under 3 minutes on a typical broadband line. This is why *store-and-forward* teleradiology is feasible in low-bandwidth rural settings — but *real-time* teleradiology (live fluoroscopy) is not. ## 3.5 KTU-Standard Numerical Answer Format When KTU asks a 7-mark problem, the valuation key expects: - **Statement of given data** (1 mark) - **Identification of applicable formula** (1 mark) - **Substitution with units** (2 marks) - **Final numerical answer with units** (2 marks) - **Interpretation / engineering comment** (1 mark) Always conclude with a one-line sentence such as *"Hence, the 4G uplink is sufficient for 12-lead ECG telemetry with > 700% capacity headroom."* # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Telemedicine Communication Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB A[PATIENT LAYER
Biosensors and Wearables] --> B[WBAN HUB
Smartphone or Tablet] B --> C[EDGE LAYER
WiFi 4G 5G Gateway] C --> D[INTERNET CORE
TCP IP Backbone Routers] D --> E[CLOUD LAYER
EHR PACS AI Engine] E --> F[CLINICIAN LAYER
Hospital or Remote Doctor] F -->|Feedback| D E -->|Alerts| B ``` **Reading the diagram (left-to-right flow):** - The **Patient Layer** consists of on-body and implanted sensors (ECG patch, pulse oximeter, glucose monitor). - These sensors form a **WBAN** and stream data to a smartphone (the **WBAN Hub**). - The hub forwards the data over a **wireless edge link** (Wi-Fi at home, 4G/5G in transit). - Data traverses the **Internet Core** (routers, fibre, undersea cables). - It lands in a **Cloud Layer** (EHR database, PACS archive, AI analytics). - The **Clinician Layer** accesses processed data through a secure hospital portal. ## 4.2 Wireless Technology Selection Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TB Q1{Is the sensor on the body or implanted} -->|Yes| Q2{Is mobility required} Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is range less than 100 m indoor} Q2 -->|Yes| A1[Use 4G LTE or 5G cellular] Q2 -->|No| A2[Use Bluetooth LE or ZigBee] Q3 -->|Yes| A3[Use WiFi 6 or 802.11ax] Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is bandwidth more than 10 Mbps needed} Q4 -->|Yes| A4[Use 5G NR or fibre backhaul] Q4 -->|No| A5[Use LoRaWAN or NB-IoT] ``` **Reading the diagram:** Starting from the question *“Where is the sensor?”*, the chart branches through clinical constraints to a final technology recommendation. This is the **decision tree KTU examiners expect** in 14-mark design questions. ## 4.3 WBAN Three-Tier Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TierA[Tier 1 Intra-WBAN] S1[ECG sensor]:::nodeStyle S2[SpO2 sensor]:::nodeStyle S3[BP cuff]:::nodeStyle S4[Glucose patch]:::nodeStyle end subgraph TierB[Tier 2 Inter-WBAN] HUB[Personal coordinator smartphone]:::hubStyle end subgraph TierC[Tier 3 Beyond-WBAN] GW[Internet gateway router]:::netStyle CLD[Cloud EHR]:::netStyle DOC[Physician dashboard]:::netStyle end S1 --> HUB S2 --> HUB S3 --> HUB S4 --> HUB HUB --> GW GW --> CLD CLD --> DOC classDef nodeStyle fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000 classDef hubStyle fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000 classDef netStyle fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Tier 1** (red zone) is *inside or on the body*. Short range, ultra-low power. - **Tier 2** (amber zone) is the *coordinator* — a personal device the patient carries. - **Tier 3** (blue zone) is the *wide-area infrastructure* reaching the doctor. ## 4.4 Hospital Network Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SEN[Sensor Floor - Inpatient Wards] WB1[WBAN patient bed 1]:::nodeStyle WB2[WBAN patient bed 2]:::nodeStyle AP1[WiFi access point ward]:::apStyle end subgraph OPR[Operation Theatre and ICU] WB3[High def wireless video]:::nodeStyle AP2[WiFi access point OT ceiling]:::apStyle end subgraph CORE[Hospital Network Core] SW1[Core layer 3 switch]:::coreStyle FW[Firewall and VPN]:::coreStyle SRV[HIS PACS DICOM server]:::coreStyle end subgraph EXT[External] ISP[Fibre ISP uplink]:::extStyle CLOUD[Public cloud telemedicine]:::extStyle end AP1 --> SW1 AP2 --> SW1 WB1 --> AP1 WB2 --> AP1 WB3 --> AP2 SW1 --> FW FW --> SRV FW --> ISP ISP --> CLOUD classDef nodeStyle fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000 classDef apStyle fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000 classDef coreStyle fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000 classDef extStyle fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** This is the *block-level functional architecture* of a real hospital network. Notice the **firewall and VPN** placed between the hospital core and the ISP — this is mandatory under the *Information Technology Act, 2000 (India)* and HIPAA-equivalent regulations for protecting patient data in transit. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Style]** ### Q1. Differentiate between TCP and UDP in the context of telemedicine data transmission. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) | UDP (User Datagram Protocol) | |---|---|---| | Connection type | Connection-oriented (3-way handshake) | Connectionless (fire-and-forget) | | Reliability | Guaranteed delivery, retransmission of lost packets | No guarantee; packets may be lost | | Ordering | In-order delivery | No ordering | | Header size | 20 bytes (larger overhead) | 8 bytes (lighter) | | Speed | Slower due to acknowledgments | Faster, minimal latency | | Use in telemedicine | EHR uploads, DICOM image transfer, lab reports | Live video consultation, real-time ECG, VoIP, telesurgery control | > [!NOTE] > **Key point (must appear in 3-mark answer):** TCP is preferred for **store-and-forward** (data integrity critical), UDP for **real-time streaming** (latency critical). A lost ECG beat is acceptable; a 500 ms delay is dangerous. --- ### Q2. What is a Wireless Body Area Network (WBAN)? List any two medical applications. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Model Answer:** A **Wireless Body Area Network (WBAN)** is a short-range, low-power wireless network of intelligent biosensor nodes placed **on, around, or inside the human body** to monitor physiological signals and communicate with a personal coordinator (typically a smartphone). The IEEE 802.15.6 standard (published 2012) is the dedicated medical WBAN standard. WBANs typically operate in three frequency bands: **402–405 MHz** (Medical Implant Communication Service, MICS), **2.36–2.4 GHz**, and the ultra-wideband (UWB) **3.1–10.6 GHz**. **Two medical applications (any two accepted):** 1. **Continuous ECG / Holter monitoring** — wireless chest patch streams 3-lead or 12-lead ECG to a smartphone. 2. **Implanted cardiac defibrillator (ICD) follow-up** — automatic wireless telemetry of arrhythmia episodes to the cardiologist. 3. *(Alternative)* Remote glucose monitoring for diabetic patients using an adhesive patch sensor. 4. *(Alternative)* Wireless fetal monitoring during high-risk pregnancy. 5. *(Alternative)* Post-operative rehabilitation movement tracking with on-body accelerometers. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice > **[KTU End Semester Exam Pattern]** --- ### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **Q.A (a)** Explain the layered architecture of the Internet with reference to the TCP/IP model. Discuss how each layer contributes to reliable medical data exchange. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** The Internet's TCP/IP model consists of **four functional layers**, each abstracting a specific aspect of communication. In telemedicine, each layer must be engineered to preserve **data integrity, security, and timeliness**. **1. Application Layer (Top)** This is the layer closest to the user. In telemedicine, it includes applications such as the **hospital information system (HIS), electronic health record (EHR) portal, DICOM image viewer, video consultation app, and HL7 messaging gateway.** Protocols: HTTP/HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DICOM, HL7. *Contribution to medical data exchange:* It defines **how the clinical user interacts with data** — pulling up a patient's X-ray, signing off a prescription, or starting a video call with a remote specialist. **2. Transport Layer** Provides **end-to-end communication services** between two hosts. Two main protocols: - **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol):** Connection-oriented, reliable, ordered. Used for EHR uploads, DICOM file transfer, lab report downloads. Guarantees every byte arrives correctly via acknowledgments and retransmission. - **UDP (User Datagram Protocol):** Connectionless, unreliable, low-latency. Used for real-time vitals streaming, live video consultation, and robotic control signals. *Contribution:* **TCP guarantees integrity for store-and-forward clinical files; UDP guarantees timeliness for real-time physiological signals.** This duality is the foundation of telemedicine networking. **3. Internet Layer** Handles **logical addressing and routing** of packets across multiple networks. Protocol: **IP (Internet Protocol)** — both IPv4 (32-bit) and IPv6 (128-bit). IPv6 is particularly important for telemedicine because every medical IoT device can have a unique global address. *Contribution:* Routes an ECG packet from a village clinic in Wayanad to a cardiologist at Amrita Hospital, Kochi, possibly across 15 routers, choosing optimal paths via protocols like BGP and OSPF. **4. Link / Network Access Layer (Bottom)** Defines **how bits are physically framed and transmitted** over the actual medium — copper cable, optical fibre, or radio waves. Protocols: Ethernet (wired), Wi-Fi (802.11), LTE/5G MAC (wireless). *Contribution:* Converts IP packets into radio waves via a Wi-Fi NIC or 5G modem. Includes error detection (CRC) and physical-layer modulation (OFDM, QAM). > [!NOTE] > **KTU examiner's mark-distribution tip (7 marks):** Award 1 mark each for correctly naming the 4 layers, 2 marks for explaining the function of TCP vs UDP clearly, 1 mark for a telemedicine example per layer, and 1 mark for concluding with a clinical example (e.g., "an ECG packet from a village clinic traverses all four layers in < 200 ms"). --- **Q.A (b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Wireless Body Area Network (WBAN)**. List the IEEE standard, frequency bands, and any four design challenges. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Model Answer:** **Definition and Standard:** A WBAN is a network of low-power, intelligent sensor nodes placed on, in, or around the human body, communicating with a personal coordinator. The dedicated standard is **IEEE 802.15.6**, published in 2012 and revised in 2020. **Block Diagram (3-Tier Architecture):** - **Tier 1 — Intra-WBAN (on/in body):** Sensor nodes → coordinator. Range < 3 m, ultra-low power. - **Tier 2 — Inter-WBAN (around body):** Coordinator → Internet gateway. Range ~10 m, low power. - **Tier 3 — Beyond-WBAN (off body):** Gateway → cloud → clinician.
