Wide-area network. **Frequency Bands (per IEEE 802.15.6):** | Band | Frequency | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | MICS (Medical Implant Communication Service) | 402 – 405 MHz | Implanted devices (pacemaker, ICD) | | WMTS (Wireless Medical Telemetry Service) | 608 – 614 MHz, 1395 – 1400 MHz, 1427 – 1432 MHz | Hospital-patient telemetry | | ISM | 2.36 – 2.4 GHz | Wearable sensors | | UWB (Ultra-Wideband) | 3.1 – 10.6 GHz | High-precision localization | **Four Design Challenges:** 1. **Power consumption:** Implanted sensors must run for years on a coin cell. Requires duty cycling and energy harvesting (body heat, motion). 2. **Data security & privacy:** Wireless medical data is vulnerable to eavesdropping. Mandatory use of AES-128 encryption and authentication per HIPAA/IT-Act. 3. **Body shadowing & path loss:** Human tissue attenuates radio waves, especially at higher frequencies. Antenna design must account for variable body posture. 4. **Coexistence & interference:** Multiple WBANs in a hospital ward (e.g., 20 patients) must not interfere. The standard uses CSMA/CA and frequency hopping. 5. *(Optional 5th)* **Thermal effects (SAR limits):** Specific Absorption Rate must remain below **1.6 W/kg (FCC) / 2.0 W/kg (ICNIRP)** to avoid tissue heating. > [!WARNING] > **Common student mistakes costing 2 marks:** (1) Confusing IEEE 802.15.6 (WBAN) with IEEE 802.15.1 (Bluetooth) or 802.15.4 (ZigBee) — these are different standards. (2) Forgetting the MICS band (402–405 MHz) is **reserved worldwide for implants** by the ITU. (3) Drawing a 2-tier diagram instead of 3-tier. --- ### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice **Q.B (a)** Compare the following wireless technologies for telemedicine applications: **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), Bluetooth, ZigBee, and 5G NR**. Your comparison must cover range, data rate, power consumption, and a suitable telemedicine use case for each. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Wi-Fi (802.11ax) | Bluetooth (LE 5.x) | ZigBee (802.15.4) | 5G NR | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Standard body** | IEEE 802 | IEEE 802.15.1 | IEEE 802.15.4 | 3GPP | | **Frequency band** | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz | 2.4 GHz (ISM) | 868 MHz / 915 MHz / 2.4 GHz | Sub-6 GHz + mmWave (24–52 GHz) | | **Range (typical)** | 30 – 100 m | 10 – 100 m | 10 – 100 m | Up to several km (cell radius) | | **Data rate** | Up to 9.6 Gbps (Wi-Fi 6) | 1 – 2 Mbps (BLE) | 20 – 250 kbps | Up to 10 Gbps (eMBB) | | **Latency** | 10 – 50 ms | 7.5 ms (BLE) | 30 – 100 ms | < 1 ms (URLLC) | | **Power consumption** | High (100 mW) | Low (1 – 10 mW) | Very low (< 1 mW) | Variable, optimized for IoT (NB-IoT, LTE-M) | | **Modulation** | OFDM (BPSK to 1024-QAM) | GFSK | BPSK / O-QPSK | OFDM, FBMC (5G NR) | | **Network topology** | Star (AP-client) | Piconet / Mesh (scatternet) | Mesh / Star | Cellular (gNB-UE) | | **Telemedicine use case** | In-hospital tablet carts, OR video, VoIP paging | Wearable to smartphone ECG patch, pulse oximeter | Hospital asset tracking, vaccine cold-chain, smoke/door sensors | Ambulance telemetry, mobile mHealth, remote robotic surgery, rural clinic backhaul | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering conclusion (must appear in answer):** There is **no single best wireless technology** for telemedicine. The choice is governed by the **clinical data-rate requirement, mobility profile, power budget, and latency tolerance**. Modern hospitals deploy a **heterogeneous wireless network** combining all four — Bluetooth for wearables, ZigBee for sensors, Wi-Fi for bedside devices, and 5G for wide-area mobile and remote surgery. --- **Q.B (b)** A telemedicine link operates at a center frequency of **2.4 GHz** with a bandwidth of **20 MHz** and an SNR of **20 dB**. Calculate (i) the theoretical Shannon capacity, (ii) the wavelength, and (iii) comment on whether this link is suitable for transmitting a 5 Mbps ultrasound video stream. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert SNR to linear ratio:** $$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N) \;\Rightarrow\; 20 = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$$ $$S/N = 10^{2.0} = 100$$ **Step 2 — Compute Shannon capacity:** $$C = B \log_2(1 + S/N) = 20 \times 10^{6} \times \log_2(1 + 100) = 20 \times 10^{6} \times \log_2(101)$$ $$C = 20 \times 10^{6} \times 6.658 \approx 133.2 \;\text{Mbps}$$ **Step 3 — Compute wavelength:** $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2.4 \times 10^{9}} = 0.125 \;\text{m} = 12.5 \;\text{cm}$$ **Step 4 — Compare capacity with ultrasound requirement:** $$C = 133.2 \;\text{Mbps} \;\gg\; R_{required} = 5 \;\text{Mbps}$$ **Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:** The link capacity (133.2 Mbps) is approximately **26.6 times** the required rate (5 Mbps). This provides a headroom of more than 2,500% — sufficient to absorb multipath fading, hospital interference, MAC-layer retransmissions, and AES-128 encryption overhead (typically 5–10% of payload). The wavelength of 12.5 cm is well-suited to compact patch antennas on ultrasound carts and Wi-Fi access points. **Conclusion:** The 2.4 GHz / 20 MHz / 20 dB SNR link is **highly suitable** for the 5 Mbps ultrasound video stream and will deliver robust, low-latency performance. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students lose marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the "+1" inside the log.** Shannon's formula is $B \log_2(1 + S/N)$, not $B \log_2(S/N)$. The "+1" accounts for the channel noise floor and is mathematically non-negotiable. > 2. **Mixing dB conventions.** Power ratios use $10 \log_{10}$; voltage ratios use $20 \log_{10}$. SNR is a *power* ratio, so $10 \log_{10}$. > 3. **Skipping the final interpretation sentence.** A numerical answer without an engineering conclusion loses 1 mark per KTU 2024 scheme. > 4. **Unit errors.** Writing "133.2" without units (Mbps) or writing "0.125" without units (m) — both are common deductions. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density revision checklist for last-minute KTU preparation:** - **TCP vs UDP:** TCP = integrity (store-and-forward), UDP = timeliness (real-time). This is the single most-tested distinction in Part A. - **Shannon capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$. Always include the **"+1"** inside the logarithm. - **Friis equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi d)^{2}$. Doubling distance quarters the received power. - **Wavelength:** $\lambda = c / f$. At 2.4 GHz, $\lambda = 12.5$ cm. At 5 GHz, $\lambda = 6$ cm. - **WBAN standard:** **IEEE 802.15.6** (NOT 802.15.1 which is Bluetooth, and NOT 802.15.4 which is ZigBee). - **WBAN 3 tiers:** Intra-WBAN → Inter-WBAN → Beyond-WBAN. - **MICS band:** **402 – 405 MHz** — reserved globally for medical implants by the ITU. - **ISM band:** **2.4 – 2.4835 GHz** — unlicensed, shared with Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, microwave ovens (source of interference!). - **5G capability:** Three pillars — **eMBB** (high bandwidth), **URLLC** (< 1 ms latency), **mMTC** (millions of IoT devices per km²). Telesurgery uses URLLC. - **ECG sampling rate:** Clinical standard is **500 Hz**, with 12 bits per sample. 12-lead ECG raw rate ≈ 96 kbps. - **Nyquist theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. To digitize an ECG with maximum frequency 150 Hz, sample at ≥ 300 Hz. - **Hospital security layers:** Firewall + VPN + AES-128 + DICOM TLS. The *Information Technology Act, 2000 (India)* and *DISHA / Digital Information Security in Healthcare Act (draft)* mandate these. - **DICOM image sizing:** A typical chest X-ray (2048 × 2048 × 12 bits) is approximately **6 MB uncompressed**. Lossless JPEG 2000 typically achieves **3:1** compression. - **Wi-Fi receiver sensitivity at 54 Mbps:** approximately **$-65$ dBm** for 802.11g. Always leave ≥ 10 dB link margin. - **Heterogeneous networks:** Modern hospitals run **Bluetooth + ZigBee + Wi-Fi + 5G** simultaneously, segmented by VLAN and QoS class. - **Hospital network layer functions:** Core L3 switch, firewall, HIS/PACS server, ISP uplink — *in that order*, never reversed. - **Common pitfalls to avoid:** (1) Confusing Bluetooth and ZigBee standards; (2) writing $B \log_2(S/N)$ instead of $B \log_2(1 + S/N)$; (3) forgetting units; (4) ending a numerical answer without an engineering comment. > [!NOTE] > **Last-mile revision mantra for KTU viva:** *"For every clinical requirement, there is exactly one optimal wireless technology — chosen by the *quad-triangle* of data rate, latency, power, and mobility. The Internet is the highway; wireless is the last-mile ambulance. WBAN is the stretcher inside the ambulance."*

topics.title: Data Communication Networks - Protocols and Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT861 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Communication Networks - Protocols and Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Communication Networks** refer to the interconnected system of hardware (routers, switches, servers, end-devices) and software (protocols, operating procedures) that permit the **exchange, transmission, and reception of medical data** between geographically distributed entities (hospitals, clinics, patients, diagnostic centers) for clinical, educational, and administrative purposes.

In the context of **Telemedicine (PEEBT861)**, a data communication network is the *technical backbone* that enables the digital transfer of **EHR (Electronic Health Records)**, **DICOM medical images**, **real-time video consultations**, **vital sign telemetry from wearable sensors**, and **pathology reports** between patients and healthcare providers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Protocols** are the formal sets of **rules, conventions, and data structures** that govern *how* devices on a network exchange information. **Standards** are the *agreed-upon* technical specifications (often published by international bodies) that ensure **interoperability**, **security**, and **reliability** across heterogeneous medical systems.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Glossary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Protocol** | A formal set of rules for data exchange (e.g., TCP/IP, HTTP, HL7) |
| **Standard** | A documented technical specification recognized by a standards body (e.g., ISO, IEEE) |
| **Interoperability** | The ability of diverse systems to exchange and use data meaningfully |
| **Bandwidth** | Maximum data transfer rate of a network link (measured in **bits per second / bps**) |
| **Throughput** | Actual *successful* data delivery rate over a channel (bps) |
| **Latency** | Time delay between data transmission and reception (measured in **milliseconds / ms**) |
| **Packet** | A formatted unit of data carried by a packet-switched network |
| **PING (Packet InterNet Groper)** | A diagnostic utility that measures round-trip time (RTT) |
| **QoS (Quality of Service)** | Network capability to prioritize traffic (critical for ICU telemetry) |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Postal Network" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a Data Communication Network like the International Postal System:**
> - The **Network Infrastructure** = roads, post offices, sorting centers (analogous to routers, switches, fiber-optic cables).
> - The **Protocols** = the rules for addressing the envelope, writing the postal code, and sorting logic (analogous to TCP, IP, HTTP, SMTP).
> - The **Standards** = the universally agreed-upon envelope size, address format, and postmark conventions (analogous to ISO, IEEE 802.x, HL7, DICOM).
> - The **Data Payload** = the actual letter/document inside the envelope (analogous to patient records, MRI scans, ECG waveforms).
>
> Just as two people from different countries can exchange letters only because they both follow a *common standard* (universal postal union), medical devices from different manufacturers interoperate only because they adhere to *common communication standards*.

## 1.4 Classification of Networks by Geographic Scale

| Network Type | Abbreviation | Typical Range | Telemedicine Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Personal Area Network** | PAN | $< 10$ m | Bluetooth-connected glucose monitor to smartphone |
| **Body Area Network** | BAN / WBAN | $< 2$ m (on-body) | ECG wearable patch streaming to phone |
| **Local Area Network** | LAN | Building / Campus | Hospital intranet connecting PACS servers |
| **Metropolitan Area Network** | MAN | City-wide | Inter-hospital data sharing within a district |
| **Wide Area Network** | WAN | Country / Global | Cross-border teleconsultation with foreign specialist |
| **Internet** | – | Global | Cloud-based EHR platforms (e.g., AWS HealthLake) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The OSI 7-Layer Reference Model (ISO/IEC 7498-1)

The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI)** model is the foundational conceptual framework. Each layer performs a *distinct function* and provides services to the layer above while consuming services from the layer below.

| Layer # | Layer Name | PDU (Protocol Data Unit) | Telemedicine Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | **Application** | Data | HL7 messaging, DICOM C-STORE, HTTPS for EHR portal |
| 6 | **Presentation** | Data | TLS/SSL encryption of patient data, JPEG2000 compression of CT images |
| 5 | **Session** | Data | SIP session for VoIP teleconsultation between doctor and patient |
| 4 | **Transport** | Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP) | TCP (reliable, for EHR sync) vs UDP (fast, for real-time ECG) |
| 3 | **Network** | Packet | IP addressing, routing between hospital networks |
| 2 | **Data Link** | Frame | Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11) MAC addressing |
| 1 | **Physical** | Bit | Fiber-optic cable, twisted-pair copper, radio waves (5G) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic (top-down): "All People Seem To Need Data Processing"** and (bottom-up): **"Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"**.

## 2.2 The TCP/IP 4-Layer Practical Model (DARPA / RFC 1122)

While the OSI model is theoretical, the **TCP/IP model** is what the real Internet uses. It collapses OSI's top 3 layers into a single **Application** layer.

| TCP/IP Layer | Maps to OSI Layers | Key Protocols |
|---|---|---|
| **Application** | 7 + 6 + 5 | HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DNS, HL7, DICOM, SIP |
| **Transport** | 4 | TCP (reliable), UDP (fast) |
| **Internet** | 3 | IP (IPv4 / IPv6), ICMP, ARP |
| **Link (Network Access)** | 2 + 1 | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP, Frame Relay |

## 2.3 Telemedicine-Specific Protocol Suite

| Layer / Function | Protocol / Standard | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Application** | **HL7 v2.x / FHIR** | Clinical data exchange (patient demographics, lab results) |
| **Application** | **DICOM** | Medical imaging (CT, MRI, X-Ray) storage and transfer |
| **Application** | **IHE (Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise)** | Profiles integrating HL7 + DICOM workflows |
| **Application** | **SNMP** | Network device monitoring (router uptime, server load) |
| **Transport** | **TCP (port 80, 443)** | Reliable EHR download, DICOM C-MOVE |
| **Transport** | **UDP** | Real-time vital signs, VoIP/RTP for telesurgery |
| **Network** | **IP (Internet Protocol)** | Addressing & routing |
| **Network** | **ICMP** | `ping`, `traceroute` for network diagnostics |
| **Data Link** | **IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi)** | Wireless bedside monitoring |
| **Data Link** | **IEEE 802.15.1 (Bluetooth)** | Wearable ECG, pulse oximeter |
| **Data Link** | **IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee** | Low-power wireless body area networks (WBAN) |
| **Physical** | **5G NR (New Radio)** | Ultra-low latency remote surgery (< 1 ms) |

## 2.4 Standards Developing Organizations (SDOs)

| SDO | Domain | Notable Standards for Telemedicine |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)** | General | ISO/IEEE 11073 (Personal Health Data) |
| **IEEE (Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers)** | Networking & Biomed | IEEE 802.11, 802.15, 11073 |
| **ITU-T (International Telecommunication Union)** | Telecom | H.323, H.264 video codecs |
| **IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force)** | Internet | TCP, IP, HTTP, SIP, RTP (RFC series) |
| **HL7 International** | Healthcare data | HL7 v2, HL7 FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) |
| **DICOM Standards Committee** | Medical imaging | DICOM PS 3.1 - 3.22 |
| **WHO** | Global health | eHealth strategy documents |
| **MoHFW / ICMR (India)** | National | EHR Standards (2016), Telemedicine Practice Guidelines (2020) |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **frequently tested** in KTU University Examinations for telemedicine / biomedical engineering papers. Memorize units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{total} = T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{queue} + T_{proc}$ | Total packet delay | $ms$ | End-to-end latency budget |
| 2 | $T_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Transmission delay (packet length / link rate) | $s$ | Time to push bits onto wire |
| 3 | $T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{s}$ | Propagation delay (distance / signal speed) | $s$ | Fiber ~ $2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s |
| 4 | $BDP = R \times T_{prop}$ | Bandwidth-Delay Product (max unACKed bits) | $bits$ | Window size for full utilization |
| 5 | $C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Shannon channel capacity | $bps$ | Theoretical max throughput |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{useful}}{T_{useful} + T_{overhead}}$ | Protocol efficiency | unitless $\in [0,1]$ | Effective data fraction |
| 7 | $S = R \times \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{pp}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Spectral efficiency analog approx | $bps/Hz$ | Modulation quality |
| 8 | $P_{loss} = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$ | Probability of $n$ packets lost (BER $p$) | unitless | Reliable transfer design |
| 9 | $T_{round} = 2 \times T_{prop} + T_{proc}$ | Round-trip time (RTT) | $ms$ | TCP ACK delay |
| 10 | $T_{jitter} = \sigma(T_{arrival})$ | Jitter = std dev of packet delay | $ms$ | Real-time VoIP quality |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE**: All absolute-value bars in the table above use $\vert$ or $\mid$ notation. When writing these on paper, remember: **$\vert x \vert$ is the modulus, NOT the pipe character `\|`**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **In Tele-ICU:** $T_{total}$ must be $< 200$ ms for stable remote patient monitoring; otherwise, ventilator synchronization fails.
- **In Teleradiology:** DICOM file size of a chest CT $\approx 250$ MB. On a **10 Mbps** link, $T_{trans} = 250 \times 8 \times 10^{6} / 10^{7} = 200$ s — necessitating compression and higher bandwidth.
- **In Tele-Surgery (5G):** Haptic feedback demands $T_{prop} < 1$ ms — only achievable with edge computing + 5G URLLC.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Problem Solving

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — End-to-End Delay Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):** A telemedicine server in Thiruvananthapuram sends a DICOM image packet of size $L = 12{,}000$ bits to a remote radiologist in Kochi. The link bandwidth is $R = 2$ Mbps. The physical distance is $d = 220$ km over a fiber-optic link with signal propagation speed $s = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. The router introduces a queuing + processing delay of $T_{queue+proc} = 5$ ms. Compute the **total end-to-end delay**.

### Step 1: Identify the relevant delay components

The total end-to-end delay for a single packet is the sum of four components:

$$T_{total} = T_{trans} + T_{prop} + T_{queue} + T_{proc}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Transmission Delay

The transmission delay is the time required to push all of the packet's bits onto the link.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{trans} &= \frac{L}{R} \\
&= \frac{12{,}000 \text{ bits}}{2 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}} \\
&= 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\
&= 6 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Every bit of the 12,000-bit packet must be clocked out at 2 Mbps. So 12,000 / 2,000,000 = 0.006 s.

### Step 3: Compute the Propagation Delay

The propagation delay is the time for a single bit to physically traverse the medium.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{prop} &= \frac{d}{s} \\
&= \frac{220 \times 10^{3} \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}} \\
&= 1.1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\
&= 1.1 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Sum All Components

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= T_{trans} + T_{prop} + T_{queue+proc} \\
&= 6 \text{ ms} + 1.1 \text{ ms} + 5 \text{ ms} \\
&= 12.1 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $T_{total} = 12.1$ ms. This is well within the **< 100 ms** threshold for stable tele-radiology interaction.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** For a satellite telemedicine link with $R = 1$ Mbps uplink and $T_{prop} = 270$ ms (geostationary satellite), find the **Bandwidth-Delay Product** and explain its engineering significance.

### Step 1: Apply the BDP formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
BDP &= R \times T_{prop} \\
&= 1 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s} \times 0.270 \text{ s} \\
&= 270{,}000 \text{ bits} \\
&\approx 33.75 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Engineering Interpretation

> **Why BDP matters in telemedicine:** The BDP tells us the **maximum number of bits that can be "in flight"** (unacknowledged) in the network at any instant. For a satellite link, the sender can transmit **~270,000 bits** before receiving the first ACK.
>
> - If the **TCP window size $< BDP$**, the sender must *stop and wait* for ACKs — link is **under-utilized**.
> - To fully utilize a 1 Mbps satellite link, the TCP receive window must be $\geq 34$ KB (a critical parameter for satellite telemedicine using RFC 1323 window scaling).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Shannon Capacity for an ECG Telemetry Link

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A WBAN (Wireless Body Area Network) ECG transmitter has channel bandwidth $B = 100$ kHz. The measured SNR is $S/N = 15$ dB. Find the **maximum theoretical data rate** in kbps.

### Step 1: Convert SNR from dB to linear ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{S}{N}\bigg|_{linear} &= 10^{\,\frac{S/N \text{(dB)}}{10}} \\
&= 10^{\,\frac{15}{10}} \\
&= 10^{1.5} \\
&\approx 31.62
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Apply Shannon-Hartley Theorem

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \\
&= 100 \times 10^{3} \times \log_{2}\!\left(1 + 31.62\right) \\
&= 100 \times 10^{3} \times \log_{2}\!\left(32.62\right) \\
&= 100 \times 10^{3} \times 5.027 \text{ bits/s} \\
&\approx 502.7 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A 12-lead ECG sampled at 500 Hz with 12-bit resolution produces $500 \times 12 \times 12 = 72$ kbps raw stream. The Shannon cap of **~502 kbps** easily accommodates this with margin for encryption headers.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: TCP vs UDP for Telemedicine

| Parameter | **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** | **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** |
|---|---|---|
| Connection | Connection-oriented (3-way handshake) | Connectionless |
| Reliability | ACK-based, retransmits lost packets | No ACK, no retransmission |
| Ordering | In-sequence delivery | No ordering guarantee |
| Header size | $20 - 60$ bytes | $8$ bytes |
| Flow control | Sliding window | None |
| Congestion control | Yes (Reno, CUBIC, BBR) | None |
| Speed | Slower (overhead) | Faster |
| **Use in telemedicine** | EHR download, DICOM C-STORE, e-mail of reports | Real-time ECG, VoIP teleconsultation, RTP video, telesurgery haptics |
| **When to choose** | Data integrity > speed | Speed > perfect accuracy |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing "TCP is faster than UDP" — this is **incorrect** in raw throughput terms. UDP is faster due to no handshake/ACK overhead. The correct phrasing: "TCP is more *reliable*; UDP has *lower latency*."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — OSI 7-Layer Model with Telemedicine Annotations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app["LAYER 7 APPLICATION\nHL7 FHIR | DICOM | HTTPS | SIP"]
    pres["LAYER 6 PRESENTATION\nTLS SSL Encryption | JPEG2000 Compression"]
    sess["LAYER 5 SESSION\nSIP Dialog Management | RPC Sessions"]
    trans["LAYER 4 TRANSPORT\nTCP Reliable | UDP Fast | Port Numbers"]
    net["LAYER 3 NETWORK\nIP Addressing | ICMP PING | Routers"]
    dl["LAYER 2 DATA LINK\nIEEE 802.3 Ethernet | 802.11 WiFi | MAC"]
    phy["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL\nFiber Optic | Twisted Pair | 5G Radio Waves"]

    app --> pres
    pres --> sess
    sess --> trans
    trans --> net
    net --> dl
    dl --> phy

    phy -. Encapsulation Downward .-> dl
    dl -. Decapsulation Upward .-> app
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Telemedicine Network Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    patient["PATIENT\nWBAN Sensors\nECG SpO2 BP"] -->|Bluetooth BLE| phone["SMARTPHONE\nGateway App"]
    phone -->|WiFi 4G 5G| cloud["CLOUD SERVER\nHL7 FHIR API\nEHR Database"]
    cloud -->|HTTPS| doctor["DOCTOR\nWeb Portal\nDICOM Viewer"]
    cloud -->|HL7 v2| lab["LAB INFO SYSTEM\nPathology Reports"]
    cloud -->|DICOM| pacs["PACS SERVER\nCT MRI XRAY Archive"]
    doctor -->|SIP VoIP| patient
    admin["ADMIN MoHFW\nStandards Body"] -.->|Audits| cloud
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Protocol Encapsulation Flow (TCP/IP)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    msg["Application Data\nHL7 Patient Record"]
    seg["TCP Segment\nHeader SrcPort DstPort Seq Ack"]
    pkt["IP Packet\nHeader SrcIP DstIP TTL"]
    frm["Ethernet Frame\nHeader MAC Trailer CRC"]
    bit["Bits on Physical Medium\nFiber Radio Copper"]

    msg --> seg
    seg --> pkt
    pkt --> frm
    frm --> bit
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Telemedicine Protocol Stack Reference

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["APPLICATION LAYER\nHL7 FHIR for clinical data\nDICOM for imaging\nSNMP for network mgmt\nSIP for VoIP"]
    B["TRANSPORT LAYER\nTCP for reliable EHR sync\nUDP for real time vitals\nRTP for video audio"]
    C["INTERNET LAYER\nIPv4 IPv6 addressing\nICMP for ping trace\nOSPF BGP for routing"]
    D["LINK LAYER\nEthernet 802.3 wired\nWiFi 802.11 wireless\nBluetooth 802.15.1 BAN\nZigBee 802.15.4 WBAN"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

### **Q1. Define a Communication Protocol. List any FOUR examples of protocols used in telemedicine networks. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A *communication protocol* is a formal, pre-agreed set of **rules, syntax, semantics, and synchronization** procedures that govern the exchange of data between two or more communicating entities in a network.

> **Four Telemedicine Protocol Examples:**
> 1. **HL7 (Health Level Seven)** — for clinical data exchange (patient demographics, lab orders, billing).
> 2. **DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine)** — for medical imaging transfer (CT, MRI, X-Ray, Ultrasound).
> 3. **TCP/IP (Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol)** — the foundational suite enabling internet-based telemedicine.
> 4. **SIP (Session Initiation Protocol)** — for establishing VoIP / video teleconsultation sessions.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for 4 examples = 3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**

### **Q2. Differentiate between TCP and UDP. State which is preferred for real-time ECG transmission and justify. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | TCP | UDP |
|---|---|---|
| Connection | Connection-oriented (handshake) | Connectionless |
| Reliability | ACK + retransmission | No retransmission |
| Overhead | High (20–60 byte header) | Low (8 byte header) |
| Speed | Slower | Faster |
| Use case | EHR, DICOM file transfer | Real-time vitals, VoIP |

> **Answer:** **UDP** is preferred for real-time ECG transmission because ECG signals are **time-critical** and **continuously streaming**. A lost packet can be *interpolated* from adjacent samples, but a TCP retransmission would cause *jitter and clinical misinterpretation*. The lower overhead of UDP also keeps latency consistent. *(2 marks for table + 1 mark for justification)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Model Paper, Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. (a)** Explain the **OSI 7-layer reference model** in detail. For each layer, state **one telemedicine-relevant protocol or function**. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution:

The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) Model**, defined by the **ISO in ISO/IEC 7498-1 (1994)**, is a **7-layer conceptual framework** that standardizes the functions of a telecommunication or computing system into abstract categories. It enables *vendor-independent interoperability*.

| Layer | Function | Telemedicine Protocol / Example |
|---|---|---|
| **7. Application** | Interface to end-user; network services | **HL7 v2.x / FHIR** for clinical messaging, DICOM C-STORE |
| **6. Presentation** | Data formatting, encryption, compression | **TLS 1.3 / SSL** for HIPAA-grade encryption of patient records; **JPEG 2000** for lossless DICOM compression |
| **5. Session** | Establishes, manages, terminates dialogues | **SIP (RFC 3261)** for teleconsultation call setup |
| **4. Transport** | End-to-end error recovery, flow control | **TCP (port 443)** for reliable HTTPS EHR; **UDP** for RTP video |
| **3. Network** | Logical addressing, routing | **IPv4 / IPv6** for hospital-wide addressing; **OSPF** for intra-hospital routing |
| **2. Data Link** | Framing, MAC addressing, link access | **IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi)** for wireless bedside monitor; **Ethernet (802.3)** for wired PACS |
| **1. Physical** | Bit transmission on physical medium | **Fiber-optic cable (single-mode)**; **5G NR radio** for tele-surgery |

**Mnemonic (Bottom-up):** **"Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"**
**Mnemonic (Top-down):** **"All People Seem To Need Data Processing"**

> *(7 marks: 0.5 for layer name + 0.5 for function + 0.5 for protocol example ≈ 5.25 + 1.75 for structure = 7 marks distributed across 7 layers)*

---

**Q.A. (b)** With a **neat block diagram**, explain the **architecture of a Telemedicine Network** comprising Patient, Gateway, Communication Network, and Specialist Doctor. Discuss **HL7 and DICOM** standards. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Architecture of a Telemedicine Network** consists of four major subsystems:

1. **Patient-Side Acquisition System:** Includes WBAN / BAN sensors (ECG, SpO₂, BP cuff, glucometer), bedside monitor, and patient-side application. Data captured via Bluetooth (IEEE 802.15.1) or ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4).

2. **Gateway / Communication Link:** Aggregates sensor data into a smartphone/tablet/router. The gateway connects to the wider network via **Wi-Fi, 4G/5G cellular, satellite, or fiber-optic link**.

3. **Communication Network Backbone:** The **Internet (TCP/IP cloud)** or a **dedicated MPLS / VPN tunnel** (for HIPAA / DISHA compliance in India) carries the data.

4. **Specialist / Hospital End-System:** Receives data via DICOM viewer, EHR portal (web/mobile), or VoIP console. The PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System) archives medical images.

**HL7 (Health Level Seven) Standard:**
- Developed by **Health Level Seven International** (founded 1987).
- Versions: **HL7 v2.x** (pipe-delimited, widely deployed), **HL7 v3** (XML-based, CDA), **HL7 FHIR** (RESTful JSON/XML, modern).
- Function: **exchange of clinical and administrative data** (admission-discharge-transfer, lab results, medication orders, billing).
- Layer-7 (Application) protocol on the OSI model.

**DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine) Standard:**
- Maintained by **NEMA (National Electrical Manufacturers Association) + ACCR**.
- Current version: **DICOM PS 3.1 – 3.22** (covers file format + network protocol).
- Function: **storage, retrieval, printing, and transmission of medical images** (CT, MRI, Ultrasound, Endoscopy).
- DICOM integrates with **HL7** via **IHE (Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise)** profiles like *Scheduled Workflow* and *Image Object Change Management*.

> *(7 marks: 3 for architecture explanation, 2 for HL7, 2 for DICOM)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)**

**Q.B. (a)** Derive the **Shannon-Hartley theorem** formula and compute the **maximum channel capacity** for a 4G telemedicine uplink with $B = 5$ MHz and $S/N = 20$ dB. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Statement of Shannon-Hartley Theorem:**

The maximum achievable error-free data rate ($C$, in bps) over a band-limited communication channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz, in the presence of additive white Gaussian noise with signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$, is given by:

$$C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

**Intuitive Derivation Logic (Nyquist + Shannon):**

1. **Nyquist's noise-free rate** (no noise) says: with $M$ discrete signal levels, $C = 2B \log_{2} M$ bps.
2. With noise, the *number of distinguishable signal levels* becomes limited by the SNR.
3. Shannon proved that the maximum number of distinguishable levels is approximately $\sqrt{1 + S/N}$ per dimension.
4. Combining: $M \approx \sqrt{1 + S/N}$, leading to $C = 2B \log_{2}\sqrt{1 + S/N} = B \log_{2}(1 + S/N)$.

**Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1: Convert SNR from dB to linear scale.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{S}{N}\bigg|_{linear} &= 10^{\,\frac{20}{10}} = 10^{2} = 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Shannon's formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \\
&= 5 \times 10^{6} \times \log_{2}\!\left(1 + 100\right) \\
&= 5 \times 10^{6} \times \log_{2}\!\left(101\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate $\log_{2}(101)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{2}(101) &= \frac{\log_{10}(101)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{2.0043}{0.3010} \approx 6.6582
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Final capacity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 5 \times 10^{6} \times 6.6582 \\
&\approx 33.29 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps} \\
&\approx 33.29 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** The 4G uplink can theoretically support a **maximum data rate of $\approx 33.29$ Mbps** — sufficient for a 2-lead real-time ECG (72 kbps) with abundant margin for DICOM image transfer (peak ~250 MB / file) using burst bandwidth.

---

**Q.B. (b)** Compare **circuit switching, packet switching, and message switching** in the context of telemedicine data transfer. State which is **most appropriate for real-time telesurgery** and justify. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution:

| Switching Type | Mechanism | Delay | Resource Use | Reliability | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Circuit Switching** | Dedicated end-to-end path reserved for entire session | Low & constant after setup | Wastes bandwidth during silence | High (dedicated) | Traditional voice calls |
| **Message Switching** | Entire message stored at each hop, then forwarded | Very high (store-and-forward) | Better than circuit | High | Non-time-critical emails |
| **Packet Switching** | Message broken into packets; routed independently | Variable (jitter possible) | Most efficient (statistical multiplexing) | High (with TCP) | **Telemedicine (TCP/IP Internet)** |

**Justification for Telesurgery — Packet Switching (specifically, 5G + UDP/RTP):**

1. **Lowest possible end-to-end latency** is critical (haptic feedback requires < 1 ms response time).
2. **Circuit switching** is impossible to dynamically scale for variable surgical video bitrates.
3. **Message switching** introduces unacceptable delay (each hop stores the entire surgical command).
4. **Packet switching** over **5G URLLC (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication)** combined with **edge computing** achieves $< 1$ ms one-way latency — the only feasible option for telesurgery.
5. Modern hybrid approaches use **packet switching with QoS prioritization** (DSCP, MPLS-TE) to mimic circuit-switched determinism while retaining statistical multiplexing efficiency.

> *(7 marks: 3 for table, 2 for telesurgery justification, 2 for conclusion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read Carefully!**
> - **DONOT confuse** the OSI model layer numbers (1=Physical, 7=Application) with TCP/IP's 4 layers — students frequently write "Layer 1 = Application" and lose **2 marks instantly**.
> - **DO NOT** write `$\vert$` (pipe) in mathematical answers for absolute value — KTU expects proper modulus notation $\vert x \vert$ or absolute value phrasing.
> - **DO NOT** skip units in numerical problems. Writing $T_{prop} = 1.1$ without the unit **ms** loses 0.5 mark.
> - **DO NOT** write "OSI is better than TCP/IP" or vice versa. The correct answer: "**OSI is a reference model; TCP/IP is an implementation model.**"
> - For Shannon's theorem, **always show the dB-to-linear conversion step** explicitly. Skipping it is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Fire Revision):**

- [ ] **OSI has 7 layers; TCP/IP has 4 layers.** OSI is the *reference* model; TCP/IP is the *implementation*.
- [ ] **PDU names:** Bit (L1) → Frame (L2) → Packet (L3) → Segment/Datagram (L4) → Data (L5/6/7).
- [ ] **TCP = reliable but slow; UDP = fast but unreliable.** Choose UDP for real-time vitals, TCP for EHR/DICOM files.
- [ ] **HL7** = clinical data (text). **DICOM** = medical images (binary). **FHIR** = modern RESTful HL7 (JSON/XML).
- [ ] **Key SDOs:** ISO, IEEE (802.11/15/11073), ITU-T, IETF, HL7 Intl, DICOM Committee, WHO, ICMR.
- [ ] **5 telemedicine network classes:** PAN → BAN/WBAN → LAN → MAN → WAN → Internet.
- [ ] **Total delay formula:** $T_{total} = T_{trans} + T_{prop} + T_{queue} + T_{proc}$.
- [ ] **Transmission delay** = $L/R$ (packet size / bandwidth). **Propagation delay** = $d/s$ (distance / signal speed).
- [ ] **BDP** = $R \times T_{prop}$ — sets the **TCP window size** required for full link utilization.
- [ ] **Shannon Capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$. Always convert dB → linear via $10^{(dB/10)}$.
- [ ] **Round-Trip Time:** $RTT = 2 T_{prop} + T_{proc}$.
- [ ] **IEEE standards:** 802.3 (Ethernet), 802.11 (Wi-Fi), 802.15.1 (Bluetooth), 802.15.4 (ZigBee), 11073 (Medical device communication).
- [ ] **5G URLLC** offers $< 1$ ms latency — enabler of telesurgery.
- [ ] **Packet switching** is the dominant telemedicine mode. Circuit switching is for legacy PSTN; message switching is obsolete for real-time care.
- [ ] **Mnemonic – OSI bottom-up:** *"Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"*.
- [ ] **Telemedicine Practice Guidelines (India, 2020)** are issued jointly by **MoHFW + NITI Aayog + MCI (now NMC)** — mention in long answers for bonus credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Models - OSI Model and TCP/IP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT861 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Network Models - OSI Model and TCP/IP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Network Model?

A **Network Model** (or Network Architecture) is a conceptual blueprint that standardizes the communication functions of a telecommunication or computing system into abstract **layers**. Each layer provides specific services to the layer above it and requests services from the layer below it, allowing heterogeneous hardware and software from different vendors to interoperate seamlessly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEEBT861 - Module 2):**
> *Network Models* are layered reference frameworks that break the complex task of data communication into smaller, manageable sub-tasks. The two dominant reference models in data communication are the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Model** defined by **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)** and the **TCP/IP (Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol) Model** defined by **IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force)**.

In the context of **Telemedicine**, network models are the backbone that allows medical data — such as Electronic Health Records (EHR), DICOM medical images, real-time vital signs, and video consultations — to be transmitted reliably between geographically separated healthcare providers, patients, and hospitals.

---

## 1.2 Why Do We Need a Layered Model? — The Intuitive Analogy

### 🍱 The Bento Box Analogy (Layered Service Delivery)

Imagine you ordered a multi-course Japanese meal delivered to your home. The meal arrives in a **Bento box** divided into compartments (layers):

| Compartment (Layer) | Job | If it Fails |
|---|---|---|
| 🥢 Outer Packaging | Protects against rain & dust | Food gets wet |
| 🍱 Box Container | Holds everything together | Spillage |
| 🍚 Rice Layer | Provides base energy | You are still hungry |
| 🍣 Sushi Layer | Provides the main flavor | The meal is incomplete |
| 🍵 Top Cover | Keeps it warm & presentable | Food goes cold |

You, the **customer (Application)**, only interact with the **top cover (Application Layer)**. You don't care how the rice was cooked or how the delivery bike works. Each compartment performs **one well-defined job** and hands off to the next. If one layer is upgraded (say, a better outer packaging), the rest of the system works unchanged.

**This is exactly how layered network models function.** Each layer:
1. Performs **one specific function** (single responsibility).
2. **Provides services** to the layer above.
3. **Uses services** from the layer below.
4. Can be **modified or replaced** without affecting other layers (modularity).

---

## 1.3 The Two Reference Models — Headline Comparison

| Feature | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Name** | Open Systems Interconnection | Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol |
| **Developed By** | ISO (International Organization for Standardization) | DARPA → IETF |
| **Number of Layers** | **7 Layers** | **4 Layers** (sometimes shown as 5) |
| **Year Introduced** | 1984 (ISO 7498) | 1974 (Vint Cerf & Bob Kahn), formalized 1980s |
| **Approach** | Conceptual/Reference Model | Practical/Implementation Model |
| **Adoption** | Reference & teaching | Real-world Internet standard |
| **Key Protocols** | Not tied to protocols | TCP, UDP, IP, HTTP, FTP, SMTP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Telemedicine Relevance Hook:**
> When a doctor in a district hospital in Wayanad transmits an **ECG signal** over the internet to a cardiologist in Thiruvananthapuram, the data passes through both models — the **OSI model** to understand what each conceptual layer does, and the **TCP/IP model** to actually carry out the transmission over the public Internet.

---

## 1.4 The Seven Layers of the OSI Model — Quick Mnemonic

> [!TIP]
> 🧠 **Mnemonic — Top to Bottom: "All People Seem To Need Data Processing"**
> 1. **A** — Application
> 2. **P** — Presentation
> 3. **S** — Session
> 4. **T** — Transport
> 5. **N** — Network
> 6. **D** — Data Link
> 7. **P** — Physical

> 🧠 **Bottom to Top Mnemonic: "Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"**
> 1. **P** — Physical
> 2. **D** — Data Link
> 3. **N** — Network
> 4. **T** — Transport
> 5. **S** — Session
> 6. **P** — Presentation
> 7. **A** — Application

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The OSI Seven-Layer Model — Exhaustive Layer-by-Layer Analysis

The **OSI Model (ISO/IEC 7498)** partitions the abstract task of moving bits from one device to another into **seven hierarchical layers**. Each layer N communicates with its peer layer N on the remote machine using **layer-N protocols**, while interacting locally with layers N−1 and N+1 through **Service Access Points (SAPs)**.

---

### 🔩 Layer 7 — Application Layer

| Attribute | Detail |
|---|---|
| **PDU (Protocol Data Unit)** | Data / Message |
| **Function** | Provides network services directly to the end-user application |
| **Key Protocols** | HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, POP3, IMAP, DNS, SNMP, **HL7 (Health Level Seven)**, **DICOM** |
| **Telemedicine Use** | HL7 for exchanging EHR, DICOM for medical image transfer |
| **Devices/Services** | Web browsers, email clients, telemedicine software (e.g., eSanjeevani) |

> **Closest to the user.** This is where the user's software (telemedicine app) interacts with the network.

---

### 🎭 Layer 6 — Presentation Layer

| Attribute | Detail |
|---|---|
| **PDU** | Data / Message |
| **Function** | Data translation, encryption/decryption, compression/decompression |
| **Standards** | SSL/TLS, JPEG, MPEG, ASCII, EBCDIC, AES, DES |
| **Telemedicine Use** | Encrypts PHI (Protected Health Information) for HIPAA compliance; compresses DICOM images |

---

### 🤝 Layer 5 — Session Layer

| Attribute | Detail |
|---|---|
| **PDU** | Data / Message |
| **Function** | Establishes, manages, and terminates sessions between applications; dialog control (half/full duplex); synchronization checkpoints |
| **Standards/Protocols** | NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP, SIP (Session Initiation Protocol) |
| **Telemedicine Use** | Manages video consultation sessions between doctor & patient |

---

### 🚚 Layer 4 — Transport Layer

| Attribute | Detail |
|---|---|
| **PDU** | **Segment** (TCP) / **Datagram** (UDP) |
| **Function** | End-to-end logical connection; reliability, flow control, error recovery, multiplexing |
| **Key Protocols** | **TCP** (reliable, connection-oriented) and **UDP** (best-effort, connectionless) |
| **Port Numbers** | 0–65535 (Well-known: 0–1023) |
| **Telemedicine Use** | TCP for reliable transfer of patient records; UDP for real-time vital sign streaming |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Transport Layer is the heart of reliable communication.** It is the **first layer** that deals with end-to-end logical communication rather than physical hop-to-hop transmission.

---

### 🗺️ Layer 3 — Network Layer

| Attribute | Detail |
|---|---|
| **PDU** | **Packet** (also called datagram in IP context) |
| **Function** | Logical addressing (IP), routing of packets across multiple networks, packet forwarding, fragmentation & reassembly |
| **Key Protocols** | **IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, IGMP, OSPF, BGP, RIP, ARP** |
| **Devices** | **Routers** (the signature device of this layer) |
| **Telemedicine Use** | IP addressing of remote diagnostic devices, routing through hospital WANs |

---

### 🔗 Layer 2 — Data Link Layer

| Attribute | Detail |
|---|---|
| **PDU** | **Frame** |
| **Function** | Node-to-node delivery, MAC addressing, error detection/correction on the link, medium access control |
| **Sub-layers** | **LLC (Logical Link Control)** — upper; **MAC (Media Access Control)** — lower |
| **Key Protocols** | Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), PPP, HDLC, Frame Relay |
| **Devices** | **Switches, Bridges, NICs (Network Interface Cards)** |
| **Telemedicine Use** | MAC addressing of bedside monitoring devices on the hospital LAN |

---

### ⚡ Layer 1 — Physical Layer

| Attribute | Detail |
|---|---|
| **PDU** | **Bit** |
| **Function** | Transmits raw bits over a physical medium; defines voltages, timings, physical topology, cable specs, connector pinouts |
| **Standards** | RS-232, V.35, RJ-45, Fiber-optic specs, IEEE 802.3 physical layer |
| **Devices** | **Hubs, Repeaters, Cables, Connectors, Network Interface Cards (physical side)** |
| **Telemedicine Use** | Fiber backbone connecting multi-specialty hospitals; wireless RF links for rural PHCs |

---

## 2.2 The TCP/IP Four-Layer Model — Detailed Breakdown

The **TCP/IP Model (also called the Internet Protocol Suite)** was developed by **DARPA** for the ARPANET and is the practical protocol stack that powers the modern Internet. It condenses the seven OSI layers into **four** (sometimes five, when Link is split into Physical + Data Link).

### Layer 1 — Network Access (Link) Layer
- **Combines** OSI Physical + Data Link layers.
- **Concerns:** How bits are placed on the medium; framing; MAC addressing.
- **Examples:** Ethernet, Wi-Fi, ARP, PPP.

### Layer 2 — Internet Layer
- **Maps to** OSI Network Layer.
- **Concerns:** Logical addressing, routing, packet forwarding.
- **Core Protocol:** **IP (Internet Protocol)** — IPv4/IPv6.
- **Supporting Protocols:** ICMP, ARP, IGMP.

### Layer 3 — Transport Layer
- **Identical in role** to OSI Transport Layer.
- **Core Protocols:** **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** and **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)**.

### Layer 4 — Application Layer
- **Combines** OSI Session + Presentation + Application layers.
- **Examples:** HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DNS, **HL7 over TCP**, **DICOM over TCP**, SSH, Telnet, SNMP.

---

## 2.3 🧮 KTU Formula Sheet & Engineering Metrics Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although networking models are largely conceptual, the following formulas are essential for solving **delay, throughput, and bandwidth problems** that frequently appear in KTU Module 2 examinations on Telemedicine data transmission.

### 📋 A. Core Transmission Delay Formulas

| Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{s}$ | **Propagation Delay** — time for one bit to travel the link | $d$ = distance (m), $s$ = propagation speed (m/s). In copper/fiber: $s \approx 2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$ |
| $T_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | **Transmission Delay** — time to push all packet bits onto the wire | $L$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = link bandwidth (bps) |
| $T_{proc}$ | **Processing Delay** — time routers spend examining header & deciding next hop | Typically $\mu$s range |
| $T_{queue}$ | **Queuing Delay** — time packet waits in router buffer | Depends on traffic load |
| $T_{total} = T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{proc} + T_{queue}$ | **Total End-to-End Delay** | Sum of all four components |
| $BDP = R \times RTT$ | **Bandwidth-Delay Product** — max unacknowledged bits in flight | $R$ (bps) × RTT (s) → bits |

### 📋 B. Channel Capacity (Nyquist & Shannon)

| Theorem | Formula | Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| **Nyquist** (Noiseless Channel) | $C = 2B \log_{2}(M)$ | $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $M$ = signal levels |
| **Shannon** (Noisy Channel) | $C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio (not in dB!) |
| **S/N in dB** | $S/N_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Use to convert from dB to linear ratio |

### 📋 C. PDU Naming Table (MUST MEMORIZE for KTU)

| OSI Layer | PDU Name | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|
| Application / Presentation / Session | **Data / Message** | Calling it "packet" ✗ |
| Transport | **Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP)** | Calling it "frame" ✗ |
| Network | **Packet / Datagram** | Calling it "frame" ✗ |
| Data Link | **Frame** | Calling it "packet" ✗ |
| Physical | **Bit** | Calling it "frame" ✗ |

### 📋 D. Throughput & Goodput Metrics in Telemedicine

| Metric | Definition | Telemedicine Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Throughput** | Actual bits successfully delivered per second | End-to-end bandwidth utilization for DICOM uploads |
| **Goodput** | Useful application-layer data per second (excludes overhead) | Critical for measuring actual EHR transfer rate |
| **Jitter** | Variation in packet delay | Critical for VoIP/video telemedicine — high jitter = poor call quality |
| **Packet Loss Rate** | $\dfrac{Lost\ Packets}{Total\ Sent\ Packets} \times 100\%$ | Loss > 2% ruins video consultation |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Telemedicine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in Telemedicine (PEEBT861 Context):**
> 1. **HL7 (Health Level Seven)** — The name literally comes from **Layer 7 (Application Layer)** of the OSI model. It defines standards for exchanging clinical and administrative healthcare data.
> 2. **DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine)** — Operates at the Application Layer, relying on TCP (Transport Layer) for reliable image transfer.
> 3. **Telemedicine Video Conferencing** — Uses **RTP (Real-time Transport Protocol)** at the Application Layer over **UDP** at the Transport Layer because real-time video tolerates loss but cannot tolerate delay.
> 4. **Hospital Network Design** — Layer-1 fiber backbones, Layer-2 Ethernet switches for LAN segmentation, Layer-3 routers for inter-departmental routing, Layer-4 load balancers for telemedicine servers.
> 5. **HIPAA Compliance** — Requires encryption (Presentation Layer / TLS) and access control (Application Layer authentication) for all PHI transmission.

# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Encapsulation / Decapsulation Process — End-to-End Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> **Encapsulation** is the process of adding a header (and sometimes a trailer) at each layer as data moves **down** the protocol stack on the **sender side**. **Decapsulation** is the reverse process as data moves **up** the stack on the **receiver side**. This is a guaranteed **14-mark KTU question** pattern.

### 🔁 Sender Side — Encapsulation (Top-Down)

We trace a **500 KB DICOM medical image** sent from a CT scanner to a radiologist's workstation.

---

**Step 1: Application Layer (Layer 7) — DICOM Protocol**

The DICOM application generates the user data.

$$
\text{User Data} = 500 \text{ KB} = 500 \times 1024 \times 8 = 4{,}096{,}000 \text{ bits}
$$

The DICOM application may add an **Application Header (AH)** of, say, **200 bytes** containing patient metadata, study UID, and series number.

$$
L_{7} = 4{,}096{,}000 + (200 \times 8) = 4{,}097{,}600 \text{ bits}
$$

The PDU at this stage is called **Data** or **Message**.

---

**Step 2: Transport Layer (Layer 4) — TCP Segment**

TCP adds a **20-byte header** (minimum, no options) containing source port, destination port (e.g., 104 for DICOM), sequence number, ACK number, flags, window size, checksum, urgent pointer.

$$
\text{TCP Header} = 20 \text{ bytes} = 160 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
L_{4} = 4{,}097{,}600 + 160 = 4{,}097{,}760 \text{ bits}
$$

The PDU is now called a **TCP Segment**.

---

**Step 3: Network Layer (Layer 3) — IP Packet**

IP adds a **20-byte header** (IPv4, no options) containing version, IHL, total length, TTL, protocol (6 = TCP), header checksum, source IP, destination IP.

$$
\text{IP Header} = 20 \text{ bytes} = 160 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
L_{3} = 4{,}097{,}760 + 160 = 4{,}097{,}920 \text{ bits}
$$

The PDU is now called a **Packet** (or IP Datagram).

---

**Step 4: Data Link Layer (Layer 2) — Ethernet Frame**

Ethernet II adds a **14-byte header** (6-byte destination MAC + 6-byte source MAC + 2-byte EtherType) and a **4-byte trailer** (Frame Check Sequence / CRC).

$$
\text{Ethernet Overhead} = 14 + 4 = 18 \text{ bytes} = 144 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
L_{2} = 4{,}097{,}920 + 144 = 4{,}098{,}064 \text{ bits}
$$

The PDU is now called a **Frame**.

---

**Step 5: Physical Layer (Layer 1) — Bits on the Wire**

The frame is converted into a sequence of **bits** and transmitted as electrical/optical/RF signals. **Preamble** (7 bytes = 56 bits) and **SFD** (1 byte = 8 bits) precede the frame, but we treat them as part of the physical encoding, not the data PDU.

$$
L_{1\_{total\_{on\_{wire}}} = 4{,}098{,}064 + (8 \times 64) = 4{,}098{,}576 \text{ bits}
$$

The PDU is now a stream of **Bits**.

---

### 📊 Encapsulation Overhead Analysis Matrix

| Layer | PDU Name | Header Added | Header Size (bits) | Cumulative Size (bits) | % Overhead |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 7 - Application | Data | DICOM AH | 1600 | 4,097,600 | 0.039% |
| 4 - Transport | Segment | TCP Header | 160 | 4,097,760 | 0.043% |
| 3 - Network | Packet | IP Header | 160 | 4,097,920 | 0.047% |
| 2 - Data Link | Frame | Ethernet Header + Trailer | 144 | 4,098,064 | 0.050% |
| 1 - Physical | Bits | Preamble + SFD | 512 | 4,098,576 | 0.063% |

**Overhead Formula (General):**

$$
\% \text{Overhead} = \dfrac{\text{Total Headers + Trailers}}{\text{Total Transmitted Bits}} \times 100\%
$$

**Key Insight:** For large payloads (like DICOM images), the overhead is **negligible (< 0.1%)**. For small telemetry packets (e.g., a 32-byte heart rate reading), the same headers dominate, and overhead can exceed **70%** — a critical concern in IoT-based remote patient monitoring.

---

### 🔁 Receiver Side — Decapsulation (Bottom-Up)

The receiver reverses each step:

1. **Physical Layer** receives bits, recovers clock, and hands bytes to Data Link.
2. **Data Link Layer** checks CRC, strips Ethernet header/trailer, hands Packet to Network.
3. **Network Layer** verifies IP header checksum, decrements TTL, routes to Transport.
4. **Transport Layer** reassembles segments in order using sequence numbers, ACKs sender, hands Data to Application.
5. **Application Layer** interprets the DICOM payload, displays the CT image in the radiologist's PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical: Telemedicine Network Delay Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Problem (Dec 2024 pattern):**
> *A telemedicine link connects a Primary Health Centre (PHC) in a rural village to a district hospital. The distance is 50 km, the link bandwidth is 1 Gbps, the packet size is 1500 bytes, the propagation speed is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s, the processing delay at each router is 0.5 ms, and the queuing delay is 1 ms. The path passes through 3 routers. Calculate the total end-to-end delay.*

---

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= 50 \text{ km} = 50{,}000 \text{ m} \\
R &= 1 \text{ Gbps} = 10^9 \text{ bps} \\
L &= 1500 \text{ bytes} = 12{,}000 \text{ bits} \\
s &= 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} \\
T_{proc\_{per\_{hop}}} &= 0.5 \text{ ms} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\
T_{queue} &= 1 \text{ ms} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s (per hop)} \\
\text{Number of routers} &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 1: Calculate Transmission Delay**

$$
T_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12{,}000}{10^9} = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 12 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Propagation Delay**

$$
T_{prop} = \frac{d}{s} = \frac{50{,}000}{2 \times 10^8} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.25 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Total Processing Delay (3 routers + 1 destination)**

$$
T_{proc\_{total}} = 4 \times 0.5 \text{ ms} = 2.0 \text{ ms}
$$

> The packet is processed at the source NIC, at each of 3 routers, and at the destination NIC = **4 processing nodes**.

**Step 4: Calculate Total Queuing Delay (at each router)**

$$
T_{queue\_{total}} = 3 \times 1 \text{ ms} = 3.0 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5: Sum All Delays**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{proc\_{total}} + T_{queue\_{total}} \\
&= 0.25 \text{ ms} + 0.012 \text{ ms} + 2.0 \text{ ms} + 3.0 \text{ ms} \\
&= 5.262 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** $T_{total} = 5.262 \text{ ms}$

**Engineering Verdict:** A delay of ~5.26 ms is **excellent for telemedicine** — well below the **150 ms** ITU-T threshold for real-time voice/video. The dominant delays are **queuing** (57%) and **processing** (38%); upgrading routers would yield the biggest improvement.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: TCP vs UDP for Telemedicine

| Property | TCP | UDP | Telemedicine Choice |
|---|---|---|---|
| Connection | Connection-oriented (3-way handshake) | Connectionless | TCP for records, UDP for live vitals |
| Reliability | ACK-based, retransmission | No ACK, no retransmit | TCP for EHR, UDP for ECG stream |
| Ordering | In-order delivery guaranteed | No ordering | TCP for DICOM |
| Header Size | **20 bytes** (minimum) | **8 bytes** | UDP lighter for IoMT sensors |
| Flow Control | Sliding window | None | TCP for hospital servers |
| Congestion Control | Yes (cubic, Reno) | None | TCP for WAN backups |
| Speed | Slower (overhead) | Faster | UDP for video |
| Used By | HTTP, FTP, SSH, HL7, DICOM | DNS, VoIP, RTP, live streaming | Telemedicine is split-use |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: OSI vs TCP/IP Layer Mapping

| OSI Layer | Function | TCP/IP Layer | Example Protocols |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 - Application | User interface & network services | **Application** | HTTP, FTP, **HL7**, **DICOM**, SMTP, DNS |
| 6 - Presentation | Encryption, compression, encoding | *(combined)* | TLS/SSL, JPEG |
| 5 - Session | Dialog management, sync | *(combined)* | SIP, RPC |
| 4 - Transport | End-to-end reliability | **Transport** | **TCP, UDP** |
| 3 - Network | Routing, logical addressing | **Internet** | **IP, ICMP, ARP, OSPF** |
| 2 - Data Link | Framing, MAC addressing | **Link (Network Access)** | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP |
| 1 - Physical | Bits on wire | *(included in Link)* | RS-232, Fiber, RJ-45 |

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three differences between the OSI model and the TCP/IP model.*

#### **Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Has **7 layers** — Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical | Has **4 layers** — Application, Transport, Internet, Link |
| 2 | Developed by **ISO** as a theoretical reference model | Developed by **DARPA/IETF** as a practical implementation model |
| 3 | OSI is **protocol-independent**; protocols were never fully adopted globally | TCP/IP is **protocol-specific** (TCP, IP, UDP, HTTP); it is the foundation of the Internet |
| 4 *(bonus for 3 marks)* | Strict layering; clear separation of session & presentation | Loose layering; session & presentation functions merged into application |

> **Awarding scheme:** 1 mark per correctly explained difference × 3 = 3 marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the Protocol Data Unit (PDU) at each of the following OSI layers: (i) Network Layer, (ii) Data Link Layer, (iii) Transport Layer? Name one device that operates at the Network Layer.*

#### **Model Answer (3 marks):**

| OSI Layer | PDU Name | Device Example |
|---|---|---|
| Network Layer (Layer 3) | **Packet** (or Datagram) | **Router** |
| Data Link Layer (Layer 2) | **Frame** | Switch / Bridge / NIC |
| Transport Layer (Layer 4) | **Segment** (for TCP) or **Datagram** (for UDP) | Gateway (operates across layers) |

> **Awarding scheme:** 1 mark each for (i), (ii), (iii). Full 3 marks require correct PDU names + device for at least the Network Layer.

## 5.2 Part B Questions — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**
*(a)* Explain the seven layers of the OSI reference model with the function and at least one protocol/device example for each layer. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Describe the TCP/IP model. Compare it with the OSI model. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **📝 Model Solution**

**Part (a) — 7 Layers of the OSI Model (7 marks)**

*Each layer is awarded 1 mark — ½ mark for function, ½ mark for protocol/device example.*

**1. Physical Layer (Layer 1)**
- **Function:** Transmits raw bits as electrical, optical, or RF signals over the physical medium. Defines voltage levels, cable specs, connector pinouts, and physical topology.
- **Protocols/Devices:** **Ethernet physical specs (IEEE 802.3), RJ-45, Fiber-optic cable, Hub, Repeater**

**2. Data Link Layer (Layer 2)**
- **Function:** Organizes bits into **frames**, performs **MAC addressing**, **error detection** (CRC), and **medium access control**. Sub-divided into **LLC** (Logical Link Control) and **MAC** (Media Access Control).
- **Protocols/Devices:** **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), PPP, Switch, Bridge**

**3. Network Layer (Layer 3)**
- **Function:** Provides **logical addressing (IP)**, **routing** of packets across multiple networks, **fragmentation & reassembly**, and **packet forwarding** through routers.
- **Protocols/Devices:** **IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, OSPF, BGP, Router**

**4. Transport Layer (Layer 4)**
- **Function:** Ensures **end-to-end logical communication** between source and destination processes. Provides **reliable (TCP)** or **best-effort (UDP)** delivery, **flow control**, **error recovery**, and **multiplexing via port numbers**.
- **Protocols/Devices:** **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol), UDP (User Datagram Protocol), Port numbers (0–65535)**

**5. Session Layer (Layer 5)**
- **Function:** Establishes, manages, and terminates **sessions/dialogs** between two communicating applications. Provides **dialog control (half/full duplex)** and **synchronization checkpoints** for long transfers.
- **Protocols/Devices:** **SIP (Session Initiation Protocol), RPC (Remote Procedure Call), NetBIOS, PPTP**

**6. Presentation Layer (Layer 6)**
- **Function:** Handles **data translation** (ASCII ↔ EBCDIC), **encryption/decryption** (e.g., AES, TLS), **data compression** (JPEG, MPEG), and **serialization/deserialization**.
- **Protocols/Devices:** **TLS/SSL, JPEG, MPEG, ASCII, XDR**

**7. Application Layer (Layer 7)**
- **Function:** Provides **network services directly to the end-user application** — the closest layer to the user. Protocols for email, file transfer, web browsing, name resolution.
- **Protocols/Devices:** **HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DNS, HL7, DICOM, SNMP, Web browsers, Email clients**

---

**Part (b) — TCP/IP Model & Comparison (7 marks)**

*TCP/IP Description (3 marks) + Comparison Table (4 marks)*

**TCP/IP Description (3 marks):**

The TCP/IP (Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol) model, also called the **Internet Protocol Suite**, was developed by **DARPA (Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency)** in the 1970s and is maintained by the **IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force)**. It has **4 layers**:

1. **Application Layer** — Combines OSI's Session, Presentation, and Application layers. Hosts high-level protocols such as HTTP, FTP, DNS, **HL7, DICOM**.
2. **Transport Layer** — Same role as OSI Layer 4. Hosts **TCP** (reliable, connection-oriented) and **UDP** (unreliable, connectionless).
3. **Internet Layer** — Same role as OSI Layer 3. Hosts **IP (IPv4, IPv6), ICMP, ARP**.
4. **Link (Network Access) Layer** — Combines OSI's Data Link and Physical layers. Hosts **Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP**.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| S.No. | Feature | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of Layers | 7 | 4 |
| 2 | Origin | ISO | DARPA / IETF |
| 3 | Approach | Theoretical / Reference | Practical / Implementation |
| 4 | Protocol Dependency | Protocol-independent | Protocol-specific (TCP, IP, UDP) |
| 5 | Session & Presentation | Separate layers | Merged into Application |
| 6 | Usage | Teaching & reference | Backbone of the modern Internet |
| 7 | Reliability of stack | Conceptually rigorous; not always implemented | Actually deployed worldwide |
| 8 | Strict Layering | Strict; each layer uses only the layer below | Loose; Application can bypass Transport if needed |

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*(a)* With a neat diagram, explain the **encapsulation and decapsulation process** during data transmission through the OSI layers. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A telemedicine link connects a rural clinic to a city hospital. The distance is **100 km**, bandwidth is **2 Mbps**, packet size is **2000 bytes**, propagation speed is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s, processing delay per router is **1 ms**, queuing delay is **2 ms**, and there are **4 routers** in the path. Calculate the total end-to-end delay. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **📝 Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Encapsulation & Decapsulation (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
**Encapsulation** is the process of adding protocol-specific headers (and sometimes trailers) to user data as it moves **down** the OSI layers on the sender side. **Decapsulation** is the reverse process on the receiver side, where each layer strips its corresponding header and passes the payload up.

**Encapsulation (Sender Side) — Step-by-Step (3 marks):**

| Step | Layer | Header Added | Resulting PDU |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 7 - Application | Application Header (e.g., DICOM, HTTP) | **Data / Message** |
| 2 | 6 - Presentation | TLS / Encoding header | **Data** |
| 3 | 5 - Session | Session token / control info | **Data** |
| 4 | 4 - Transport | TCP (20 B) or UDP (8 B) header | **Segment / Datagram** |
| 5 | 3 - Network | IP header (20 B for IPv4) | **Packet** |
| 6 | 2 - Data Link | Ethernet header (14 B) + CRC trailer (4 B) | **Frame** |
| 7 | 1 - Physical | Bit encoding (e.g., NRZ, Manchester) | **Bits** |

**Decapsulation (Receiver Side) (2 marks):**

Each layer on the receiver performs the **reverse** operation:
- **Layer 1:** Receives bits, recovers clock signal.
- **Layer 2:** Verifies CRC, strips Ethernet header & trailer.
- **Layer 3:** Verifies IP header checksum, decrements TTL, routes packet.
- **Layer 4:** Reassembles TCP segments in correct order using sequence numbers, ACKs sender.
- **Layer 5:** Manages session termination.
- **Layer 6:** Decrypts (TLS) and decompresses (JPEG) data.
- **Layer 7:** Delivers original data (e.g., DICOM image) to the application (PACS viewer).

**Diagram (1 mark):** Vertical stack of 7 layers with downward arrows on sender side, upward arrows on receiver side, labeled PDUs at each step (see SECTION 4.3).

---

**Part (b) — Total End-to-End Delay Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= 100 \text{ km} = 100{,}000 \text{ m} \\
R &= 2 \text{ Mbps} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} \\
L &= 2000 \text{ bytes} = 16{,}000 \text{ bits} \\
s &= 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} \\
T_{proc\_{per\_{hop}}} &= 1 \text{ ms} \\
T_{queue\_{per\_{hop}}} &= 2 \text{ ms} \\
n_{routers} &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: Transmission Delay (1 mark)**

$$
T_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{16{,}000}{2 \times 10^6} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 8 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Propagation Delay (1 mark)**

$$
T_{prop} = \frac{d}{s} = \frac{100{,}000}{2 \times 10^8} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Total Processing Delay (1 mark)**

Packet is processed at source + 4 routers + destination = **6 processing nodes**.

$$
T_{proc\_{total}} = 6 \times 1 \text{ ms} = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4: Total Queuing Delay (1 mark)**

Queuing happens at each of the 4 routers.

$$
T_{queue\_{total}} = 4 \times 2 \text{ ms} = 8 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5: Sum All Delays (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{proc\_{total}} + T_{queue\_{total}} \\
&= 0.5 \text{ ms} + 8 \text{ ms} + 6 \text{ ms} + 8 \text{ ms} \\
&= 22.5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer (2 marks):**

$$
\boxed{T_{total} = 22.5 \text{ ms}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A 22.5 ms delay is **well within the ITU-T recommended 150 ms** threshold for acceptable voice/video telemedicine quality. The dominant component is **queuing (35.6%)** followed by **transmission (35.6%)** and **processing (26.7%)**; propagation is negligible (2.2%) at this bandwidth–distance combination.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **PDU Naming Errors** — Students often call a Transport layer PDU a "packet" or a Network layer PDU a "frame". Memorize the table in SECTION 2.3.C. Each wrong PDU name = **−½ to −1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing OSI and TCP/IP Layer Counts** — Do not say "TCP/IP has 7 layers" or "OSI has 4 layers". This is a 1-mark killer.
> 3. **Device-Layer Mismatch** — Routers operate at **Layer 3** (not Layer 2). Switches operate at **Layer 2** (not Layer 3). Hubs/Repeaters are **Layer 1**.
> 4. **Delay Calculation Forgetting Nodes** — Many students apply processing delay to **only the routers**, forgetting that the source and destination also process the packet. **Always count** = (1 source + N routers + 1 destination) = **N+2 processing nodes**.
> 5. **Unit Conversion Blunder** — Distance must be in **meters** (not km) and bandwidth in **bps** (not Mbps) before substituting into formulas. This is the **#1 cause of arithmetic errors** in KTU papers.
> 6. **Application vs Network Layer Protocols** — HL7 is an **Application Layer** protocol (despite the "7" in its name, which refers to Layer 7 of OSI). Do not confuse students or examiners.
> 7. **Skipping the Diagram in 14-mark Answers** — Part (a) on layers/encapsulation **must** include a diagram. Missing diagram = **−1 to −2 marks** even if content is correct.

---

## 5.3 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> 🎯 **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Print This Section!**

- ✅ **OSI = 7 layers; TCP/IP = 4 layers** — Most-asked question in Part A.
- ✅ **PDU Memory Code (top→down): "D-D-S-P-D-N-B" → Data, Data, Data, Segment, Packet, Frame, Bit** (Layers 7→1).
- ✅ **Mnemonic for OSI Layers (Top→Bottom): "All People Seem To Need Data Processing"**.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for OSI Layers (Bottom→Top): "Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"**.
- ✅ **Routers = Layer 3; Switches = Layer 2; Hubs/Repeaters = Layer 1; Gateways = Multi-layer**.
- ✅ **TCP header = 20 bytes (min); UDP header = 8 bytes; IPv4 header = 20 bytes; Ethernet frame = 14 B header + 4 B trailer**.
- ✅ **HL7 (Health Level Seven) = Layer 7 protocol for healthcare data exchange — name comes from OSI Layer 7**.
- ✅ **DICOM = Application layer protocol for medical imaging (CT, MRI, X-Ray) — uses TCP port 104**.
- ✅ **TCP = reliable, connection-oriented, used for EHR/DICOM; UDP = fast, connectionless, used for real-time ECG/video**.
- ✅ **Total Delay Formula:** $T_{total} = T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{proc} + T_{queue}$.
- ✅ **Always count processing nodes as (N routers + 2) — including source and destination**.
- ✅ **Convert all units to SI before calculating** — distance in m, bandwidth in bps, time in seconds.
- ✅ **Encapsulation goes DOWN the stack on sender; Decapsulation goes UP the stack on receiver**.
- ✅ **Each layer provides services to the layer above and uses services of the layer below** — modularity principle.
- ✅ **TCP/IP Application Layer = OSI (Application + Presentation + Session)** combined.
- ✅ **TCP/IP Link Layer = OSI (Data Link + Physical)** combined.
- ✅ **ITU-T acceptable delay for telemedicine voice/video = 150 ms (one-way); > 400 ms is unusable**.
- ✅ **TLS encryption = Layer 6 (Presentation) in OSI; functions absorbed into TCP/IP Application Layer**.

> 🏁 **All the best for your KTU ESE — remember to draw diagrams, label PDUs, and convert units!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Addressing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT861 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Addressing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Telemedicine (PEEBT861)**, **Addressing** refers to the systematic methodology of uniquely identifying, locating, and routing entities (patients, healthcare providers, medical records, devices, and information packets) within a distributed healthcare communication network. Addressing is the foundational layer that ensures the correct delivery of clinical data, diagnostic signals, and administrative information between spatially separated nodes in a telemedicine ecosystem.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for Module 2, addressing encompasses three primary dimensions:
1. **Patient Addressing** — Unique identification of individuals receiving remote healthcare services
2. **Information/Data Addressing** — Routing of medical data packets, EHR records, and clinical communications
3. **Network/Spatial Addressing** — Logical and physical addressing schemes enabling connectivity between telemedicine endpoints

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition**: Addressing in Telemedicine is the process of assigning unique identifiers (logical or physical) to patients, healthcare providers, medical devices, and data packets to ensure unambiguous identification, secure routing, and reliable delivery of healthcare information across distributed telemedicine networks.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

**Real-World Analogy — The Postal System**:

Imagine you want to send a letter to your friend. Without a proper address (house number, street, city, PIN code), the postal system cannot deliver the letter. Similarly, in telemedicine:
- **The Letter** = Medical data (ECG signal, X-ray, prescription, EHR)
- **The Address** = Unique Patient ID, Provider ID, IP Address, or URL
- **The Postal Service** = Telemedicine network infrastructure
- **The Delivery Confirmation** = Acknowledgment (ACK) signal in the communication protocol

Just as the postal system uses hierarchical addressing (Country → State → City → Street → House), telemedicine uses hierarchical addressing (Country Code → Hospital Code → Department Code → Patient ID) to ensure clinical data reaches the **correct recipient without ambiguity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Addressing is Critical in Telemedicine**:
> - Prevents **medical errors** caused by misrouted patient data
> - Ensures **data privacy and security** through authenticated addressing
> - Enables **interoperability** between heterogeneous healthcare systems
> - Supports **regulatory compliance** (e.g., HIPAA, DISHA in India) for traceability

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Standard/Reference |
|------|------------|-------------------|
| **Unique Patient Identifier (UPI)** | A single, non-repeating ID assigned to each patient | ISO 27799, ABDM (India) |
| **HL7 Address** | Healthcare-specific addressing within HL7 messaging standards | HL7 v2.x / FHIR |
| **IP Address (IPv4/IPv6)** | Logical network address for telemedicine endpoints | IETF RFC 791 / RFC 8200 |
| **MAC Address** | Physical hardware address of telemedicine devices | IEEE 802 |
| **Geospatial Address** | GPS/location-based address for mobile telemedicine units | WGS-84 standard |
| **National Provider Identifier (NPI)** | Unique ID for healthcare providers | NPI Registry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric**: A valid telemedicine address must be **globally unique**, **persistent** (unchanged over time), and **non-reversible** (cannot derive personal identity from the ID alone for privacy).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Addressing in Telemedicine

Addressing in telemedicine can be classified into a **four-tier hierarchy** based on the entity being addressed:

### Tier 1: Patient-Level Addressing
- **Purpose**: Uniquely identify each individual patient across the healthcare system
- **Methods**:
  - **Biometric Addressing**: Fingerprint, iris scan, facial recognition
  - **Demographic-Based**: Name + DOB + Gender hashing
  - **National ID Integration**: Aadhaar (India), SSN (USA), NHS Number (UK)
  - **ABHA ID** (Ayushman Bharat Health Account) — India's flagship health addressing standard

### Tier 2: Provider-Level Addressing
- **Purpose**: Identify healthcare professionals, specialists, and institutions
- **Methods**:
  - National Provider Identifier (NPI)
  - Medical Council Registration Numbers
  - Hospital/Clinic Unique IDs
  - Department-level sub-addressing (Cardiology, Radiology, etc.)

### Tier 3: Data/Packet-Level Addressing
- **Purpose**: Route medical information packets through the network
- **Methods**:
  - IP Addressing (logical)
  - MAC Addressing (physical)
  - Port Addressing (application-level)
  - HL7 Segment Addressing (field-level routing)

### Tier 4: Spatial/Geospatial Addressing
- **Purpose**: Identify the physical location of telemedicine nodes
- **Methods**:
  - GPS Coordinates (Latitude/Longitude)
  - Postal Address with PIN/ZIP code
  - Geo-fenced addressing for mobile telemedicine vans
  - GIS-based location addressing

## 2.2 Logical Address Structure for Telemedicine

A typical telemedicine address follows a **hierarchical concatenation model**:

$$\text{Address} = \text{CountryCode} \mid \text{StateCode} \mid \text{HospitalID} \mid \text{DepartmentCode} \mid \text{PatientID}$$

### Example (India — ABDM Compliant):
