- $N$ = number of switches in the path
- $t_{s}$ = per-switch setup time
- $t_{p}$ = propagation delay per link
- $T_{d}$ = data transmission time
- $L$ = total data to transfer (bits)
- $R$ = link bit-rate (bits/s)

The **total elapsed time** for a circuit-switched transmission is:

$$
T_{total}^{CS} = N \cdot t_s + (N+1) \cdot t_p + \frac{L}{R}
$$

---

## 2.2 Datagram Packet-Switched Networks

### 2.2.1 Operational Logic

1. The message is broken into **packets** of fixed or variable size, each with a **header** containing full source/destination addresses.
2. Each switch consults its **routing table** independently and chooses the next hop.
3. Packets may follow **divergent paths** and arrive **out of order**.
4. The destination reassembles the original message.

> [!NOTE]
> The Internet Protocol (IP) is the canonical example. Each IP datagram is routed independently — making the network *connectionless* and *best-effort*.

### 2.2.2 Total Time for Datagram Network

$$
T_{total}^{DG} = \frac{L}{R} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{P_{size}}\right) \cdot (N+1) \cdot t_p + N \cdot t_{proc}
$$

where:
- $P_{size}$ = packet size in bits
- $t_{proc}$ = per-switch processing/queueing time

---

## 2.3 Virtual-Circuit-Switched Networks

### 2.3.1 Types of Virtual Circuits

| Type | Full Form | Description | Example Network |
|------|-----------|-------------|-----------------|
| **PVC** | Permanent / Permanent Virtual Circuit | Pre-configured by the network operator; always available; no call-setup. | Frame Relay, ATM leased lines |
| **SVC** | Switched Virtual Circuit | Established dynamically per session; similar call-setup to circuit switching. | X.25, ATM signalling, MPLS LSPs |

### 2.3.2 Two Sub-Phases of SVC

1. **VC Establishment Phase** — A signaling message traverses the network, allocating a **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)** at each switch. The path is recorded in each switch's VC table.
2. **Data Transfer Phase** — All subsequent packets carry only the short **VCI** (not the full address). Each switch performs a fast table lookup and forwards the packet along the pre-determined path.
3. **VC Teardown Phase** — A signaling message removes the VC entries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Header Efficiency:** The use of short VCIs instead of full IP addresses (32/128 bits) drastically reduces per-packet overhead — a vital advantage for low-bandwidth medical telemetry channels.

### 2.3.3 Total Time for Virtual Circuit

$$
T_{total}^{VC} = N \cdot t_s + (N+1) \cdot t_p + \frac{L}{R} \cdot k
$$

where $k$ is the number of packets (so $L/R$ is replaced by the per-packet transmission time multiplied by the number of packets). Note that the **per-switch processing is reduced** because no full routing table lookup is needed — only a VCI table lookup.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Master these expressions — they are heavily tested in KTU numerical problems.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Total circuit-switched time | $T_{total}^{CS} = N \cdot t_s + (N+1) \cdot t_p + \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $N, t_s, t_p, L, R$ | seconds |
| 2 | Number of packets | $k = \left\lceil \dfrac{L}{P_{size}} \right\rceil$ | $L, P_{size}$ | packets |
| 3 | Transmission time per packet | $t_{tx} = \dfrac{P_{size}}{R}$ | $P_{size}, R$ | seconds |
| 4 | Last-packet arrival (datagram) | $T_{last}^{DG} = (N+1)\left(\dfrac{L}{R}+t_p\right) + N \cdot t_{proc}$ | — | seconds |
| 5 | Last-packet arrival (VC, store-and-forward) | $T_{last}^{VC} = (N+k-1)\left(t_{tx}+t_p\right) + N \cdot t_s$ | $k$ packets, $N$ hops | seconds |
| 6 | Throughput efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{size}-H}{P_{size}}$ | $H$ = header size (bits) | ratio (0–1) |
| 7 | End-to-end delay bound (VC) | $D_{max} = \sum_{i=1}^{N+1}\left(t_{tx}+t_p+t_{q,i}\right) + N \cdot t_{proc}$ | $t_{q,i}$ = queueing at hop $i$ | seconds |
| 8 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = R \cdot t_p$ | $R, t_p$ | bits |
| 9 | Call-blocking probability (Erlang B) | $B(C, A) = \dfrac{\dfrac{A^C}{C!}}{\sum_{i=0}^{C}\dfrac{A^i}{i!}}$ | $A$ = offered traffic (Erlangs), $C$ = circuits | dimensionless |
| 10 | Circuit utilization | $U = \dfrac{T_{data}}{T_{data}+T_{idle}}$ | data time, idle time | ratio (0–1) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Header convention:** $P_{size}$ denotes the *total* packet size (header + payload). To get payload only, subtract header $H$ bits.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of These Models

- **Network Dimensioning:** Erlang B formula helps telemedical network planners size the number of voice circuits for a tele-consultation call center.
- **QoS Engineering:** BDP and queueing delay bounds are used to design jitter buffers in ECG-over-IP equipment.
- **Comparative Cost Analysis:** The expressions quantify the trade-off between setup latency (CS, SVC) and per-packet processing overhead (DG, VC).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Transmission Time in a Circuit-Switched Network

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
Suppose a telemedicine session is set up between a rural Primary Health Centre (PHC) and a tertiary hospital. The signal path traverses $N = 4$ intermediate switches. The end-to-end message has $L = 32{,}000$ bits. Each link has bit-rate $R = 8{,}000$ bits/s. Setup time per switch is $t_s = 0.5$ s. Propagation delay per link is $t_p = 0.1$ s. Compute the total elapsed time.

### 3.1.2 Derivation

We model the total time as the sum of three additive contributions: connection setup, propagation, and pure data transmission.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total}^{CS} &= N \cdot t_s + (N+1) \cdot t_p + \frac{L}{R} \\[4pt]
&= 4 \cdot 0.5 + (4+1) \cdot 0.1 + \frac{32{,}000}{8{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= 2.0 + 0.5 + 4.0 \\[4pt]
&= 6.5 \; \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The telemedicine call takes 6.5 seconds from request initiation to fully received data — comprising 2.0 s of setup, 0.5 s of propagation, and 4.0 s of data transmission.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Last-Packet Arrival Time in a Virtual-Circuit Network (Store-and-Forward)

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup
Consider a virtual-circuit telemedicine link crossing $N = 3$ hops. A message of $L = 12{,}000$ bits is segmented into $k = 3$ packets of $P_{size} = 4{,}000$ bits each. Link rate is $R = 4{,}000$ bits/s. Propagation delay per hop is $t_p = 0.05$ s. Per-switch processing time is $t_{proc} = 0.02$ s. The switch uses *store-and-forward* behaviour, and the virtual circuit is pre-established (no setup delay). Compute the time at which the destination receives the **last** packet.

### 3.2.2 Derivation

**Step 1 — Per-packet transmission time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{tx} = \frac{P_{size}}{R} = \frac{4{,}000}{4{,}000} = 1.0 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — End-to-end propagation and processing of a single packet:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{end-to-end} = (N+1) \cdot t_p + N \cdot t_{proc} = 4 \cdot 0.05 + 3 \cdot 0.02 = 0.20 + 0.06 = 0.26 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Time the last packet *starts* being sent:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{start,last} = (k-1) \cdot t_{tx} = (3-1) \cdot 1.0 = 2.0 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the last packet's end-to-end transit (using the well-known store-and-forward formula for the *last* packet):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{last}^{VC} &= (N + k - 1) \cdot t_{tx} + (N+1) \cdot t_p + N \cdot t_{proc} \\[4pt]
&= (3 + 3 - 1) \cdot 1.0 + 0.20 + 0.06 \\[4pt]
&= 5.0 + 0.20 + 0.06 \\[4pt]
&= 5.26 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $(N+k-1)$?** Each of the $k$ packets occupies the first link for one $t_{tx}$, and the *last* packet itself traverses $N$ links × $t_{tx}$. Hence the last packet emerges from the source after $(k-1) \cdot t_{tx}$ (its predecessors have already cleared) and then takes $N \cdot t_{tx}$ to traverse the network.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Comparative Numerical Analysis (CS vs VC vs Datagram)

### 3.3.1 Common Parameters

- $N = 3$ hops
- $L = 24{,}000$ bits
- $R = 6{,}000$ bits/s
- $t_p = 0.05$ s per hop
- $t_s = 0.3$ s per switch (for CS and SVC)
- $t_{proc} = 0.01$ s per switch
- $k = 3$ packets (for packet-based schemes)
- $P_{size} = 8{,}000$ bits per packet

### 3.3.2 Case A — Circuit-Switched Total Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total}^{CS} &= N \cdot t_s + (N+1) \cdot t_p + \frac{L}{R} \\[4pt]
&= 3 \cdot 0.3 + 4 \cdot 0.05 + \frac{24{,}000}{6{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= 0.9 + 0.2 + 4.0 \\[4pt]
&= 5.1 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.3 Case B — Datagram Last-Packet Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{tx} &= \frac{8{,}000}{6{,}000} = 1.333 \; \text{s} \\[4pt]
T_{last}^{DG} &= (N+k-1) \cdot t_{tx} + (N+1) \cdot t_p + N \cdot t_{proc} \\[4pt]
&= (3+3-1) \cdot 1.333 + 4 \cdot 0.05 + 3 \cdot 0.01 \\[4pt]
&= 5 \cdot 1.333 + 0.20 + 0.03 \\[4pt]
&= 6.667 + 0.20 + 0.03 \\[4pt]
&= 6.897 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.4 Case C — Virtual-Circuit Last-Packet Time (with VC setup added)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{last}^{VC} &= N \cdot t_s + (N+k-1) \cdot t_{tx} + (N+1) \cdot t_p + N \cdot t_{proc} \\[4pt]
&= 0.9 + 5 \cdot 1.333 + 0.20 + 0.03 \\[4pt]
&= 0.9 + 6.667 + 0.20 + 0.03 \\[4pt]
&= 7.797 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Metric | Circuit-Switched | Virtual Circuit | Datagram |
|--------|:----------------:|:---------------:|:--------:|
| Setup latency | **High (0.9 s)** | Medium (0.9 s) | **None (0 s)** |
| Last-packet time | 5.1 s | 7.8 s | 6.9 s |
| Header overhead | **None** | Low (VCI) | **High (full address)** |
| Path consistency | Fixed | Fixed | **Variable** |
| Resource use | Wasted if idle | Shared | **Best (statistical mux)** |
| Best telemedicine use | Live ECG / Voice | ATM, Frame Relay, MPLS | Email, Web, DICOM upload |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Insight:** Circuit switching minimizes data transfer delay but pays a heavy setup tax. Datagram switching avoids setup but inflates per-packet processing. Virtual circuit is a balanced middle ground — ideal for **sustained telemedicine sessions** such as remote ICU monitoring.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Erlang B Blocking Probability for a Telemedicine Call Center

A district tele-consultation hub has $C = 5$ telephone circuits (trunks). The offered traffic from remote clinics is $A = 3$ Erlangs. Find the call-blocking probability.

$$
\begin{aligned}
B(C, A) &= \frac{\dfrac{A^C}{C!}}{\sum_{i=0}^{C} \dfrac{A^i}{i!}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\dfrac{3^5}{5!}}{\dfrac{3^0}{0!} + \dfrac{3^1}{1!} + \dfrac{3^2}{2!} + \dfrac{3^3}{3!} + \dfrac{3^4}{4!} + \dfrac{3^5}{5!}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\dfrac{243}{120}}{1 + 3 + 4.5 + 4.5 + 3.375 + 2.025} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2.025}{18.4} \\[6pt]
&= 0.110 \; \text{(approximately 11 %)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Roughly **11 % of incoming tele-consultation calls will be blocked** — a key input for capacity planning and Quality of Service compliance under KTU/TELECOM regulatory norms.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Phase Operation of Circuit Switching

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Patient PHC Source"] -->|Call Request + Destination| B["Switch S1"]
    B -->|Reserve Trunk| C["Switch S2"]
    C -->|Reserve Trunk| D["Switch S3"]
    D -->|Acknowledge| C
    C -->|Acknowledge| B
    B -->|Ring Tone| A
    A -->|Data Transmission Begins| B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D -->|Deliver Data| E["Hospital Specialist"]
    A -->|Teardown Signal| B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D -->|Release All Trunks| E
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above flowchart shows the three phases: (1) **Connection Establishment** (top section), (2) **Data Transfer** (middle), and (3) **Teardown** (bottom).

---

## 4.2 Datagram Switching — Independent Path Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Src["Source: PHC ECG Device"] --> R1["Router R1"]
    R1 -->|Packet 1| R2["Router R2"]
    R1 -->|Packet 2| R3["Router R3"]
    R1 -->|Packet 3| R4["Router R4"]
    R2 --> Dest["Destination: Hospital"]
    R3 --> Dest
    R4 --> Dest
    Dest --> Out["Out-of-Order Reassembly Buffer"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Observe how **Packet 1, 2, 3** can take **three different paths** — the hallmark of connectionless datagram service.

---

## 4.3 Virtual-Circuit Switching — Logical Path Reservation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Src["Source: Remote ICU Monitor"] -->|VCI = 42| S1["Switch S1"]
    S1 -->|VCI = 17| S2["Switch S2"]
    S2 -->|VCI = 09| S3["Switch S3"]
    S3 --> Dest["Destination: Specialist Console"]
    note1["All packets in this session carry VCI tags, not full IP addresses. Each switch uses a fast VCI table lookup."]
    note1 -.- S1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **VCI labels** (42 → 17 → 09) illustrate the *per-hop* local identifier that is swapped at each switch — analogous to MPLS label swapping.

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture of a Telemedicine Switching Node

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IN["Input Port Module"]
        InLine["Incoming Trunk Lines"]
        Demux["Time / Wavelength Demultiplexer"]
    end
    subgraph CTRL["Control & Signalling Unit"]
        SigProc["Signalling Processor (ISUP / SIP)"]
        RouteTab["Routing / VCI Table"]
        QMgr["Queue Manager"]
    end
    subgraph FAB["Switching Fabric"]
        TDMfab["TDM Space Switch"]
        PKTfab["Packet / Cell Fabric"]
    end
    subgraph OUT["Output Port Module"]
        Mux["Time / Wavelength Multiplexer"]
        OutLine["Outgoing Trunk Lines"]
    end
    InLine --> Demux --> FAB --> Mux --> OutLine
    CTRL --> FAB
    SigProc --> RouteTab
    RouteTab --> QMgr
    QMgr --> FAB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This block diagram shows the **internal anatomy** of a modern multi-service switching node capable of handling circuit-switched voice, ATM cells, IP packets, and medical telemetry on a common platform — a typical architecture found in next-generation telemedical backbone equipment.

---

## 4.5 Comparison Block Diagram — CS vs VC vs Datagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CS["CIRCUIT SWITCHING"] -->|Dedicated Physical Path| CSout["Constant Bandwidth, High Setup, Wasted Idle Capacity"]
    VC["VIRTUAL CIRCUIT"] -->|Logical Path with VCI| VCout["Predictable Delay, Efficient Resource Use, Moderate Setup"]
    DG["DATAGRAM"] -->|Independent Routing per Packet| DGout["Best-Effort, Robust, Variable Delay, High Header Cost"]
    CSout -->|Best For| UseCS["Real-time ECG Voice Telephony"]
    VCout -->|Best For| UseVC["Sustained Sessions Frame Relay ATM MPLS Telemetry"]
    DGout -->|Best For| UseDG["Email DICOM Upload Store and Forward"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Modeled on KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Part A pattern — concise, definition-oriented, and tagged with course outcomes.

---

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between circuit switching and virtual circuit switching in the context of telemedicine data transmission. Mention any two advantages of virtual circuit switching.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Circuit Switching | Virtual Circuit Switching |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------------|
| Path Type | Physical, dedicated end-to-end circuit | Logical path with VCI tags |
| Setup Phase | Required (3-way handshake) | Required (signaling) |
| Packetization | Continuous bit-stream (no packets) | Data is packetized |
| Header | None | Small VCI |
| Resource use | Reserved exclusively | Shared (statistical mux) |
| Best Suited For | Real-time voice / ECG | Burst-tolerant streams |

**Two advantages of Virtual Circuit Switching:**
1. **Reduced header overhead** — short VCI replaces full addressing, saving bandwidth.
2. **Guaranteed in-order delivery** along the established logical path, simplifying the receiver's design.

---

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is a datagram network? Why is it called "connectionless"? State one telemedicine application best served by datagram service.**

**Model Answer:**

A **datagram network** is a packet-switched network in which each packet (datagram) is routed **independently** based on the destination address in its header. There is **no pre-established path**, and packets of the same session may traverse different routes, arriving out of order.

It is called **connectionless** because no logical or physical connection is set up before data transfer — every datagram is self-routing.

> [!TIP]
> **Telemedicine Application:** **Store-and-forward transmission of DICOM radiological images** and **asynchronous email consultation reports** — both tolerate variable delay and do not require a pre-established session.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Part B carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. The two alternative questions below are independent choices.

---

### Question B-A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**Q: With neat diagrams, explain the three phases of operation in a circuit-switched network used for telemedicine. Compare circuit switching with virtual circuit switching in terms of delay, overhead, and resource utilization.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Three Phases of Circuit Switching

**Model Solution:**

1. **Connection Establishment Phase (Setup Phase):**
   The telemedicine source (e.g., a rural ECG transmitter) sends a call request to the destination through a series of switches.
   - Each switch examines the destination address.
   - A free trunk is reserved on each link along the path.
   - If all trunks are busy, the call is blocked (Erlang B analysis applies).
   - An acknowledgement is returned to the source.

2. **Data Transfer Phase:**
   - A dedicated, **fixed** path is now available.
   - Information (voice, ECG, control signals) flows as a continuous bit-stream.
   - All bits traverse the **same route** — no per-bit routing decisions.
   - Guaranteed bandwidth → constant end-to-end delay.

3. **Connection Teardown Phase:**
   - Either party signals disconnection.
   - All intermediate switches release the reserved trunks.
   - Network resources become available to other calls.

> **Diagram:** See the Mermaid flowchart in **Section 4.1** for a complete schematic.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Comparative Analysis (CS vs VC)

| Parameter | Circuit Switching | Virtual Circuit Switching |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------------|
| **Path** | Physical, dedicated | Logical, identified by VCI |
| **Setup Delay** | High (full circuit reservation) | Moderate (signaling + VCI table) |
| **Data Delay** | Constant, predictable | Bounded, nearly constant |
| **Header Overhead** | **None** | Small fixed VCI per packet |
| **Resource Use** | Poor if idle (wasteful) | Efficient (statistical mux) |
| **Failure Recovery** | Difficult (reroute whole circuit) | Easier (reroute VCI entries) |
| **Telemedicine Fit** | Voice, real-time ECG | Tele-radiology streams, ICU telemetry |

---

### Question B-B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**Q: A telemedicine link uses a virtual-circuit-switched network. The session crosses $N = 4$ intermediate switches. The total data is $L = 48{,}000$ bits, divided into $k = 6$ packets of equal size. The link bit-rate is $R = 12{,}000$ bits/s. Each switch has a processing time of $t_{proc} = 0.02$ s, and the propagation delay per hop is $t_p = 0.05$ s. The VC setup time per switch is $t_s = 0.3$ s.**

**(a)** Compute the time at which the destination receives the **last** packet.
**(b)** If the same data were sent via a circuit-switched network (with the same per-switch setup time), how much **additional** delay would circuit switching introduce compared to the virtual-circuit scheme? Comment on the result.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: VC Last-Packet Arrival Time

**Step 1 — Packet size:**

$$
P_{size} = \frac{L}{k} = \frac{48{,}000}{6} = 8{,}000 \; \text{bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Per-packet transmission time:**

$$
t_{tx} = \frac{P_{size}}{R} = \frac{8{,}000}{12{,}000} = 0.6667 \; \text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — VC setup time across $N = 4$ switches:**

$$
T_{setup} = N \cdot t_s = 4 \cdot 0.3 = 1.2 \; \text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of links** (including the final link to the destination):

$$
\text{links} = N + 1 = 5
$$

**Step 5 — End-to-end propagation + processing for one packet:**

$$
T_{end} = (N+1) \cdot t_p + N \cdot t_{proc} = 5 \cdot 0.05 + 4 \cdot 0.02 = 0.25 + 0.08 = 0.33 \; \text{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Last-packet arrival time using store-and-forward formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{last}^{VC} &= T_{setup} + (N + k - 1) \cdot t_{tx} + T_{end} \\[4pt]
&= 1.2 + (4 + 6 - 1) \cdot 0.6667 + 0.33 \\[4pt]
&= 1.2 + 9 \cdot 0.6667 + 0.33 \\[4pt]
&= 1.2 + 6.0 + 0.33 \\[4pt]
&= 7.53 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer (a):** The destination receives the last packet at $\boxed{T_{last}^{VC} = 7.53 \; \text{s}}$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Comparison with Circuit Switching

**Step 1 — Circuit-switched data transmission time** (no packetization):

$$
t_{data}^{CS} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{48{,}000}{12{,}000} = 4.0 \; \text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Total circuit-switched time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total}^{CS} &= N \cdot t_s + (N+1) \cdot t_p + t_{data}^{CS} \\[4pt]
&= 1.2 + 0.25 + 4.0 \\[4pt]
&= 5.45 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Additional delay of VC over CS:**

$$
\Delta T = T_{last}^{VC} - T_{total}^{CS} = 7.53 - 5.45 = 2.08 \; \text{s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comment:** Circuit switching delivers the **entire** 48,000 bits in 5.45 s because data is transmitted as one continuous stream. The VC scheme, although sharing the network with other sessions, incurs an *extra 2.08 s* due to **packetization overhead** — the time spent transmitting packet headers plus the store-and-forward pipelining delay. This is the **cost of statistical multiplexing**. For sustained, bulk telemedicine file transfers, packetization overhead is offset by far better link utilization. For *short, real-time* samples (e.g., 1-second ECG frame), the circuit-switched model would be preferable.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in this topic — read carefully!**
>
> 1. **Confusing $N$ with the number of *links*:** There are $N$ switches, hence $N+1$ links. The propagation term must use $N+1$, not $N$.
> 2. **Forgetting the VC setup time** $N \cdot t_s$ in SVC problems. PVCs do NOT have setup time — read the question!
> 3. **Wrong store-and-forward term:** For the *last* packet, the total transmission delay is $(N + k - 1) \cdot t_{tx}$, not $k \cdot t_{tx}$. This is a classic KTU trap.
> 4. **Mixing datagram and VC formulas:** Datagram has NO setup phase, so do not add $N \cdot t_s$ to datagram problems.
> 5. **Erlang B confusion:** Erlang B computes **blocking** probability, not utilization. Do not invert the formula.
> 6. **Unit slip-ups:** $L$ is in **bits**, $R$ in **bits/s** → $L/R$ is in **seconds**. Always annotate units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — memorize before the exam!**

- ☐ **Circuit Switching** = 3 phases: Setup → Transfer → Teardown; dedicated path; no headers; constant bandwidth; inefficient if idle.
- ☐ **Datagram Switching** = Connectionless; per-packet routing; full header; variable delay; high robustness; used in IP Internet.
- ☐ **Virtual Circuit Switching** = Connection-oriented logical path; VCI labels; in-order delivery; two types: **PVC** (permanent, no setup) and **SVC** (switched, with setup).
- ☐ **Three Time Components in any switching network:** (i) **Setup latency** $N \cdot t_s$ (CS / SVC only), (ii) **Propagation delay** $(N+1) \cdot t_p$, (iii) **Data transmission delay** (continuous for CS, packetized for VC/DG).
- ☐ **Key formula for last packet:** $T_{last} = (N + k - 1) \cdot t_{tx} + (N+1) \cdot t_p + N \cdot t_{proc} + T_{setup}$.
- ☐ **Erlang B** gives **call blocking probability**; input is offered traffic $A$ (Erlangs) and trunk count $C$.
- ☐ **Throughput efficiency** $\eta = (P_{size} - H)/P_{size}$ — larger packets → higher efficiency.
- ☐ **Telemedicine mapping:** Live voice / ECG → **CS**; sustained ICU telemetry / MPLS telemedicine → **VC**; DICOM upload / email → **Datagram (IP)**.
- ☐ **Bandwidth-Delay Product $BDP = R \cdot t_p$** determines the number of bits "in flight" — important for buffer sizing in telemedicine gateways.
- ☐ **Header overhead in datagram** is the *price* of connectionless flexibility; **VCI** in VC is a *trade* between flexibility and efficiency.
- ☐ **PVC vs SVC:** PVC is operator-provisioned (no signalling); SVC is on-demand (signalling required, like a phone call).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structure of a Switch
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT861 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Structure of a Switch

# Structure of a Switch

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
A **Telecommunication Switch** is a multi-port network element that establishes, maintains, and releases temporary logical or physical connections (called *call paths* or *circuits*) between two or more subscriber lines or trunks. In a modern digital exchange, the switch is built as a layered architecture of three fundamental subsystems:
1. **Switching Fabric (Network)** – the actual physical path that connects input ports to output ports.
2. **Control Subsystem** – the "brain" (CPU/processor) that decides *how* and *when* the fabric is to be reconfigured.
3. **Interface / Signaling Subsystem** – adapts user-side analog/digital signals, handles ringing, billing pulses, and inter-switch signaling (SS7 / ISUP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term *Structure of a Switch* specifically refers to the **functional block layout** of a stored-program-controlled (SPC) digital exchange — not the algorithm of switching. Students must learn the *internal blocks*, not just the abstract "black box."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a switch as the **internal air-traffic control tower of an airport**:

- **Runways & Gates** = *Subscriber Line Interfaces* (where planes—your voice—land and take off).
- **Control Tower** = *Central Processor / Control Unit* (decides which gate connects to which).
- **Underground baggage tunnel network** = *Switching Fabric* (carries the signal/voice from one gate to another without ever being seen).
- **Radio channel between two towers** = *Common Channel Signaling (SS7)* (lets the tower talk to other towers to coordinate handoffs).
- **Power room & backup generators** = *Power Supply & Battery Plant* (keeps the whole airport running 24×7×365).

Without the tower, the runways are useless. Without the tunnel, the tower's decisions mean nothing. Without signaling, the tower cannot pass a plane to a neighbouring airport. **All three must coexist.**

### 1.3 Key Physical Standards & Metrics
The following are standard quantitative descriptors of a switch:
- **Capacity (N)** – number of subscriber lines supported (typically **2,048 to 1,00,000+** in modern exchanges).
- **Switching Speed (setup time)** – **≤ 300 ms** for call setup as per **ITU-T E.721**.
- **Grade of Service (GoS)** – probability of call blockage; for digital local exchanges: **GoS ≤ 0.01** (i.e., **1%**) during busy hour.
- **Busy Hour Call Attempts (BHCA)** – typically **≥ 1,00,000 BHCA** for a Class-5 exchange.
- **Voice Sampling Rate** – **8 kHz** (Nyquist of 4 kHz audio) and **8-bit PCM** → **64 kbps** per voice channel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** A digital voice channel is always **64 kbps** because $8 \text{ k samples/s} \times 8 \text{ bits/sample} = 64 \text{ kbps}$. This is the foundational unit of all digital switching fabric capacity calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Functional Architecture
Modern SPC (Stored Program Controlled) digital exchanges are organized in three layers:

| Layer | Function | Typical Blocks |
|---|---|---|
| **A — Interface / Access Layer** | Converts analog subscriber signals to digital, supervises hook, ringing, dial pulses, supplies feed voltage | SLIC, Codec, Line Card, Trunk Card, Service Circuits |
| **B — Switching Fabric Layer** | Provides time/space connectivity between any two time slots of input/output PCM highways | Space Switch, Time Switch (TSI), Combined T-S-T / S-T-S matrices |
| **C — Control Layer** | Executes call-processing software, maintains call state, handles signaling, drives billing | Central Processor (CP), Memory (RAM/ROM/CM), Man–Machine Interface |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **KTU Examiner's golden rule** is: if asked "list the blocks of a switch," write exactly these three layers with their sub-blocks. Do *not* confuse it with the OSI model or the TCP/IP stack.

## 2.2 Detailed Subsystem Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Switching Fabric (Network)
The fabric can be of three types, and KTU students must compare them:

- **Space Division Switching (SDS):** A physical cross-point matrix ($N \times N$ crosspoints). Connects any input wire to any output wire simultaneously.
- **Time Division Switching (TDS):** Uses a Time Slot Interchanger (TSI) implemented with a **Control Memory (CM)** and a **Data Memory (DM)**. No physical matrix; reorders samples in time.
- **Hybrid T–S–T / S–T–S:** Combines time and space switching for large $N$ (e.g., $N > 512$).

### 2.2.2 Control Unit
- **Centralized Control:** One powerful processor handles all calls. Bottleneck under heavy load.
- **Distributed Control:** Multiple processors share load — e.g., one per line module.
- **Decentralized Control:** Each port has its own micro-controller; the most fault-tolerant.

### 2.2.3 Interface Modules
- **Subscriber Line Interface Circuit (SLIC):** Performs **BORSCHT** functions:
  - **B**attery feed (–48 V DC)
  - **O**vervoltage protection
  - **R**inging (typically **90 Vrms @ 20/25 Hz**)
  - **S**upervision (off-hook/on-hook detection)
  - **C**oding (Analog ↔ PCM)
  - **H**ybrid (2-wire to 4-wire conversion)
  - **T**est access

> [!NOTE]
> **BORSCHT** is a high-frequency KTU exam mnemonic. Memorize it as one string.

### 2.2.4 Signaling
- **Channel-Associated Signaling (CAS):** Signaling bits share the voice channel (e.g., E1 bit 16 robbed-bit signaling).
- **Common-Channel Signaling (CCS):** A separate dedicated channel for all signaling — e.g., **Signaling System No. 7 (SS7)**.

### 2.2.5 Peripheral & Support
- **Power Plant:** –48 V DC battery backed by SMPS chargers; powers the entire exchange.
- **Man–Machine Interface (MMI):** Console for operator/OAM (Operation, Administration & Maintenance).
- **Billing & CDR subsystem:** Generates Call Detail Records.
- **Test & Diagnostic subsystem:** Loopback, line tests.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute value bars in the table below use `\vert` instead of `|` to keep the markdown table valid.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voice bit rate (PCM) | $R_{voice} = f_s \times n$ | $f_s = 8 \text{ kHz},\ n = 8 \text{ bits} \Rightarrow R_{voice} = 64 \text{ kbps}$ |
| 2 | E1 frame duration | $T_f = 125 \ \mu s$ | 32 time slots × 8 bits = 256 bits per frame |
| 3 | TSI Memory size | $M_{TSI} = N$ words | $N$ = number of time slots (e.g., 32, 128, 256, 512) |
| 4 | TSI Data word width | $n$ bits | Typically 8 bits for PCM voice |
| 5 | Space switch size | $N \times N$ crosspoints | $N$ = number of input lines, $N$ = number of output lines |
| 6 | Clos network condition (strictly non-blocking) | $n \ \geq \ 2k - 1$ | For a 3-stage Clos with $r$ middle switches; KTU expects $n \geq 2k-1$ for SS-separable switching |
| 7 | Erlang B (blocking) | $B = \dfrac{A^{N}/N!}{\sum_{i=0}^{N} A^{i}/i!}$ | $A$ = offered traffic in Erlangs, $N$ = servers |
| 8 | Total cross-points (3-stage Clos) | $C_{total} = 2 \cdot N \cdot k + r \cdot k^{2}$ | With $r = 2k-1$ for non-blocking |
| 9 | Switching capacity | $C = N \times R_{voice}$ | $C$ in bps, $N$ = simultaneous calls |
| 10 | Mean Setup Delay (SPC) | $t_{setup} = \dfrac{N_{calls}}{BHCA/3600}$ | $BHCA$ = Busy Hour Call Attempts |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **In Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN):** Class-4 tandem exchanges and Class-5 local exchanges use this exact structure (e.g., Alcatel-Lucent 1000 S, Huawei MA5800 OLT — but architecturally the same).
- **In Mobile Networks:** The Mobile Switching Center (MSC) is *exactly* a switch with additional mobility-management blocks (VLR, HLR) added to the control layer.
- **In Telemedicine:** Switches form the backbone that carries DICOM images, tele-ECG, and VoIP between remote hospitals. A telemedicine-grade switch adds **QoS queues** and **priority paths** for life-critical traffic (trauma ECG) over routine traffic (e-mail).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Voice Bit Rate & E1 Multiplexing

**Given:** Voice is band-limited to **4 kHz**. By Nyquist's sampling theorem, minimum sampling rate is $f_s = 2 \times 4 \text{ kHz} = 8 \text{ kHz}$. Each sample is encoded with $n = 8$ bits (PCM).

**Step 1 — Samples per second**

$$
f_s = 2 \times f_{max} = 2 \times 4000 = 8000 \text{ samples/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Bit rate per voice channel**

$$
R_{voice} = f_s \times n = 8000 \times 8 = 64000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of voice channels multiplexed in E1 (CEPT-1) PCM frame**

E1 multiplexes **30 voice + 1 signaling (TS-16) + 1 framing (TS-0)** = **32 time slots** per frame.

**Step 4 — E1 bit rate**

$$
R_{E1} = 32 \text{ TS} \times 8 \text{ bits/TS} \times 8000 \text{ frames/s}
$$

$$
R_{E1} = 32 \times 8 \times 8000 = 2,048,000 \text{ bps} = 2.048 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Conversion logic:** $32 \times 8 = 256$ bits/frame, and $8000$ frames/s $\Rightarrow 256 \times 8000 = 2.048$ Mbps. ✔

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — TSI Memory Sizing

**Given:** A Time Slot Interchanger for an **$N = 32$** channel E1 stream.

**Step 1 — Data Memory (DM):** Stores the 8-bit PCM sample of every incoming time slot.

$$
\text{DM size} = N \text{ words} = 32 \text{ words} \quad (\text{each 8 bits wide})
$$

**Step 2 — Control Memory (CM):** Stores the *output order* — i.e., from which input time slot each output time slot should read.

$$
\text{CM size} = N \text{ words} = 32 \text{ words} \quad (\text{address width} = \log_2 N \text{ bits})
$$

**Step 3 — Address width**

$$
W_{addr} = \log_2 N = \log_2 32 = 5 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Total bits in CM**

$$
B_{CM} = N \times W_{addr} = 32 \times 5 = 160 \text{ bits}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Each output time slot must remember *which input slot to grab from*. 32 outputs need 32 entries; each entry needs 5 bits to point to one of 32 inputs. ✔

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Three-Stage Clos Network Crosspoint Count

**Given:** A 3-stage Clos network with **$N = 64$** input lines, partitioned into **$k = 8$** groups of size **$n = 8$** each, with **$r$** middle-stage switches.

**Step 1 — Clos non-blocking condition**

$$
r \ \geq \ 2k - 1 = 2(8) - 1 = 15
$$

Take $r = 15$ for the strictly non-blocking minimum.

**Step 2 — Total crosspoints**

$$
C_{total} = 2 \cdot N \cdot n + r \cdot k^{2}
$$

$$
C_{total} = 2(64)(8) + (15)(8^{2}) = 1024 + 960 = 1984
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with single-stage crossbar**

$$
C_{crossbar} = N^{2} = 64^{2} = 4096
$$

**Step 4 — Savings**

$$
\text{Savings} = \dfrac{C_{crossbar} - C_{Clos}}{C_{crossbar}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{4096 - 1984}{4096} \times 100\% \approx 51.6\%
$$

**Conversion logic:** Clos networking roughly halves the number of crosspoints compared to a brute-force $N \times N$ crossbar for moderate $N$ — this is precisely why large digital exchanges use Clos fabric. ✔

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Switching Capacity & GoS Check

**Given:** A Class-5 local exchange serving $N = 10{,}000$ subscribers. Each subscriber generates $a = 0.1$ Erlangs of traffic during the busy hour. Target Grade of Service (GoS) $= 1\%$.

**Step 1 — Total offered traffic**

$$
A = N \times a = 10{,}000 \times 0.1 = 1000 \text{ Erlangs}
$$

**Step 2 — Find trunk count $C$ from Erlang B table for $A = 1000$, $B = 0.01$**

Using Erlang B inversion: $C \approx 1040$ trunks (approximately $A + \sqrt{2A \cdot \ln(1/B)}$).

Approximate via the **Rüsch / ECST formula** is unnecessary — the standard Erlang B table is used. For $A=1000$ Erlangs, $B=1\%$, trunks required:

$$
C \approx 1037 \text{ trunks (rounded to a multiple of 30, the E1 size)}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of E1 trunk groups**

$$
\text{E1 groups} = \left\lceil \dfrac{1037}{30} \right\rceil = \lceil 34.57 \rceil = 35 \text{ E1s}
$$

**Step 4 — Voice channels required in fabric**

$$
N_{fabric} = 35 \times 30 = 1050 \text{ time slots}
$$

**Step 5 — Fabric throughput**

$$
T_{fabric} = N_{fabric} \times 64 \text{ kbps} = 1050 \times 64{,}000 = 67.2 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Conversion logic:** $1037$ trunks must be carried simultaneously. Group them in E1s of 30 voice channels each → 35 E1s. Each E1 has 30 voice TSs → 1050 simultaneous PCM channels. Multiply by 64 kbps to get fabric throughput. ✔

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — TSI vs Space Switch vs Clos

| Parameter | Space Switch (Crossbar) | Time Switch (TSI) | 3-Stage Clos |
|---|---|---|---|
| Physical crosspoints | $N^{2}$ | 0 (no crosspoints) | $2Nn + rk^{2}$ |
| Time slot delay | 0 | 1 frame (125 µs) | 1–3 frames |
| Blocking | Non-blocking | Always non-blocking | Strictly non-blocking if $r \geq 2k-1$ |
| Scalability to large $N$ | Poor (crosspoints grow as $N^{2}$) | Excellent for $N \leq 512$ | Excellent (≈ 51% savings) |
| Used in | Small PABX (≤ 64 lines) | E1/T1 multiplexers | Class-4 tandem exchanges |
| Best for | Voice, data, video | Pure voice TDM | Large digital exchanges |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of a Digital Switch

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ACCESS["A - Interface and Access Layer"]
        SLIC["Subscriber Line Interface Card SLIC"]
        SLI2["Line Card 2"]
        SLI3["Trunk Interface Card E1 T1"]
        SRV["Service Circuits DTMF Tones Announcements"]
    end

    subgraph FABRIC["B - Switching Fabric Layer"]
        TS["Time Switch TSI"]
        SP["Space Switch Crossbar"]
        TST["Combined T-S-T Stage"]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL["C - Control Layer"]
        CPU["Central Processor CPU"]
        MEM["Memory RAM CM DM"]
        SIG["Signaling Handler SS7 ISUP"]
        OAM["Man Machine Interface OAM"]
        BIL["Billing and CDR Subsystem"]
    end

    subgraph POWER["D - Support Subsystems"]
        PWR["Power Supply minus 48 V DC"]
        BAT["Battery Bank SMPS"]
    end

    SLIC --> TS
    SLI2 --> TS
    SLI3 --> TST
    SRV --> TST
    TS --> SP
    SP --> TST
    TST --> SLIC
    TST --> SLI2
    TST --> SLI3
    CPU --> TS
    CPU --> SP
    CPU --> MEM
    CPU --> SIG
    CPU --> OAM
    CPU --> BIL
    PWR --> ACCESS
    PWR --> FABRIC
    PWR --> CONTROL
    BAT --> PWR
```

**Figure 1.** Functional block diagram of a modern SPC digital exchange. Note that the **CPU** drives the fabric; it does not lie in the voice path. Also note the **bidirectional arrows** between the fabric and the line cards (the TSI is read-write in both directions for full-duplex voice).

## 4.2 Three-Stage Clos Network — Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN["Input Stage k groups of n lines"]
        I1["Group 1 n lines"]
        I2["Group 2 n lines"]
        I3["Group k n lines"]
    end

    subgraph MID["Middle Stage r switches"]
        M1["M1"]
        M2["M2"]
        M3["M3"]
        M4["Mr"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["Output Stage k groups of n lines"]
        O1["Group 1 n lines"]
        O2["Group 2 n lines"]
        O3["Group k n lines"]
    end

    I1 --> M1
    I1 --> M2
    I1 --> M3
    I1 --> M4
    I2 --> M1
    I2 --> M2
    I2 --> M3
    I2 --> M4
    I3 --> M1
    I3 --> M2
    I3 --> M3
    I3 --> M4
    M1 --> O1
    M1 --> O2
    M1 --> O3
    M2 --> O1
    M2 --> O2
    M2 --> O3
    M3 --> O1
    M3 --> O2
    M3 --> O3
    M4 --> O1
    M4 --> O2
    M4 --> O3
```

**Figure 2.** 3-stage Clos network with $k$ input groups, $k$ output groups, and $r$ middle-stage switches. For strictly non-blocking operation, $r \geq 2k-1$.

## 4.3 Signal Path Through BORSCHT Line Interface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["2-wire subscriber loop"] --> B["Battery feed minus 48 V DC"]
    B --> C["Overvoltage protection"]
    C --> D["Ringing injection 90 Vrms 20 Hz"]
    D --> E["Supervision off hook detect"]
    E --> F["Hybrid 2 wire to 4 wire"]
    F --> G["Codec analog to PCM"]
    G --> H["Test access relay"]
    H --> I["4 wire PCM bus into TSI"]
    I --> J["Switching fabric T-S-T"]
    J --> K["4 wire PCM bus out"]
    K --> L["Codec PCM to analog"]
    L --> M["Hybrid 4 wire to 2 wire"]
    M --> N["Subscriber B"]
```

**Figure 3.** Reverse path B → A applies for the called subscriber. Together they form the BORSCHT chain.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**List the major functional blocks of a digital telephone switch and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A digital SPC telephone switch consists of three main functional layers:
1. **Interface Layer** — Contains **Subscriber Line Interface Cards (SLIC)** that perform **BORSCHT** functions (Battery feed, Overvoltage protection, Ringing, Supervision, Coding, Hybrid, Test). Trunk Interface Cards connect to other exchanges.
2. **Switching Fabric Layer** — Contains the **Time Slot Interchanger (TSI)**, **Space Switch (Crossbar)**, or combined **T–S–T** matrices. It provides the physical path that interconnects any two time slots.
3. **Control Layer** — Contains the **Central Processor**, **Memory (CM/DM)**, and **Signaling Handler (SS7)**. It executes the call-processing software, drives the fabric, and maintains billing records.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's tip:** Always draw the three-layer block diagram first; then label each block. A diagram carries at least 1 of the 3 marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Explain the BORSCHT functions of a subscriber line interface circuit.**

**Model Answer:**

The SLIC performs seven essential functions, remembered by the mnemonic **BORSCHT**:
- **B** — Battery feed: supplies –48 V DC talk battery to the subscriber loop.
- **O** — Overvoltage protection: lightning/surge arrestors (typically 240 V clamp).
- **R** — Ringing: injects 90 Vrms, 20/25 Hz AC ringing voltage.
- **S** — Supervision: detects on-hook / off-hook, dial pulses, and flash.
- **C** — Coding: A-law/µ-law PCM encoding/decoding at 64 kbps.
- **H** — Hybrid: 2-wire ↔ 4-wire conversion for full-duplex operation.
- **T** — Test access: provides relay-controlled loopback to test equipment.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the structure of a stored-program-controlled (SPC) digital telephone switch. Identify each functional block and state its role.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Space Division Switching (SDS)**, **Time Division Switching (TDS)**, and **three-stage Clos network** with respect to crosspoint count, blocking probability, scalability, and typical use case. Use the configuration $N = 64$ lines, $k = 8$, $n = 8$, $r = 15$ to compute the exact crosspoint savings of Clos over a single-stage crossbar.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram (7 marks):**

| Block | Role |
|---|---|
| **SLIC / Line Card** | Interface analog subscriber to 64 kbps PCM; BORSCHT |
| **Trunk Interface** | Interface to E1/T1 PCM highways from other exchanges |
| **Service Circuits** | DTMF detectors, recorded announcements, conference bridges |
| **Time Switch (TSI)** | Reorders time slots; memory $N$ words |
| **Space Switch (Crossbar)** | Connects any input PCM highway to any output PCM highway |
| **Central Processor (CPU)** | Runs call-processing software; executes CHILL/SDL logic |
| **Memory (CM + DM)** | Holds call records, billing, routing tables |
| **Signaling Handler (SS7)** | Handles inter-exchange ISUP messages |
| **MMI / OAM** | Man-machine interface for operator & maintenance |
| **Power Supply** | –48 V DC plant with battery backup |

(Candidates should draw Figure 1 from Section 4 and label all blocks.)

**(b) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Space (Crossbar) | Time (TSI) | 3-Stage Clos |
|---|---|---|---|
| Crosspoints | $N^{2} = 4096$ | 0 | $2Nn + rk^{2} = 1984$ |
| Blocking | Non-blocking | Non-blocking | Strictly non-blocking if $r \geq 2k-1$ |
| Scalability | Poor ($N^{2}$ growth) | Good up to $N = 512$ | Excellent (linear in $N$ for fixed $k$) |
| Use Case | Small PABX ≤ 64 lines | TDM multiplexers | Class-4/5 exchanges, mobile MSCs |

**Crosspoint savings calculation:**

$$
\text{Savings} = \frac{4096 - 1984}{4096} \times 100\% = 51.6\%
$$

**Conclusion:** For $N=64$, the Clos network achieves a **51.6% reduction in crosspoints** while remaining strictly non-blocking, making it the architecture of choice for large digital exchanges.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a Time Slot Interchanger (TSI)? With a block diagram, explain how an 8-channel TSI reorders the input time slots $\{1, 3, 5, 7, 2, 4, 6, 8\}$ into the output time slots $\{7, 5, 3, 1, 8, 6, 4, 2\}$. Calculate the required Data Memory and Control Memory size.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define the **Clos non-blocking condition**. For a 3-stage Clos network with $N = 192$ input lines, $k = 8$ first-stage groups of $n = 24$ lines each, find the minimum number of middle-stage switches $r$ for strictly non-blocking operation. Compute the total crosspoint count.

#### Model Solution

**(a) TSI — Concept (3 marks):**

A **Time Slot Interchanger (TSI)** is a memory-based switching element that changes the *time position* of PCM samples from one time slot to another, *without* a physical crosspoint matrix. It uses two memories:
- **Data Memory (DM)** — sequential write, random read.
- **Control Memory (CM)** — stores the read address for each output time slot.

**Reordering example (2 marks):**

| Input TS | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sample | A | B | C | D | E | F | G | H |
| Output TS | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
| CM value | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |

After reordering, output TS1 should contain input sample G → CM[1] = 7; output TS2 → CM[2] = 5; output TS3 → CM[3] = 3; output TS4 → CM[4] = 1; output TS5 → CM[5] = 8; output TS6 → CM[6] = 6; output TS7 → CM[7] = 4; output TS8 → CM[8] = 2.

**Memory sizing (2 marks):**

$$
\text{DM size} = N = 8 \text{ words} \times 8 \text{ bits} = 64 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
W_{addr} = \log_2 8 = 3 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
\text{CM size} = 8 \text{ words} \times 3 \text{ bits/word} = 24 \text{ bits}
$$

**(b) Clos Non-Blocking Condition (3 marks):**

The Clos network with input stage of $k$ groups, $n$ lines per group, and $r$ middle-stage switches is **strictly non-blocking** if and only if:

$$
r \ \geq \ 2k - 1
$$

**Numerical calculation (4 marks):**

Given $N = 192$, $k = 8$, $n = 24$. Verify: $k \times n = 8 \times 24 = 192$ ✔.

$$
r_{min} = 2k - 1 = 2(8) - 1 = 15 \text{ middle-stage switches}
$$

Total crosspoints:

$$
C_{total} = 2 N n + r k^{2} = 2(192)(24) + (15)(8^{2})
$$

$$
C_{total} = 9216 + 960 = 10{,}176 \text{ crosspoints}
$$

Compare with a single-stage crossbar: $C_{crossbar} = N^{2} = 192^{2} = 36{,}864$.

**Savings:** $\frac{36864 - 10176}{36864} \times 100\% \approx 72.4\%$ — showing the dramatic advantage of Clos fabric for large $N$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Triggers:**
> 1. **Confusing "Switch" with "Router":** In Telemedicine (PEEBT861), a *switch* is the PSTN/ISDN circuit-switching device, **not** an IP router. Writing "Layer-2/Layer-3 switch" loses marks if the question is on the *telephone* switch architecture.
> 2. **Omitting BORSCHT:** If asked to explain the SLIC, failing to list all **seven** BORSCHT functions typically costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting PCM bit rate derivation:** Examiners expect the explicit steps $f_s = 8$ kHz, $n = 8$ bits, hence $R = 64$ kbps — not just stating the result.
> 4. **Wrong Clos formula:** Students often write $r \geq k$ instead of $r \geq 2k-1$. The "$2k-1$" is *the* non-blocking condition.
> 5. **Mixing Time and Space switches:** A TSI has *no* crosspoints; a space switch has *no* time delay. Conflating the two is a frequent error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A modern digital switch has **three layers**: Interface, Fabric, Control.
- ✅ The **BORSCHT** mnemonic gives the **seven** functions of the SLIC.
- ✅ A **voice channel** is always **64 kbps** because of $8$ kHz × $8$ bits PCM.
- ✅ An **E1 frame** carries 30 voice + 1 signaling + 1 framing = **32 time slots** at **2.048 Mbps**.
- ✅ A **TSI** uses Data Memory and Control Memory of $N$ words each; CM address width $= \log_2 N$ bits.
- ✅ A **single-stage $N \times N$ crossbar** has $N^2$ crosspoints and is non-blocking but does not scale.
- ✅ A **3-stage Clos** is strictly non-blocking when $r \geq 2k-1$; it drastically reduces crosspoints.
- ✅ **Centralized control** uses one CPU; **distributed** uses many — modern MSC/MSC server uses distributed.
- ✅ **CAS** (Channel-Associated) uses robbed bits; **CCS** uses a separate channel (e.g., **SS7**).
- ✅ Switches are classified as **Class-5 (local exchange)** and **Class-4 (tandem/transit exchange)**.
- ✅ Telemedicine adds **QoS priorities** to the fabric so emergency ECG/Voice bypass routine traffic.
- ✅ For KTU numerical problems, always show: (i) the formula, (ii) substitution of values, (iii) the final answer with **unit**. Partial marks are awarded for the first two even if arithmetic slips.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cryptography - Symmetric-Key and Asymmetric-Key
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT861 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cryptography - Symmetric-Key and Asymmetric-Key

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cryptography — Formal Definition

**Cryptography** is the science and art of transforming intelligible information (plaintext) into an unintelligible form (ciphertext) and back again, using mathematical algorithms and keys, so that only authorised parties can read the message. In a telemedicine context, cryptography protects **Electronic Medical Records (EMR)**, **telecommunication sessions between doctor and patient**, and **data stored on cloud servers** from eavesdropping, tampering, and impersonation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Cryptography is the practice and study of techniques for secure communication in the presence of adversarial behaviour. It deals with **Confidentiality**, **Integrity**, **Authentication**, and **Non-repudiation** — collectively called the **CIA+N** security triad.

The general transformation is expressed as:

$$
C = E_k(P) \quad \text{and} \quad P = D_k(C)
$$

where $P$ is the plaintext, $C$ is the ciphertext, $E_k$ is the encryption function under key $k$, and $D_k$ is the decryption function.

## 1.2 The Two Fundamental Families

Cryptographic systems are broadly classified based on **how the key is shared** between sender and receiver:

| Aspect | Symmetric-Key Cryptography | Asymmetric-Key Cryptography |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Keys | Single shared secret key | A key-pair (public + private) |
| Key Distribution | Difficult — must be shared secretly | Easy — public key is published freely |
| Speed | **Very fast** (orders of magnitude faster) | **Slow** (heavy modular arithmetic) |
| Key Length for equal security | **128 / 256 bits** | **2048 / 4096 bits** |
| Primary Use | Bulk data encryption (sessions, EMR files) | Key exchange, digital signatures |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Lockbox and the Mailbox

**Imagine you want to send a sealed diary to a friend living in another city:**

- **Symmetric-Key Analogy (Single Key Lockbox):** You buy a lockbox that opens with a single physical key. You must personally hand over a copy of that key to your friend through a trusted courier before sending the box. Both of you use the **same key** to lock and unlock. If the courier is intercepted, the secret is lost. This is **fast** (just one key turn) but **logistically painful**.

- **Asymmetric-Key Analogy (Padlock with Two Keys):** Your friend sends you an **open padlock** (his public key) by ordinary post — anyone can see it. You put the diary in a box, close it with his padlock, and post it back. Only your friend has the **private key** that opens the padlock. Nobody — not even you — can reopen it once locked. This is **logistically easy** (no secret courier) but **slower** (heavier mechanism).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Telemedicine Uses BOTH:**
> Modern telemedicine platforms use **asymmetric cryptography to securely exchange a symmetric session key**, and then use the **fast symmetric key (e.g., AES-256)** to encrypt the actual video/audio stream and EMR. This combination is called a **Hybrid Cryptosystem**.

## 1.4 Core Terminology (KTU 2024 Module Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Plaintext (P):** The original readable message (e.g., an ECG report).
> - **Ciphertext (C):** The scrambled, unreadable output of encryption.
> - **Key (K):** A secret parameter (a binary string) that controls the encryption algorithm.
> - **Encryption (E):** Converting P → C using key K.
> - **Decryption (D):** Converting C → P using key K.
> - **Cryptanalysis:** The science of breaking ciphers without knowing the key.
> - **Kerckhoffs's Principle:** *"The security of a cryptosystem must lie only in the secrecy of the key — not in the secrecy of the algorithm."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Symmetric-Key Cryptography — Operational Analysis

In symmetric-key (also called *secret-key* or *private-key*) cryptography, the **same secret key $K$** is used for both encryption and decryption. The transformation is bijective and invertible only with $K$.

### Operational Steps (Bulleted Logic)
- **Step 1 — Key Agreement:** The sender (e.g., a remote patient device) and the receiver (e.g., the hospital server) must agree on a shared secret key $K$ via a secure channel **before** communication begins.
- **Step 2 — Encryption:** The sender computes $C = E_K(P)$ using an algorithm such as **DES, 3DES, AES, Blowfish, or RC4**.
- **Step 3 — Transmission:** The ciphertext $C$ travels over the insecure network (the internet, wireless link, etc.).
- **Step 4 — Decryption:** The receiver applies the inverse transformation $P = D_K(C)$ using the **same** $K$.
- **Step 5 — Authentication problem:** Symmetric crypto alone cannot prove *who* sent the message, because both parties hold the same key.

### Two Sub-Classes of Symmetric Ciphers
- **Block Ciphers:** Operate on fixed-size blocks of plaintext (e.g., AES uses **128-bit** blocks). Modes: **ECB, CBC, CFB, OFB, GCM**.
- **Stream Ciphers:** Operate on one bit or one byte at a time (e.g., RC4, ChaCha20), generating a *keystream* XORed with plaintext.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)** is the global gold standard for symmetric encryption. It supports **128, 192, or 256-bit** keys and is mandated in healthcare standards such as **HIPAA** and **HL7 FHIR security profiles** — directly relevant to telemedicine data protection.

### The $n^2$ Key Distribution Problem
For a network of $n$ users using symmetric-key cryptography, the number of unique secret keys required is:

$$
N_{\text{keys}} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

> **Example:** For $n = 100$ patients and doctors, $\frac{100 \times 99}{2} = 4950$ unique secret keys must be securely distributed. This scalability bottleneck is the primary motivation for asymmetric cryptography.

---

## 2.2 Asymmetric-Key Cryptography — Operational Analysis

In asymmetric-key (also called *public-key*) cryptography, **each user has a key-pair $(K_{\text{public}}, K_{\text{private}})$**. The public key is published in a digital certificate (e.g., X.509), and the private key is kept secret by the owner.

### Operational Steps — Confidentiality Scenario
- **Step 1 — Key Generation:** The receiver (Bob) generates a key-pair using a one-way trapdoor function.
- **Step 2 — Public Key Publication:** Bob's $K_{\text{public}}$ is published in a public directory or certificate.
- **Step 3 — Encryption by Sender:** Alice fetches Bob's public key and computes $C = E_{K_{\text{public, Bob}}}(P)$.
- **Step 4 — Decryption by Receiver:** Bob uses his private key $P = D_{K_{\text{private, Bob}}}(C)$. Only Bob can do this.

### Operational Steps — Digital Signature Scenario
- **Step 1:** Alice computes a **hash** $h = H(P)$ of her message using SHA-256.
- **Step 2:** She signs the hash: $S = E_{K_{\text{private, Alice}}}(h)$.
- **Step 3:** She sends $(P, S)$ to Bob.
- **Step 4:** Bob computes $h' = H(P)$ on the received plaintext.
- **Step 5:** Bob recovers $h = D_{K_{\text{public, Alice}}}(S)$ and checks $h' \stackrel{?}{=} h$. **Match ⇒ authenticity and integrity verified.**

> [!NOTE]
> **The one-way trapdoor function** is the mathematical heart of public-key crypto. Easy to compute in one direction, infeasible to invert *unless* you possess the trapdoor (the private key).

### Common Asymmetric Algorithms
- **RSA** (Rivest–Shamir–Adleman) — based on the hardness of factoring large semiprimes.
- **Diffie–Hellman (DH)** — for key agreement, based on the discrete logarithm problem.
- **Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)** — based on the elliptic curve discrete logarithm problem; offers equivalent security to RSA with much smaller key sizes.
- **DSA (Digital Signature Algorithm)** — signatures only, based on discrete logarithms.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Critical for Numerical Problems)

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Encryption / Decryption | $C = E_K(P),\ P = D_K(C)$ | $K$ in **bits** | Both symmetric and asymmetric |
| Number of symmetric keys for $n$ users | $N = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Dimensionless | Quadratic growth problem |
| RSA Key Generation | $n = p \times q$ | $p, q$ large primes, $n$ in **bits** | $p$ and $q$ are kept secret |
| Euler's Totient of $n$ | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Dimensionless | Used to find $e$ and $d$ |
| Public Exponent condition | $\gcd(e,\ \phi(n)) = 1$ | $1 < e < \phi(n)$ | $e$ is usually **65537** |
| Private Exponent | $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Modulo inverse | $d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ |
| RSA Encryption | $C \equiv P^{e} \pmod{n}$ | $0 \leq P < n$ | Computed by sender |
| RSA Decryption | $P \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$ | $0 \leq C < n$ | Computed by receiver |
| RSA Signature | $S \equiv P^{d} \pmod{n}$ | Signed by Alice's private key | Verified with Alice's public key |
| Diffie–Hellman Shared Secret | $K_{AB} = g^{ab} \pmod{p}$ | $a, b$ random private ints | $K_{AB} = (g^{a})^{b} \equiv (g^{b})^{a} \pmod{p}$ |
| AES Block Size | $B = 128$ bits | Fixed | 16 bytes |
| AES Key Sizes | $K \in \{128,\ 192,\ 256\}$ bits | — | Longer key ⇒ more rounds |
| Effective Key Strength (RSA vs AES) | RSA-2048 $\approx$ AES-112 | NIST SP 800-57 | Guides hybrid design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Never confuse RSA's $e$ and $d$:** The **public key is $(n, e)$** and the **private key is $(n, d)$** (or equivalently, the entire tuple $(p, q, d)$). The trapdoor is the knowledge of the factorisation $n = p \cdot q$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Telemedicine Engineering

- **AES-256** encrypts live video streams between a rural clinic and a tertiary hospital.
- **RSA-2048 / ECC-256** protects the TLS handshake that establishes the secure video session.
- **SHA-256 + RSA** digitally signs prescriptions sent from a doctor to a pharmacy — preventing forgery.
- **Diffie–Hellman / ECDHE** provides **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)** in modern telemedicine mobile apps — even if the long-term private key is later compromised, past session keys remain safe.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 RSA Key Generation, Encryption & Decryption — Exhaustive Derivation

The RSA cryptosystem is the most commonly asked derivation in KTU examinations. We walk through **every** algebraic step.

### Step 1 — Choose Two Large Primes
Select two distinct large primes $p$ and $q$. For demonstration, let us choose small primes (real-world keys use 1024-bit primes).

$$
p = 61, \quad q = 53
$$

> Both are prime by inspection (or trial division up to $\sqrt{p}$ and $\sqrt{q}$).

### Step 2 — Compute the Modulus $n$
The modulus $n$ is the product of the two primes and forms part of **both** the public and private keys.

$$
n = p \times q
$$

Substituting values:

$$
n = 61 \times 53 = 3233
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Euler's Totient Function $\phi(n)$
For a product of two distinct primes, Euler's totient function is:

$$
\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1)
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\phi(n) = (61 - 1)(53 - 1) = 60 \times 52 = 3120
$$

> This counts the number of integers between $1$ and $n$ that are coprime to $n$.

### Step 4 — Choose the Public Exponent $e$
Choose $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. A common secure choice is $e = 65537$, but for small examples we pick $e = 17$.

$$
\gcd(17, 3120) = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

(17 is prime, and 3120 is not divisible by 17.)

### Step 5 — Compute the Private Exponent $d$
We need $d$ such that:

$$
d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}
$$

That is, $d \cdot 17 \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$. We solve this using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**.

Apply the algorithm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3120 &= 17 \times 183 + 9 \\
17 &= 9 \times 1 + 8 \\
9 &= 8 \times 1 + 1 \\
8 &= 1 \times 8 + 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Now back-substitute to express $1$ as a combination of $17$ and $3120$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 &= 9 - 8 \times 1 \\
1 &= 9 - (17 - 9 \times 1) \times 1 \\
1 &= 9 \times 2 - 17 \times 1 \\
1 &= (3120 - 17 \times 183) \times 2 - 17 \times 1 \\
1 &= 3120 \times 2 - 17 \times 366 - 17 \times 1 \\
1 &= 3120 \times 2 - 17 \times 367
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
-367 \times 17 \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}
$$

So:

$$
d = -367 \pmod{3120} = 3120 - 367 = 2753
$$

> **Verification:** $d \times e = 2753 \times 17 = 46801$. Now $46801 \div 3120 = 15$ remainder $1$, so $46801 \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$ ✓

### Step 6 — Publish the Public Key, Keep the Private Key Secret

$$
K_{\text{public}} = (n, e) = (3233,\ 17), \quad K_{\text{private}} = (n, d) = (3233,\ 2753)
$$

### Step 7 — Encryption of a Plaintext Integer
Let the sender (Alice) want to send a single-character plaintext, say the letter "H" (ASCII 72). She converts to integer $P = 72$ and computes:

$$
C \equiv P^{e} \pmod{n}
$$

$$
C \equiv 72^{17} \pmod{3233}
$$

Performing modular exponentiation (square-and-multiply):

$$
\begin{aligned}
72^1 \pmod{3233} &= 72 \\
72^2 \pmod{3233} &= 5184 \pmod{3233} = 1951 \\
72^4 \pmod{3233} &= 1951^2 \pmod{3233} = 3806401 \pmod{3233} \\
&= 3806401 - 1177 \times 3233 = 3806401 - 3805241 = 1160 \\
72^8 \pmod{3233} &= 1160^2 \pmod{3233} = 1345600 \pmod{3233} \\
&= 1345600 - 416 \times 3233 = 1345600 - 1344928 = 672 \\
72^{16} \pmod{3233} &= 672^2 \pmod{3233} = 451584 \pmod{3233} \\
&= 451584 - 139 \times 3233 = 451584 - 449387 = 2197
\end{aligned}
$$

Now $17 = 16 + 1$, so:

$$
72^{17} \equiv 72^{16} \times 72^{1} \equiv 2197 \times 72 \pmod{3233} = 158184 \pmod{3233}
$$

$$
158184 - 48 \times 3233 = 158184 - 155184 = 3000
$$

Therefore:

$$
C = 3000
$$

### Step 8 — Decryption of the Ciphertext
The receiver (Bob) recovers:

$$
P \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n} \equiv 3000^{2753} \pmod{3233}
$$

By Euler's theorem and the construction of $d$:

$$
P \equiv 3000^{2753} \pmod{3233} \equiv 72
$$

> The original message $P = 72$ is recovered, corresponding to the ASCII character **"H"** — the cryptosystem works.

### Why RSA Works — The Underlying Theorem
RSA security relies on **Euler's Theorem**:

$$
P^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n} \quad \text{for } \gcd(P, n) = 1
$$

Since $d \cdot e = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$ for some integer $k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C^{d} &\equiv (P^{e})^{d} \pmod{n} \\
&\equiv P^{ed} \pmod{n} \\
&\equiv P^{1 + k \cdot \phi(n)} \pmod{n} \\
&\equiv P \cdot (P^{\phi(n)})^{k} \pmod{n} \\
&\equiv P \cdot 1^{k} \pmod{n} \\
&\equiv P \pmod{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

> QED — decryption recovers the plaintext.

---

## 3.2 Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange — Exhaustive Derivation

Two parties (Alice and Bob) want to agree on a shared secret over an insecure channel.

### Step 1 — Public Parameters
Agree publicly on a large prime $p$ and a generator $g$ (a primitive root modulo $p$).

$$
p = 23, \quad g = 5
$$

### Step 2 — Private Keys
Alice picks a random secret integer $a$, Bob picks $b$.

$$
a = 6 \quad (\text{Alice's private key}), \quad b = 15 \quad (\text{Bob's private key})
$$

### Step 3 — Public Values
Alice computes and sends $A = g^{a} \pmod{p}$. Bob computes and sends $B = g^{b} \pmod{p}$.

$$
A = 5^{6} \pmod{23} = 15625 \pmod{23} = 8
$$

$$
B = 5^{15} \pmod{23} = 30517578125 \pmod{23} = 19
$$

### Step 4 — Shared Secret Computation

**Alice computes:**

$$
K_{AB} = B^{a} \pmod{p} = 19^{6} \pmod{23}
$$

$$
19^2 = 361 \equiv 16 \pmod{23}, \quad 19^4 \equiv 16^2 = 256 \equiv 3 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
19^6 \equiv 19^4 \cdot 19^2 \equiv 3 \cdot 16 = 48 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}
$$

**Bob computes:**

$$
K_{BA} = A^{b} \pmod{p} = 8^{15} \pmod{23}
$$

$$
8^2 = 64 \equiv 18, \quad 8^4 \equiv 18^2 = 324 \equiv 2, \quad 8^8 \equiv 2^2 = 4
$$

$$
8^{15} = 8^8 \cdot 8^4 \cdot 8^2 \cdot 8^1 \equiv 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 18 \cdot 8 = 1152 \pmod{23} = 2
$$

> **Both compute $K = 2$** — they share a common secret without ever transmitting it.

### Mathematical Justification
$$
B^{a} = (g^{b})^{a} = g^{ba} = g^{ab} = (g^{a})^{b} = A^{b} \pmod{p}
$$

> The Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP) — recovering $a$ from $g^{a} \pmod{p}$ — is computationally infeasible for large $p$ (≥ 2048 bits).

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Property | Symmetric (AES) | Asymmetric (RSA / ECC) |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical Basis | Substitution + Permutation network | Integer factorisation / DLP / ECDLP |
| Key Size for ~128-bit security | **128 bits** | RSA-3072 / ECC-256 |
| Operations per second (typical) | **Billions** | **Thousands** |
| Energy cost (mobile/IoMT devices) | Low | High |
| Provides Digital Signature | **No** | **Yes** |
| Provides Confidentiality | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Provides Non-repudiation | **No** | **Yes** |
| Suitable for bulk EMR encryption | **Yes** | No (too slow) |
| Key Distribution Problem | Yes ($n^2$ keys) | Solved (public key freely available) |

> [!NOTE]
> In a **telemedicine IoT sensor** (e.g., wearable ECG), symmetric AES is preferred due to battery constraints. The asymmetric handshake is done once at session start.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between symmetric-key and asymmetric-key cryptography. List any two algorithms used in each category.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Symmetric-Key | Asymmetric-Key |
|---|---|---|
| Number of keys | One shared secret key | Key-pair (public + private) |
| Key distribution | Difficult | Easy (public key published) |
| Speed | Fast | Slow |
| Examples | **AES, DES, 3DES, Blowfish, RC4** | **RSA, ECC, Diffie–Hellman, DSA** |
| Provides non-repudiation | No | Yes |

*(Award 1 mark for the table headings and key idea, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for any two valid distinguishing points.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is the role of Euler's totient function in the RSA algorithm? How is it computed for a modulus $n = p \cdot q$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Euler's totient function $\phi(n)$ counts the integers from $1$ to $n-1$ that are coprime to $n$. In RSA, $\phi(n)$ is used to generate the public exponent $e$ and to compute the private exponent $d$ such that $d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$. For $n = p \cdot q$ (product of two distinct primes):

$$
\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)
$$

This is the *trapdoor* — knowing $\phi(n)$ lets the owner compute $d$, but a cryptanalyst who only knows $n$ must factor it, which is infeasible for large primes. *(1 mark: definition, 1 mark: formula, 1 mark: security rationale.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Losses:**
> 1. Forgetting to verify $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ before accepting $e$ — **loses 1 mark**.
> 2. Not showing the Extended Euclidean Algorithm steps for $d$ — **loses 2 marks**.
> 3. For RSA small-number examples, **forgetting to check $P < n$** before encryption — **loses 1 mark**.
> 4. Confusing which key signs vs. which key verifies in digital signatures — **loses 2 marks**.
> 5. Failing to state the underlying hard mathematical problem (factorisation / DLP) — **loses 1 mark**.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of **symmetric-key cryptography**. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Perform **RSA encryption and decryption** for the message $P = 10$ using $p = 11$ and $q = 13$. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Symmetric-Key Cryptography (7 marks):**

A symmetric-key cryptosystem uses the **same secret key $K$** for both encryption and decryption.

*Block Diagram Description (3 marks):*
```
Plaintext (P)  →  [Encryption Algorithm E with Key K]  →  Ciphertext (C)
Ciphertext (C) →  [Decryption Algorithm D with Key K]  →  Plaintext (P)
```
Sender and receiver **share $K$** via a secure out-of-band channel **before** communication. The algorithm may be a **block cipher (AES, DES)** operating on $n$-bit blocks, or a **stream cipher (RC4)** generating a keystream XORed bitwise with the plaintext.

*Advantages (2 marks):*
- **Very fast** — typically 1000× faster than asymmetric crypto.
- **Well-suited for bulk data encryption** — EMR files, video, audio.
- **Smaller key sizes** provide strong security (AES-128 is robust).
- **Lower computational cost** — ideal for IoMT and wearable devices.

*Limitations (2 marks):*
- **Key distribution problem** — $n(n-1)/2$ keys for $n$ users.
- **No digital signature / non-repudiation** — both parties can deny or forge.
- **Single point of failure** — if the shared key is leaked, all security is lost.

---

**(b) RSA Encryption and Decryption (7 marks):**

**Given:** $p = 11$, $q = 13$, $P = 10$.

**Step 1 — Compute $n$ (1 mark):**

$$
n = p \times q = 11 \times 13 = 143
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\phi(n)$ (1 mark):**

$$
\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 10 \times 12 = 120
$$

**Step 3 — Choose $e$ (1 mark):**
Pick $e$ such that $\gcd(e, 120) = 1$ and $1 < e < 120$. A standard choice is $e = 7$ (prime, not a divisor of 120).

**Step 4 — Compute $d$ using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm (2 marks):**

We need $d$ such that $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{120}$.

Apply the algorithm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
120 &= 7 \times 17 + 1 \\
7 &= 1 \times 7 + 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Back-substitute:

$$
1 = 120 - 7 \times 17 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 = 120 - 17 \times 7
$$

So $d \equiv -17 \equiv 103 \pmod{120}$.

$$
K_{\text{public}} = (143,\ 7), \quad K_{\text{private}} = (143,\ 103)
$$

**Step 5 — Encryption (1 mark):**

$$
C \equiv P^{e} \pmod{n} = 10^{7} \pmod{143}
$$

Compute via square-and-multiply:

$$
10^2 = 100,\quad 10^4 = 10000 \equiv 10000 - 69 \times 143 = 10000 - 9867 = 133
$$

$$
10^7 = 10^4 \cdot 10^2 \cdot 10^1 \equiv 133 \times 100 \times 10 = 133000 \pmod{143}
$$

$$
133000 \div 143 \approx 930.07, \quad 930 \times 143 = 132990, \quad 133000 - 132990 = 10
$$

$$
C = 10 \pmod{143}
$$

> (This is a coincidence due to the small numbers — in general $C \neq P$.)

**Step 6 — Decryption (1 mark):**

$$
P \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n} = 10^{103} \pmod{143}
$$

Using the property that $10^{120} \equiv 1 \pmod{143}$ and $103 = 120 - 17$:

$$
10^{103} \equiv 10^{-17} \equiv (10^{17})^{-1} \pmod{143}
$$

We already computed $10^{17} \equiv 10 \pmod{143}$. The modular inverse of $10$ modulo $143$: since $\gcd(10, 143) = 1$ and $10 \times 14.3 \approx 143$, we test $10 \times 43 = 430 \equiv 430 - 3 \times 143 = 430 - 429 = 1 \pmod{143}$. So $10^{-1} \equiv 43 \pmod{143}$.

Therefore:

$$
P \equiv 10 \times 43 = 430 \equiv 430 - 3 \times 143 = 430 - 429 = 1 \pmod{143}
$$

Wait — this is wrong. Let us recompute correctly. **Verification by Euler's theorem:**

$$
10^{7 \times 103} = 10^{721} = 10^{1 + 6 \times 120} \equiv 10 \cdot (10^{120})^{6} \equiv 10 \cdot 1 = 10 \pmod{143}
$$

So decryption recovers $P = 10$ ✓

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Diffie–Hellman key exchange algorithm** with a neat diagram. Discuss its vulnerability to the man-in-the-middle attack and how it is mitigated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **symmetric-key and asymmetric-key cryptography** across any **seven** relevant parameters. State the role of each in a typical telemedicine video-consultation system. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange (7 marks):**

*Algorithm Steps (4 marks):*

1. Alice and Bob publicly agree on a large prime $p$ and a generator $g$ (primitive root modulo $p$).
2. Alice picks a secret random integer $a$, computes $A = g^{a} \pmod{p}$, and sends $A$ to Bob.
3. Bob picks a secret random integer $b$, computes $B = g^{b} \pmod{p}$, and sends $B$ to Alice.
4. Alice computes the shared secret $K = B^{a} \pmod{p}$.
5. Bob computes the shared secret $K = A^{b} \pmod{p}$.
6. Both arrive at the same $K$ because $B^{a} = (g^{b})^{a} = g^{ab} = (g^{a})^{b} = A^{b} \pmod{p}$.

*Diagram (1 mark):*

```
Alice                              Bob
------                             -----
Knows: a                           Knows: b
Computes A = g^a mod p  --A-->  Computes K = A^b mod p
Receives B = g^b mod p  <--B--    Computes B = g^b mod p
Computes K = B^a mod p
Shared Secret = K = g^(ab) mod p
```

*Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Vulnerability & Mitigation (2 marks):*
A passive attacker Eve sees $A$ and $B$ but cannot compute $g^{ab}$ without solving the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**, which is hard. However, an **active MITM attacker (Mallory)** can replace $A$ with $A'$ and $B$ with $B'$, establishing separate secrets with Alice and Bob. **Mitigation:** Authenticate the channel using **digital signatures** (Alice signs $A$ with her private key; Bob verifies with Alice's public key from a trusted **Certificate Authority (CA)**). Modern protocols use **ECDHE + digital signatures (e.g., in TLS 1.3)** to provide **Perfect Forward Secrecy**.

---

**(b) Seven-Parameter Comparison (7 marks):**

| # | Parameter | Symmetric-Key | Asymmetric-Key |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Keys used | One shared secret | Key-pair (public + private) |
| 2 | Speed | Very fast | Slow (100–1000× slower) |
| 3 | Key size for similar security | 128 / 256 bits | 2048+ bits |
| 4 | Key distribution problem | Severe | Solved (PKI) |
| 5 | Digital signature support | No | Yes |
| 6 | Algorithms | AES, DES, 3DES, RC4, Blowfish | RSA, ECC, Diffie–Hellman, DSA |
| 7 | Resource / energy cost | Low (suitable for IoMT) | High |

*Role in a Telemedicine Video-Consultation System (1 mark, included in marks):*
- **Asymmetric (RSA / ECC):** Used during the **TLS handshake** at session start — the doctor's and patient's browsers exchange certificates, perform ECDHE key agreement, and authenticate each other.
- **Symmetric (AES-256-GCM):** Used for the **bulk encryption of the audio/video stream** and EMR file transfers throughout the session — providing both **confidentiality** and **integrity** (via GCM mode's authentication tag).
- **Hash + Signature (SHA-256 + RSA / ECDSA):** Used to **digitally sign prescriptions and reports** sent after the consultation, providing **non-repudiation** — the doctor cannot later deny issuing the prescription.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Cryptography**

- **Cryptography** = science of secure communication; provides **Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-repudiation**.
- **Symmetric-Key** = same $K$ for $E$ and $D$; **fast**; key distribution is the bottleneck; examples: **AES, DES, 3DES, RC4, Blowfish**.
- **Asymmetric-Key** = public/private key-pair; **slow**; solves key distribution; examples: **RSA, ECC, DH, DSA**.
- **AES** block size = **128 bits**; key sizes = **128, 192, 256** bits.
- **Number of symmetric keys** for $n$ users = $n(n-1)/2$.
- **RSA security** rests on the hardness of **integer factorisation** of $n = p \cdot q$.
- **DH security** rests on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**.
- **ECC security** rests on the **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)**.
- **RSA public key = $(n, e)$**, **private key = $(n, d)$**; $d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.
- **$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$** for RSA.
- **Digital Signature** = sign with **private key**, verify with **public key** (the *opposite* of encryption).
- **Encryption for confidentiality** = encrypt with **receiver's public key**, decrypt with **receiver's private key**.
- **Hybrid cryptosystem** = asymmetric for **key exchange**, symmetric for **bulk data** — used in **TLS/HTTPS, telemedicine apps**.
- **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)** = achieved via **ephemeral DH (DHE / ECDHE)** — even if long-term key leaks, past sessions remain safe.
- **Kerckhoffs's Principle** = security lies in the **key**, not in keeping the **algorithm** secret.
- **MITM attack on DH** is mitigated by **digital signatures + PKI certificates**.
- **In telemedicine**: AES protects data-at-rest and live streams; RSA/ECC protects the handshake; SHA-256 ensures integrity; digital signatures provide non-repudiation for prescriptions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
