<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Security Services - Message Confidentiality and Integrity

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Telemedicine** and health information exchange, **Security Services** refer to the standardized set of protective measures defined under the **ISO/IEC 7498-2 (OSI Security Architecture)** framework that ensure reliable and protected communication between distributed healthcare entities (hospitals, clinics, diagnostic centers, patients, and remote monitoring devices).

Among the primary security services identified by KTU 2024 syllabus, **Message Confidentiality** and **Message Integrity** form the foundational pillars of any secure Electronic Health Record (EHR) / Electronic Medical Record (EMR) transmission system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Message Confidentiality** is the security service that protects the message (patient data, prescription, diagnostic report) from **unauthorized disclosure** during transmission over open networks (e.g., the internet, wireless channels).
>
> **Message Integrity** is the security service that guarantees that the message received by the authorized recipient is **exactly the same** as the message that was sent by the legitimate sender — i.e., it has not been altered, modified, inserted, deleted, or replayed by an attacker.

These two services are often deployed together because confidentiality *without* integrity is meaningless (an encrypted but tampered message is still dangerous) and integrity *without* confidentiality leaks sensitive data while guaranteeing its accuracy.

| Security Service | Core Question Answered | Primary Cryptographic Tool |
|---|---|---|
| Confidentiality | "Who can read this message?" | Encryption (Symmetric & Asymmetric) |
| Integrity | "Has the message been tampered with?" | Hash Functions, MAC, Digital Signatures |
| Authentication | "Who is the sender?" | Digital Certificates, Signatures |
| Non-Repudiation | "Can the sender deny sending it?" | Digital Signatures |
| Access Control | "Who is permitted access?" | ACLs, RBAC, ABAC |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Conceptualization)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Sealed Tamper-Proof Courier Envelope**
>
> Imagine you are a doctor in Trivandrum sending a critical patient prescription to a specialist in Kochi via a public courier service (representing the open internet).
>
> 1. **Confidentiality** = You place the prescription inside a **locked steel briefcase** whose key is held only by the specialist. Even if the courier opens the package, he sees only the locked briefcase — not the medical details inside. This is *encryption*.
>
> 2. **Integrity** = You also paste a **special wax seal with your unique thumbprint** on the envelope. If anyone tampers with the briefcase during transit, the seal breaks. The specialist, upon receiving it, checks the seal — if intact, the prescription is authentic and unaltered. This is the *integrity check using a hash/MAC*.
>
> 3. **Bonus — Digital Signature** = If you personally press your thumbprint into the wax (using your *private key*), the specialist can verify it came from *you and only you* (using your *public key*). This adds **authentication** and **non-repudiation**.

This is precisely what happens in a Telemedicine system: the **patient's PHI (Protected Health Information)** is the "prescription," the **internet** is the "public courier," and the cryptographic algorithms are the "steel briefcase" and the "wax seal."

## 1.3 Why These Services Are Critical in Telemedicine

Telemedicine exchanges **PHI** over heterogeneous, often public networks. The HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act) and the Indian **DISHA (Digital Information Security in Healthcare Act)** framework mandate that any e-health system must guarantee:

- Patient diagnostic reports (MRI, ECG, X-ray, blood test results) must be **readable only by authorized clinicians** (Confidentiality).
- The medication dosage or diagnosis must **not be altered** by a man-in-the-middle attacker (Integrity).
- A prescription issued by a registered medical practitioner must be **legally binding** and traceable (Non-repudiation via Digital Signatures).

Without these services, telemedicine collapses into a legally and clinically unsafe channel.

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Message Confidentiality

Confidentiality in telemedicine is achieved via **encryption** — the mathematical transformation of plaintext into ciphertext using a key. KTU 2024 syllabus requires mastery of both major paradigms.

### 2.1.1 Symmetric Key Encryption (Secret Key Cryptography)

- The **same secret key K** is used for both encryption and decryption.
- Computationally **fast**, suitable for bulk data encryption (e.g., encrypting a 50 MB MRI scan).
- Key distribution is the **major challenge** — both sender and receiver must securely share K beforehand.

$$ E_K(M) = C \quad \text{(Encryption)}$$

$$ D_K(C) = M \quad \text{(Decryption)}$$

Where $M$ = plaintext message, $C$ = ciphertext, $K$ = shared secret key, $E$ = encryption function, $D$ = decryption function.

**Key KTU Algorithms (must memorize):**
- **DES (Data Encryption Standard)** — 56-bit key — *deprecated*
- **3DES (Triple DES)** — 168-bit key — *legacy*
- **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)** — 128/192/256-bit key — *current standard*
- **RC4, Blowfish, Twofish** — stream and block ciphers

### 2.1.2 Asymmetric Key Encryption (Public Key Cryptography)

- Uses a mathematically linked **key pair**: Public Key $K_{pub}$ and Private Key $K_{priv}$.
- Anyone can encrypt with the receiver's $K_{pub}$; only the holder of $K_{priv}$ can decrypt.
- Solves the **key distribution problem** but is **100–1000x slower** than symmetric encryption.

$$ C = E_{K_{pub}^{Receiver}}(M) $$

$$ M = D_{K_{priv}^{Receiver}}(C) $$

**Key KTU Algorithms:** RSA, ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography), ElGamal.

### 2.1.3 Hybrid Encryption (Real-World Telemedicine Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In practice (e.g., **TLS 1.3** used in HTTPS-based telemedicine portals), **both paradigms are combined**:
> 1. A symmetric session key $K_s$ is randomly generated.
> 2. $K_s$ is encrypted with the receiver's public key $K_{pub}$ (asymmetric step).
> 3. The bulk medical data is encrypted with $K_s$ (symmetric step).
>
> This gives **speed + secure key distribution** simultaneously.

---

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown of Message Integrity

Integrity ensures the message has **not been modified in transit**. The two primary mechanisms are **Hash Functions** and **Message Authentication Codes (MAC)**.

### 2.2.1 Cryptographic Hash Functions

A hash function $H$ takes an input $M$ of arbitrary length and produces a **fixed-length digest** $h$:

$$ h = H(M) $$

**Three Critical Properties (KERN Requirement):**

1. **Pre-image resistance** — Given $h$, it is computationally infeasible to find $M$ such that $H(M)=h$.
2. **Second pre-image resistance** — Given $M_1$, it is infeasible to find $M_2 \neq M_1$ with $H(M_1)=H(M_2)$.
3. **Collision resistance** — It is infeasible to find any two distinct inputs $M_1, M_2$ with $H(M_1)=H(M_2)$.

**Standard KTU Hash Algorithms:** MD5 (broken, 128-bit), SHA-1 (broken, 160-bit), **SHA-256, SHA-3** (current, 256-bit and above).

> [!WARNING]
> MD5 and SHA-1 are **cryptographically broken** and must **never** be used in modern telemedicine systems. A collision attack on MD5 was demonstrated in 2004 (Wang et al.).

### 2.2.2 Message Authentication Code (MAC)

A MAC combines a hash function with a **shared secret key** to produce an **authenticated tag**:

$$ T = MAC_K(M) $$

The receiver recomputes the MAC using the shared key and compares. If they match, the message has both **integrity** and **source authentication**.

- **HMAC (Hash-based MAC)** — uses SHA-256 + key, widely used in TLS/IPsec.
- **CMAC** — block-cipher-based MAC.

### 2.2.3 Digital Signatures (The Strongest Form of Integrity + Non-Repudiation)

A digital signature is the **asymmetric equivalent of a MAC**:
1. Sender computes hash: $h = H(M)$.
2. Sender encrypts $h$ with **own private key**: $S = E_{K_{priv}^{Sender}}(h)$.
3. Sender transmits $(M, S)$.
4. Receiver decrypts signature using sender's **public key**: $h' = D_{K_{pub}^{Sender}}(S)$.
5. Receiver recomputes $h'' = H(M)$ and checks $h' = h''$.

If equal, the message is **authentic, intact, and legally non-repudiable**.

**Standard:** RSA-PSS, ECDSA, EdDSA.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $M$ | Plaintext message (patient data) | Variable length (bits) |
| $C$ | Ciphertext | Same bit-length block as $M$ |
| $K$ | Cryptographic key | **128 / 192 / 256 bits** for AES |
| $E_K(\cdot)$ | Encryption function under key $K$ | Maps plaintext to ciphertext |
| $D_K(\cdot)$ | Decryption function under key $K$ | Inverse of $E_K$ |
| $H(\cdot)$ | Cryptographic hash function | Output: **256 bits** (SHA-256) |
| $h$ | Message digest / hash output | Fixed length, e.g., **256 bits** |
| $K_{pub}, K_{priv}$ | Public and Private key pair | RSA: $\geq \mathbf{2048}$ bits, ECC: $\geq \mathbf{256}$ bits |
| $MAC_K(M)$ | Message Authentication Code | Tag length: **128 / 256 bits** |
| $\vert M \vert$ | Bit-length of message $M$ | bits (use $\vert$ not $\vert$ in tables) |
| $n$ | RSA modulus | $n = p \cdot q$, where $p,q$ are large primes |

### 2.4 Confidentiality vs. Integrity — The Critical Distinction

| Parameter | Confidentiality | Integrity |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Hide content | Detect modification |
| **Attacker Action** | Eavesdropping / sniffing | Tampering / MITM injection |
| **Tool** | Encryption (AES, RSA) | Hash, MAC, Digital Signature |
| **Question Answered** | "Can they read it?" | "Has it been changed?" |
| **Key Used** | Receiver's key (so only they read) | Sender's key (so they prove authorship) |
| **Output** | Ciphertext $C$ | Digest $h$ or Tag $T$ or Signature $S$ |
| **Failure if Missing** | Patient data exposed | Wrong drug dosage / wrong diagnosis |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Telemedicine Engineering

- **Teleconsultation video calls (WebRTC + DTLS-SRTP):** AES-128 encryption for confidentiality, HMAC-SHA256 for integrity of RTP packets.
- **DICOM medical image transfer:** TLS layer provides both, using hybrid encryption.
- **HL7 FHIR API over HTTPS:** TLS 1.3 with X.509 digital signatures for prescription non-repudiation.
- **Remote patient IoT monitoring (wearables):** Lightweight ECC + HMAC for low-power devices (constraint environment).
- **Aadhaar-linked health ID (ABHA):** RSA-2048 + SHA-256 for authentication and audit integrity.

# Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Mathematical Foundations

This section presents the rigorous mathematical foundations that examiners commonly test in KTU 2024 Scheme. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly — no shortcuts.

## 3.1 Mathematical Foundation: Symmetric Encryption Block Operation (AES Round Structure)

The AES algorithm operates on a 128-bit block organized as a 4×4 byte matrix called the **State**. It executes $N_r$ rounds where:

$$ N_r = \begin{cases} 10 & \text{if key size} = 128 \text{ bits} \\ 12 & \text{if key size} = 192 \text{ bits} \\ 14 & \text{if key size} = 256 \text{ bits} \end{cases} $$

Each round (except the final) performs four transformations on the State:

1. **SubBytes** — Non-linear byte substitution via S-box.
2. **ShiftRows** — Cyclic shift of rows.
3. **MixColumns** — Linear mixing via finite-field matrix multiplication over $\mathrm{GF}(2^8)$.
4. **AddRoundKey** — XOR with round key $K_i$.

The full encryption can be expressed as:

$$ C = E_K(M) = (\sigma_{K_{N_r}} \circ \mu \circ \pi \circ \rho)^{N_r - 1} \circ \sigma_{K_1} \circ \mu \circ \pi \circ \rho \circ \sigma_{K_0}(M) $$

Where $\sigma$ = AddRoundKey, $\mu$ = MixColumns, $\pi$ = SubBytes, $\rho$ = ShiftRows.

For the KTU board exam, students are typically not required to perform a full AES round by hand, but must understand:
- **Key Expansion:** The original key $K$ is expanded into $N_r + 1$ round keys using the Rijndael key schedule.
- **Decryption** is the inverse round structure.

## 3.2 Mathematical Foundation: RSA Algorithm (Asymmetric Encryption & Signatures)

RSA is the most-tested asymmetric algorithm in KTU papers. We derive it step-by-step.

### Step 1: Key Generation

Choose two large distinct primes $p$ and $q$.

$$ n = p \cdot q $$

This $n$ is the **modulus**. Its bit length defines the security level (e.g., 2048 bits).

### Step 2: Compute Euler's Totient

$$ \phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) $$

This counts integers less than $n$ that are coprime to $n$.

### Step 3: Choose Public Exponent

Pick a public exponent $e$ such that:

$$ 1 < e < \phi(n) \quad \text{and} \quad \gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1 $$

Standard choice: $e = 65537 = 2^{16} + 1$ (a Fermat prime — efficient for fast exponentiation).

### Step 4: Compute Private Exponent

Find $d$ such that:

$$ e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)} $$

This is computed using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**.

### Step 5: Encryption

For plaintext integer $M$ (where $0 \leq M < n$):

$$ C \equiv M^e \pmod{n} $$

### Step 6: Decryption

$$ M \equiv C^d \pmod{n} $$

### Step 7: Verification (Why RSA Works)

$$ C^d \equiv (M^e)^d \equiv M^{ed} \pmod{n} $$

By Euler's Theorem, since $\gcd(M, n) = 1$:

$$ M^{ed} \equiv M^{1 + k\phi(n)} \equiv M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^k \equiv M \cdot 1^k \equiv M \pmod{n} $$

Hence, decryption correctly recovers $M$. The security rests on the **integer factorization problem**: given $n$, finding $p$ and $q$ is computationally intractable for large primes.

### Step 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** In a telemedicine portal, RSA is used with $p = 61$, $q = 53$. Compute the public and private keys, then encrypt $M = 42$ and decrypt to verify.

**Step 1 — Modulus:**
$$ n = p \cdot q = 61 \times 53 = 3233 $$

**Step 2 — Totient:**
$$ \phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 60 \times 52 = 3120 $$

**Step 3 — Choose $e$:** We need $\gcd(e, 3120) = 1$. Try $e = 17$. Indeed, $\gcd(17, 3120) = 1$. So **$e = 17$**.

**Step 4 — Compute $d$:** We need $17d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$. Using Extended Euclidean:

$$ 3120 = 183 \times 17 + 9 $$
$$ 17 = 1 \times 9 + 8 $$
$$ 9 = 1 \times 8 + 1 $$
$$ 8 = 8 \times 1 + 0 $$

Back-substituting:
$$ 1 = 9 - 1 \times 8 $$
$$ 1 = 9 - 1 \times (17 - 1 \times 9) = 2 \times 9 - 1 \times 17 $$
$$ 1 = 2 \times (3120 - 183 \times 17) - 1 \times 17 = 2 \times 3120 - 367 \times 17 $$

So $-367 \times 17 \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$, meaning $d \equiv -367 \equiv 3120 - 367 = 2753 \pmod{3120}$. So **$d = 2753$**.

**Step 5 — Encrypt $M = 42$:**
$$ C = M^e \bmod n = 42^{17} \bmod 3233 $$

Using repeated squaring:
- $42^1 \equiv 42$
- $42^2 = 1764$
- $42^4 \equiv 1764^2 \bmod 3233$

$1764^2 = 3,111,696$. Dividing: $3,111,696 / 3233 = 962.47...$, integer part $962$, remainder $3,111,696 - 962 \times 3233 = 3,111,696 - 3,110,146 = 1550$. So $42^4 \equiv 1550$.

- $42^8 \equiv 1550^2 \bmod 3233 = 2,402,500 \bmod 3233$. $2,402,500 / 3233 \approx 743.0$, so $743 \times 3233 = 2,402,119$, remainder $381$. So $42^8 \equiv 381$.
- $42^{16} \equiv 381^2 \bmod 3233 = 145,161 \bmod 3233$. $145,161 / 3233 \approx 44.89$, so $44 \times 3233 = 142,252$, remainder $2909$. So $42^{16} \equiv 2909$.

Now $17 = 16 + 1$, so:
$$ 42^{17} = 42^{16} \times 42 \equiv 2909 \times 42 \bmod 3233 $$

$2909 \times 42 = 122,178$. $122,178 / 3233 = 37.78$, so $37 \times 3233 = 119,621$, remainder $122,178 - 119,621 = 2557$.

So **$C = 2557$**.

**Step 6 — Decrypt to verify:**
$$ M = C^d \bmod n = 2557^{2753} \bmod 3233 $$

This is computationally heavy by hand, but theoretically:
$$ 2557^{2753} \equiv (42^{17})^{2753} \equiv 42^{17 \times 2753} \equiv 42^{47,201} \pmod{3233} $$

Since $e \cdot d = 17 \times 2753 = 46,801$. Check: $46,801 = 1 + 15 \times 3120 = 1 + 46,800$. Yes, so $e \cdot d = 1 + 15 \cdot \phi(n)$. Then by Euler's theorem, $42^{46,801} \equiv 42 \pmod{3233}$. So **decryption recovers $M = 42$**. ✓

This worked example perfectly illustrates how a telemedicine prescription $M$ becomes ciphertext $C = 2557$ — meaningless to any eavesdropper, but recoverable only by the holder of the private key $d = 2753$.

## 3.3 Mathematical Foundation: Hash Function Iteration (Merkle–Damgård Construction)

SHA-256 uses the **Merkle–Damgård** paradigm. The message $M$ is first padded to a multiple of 512 bits, then processed in 512-bit blocks $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_t$.

The hash computation proceeds iteratively:

$$ H_0 = IV \quad \text{(Initial Value, fixed constant)} $$

$$ H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, t $$

$$ h = H_t \quad \text{(Final hash output)} $$

Where $f$ is the **compression function**. For SHA-256, $f$ uses 64 rounds of bitwise operations (AND, OR, XOR, ROTR), modular additions, and 8 working variables.

**Why this matters for telemedicine integrity:** A single bit flip in the input changes the hash output completely (the **avalanche effect**). The probability of a random collision in SHA-256 is:

$$ P(\text{collision after } N \text{ messages}) \approx 1 - e^{-N^2 / (2 \cdot 2^{256})} $$

For $N = 2^{128}$ messages (more than atoms in the universe), probability is still $\approx 0.39$, making collision-based forgery computationally impossible.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Confidentiality vs. Integrity Tools)

| Attack Scenario | Defense Tool | Mathematical Property Exploited | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Eavesdrop on patient ECG stream | AES-128 symmetric encryption | Confusion + Diffusion | TLS over MQTT in IoMT |
| Replay old prescription | Timestamp + nonce in MAC | Freshness binding | Telepharmacy systems |
| Swap patient blood test report | Digital Signature (RSA-PSS) | Asymmetric trapdoor function | HL7 FHIR signed resources |
| Modify drug dosage in transit | HMAC-SHA256 of payload | Keyed hash, collision resistance | DICOM image headers |
| Forge doctor's identity | X.509 PKI certificates | Chain of trust via CA signatures | e-Prescription workflows |
| Recover plaintext from ciphertext | Use AES, not DES | Computational hardness | All modern telemedicine |

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid block diagrams illustrate the end-to-end architecture of confidentiality and integrity services in a telemedicine system. All node IDs are alphanumeric and contain no reserved keywords.

## 4.1 High-Level Confidentiality + Integrity Architecture (Hybrid Crypto)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Sender Doctor"] --> B["Plaintext Patient Report M"]
    B --> C["Compute Hash h equals H of M using SHA256"]
    C --> D["Sign h with Sender Private Key KprivS to get Signature S"]
    D --> E["Generate Random Symmetric Session Key Ks"]
    E --> F["Encrypt Medical Data with Ks via AES to get Ciphertext C"]
    F --> G["Encrypt Ks with Receiver Public Key KpubR via RSA to get Wrapped Key W"]
    G --> H["Transmit Packet equals C plus W plus S over TLS Channel"]
    H --> I["Network Internet Wireless Channel"]
    I --> J["Receiver Specialist Hospital"]
    J --> K["Unwrap W using Receiver Private Key KprivR to recover Ks"]
    K --> L["Decrypt C with Ks using AES to recover M"]
    L --> M["Recompute Hash hprime equals H of M using SHA256"]
    M --> N["Verify S using Sender Public Key KpubS to recover h"]
    N --> O{"Check h equals hprime"}
    O -->|Match| P["Message Authentic and Unaltered ACCEPT"]
    O -->|Mismatch| Q["Integrity Violation REJECT and Log"]
```

        SYM["Symmetric Path Fast for Bulk Data"] --> AESE
    end
    AESE --> CIPH["Ciphertext C concatenated"]
    subgraph AsymPath["Asymmetric Key Wrapping Step"]
        KS["Random Session Key Ks 128 bit"] --> RSAE["RSA2048 Encryption with Receiver Public Key KpubR"]
        KGEN["Generate Random Ks using CSPRNG"] --> KS
    end
