# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are modeled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. Marks are distributed as per the official Part A (2 marks) and Part B (14 marks) structure, with the Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive level tagged for each question.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define message confidentiality and integrity in the context of telemedicine. List ONE cryptographic tool used to achieve each. (2 marks, Bloom Level: Remember)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Message Confidentiality:** A security service that protects patient health information (PHI) from unauthorized disclosure during transmission over open networks, ensuring only the intended recipient can read the contents.
- **Message Integrity:** A security service that ensures the medical message received is exactly as sent, with no modification, insertion, deletion, or replay by an attacker.
- **Confidentiality tool:** AES (Advanced Encryption Standard).
- **Integrity tool:** SHA-256 hash function / HMAC / Digital Signature.

*(Examiner's note: Both definitions + one tool each = full 2 marks. Avoid giving only one side.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Differentiate between a hash function and a Message Authentication Code (MAC). State the key property that a MAC adds over a plain hash. (2 marks, Bloom Level: Understand)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hash Function | MAC |
|---|---|---|
| Key | No key used | Uses a shared secret key $K$ |
| Output | Digest $h = H(M)$ | Tag $T = MAC_K(M)$ |
| Authentication | Provides **only integrity** | Provides **integrity + source authentication** |

**Key property added by MAC:** A MAC binds a **shared secret key** to the hash, so an attacker who can recompute the hash cannot forge a valid tag without knowing the key. Hence, MAC provides **data origin authentication** in addition to integrity.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of **symmetric encryption** for ensuring message confidentiality in a telemedicine system. Mention any **two algorithms** and state their key sizes. (7 marks, Bloom Level: Understand + Apply)

**(b)** Describe the **RSA algorithm** for public-key encryption. Choose small primes $p = 7, q = 11$, compute $n$ and $\phi(n)$, select $e = 13$, and find $d$. Then encrypt the message $M = 5$ and show the decryption recovers $M$. (7 marks, Bloom Level: Apply + Analyze)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Symmetric Encryption Architecture (7 marks breakdown):**

1. **Diagram (2 marks)** — Draw sender → plaintext → encryption algorithm $E_K$ with key $K$ → ciphertext → secure channel → receiver → decryption algorithm $D_K$ with same key $K$ → plaintext.

2. **Working (3 marks):**
   - Both sender (doctor) and receiver (specialist) **share the same secret key $K$** in advance via a secure out-of-band channel (e.g., physical courier, pre-shared USB).
   - The plaintext medical report $M$ is fed into the encryption function $E_K$ along with $K$, producing ciphertext $C = E_K(M)$.
   - $C$ is transmitted over the (insecure) public network.
   - Receiver applies the inverse decryption $D_K(C) = M$ using the same $K$.
   - Any eavesdropper seeing $C$ cannot recover $M$ without $K$.

3. **Two algorithms with key sizes (2 marks):**
   - **DES (Data Encryption Standard):** 56-bit effective key, 64-bit block size. *Deprecated due to small key size; vulnerable to brute force in hours.*
   - **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard):** 128/192/256-bit key, 128-bit block size. *Current global standard, used in TLS, IPsec, HIPAA-compliant telemedicine.*
   - *(Optional bonus: 3DES — 168-bit key; Blowfish — 32 to 448 bits; RC4 — 40 to 2048 bits stream cipher.)*

**Advantages:** Very fast (1000x RSA), suitable for bulk encryption of large medical images.
**Disadvantage:** Key distribution is hard — needs $n(n-1)/2$ keys for $n$ users.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**RSA Algorithm (7 marks breakdown):**

1. **Algorithm description (2 marks):**
   - Proposed by **Rivest, Shamir, Adleman (1978)**.
   - Based on the **mathematical hardness of factoring the product of two large primes**.
   - Each user has a **public key** $(n, e)$ published openly, and a **private key** $d$ kept secret.
   - Encryption: $C = M^e \bmod n$. Decryption: $M = C^d \bmod n$.

2. **Numerical Computation (5 marks):**

   **Step 1 — Modulus:**
   $$ n = p \times q = 7 \times 11 = 77 $$

   **Step 2 — Euler's Totient:**
   $$ \phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 6 \times 10 = 60 $$

   **Step 3 — Public exponent** (given): $e = 13$. Verify $\gcd(13, 60) = 1$. ✓

   **Step 4 — Private exponent $d$:** Find $d$ such that $13d \equiv 1 \pmod{60}$.
   Using Extended Euclidean Algorithm:
   $$ 60 = 4 \times 13 + 8 $$
   $$ 13 = 1 \times 8 + 5 $$
   $$ 8 = 1 \times 5 + 3 $$
   $$ 5 = 1 \times 3 + 2 $$
   $$ 3 = 1 \times 2 + 1 $$
   $$ 2 = 2 \times 1 + 0 $$

   Back-substituting:
   $$ 1 = 3 - 1 \times 2 $$
   $$ 1 = 3 - 1 \times (5 - 1 \times 3) = 2 \times 3 - 1 \times 5 $$
   $$ 1 = 2 \times (8 - 1 \times 5) - 1 \times 5 = 2 \times 8 - 3 \times 5 $$
   $$ 1 = 2 \times 8 - 3 \times (13 - 1 \times 8) = 5 \times 8 - 3 \times 13 $$
   $$ 1 = 5 \times (60 - 4 \times 13) - 3 \times 13 = 5 \times 60 - 23 \times 13 $$

   So $-23 \times 13 \equiv 1 \pmod{60}$, hence $d \equiv -23 \equiv 60 - 23 = 37 \pmod{60}$.

   So **$d = 37$**.

   **Public key:** $(n = 77, e = 13)$.
   **Private key:** $d = 37$.

   **Step 5 — Encrypt $M = 5$:**
   $$ C = M^e \bmod n = 5^{13} \bmod 77 $$

   Compute by repeated squaring modulo 77:
   - $5^1 \equiv 5$
   - $5^2 = 25$
   - $5^4 = 625 \equiv 625 - 8 \times 77 = 625 - 616 = 9 \pmod{77}$
   - $5^8 = 9^2 = 81 \equiv 81 - 77 = 4 \pmod{77}$

   Now $13 = 8 + 4 + 1$, so:
   $$ 5^{13} = 5^8 \times 5^4 \times 5^1 \equiv 4 \times 9 \times 5 \pmod{77} $$
   $$ 4 \times 9 = 36 $$
   $$ 36 \times 5 = 180 \equiv 180 - 2 \times 77 = 180 - 154 = 26 \pmod{77} $$

   So **ciphertext $C = 26$**.

   **Step 6 — Decrypt to verify:**
   $$ M = C^d \bmod n = 26^{37} \bmod 77 $$

   Using $26 \equiv -51 \pmod{77}$ is messy; instead use the algebraic identity:
   $$ 26^{37} \equiv (5^{13})^{37} \equiv 5^{13 \times 37} \equiv 5^{481} \pmod{77} $$

   Check: $13 \times 37 = 481 = 1 + 8 \times 60 = 1 + 8\phi(n)$. So by Euler's theorem:
   $$ 5^{481} \equiv 5^{1 + 8 \times 60} \equiv 5 \times (5^{60})^8 \equiv 5 \times 1^8 \equiv 5 \pmod{77} $$

   So **decryption recovers $M = 5$** ✓.

3. **Conclusion (in-built):** RSA is a public-key cryptosystem; the security rests on the intractability of factoring $n = 77$ into 7 and 11. For real telemedicine, $n$ must be at least **2048 bits** (using primes of $\sim 1024$ bits each).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** What is a **cryptographic hash function**? List its three essential security properties. Explain the **avalanche effect** with a small example. (7 marks, Bloom Level: Understand + Apply)

**(b)** Explain the working of a **Digital Signature** scheme for ensuring message integrity and non-repudiation in an e-prescription system. Compare it with a MAC. (7 marks, Bloom Level: Analyze + Evaluate)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Definition (2 marks):** A cryptographic hash function $H$ is a deterministic mathematical function that maps an input $M$ of arbitrary bit-length to a fixed-length output $h$ of $n$ bits, such that $h = H(M)$, where $n$ is typically 256 bits for SHA-256.

2. **Three essential security properties (3 marks):**
   - **Pre-image resistance:** Given a target hash $h$, it is computationally infeasible to find any $M$ such that $H(M) = h$. This prevents an attacker from forging a medical report that hashes to a known legitimate value.
   - **Second pre-image resistance:** Given a specific $M_1$, it is infeasible to find a different $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. This prevents substituting one prescription for another.
   - **Collision resistance:** It is infeasible to find *any* two distinct inputs $M_1, M_2$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. This is stronger than second pre-image resistance.

3. **Avalanche effect with example (2 marks):** A small change in input (e.g., flipping one bit) should cause a drastic, unpredictable change in the output — ideally, approximately 50% of output bits flip.

   **Example (illustrative, not real SHA-256):**
   - $H(\text{"Patient has diabetes"}) = 1011\,0010$
   - $H(\text{"Patient has Diabetes"}) = 0100\,1101$ (one capital letter flipped)
   - Notice: 7 out of 8 bits changed (87.5% avalanche) — input difference of 1 bit causes massive output change.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

1. **Digital Signature Working (4 marks):**

   **Setup:** Each doctor $D$ has a key pair: Private Key $K_{priv}^D$ (kept secret on doctor's HSM/smart card) and Public Key $K_{pub}^D$ (published in hospital's directory / X.509 certificate).

   **Signing an e-prescription $M$:**
   1. Doctor's device computes hash: $h = H(M)$ using SHA-256.
   2. Encrypts $h$ with $K_{priv}^D$: $S = E_{K_{priv}^D}(h) = h^{d} \bmod n$ (if RSA).
   3. Attaches $S$ to $M$. Transmits $(M, S)$ to the pharmacy over the telemedicine network.

   **Verification by the pharmacy:**
   1. Decrypts $S$ with doctor's $K_{pub}^D$: $h' = D_{K_{pub}^D}(S) = S^{e} \bmod n$.
   2. Recomputes $h'' = H(M)$ from received $M$.
   3. Checks $h' = h''$. If equal, the prescription is **authentic, intact, and the doctor cannot deny it** (non-repudiation).

2. **Comparison with MAC (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Digital Signature | MAC |
|---|---|---|
| Key type | **Asymmetric** (key pair) | **Symmetric** (single shared key) |
| Provides non-repudiation? | **Yes** — only sender has private key | **No** — both parties share the key, so either could have created the MAC |
| Verifier can prove to third party? | **Yes** — public key is publicly known | **No** — verifier also has the secret key, so cannot prove sender's authorship to a judge |
| Speed | Slower (asymmetric math) | Faster (symmetric hash) |
| Use case | E-prescriptions, legal EHRs, court-admissible | Internal API integrity, low-power IoT medical devices |
| Standard | RSA-PSS, ECDSA, EdDSA | HMAC-SHA256, CMAC-AES |

3. **Conclusion:** Digital signatures are the **gold standard** for integrity + authentication + non-repudiation in telemedicine, especially for legally binding artifacts like e-prescriptions, discharge summaries, and diagnostic reports. MACs are preferred for **performance-critical or low-power IoMT (Internet of Medical Things)** scenarios.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing confidentiality with integrity.** Many students write AES when asked about integrity. AES is for confidentiality; SHA-256/HMAC is for integrity. Mix-up = **−2 to −3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify GCD condition in RSA.** Not checking $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ before computing $d$ loses the **methodology mark** in numericals.
> 3. **Skipping the avalanche example in hash function questions.** A definition alone is incomplete; the example carries **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Writing "MAC provides non-repudiation."** This is **incorrect** and will cost marks. MAC provides integrity + authentication only; only Digital Signatures give non-repudiation.
> 5. **Not labeling diagrams.** KTU strictly requires labeled block diagrams with arrows. Unlabeled diagrams fetch partial marks only.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Confidentiality** = *preventing unauthorized reading* → Use **Encryption** (AES, RSA, 3DES).
- ⭐ **Integrity** = *preventing unauthorized modification* → Use **Hash, MAC, or Digital Signature**.
- ⭐ Confidentiality answers **"Who can read?"**; Integrity answers **"Has it been changed?"**
- ⭐ AES key sizes: **128 / 192 / 256 bits**; RSA minimum: **2048 bits**; SHA-256 output: **256 bits**.
- ⭐ RSA core formulas: $n = pq$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$, $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, $C = M^e \bmod n$.
- ⭐ Three hash properties: **Pre-image, Second pre-image, Collision resistance** — memorize all three in order.
- ⭐ **MD5 and SHA-1 are broken** — never use in modern telemedicine.
- ⭐ **MAC ≠ Digital Signature.** MAC uses symmetric key (no non-repudiation); Digital Signature uses asymmetric (full non-repudiation).
- ⭐ **Hybrid encryption** = RSA wraps AES session key → used in TLS 1.3, HTTPS, secure email, EHR portals.
- ⭐ **Digital Signature process:** Hash with SHA → Encrypt hash with sender's private key → Receiver decrypts with sender's public key → Compares recomputed hash.
- ⭐ **HMAC** = Hash + Key → provides integrity + authentication → widely used in TLS record layer.
- ⭐ **Avalanche effect** = 1-bit input change → ~50% output bits change.
- ⭐ Telemedicine requires both services **simultaneously** — neither alone is sufficient.
- ⭐ X.509 certificates + PKI provide the trust anchor for digital signatures in healthcare.
- ⭐ Memorize the RSA numerical pattern — it is the **most-asked 14-mark question** in KTU papers.
- ⭐ Remember: $e \cdot d - 1$ must be a **multiple of $\phi(n)$** — used in verification step of RSA numericals.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication Infrastructure for Telemedicine - LAN and WAN Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT861 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Communication Infrastructure for Telemedicine - LAN and WAN Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Communication Infrastructure in Telemedicine

**Communication Infrastructure** in telemedicine refers to the integrated set of hardware, software, transmission media, and protocol stacks that enable the secure, reliable, and high-fidelity exchange of biomedical data (vital signs, images, video, EHR records) between geographically separated clinical endpoints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Telemedicine communication infrastructure is the layered network architecture comprising LAN-based intra-hospital connectivity and WAN-based inter-hospital/internet connectivity that supports real-time and store-and-forward clinical applications.*

In simpler terms — it is the **"digital highway system"** of a hospital or healthcare enterprise. Just as roads connect cities, communication networks connect a bedside monitor to a doctor's smartphone, a radiology department to a remote specialist, and a rural PHC to a tertiary super-specialty hospital.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Almost every Module 2 question in Telemedicine (PEEBT861) involves the network layer. The examiner expects you to differentiate LAN vs. WAN, map applications to network types, and justify technology choices based on bandwidth, latency, and security.

## 1.2 Local Area Network (LAN) — Formal Definition

A **Local Area Network (LAN)** is a privately owned, geographically restricted (typically ≤ 10 km) data communication network that interconnects computers, biomedical devices, servers, and peripherals within a single building or campus using high-speed, low-error-rate transmission media and a shared medium access protocol.

**Key KTU-Mandated Parameters:**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Geographic span | **Room → Building → Campus (≤ 10 km)** |
| Data rate | **10 Mbps → 10 Gbps (modern Ethernet)** |
| Latency | **< 10 ms** |
| Ownership | **Private (single organization)** |
| Error rate | **Very low (BER ≈ 10⁻⁹)** |
| Example protocol | **IEEE 802.3 (Ethernet), IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi)** |

## 1.3 Wide Area Network (WAN) — Formal Definition

A **Wide Area Network (WAN)** is a data communication network that spans large geographical areas (cities, countries, continents) and connects multiple LANs through public, private, or hybrid transmission infrastructures operated by telecom carriers.

**Key KTU-Mandated Parameters:**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Geographic span | **City → Country → Global** |
| Data rate | **1.5 Mbps (T1) → 100 Gbps (modern backbone)** |
| Latency | **20 ms → 500+ ms (satellite)** |
| Ownership | **Public carrier / leased** |
| Error rate | **Moderate (BER ≈ 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁷)** |
| Example technology | **MPLS, leased lines, 4G/5G, VSAT, VPN over Internet** |

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hospital Analogy:**
> Imagine a large multi-specialty hospital as a **country**.
> - The **LAN** is the **internal road network inside the hospital** — corridors, lifts, and inter-departmental passages. Fast, short, and fully owned by the hospital. Used to shift a patient from ICU to Radiology quickly.
> - The **WAN** is the **national highway connecting this hospital to a medical college in another city** — slow, public, and subject to traffic (latency), but absolutely essential for telemedicine referrals, tele-radiology, and remote consultations.
>
> Just like ambulances use hospital roads (LAN) to reach a helipad and then highways (WAN) to reach a distant trauma center, **biomedical data travels through LANs first and then WANs** to reach the remote consultant.

## 1.5 Why LAN + WAN Together?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hybrid Principle:** Pure LAN is useless for telemedicine (too local). Pure WAN is uneconomical for in-hospital traffic. KTU emphasizes that **real telemedicine systems are hybrid LAN-WAN architectures**:
> - **LAN layer** → PACS server, HIS/RIS, bedside monitors, video-conferencing units inside the hospital.
> - **WAN layer** → Links the hospital to remote clinics, ambulances, specialists' homes, and cloud data centers.
>
> The two layers meet at a **gateway/router/firewall** — the "border checkpoint" of the network.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LAN Topologies — The Structural Backbone

A **network topology** defines the geometric arrangement of nodes and links. KTU 2024 Scheme lists five canonical topologies:

### 2.1.1 Star Topology
- All nodes connect to a **central hub/switch**.
- **Failure mode:** Central device failure = total network collapse.
- **Telemedicine use:** Hospital floor networks — bedside monitors → nursing station switch.

### 2.1.2 Bus Topology
- All nodes share a **single backbone cable** (coaxial / twisted pair).
- **Failure mode:** Cable break disrupts entire segment.
- **Legacy use:** Early Ethernet (10BASE2, 10BASE5); now obsolete in hospitals.

### 2.1.3 Ring Topology
- Nodes form a **closed loop**; data travels in one direction (or both in dual ring).
- **Token passing** controls access.
- **Telemedicine use:** FDDI rings in legacy PACS deployments.

### 2.1.4 Mesh Topology
- Every node connects to **every other node** (full mesh) or several (partial mesh).
- **High redundancy** — critical links remain up on failure.
- **Telemedicine use:** Multi-hospital trauma networks, military telemedicine.

### 2.1.5 Hybrid (Tree) Topology
- Combination of two or more topologies.
- **Telemedicine use:** Typical multi-floor hospital: star per floor + bus/ring between floors.

## 2.2 LAN Transmission Media

| Media Type | Max Data Rate | Use Case in Telemedicine |
|---|---|---|
| **Twisted Pair (Cat6/Cat7)** | 1 Gbps – 10 Gbps | HIS terminals, nursing stations |
| **Coaxial Cable** | 10 Mbps – 100 Mbps | Legacy CCTV in OTs |
| **Single-mode Fiber (SMF)** | 100 Gbps+ | PACS backbone, MRI/CT image transfer |
| **Multi-mode Fiber (MMF)** | 1 Gbps – 10 Gbps | In-building hospital backbone |
| **Wireless (Wi-Fi IEEE 802.11ax)** | Up to 9.6 Gbps | Mobile carts, tablets, IoT vitals |

## 2.3 LAN Access Control Protocols

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two dominant MAC protocols — frequently asked in KTU exams:**

1. **CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection)** — Used in classical Ethernet (IEEE 802.3). Nodes "listen" before transmitting; collisions detected cause random backoff.
2. **Token Passing** — Used in Token Ring (IEEE 802.5) and FDDI. A special "token" frame circulates; only the holder may transmit → deterministic, collision-free.

## 2.4 WAN Technologies — Comprehensive Taxonomy

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 emphasizes four WAN technology families:**

### 2.4.1 Circuit-Switched WAN
- Dedicated physical path established for the call.
- **Examples:** Traditional PSTN, ISDN (Integrated Services Digital Network).
- **Telemedicine use:** Legacy tele-cardiology (still used for low-bandwidth ECG transmission).

### 2.4.2 Packet-Switched WAN
- Data broken into packets, each routed independently.
- **Examples:** X.25, Frame Relay, ATM, **MPLS (Multiprotocol Label Switching)**.
- **Telemedicine use:** MPLS is the gold standard for multi-site hospital networks — guarantees QoS for tele-ICU video.

### 2.4.3 Cell-Switched / Wireless WAN
- **Examples:** 2G/3G/4G LTE, **5G NR (New Radio)**, satellite (VSAT).
- **Telemedicine use:** Mobile telemedicine units, ambulance-to-hospital links, rural PHC connectivity.

### 2.4.4 Internet-Based WAN (VPN Overlay)
- **Examples:** IPsec VPN, SSL/TLS VPN, SD-WAN over public Internet.
- **Telemedicine use:** Home-based tele-consultation, work-from-home radiologists.

## 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These equations appear frequently in KTU ESE problems (Module 2). Memorize them.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Propagation Delay** $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | $d$ = link length, $v$ = signal velocity (≈ 2 × 10⁸ m/s in fiber) | seconds |
| 2 | **Transmission Delay** $T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = packet size (bits), $R$ = link rate (bps) | seconds |
| 3 | **End-to-End Delay** $T_{total} = N(T_p + T_t) + T_{proc} + T_{queue}$ | $N$ = hops, $T_{proc}$ = node processing, $T_{queue}$ = queuing | seconds |
| 4 | **Throughput** $\eta = \dfrac{W_{useful}}{T_{total}}$ | Useful work / total time | bits/sec |
| 5 | **Bandwidth-Delay Product** $BDP = R \times T_p$ | Max bits "in flight" on a link | bits |
| 6 | **Utilization (Stop-and-Wait)** $U = \dfrac{1}{1+2a}$ where $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t}$ | Efficiency of Stop-and-Wait ARQ | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 7 | **Sliding Window Utilization** $U = \dfrac{W}{1+2a}$ for $W < 1+2a$, else $U = 1$ | $W$ = window size | dimensionless |
| 8 | **Shannon Capacity** $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $S/N$ = SNR | bits/sec |
| 9 | **Nyquist Bit Rate** $C = 2B \log_2 M$ | $M$ = signal levels | bits/sec |
| 10 | **Compression Ratio** $CR = \dfrac{\text{Original size}}{\text{Compressed size}}$ | Higher CR = better compression | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse **bandwidth (Hz)** of a channel with **data rate (bps)** of a link. In LAN/WAN design, "bandwidth" colloquially means data rate, but in Shannon's formula it means spectral bandwidth.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tele-radiology** requires LAN throughput ≥ 1 Gbps (DICOM images are 50–500 MB each).
- **Tele-pathology** requires WAN latency < 200 ms for real-time robotic microscopy.
- **Tele-ICU** video needs 2–4 Mbps per bed with < 100 ms latency → demands MPLS or 5G, not public Internet.
- **Store-and-forward** (dermatology, ophthalmology) is latency-tolerant → any WAN works.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix — LAN vs. WAN

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most important table for KTU Module 2 — practice reproducing it from memory.**

| Parameter | LAN | WAN |
|---|---|---|
| Geographic scope | Building / campus (≤ 10 km) | City / country / global |
| Ownership | Private organization | Public carrier / leased |
| Typical data rate | 100 Mbps – 10 Gbps | 1.5 Mbps – 100 Gbps |
| Typical latency | < 10 ms | 20 ms – 500 ms |
| Error rate (BER) | ≈ 10⁻⁹ | ≈ 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁷ |
| Transmission media | Twisted pair, fiber, wireless | Fiber, microwave, satellite, copper leased |
| Protocols | Ethernet (802.3), Wi-Fi (802.11) | MPLS, Frame Relay, PPP, HDLC, 5G NR |
| Security model | Internal trust, VLAN, 802.1X | VPN, IPsec, firewall, carrier SLAs |
| Cost per bit | Very low | Higher |
| Example in telemedicine | PACS server room, OT video | Rural PHC ↔ tertiary hospital link |
| Routing complexity | Simple (1–2 hops) | Complex (10+ autonomous systems) |
| QoS guarantee | Implicit (over-provisioned) | Explicit (MPLS, DiffServ) needed |

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Stop-and-Wait Protocol Utilization

> [!NOTE]
> **Why derive this?** KTU frequently asks: "Calculate the efficiency of a Stop-and-Wait protocol used in a WAN link carrying telemedicine data." Below is the full derivation, step-by-step.

**Problem Setup (typical KTU question):**
- Link length $d = 5000$ km (cross-country WAN)
- Propagation velocity $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s (fiber)
- Data rate $R = 1$ Mbps
- Packet size $L = 1000$ bits
- ACK is instantaneous (negligible size)

**Step 1 — Compute Propagation Delay**

$$
T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{5000 \times 10^3 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}
$$

$$
T_p = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.025 \text{ s} = 25 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Transmission Delay**

$$
T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1000 \text{ bits}}{1 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Parameter $a$**

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{25 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ ms}} = 25
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Stop-and-Wait Utilization Formula**

$$
U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 2(25)} = \frac{1}{51}
$$

$$
U \approx 0.0196 = 1.96\%
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation**

> [!WARNING]
> A utilization of **less than 2%** is catastrophic for telemedicine. This proves that **Stop-and-Wait ARQ is unusable on high-latency WANs.** Real telemedicine WANs use:
> - **Sliding Window ARQ** (Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat) with window $W \geq 2a + 1 = 51$.
> - **TCP with large receive window** — equivalent to sliding window.
> - **Forward Error Correction (FEC)** — eliminates retransmission entirely for real-time video.

## 3.3 Sliding Window Utilization — Worked Example

**Same parameters** as above, now using Sliding Window with $W = 10$.

**Step 1 — Check if $W$ is the limiting factor**

$$
W = 10 \quad \text{vs.} \quad 1 + 2a = 51
$$

Since $W < 51$, the **window is the bottleneck** (sender must stop and wait for ACKs).

**Step 2 — Apply Sliding Window Utilization Formula**

$$
U = \frac{W}{1 + 2a} = \frac{10}{51} \approx 0.196 = 19.6\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with $W = 60$**

$$
W = 60 > 51 \implies U = 1.0 = 100\%
$$

The link is now fully utilized. **Conclusion:** For a 5000 km, 1 Mbps link carrying 1000-bit telemedicine packets, a **TCP/sliding-window size of at least 51 is required** to fully exploit the link.

## 3.4 Bandwidth-Delay Product — Worked Example

**Step 1 — Compute BDP**

$$
BDP = R \times T_p = (1 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}) \times (0.025 \text{ s}) = 25{,}000 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation**

At any instant, **25,000 bits are "in the pipe"** — traveling across the WAN. The sender and receiver buffers must each hold at least this much data to avoid pipeline stalls. This justifies the **TCP receive buffer auto-tuning** feature in modern telemedicine systems.

## 3.5 Shannon Channel Capacity for Tele-cardiology Link

**Problem:** A tele-ECG WAN link uses 4 kHz voice-band channel with SNR = 30 dB.

**Step 1 — Convert SNR to linear scale**

$$
\frac{S}{N} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's Formula**

$$
C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = 4000 \times \log_2(1001)
$$

$$
\log_2(1001) \approx \log_2(1024) = 10 \text{ (slightly less, } \approx 9.97)
$$

$$
C \approx 4000 \times 9.97 \approx 39{,}880 \text{ bps} \approx 40 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Decision**

> [!NOTE]
> 40 kbps is **sufficient for a 12-lead ECG at 500 Hz sampling × 12 bits** (≈ 72 kbps raw, but with DPCM compression fits comfortably in 40 kbps). This justifies using **dial-up ISDN / 4G voice channels** for tele-cardiology in rural India.

## 3.6 Analytical Summary — KTU Problem-Solving Workflow

For any LAN/WAN performance problem on the KTU exam, follow this 4-step framework:

1. **Identify the protocol** → Stop-and-Wait, Sliding Window, or pure pipe.
2. **Compute $T_p$ and $T_t$** → units must be consistent (always convert to seconds).
3. **Compute $a = T_p / T_t$** → if $a \gg 1$, you are bandwidth-limited by latency (typical WAN).
4. **Apply the appropriate utilization formula** and **state the engineering implication**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Telemedicine Hybrid LAN–WAN Architecture (Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid safeguards applied:** all node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed; labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LAN1["LAN 1 - TERTIARY HOSPITAL CAMPUS"]
        A1[BED SIDE MONITORS] --> SW1[CORE LAYER 3 SWITCH]
        A2[OT VIDEO STREAM] --> SW1
        A3[PACS SERVER] --> SW1
        A4[HIS RIS SERVER] --> SW1
        A5[TELE ICU WORKSTATION] --> SW1
        A6[DOCTOR TABLET WIFI AP] --> SW1
        SW1 --> FW1[FIREWALL AND VPN GATEWAY]
    end

    subgraph WAN["WAN LAYER - PUBLIC CARRIER NETWORK"]
        FW1 --> MPLS1[MPLS CLOUD - CARRIER 1]
        MPLS1 --> SAT1[VSAT HUB]
        MPLS1 --> FIBER1[UNDERSEA FIBER]
        FW1 -.-> INET1[PUBLIC INTERNET FOR VPN OVERLAY]
    end

    subgraph LAN2["LAN 2 - RURAL PRIMARY HEALTH CENTRE"]
        FIBER1 --> RTR2[ROUTER AT PHC]
        SAT1 --> RTR2
        INET1 --> RTR2
        RTR2 --> SW2[SMALL SWITCH]
        SW2 --> B1[TELE CONSULTATION PC]
        SW2 --> B2[ECG DEVICE]
        SW2 --> B3[AMBULANCE DATA LINK]
    end

    subgraph LAN3["LAN 3 - SPECIALIST HOME"]
        INET1 --> RTR3[HOME ROUTER]
        RTR3 --> B4[SPECIALIST LAPTOP]
        RTR3 --> B5[RADIOLOGY REVIEW STATION]
    end

    style SW1 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style FW1 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style MPLS1 fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
    style RTR2 fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
    style RTR3 fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 LAN Topology Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STAR["STAR TOPOLOGY"]
        S_HUB((HUB)) --- S_N1[N1]
        S_HUB --- S_N2[N2]
        S_HUB --- S_N3[N3]
        S_HUB --- S_N4[N4]
    end

    subgraph RING["RING TOPOLOGY"]
        R_N1[N1] --- R_N2[N2]
        R_N2 --- R_N3[N3]
        R_N3 --- R_N4[N4]
        R_N4 --- R_N1
    end

    subgraph BUS["BUS TOPOLOGY"]
        B_BUS[BACKBONE CABLE] --- B_N1[N1]
        B_BUS --- B_N2[N2]
        B_BUS --- B_N3[N3]
        B_BUS --- B_N4[N4]
    end

    subgraph MESH["FULL MESH TOPOLOGY"]
        M_N1[N1] --- M_N2[N2]
        M_N1 --- M_N3[N3]
        M_N1 --- M_N4[N4]
        M_N2 --- M_N3
        M_N2 --- M_N4
        M_N3 --- M_N4
    end
```

## 4.3 OSI/TCP-IP Layer Mapping for Telemedicine Traffic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7[LAYER 7 APPLICATION - HL7 DICOM EHR] --> L6[LAYER 6 PRESENTATION - ENCRYPTION TLS]
    L6 --> L5[LAYER 5 SESSION - SIP H323]
    L5 --> L4[LAYER 4 TRANSPORT - TCP UDP]
    L4 --> L3[LAYER 3 NETWORK - IP MPLS]
    L3 --> L2[LAYER 2 DATA LINK - ETHERNET WIFI LTE MAC]
    L2 --> L1[LAYER 1 PHYSICAL - FIBER COPPER RADIO]

    L1 -.CARRIES.-> SIG[BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL OR DICOM IMAGE]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Which OSI layer does MPLS operate at?", the correct answer is **Layer 2.5** (between Data Link and Network). MPLS uses labels instead of IP lookups for forwarding → faster, QoS-friendly → ideal for telemedicine backbones.

## 4.4 WAN Technology Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[START - CHOOSE WAN FOR TELEMEDICINE] --> Q1{NEED REAL TIME VIDEO BELOW 100 MS LATENCY}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{BUDGET HIGH}
    Q2 -- YES --> M1[USE MPLS LEASED LINE]
    Q2 -- NO --> M2[USE 5G SLICE OR SD WAN WITH FEC]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q3{REMOTE RURAL AREA WITH NO FIBER}
    Q3 -- YES --> M3[USE VSAT OR 4G LTE]
    Q3 -- NO --> M4[USE VPN OVER PUBLIC INTERNET]
    M1 --> END[DEPLOYED TELEMEDICINE WAN]
    M2 --> END
    M3 --> END
    M4 --> END
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution reference (PEEBT861 ESE):** Part A = 3 marks × 5 Q = 15 marks; Part B = 14 marks × 2 Q (with internal choice) = 28 marks; Total Module contribution ≈ 43/70. Two-part questions split 7+7 marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between LAN and WAN in the context of telemedicine. Mention any two telemedicine applications for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — distribute as 1.5 marks per half):**

| Aspect | LAN | WAN |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Inside hospital | Between hospitals / cities |
| Speed | 1–10 Gbps | 1.5 Mbps – 1 Gbps |
| Latency | < 10 ms | 20 ms – 500 ms |

**Telemedicine applications on LAN (1.5 marks):**
1. PACS image transfer inside the radiology department.
2. Real-time vital-sign monitoring on a hospital floor.

**Telemedicine applications on WAN (1.5 marks):**
1. Tele-radiology reporting from a remote clinic to a tertiary center.
2. Tele-ECG transmission from a rural PHC ambulance to a cardiologist.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is the role of MPLS in telemedicine WANs? Why is it preferred over the public Internet for live tele-ICU?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Role of MPLS (2 marks):** MPLS (Multiprotocol Label Switching) is a packet-switched WAN technology that forwards packets based on short labels rather than IP lookups. It provides **deterministic paths, QoS guarantees, and low jitter** — essential for telemedicine traffic.
- **Why preferred over public Internet for live tele-ICU (1 mark):** The public Internet offers "best-effort" delivery with no latency or packet-loss guarantees. Tele-ICU video requires **< 100 ms latency and < 0.1% packet loss** — only MPLS Service Level Agreements (SLAs) or private 5G slices can guarantee this.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **hybrid LAN–WAN architecture of a typical telemedicine system** connecting a tertiary hospital to three rural PHCs. Label all key components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A WAN link for tele-cardiology connects a rural PHC to a cardiac hospital. The link has: length = 4000 km, signal velocity in fiber = $2 \times 10^8$ m/s, data rate = 2 Mbps, ECG packet size = 2000 bits. Compute:
1. Propagation delay
2. Transmission delay
3. The parameter $a$
4. Utilization of a Stop-and-Wait protocol
5. Minimum sliding-window size for 100% link utilization
6. State one engineering decision you would recommend for this link. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution**

**(a) Block Diagram (4 marks for diagram + 3 marks for explanation):**

```
[Refer to Section 4.1 — Telemedicine Hybrid LAN-WAN Architecture]

Key components to mention (any 6):

- LAN side: HIS server, PACS server, bedside monitors, OT video unit, Wi-Fi AP, L3 switch.
- Gateway: Firewall + VPN concentrator.
- WAN side: MPLS cloud / 5G core / VSAT hub.
- Remote PHCs: small switch, tele-consultation PC, ECG device, ambulance link.
- Specialist home LAN: laptop, review workstation, home router.
```

**Explanation (3 marks):**
The architecture is a **three-tier model**:
1. **Tier 1 (Hospital LAN):** Gigabit Ethernet + Wi-Fi 6 connecting all clinical systems.
2. **Tier 2 (WAN edge):** Firewall, VPN gateway, and router at the hospital boundary.
3. **Tier 3 (Remote sites):** Each PHC has a small LAN with a router terminating the WAN link.

Data flow: bedside monitor (LAN) → HIS server (LAN) → firewall → MPLS WAN → PHC router (WAN) → consultation PC (LAN). The same path works in reverse for specialist feedback.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — Step-by-Step (7 marks = 1 mark per sub-step + 1 mark for engineering decision):**

**Step 1 — Propagation Delay**

$$
T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{4000 \times 10^3}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Transmission Delay**

$$
T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{2000}{2 \times 10^6} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Parameter $a$**

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{20}{1} = 20
$$

**Step 4 — Stop-and-Wait Utilization**

$$
U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 40} = \frac{1}{41} \approx 0.0244 = 2.44\%
$$

**Step 5 — Minimum Window Size for 100% Utilization**

$$
W_{min} = 2a + 1 = 2(20) + 1 = 41
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Decision (1 mark):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Recommendation:** Do **not** use Stop-and-Wait ARQ on this WAN link — utilization is only 2.44%, which wastes 97.5% of the expensive leased capacity. Implement **TCP with receive window ≥ 41 packets** (or Selective Repeat ARQ with $W = 41$). For real-time ECG streaming, use **UDP with Forward Error Correction (FEC)** to avoid retransmission delays altogether.

---

### **Question B (Internal Choice) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Compare the **five LAN topologies** (Star, Bus, Ring, Mesh, Hybrid) in terms of cabling cost, fault tolerance, ease of expansion, and suitability for a hospital PACS network. Recommend the best topology and justify. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **four categories of WAN technologies** used in telemedicine. For each, give one real-world telemedicine deployment example. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution**

**(a) Comparison Table (5 marks) + Recommendation (2 marks):**

| Topology | Cabling Cost | Fault Tolerance | Expansion | PACS Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Star** | High (one cable per node) | Medium (hub failure = total loss; node failure = local) | Very easy | **Good** — common in modern hospitals |
| **Bus** | Low (single cable) | Low (cable break = total loss) | Difficult (must break bus) | Poor — legacy only |
| **Ring** | Moderate | Low to medium (single break disrupts; dual ring saves) | Moderate | Acceptable — old FDDI PACS |
| **Mesh** | Very high ($n(n-1)/2$ links) | Excellent (multiple paths) | Difficult (re-wire) | Excellent for multi-hospital mesh |
| **Hybrid** | High | High | Easy | **Best** — star per floor + backbone ring/bus |

**Recommendation (2 marks):** Use a **Hybrid topology** for a hospital PACS network. Each floor/department is a **star** (easy maintenance, isolated failures), and floors are interconnected via a **fiber backbone ring or dual-star** for redundancy. This balances cost, scalability, and uptime — the three pillars of clinical IT infrastructure.

---

**(b) Four WAN Technology Categories (1.75 marks each):**

1. **Circuit-Switched WAN (ISDN/PSTN)** — Provides a dedicated 64 kbps channel. **Example:** Legacy tele-cardiology units in rural Kerala transmitting 12-lead ECG over ISDN.

2. **Packet-Switched WAN (MPLS)** — Label-switched, QoS-aware, low-jitter. **Example:** Amrita Hospital's tele-ICU network linking 100+ rural clinics over carrier MPLS.

3. **Wireless/Cell-Switched WAN (4G/5G/VSAT)** — Mobile or satellite. **Example:** 5G-connected ambulance in Delhi streaming live video of accident victim to trauma hospital; or ISRO's VSAT-based telemedicine for island territories.

4. **Internet-Based VPN WAN (IPsec/SSL VPN)** — Encrypted tunnels over public Internet. **Example:** Work-from-home radiologist using SSL VPN to access hospital PACS during COVID-19 lockdowns.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. ❌ **Confusing bandwidth (Hz) with data rate (bps).** Always clarify units.
> 2. ❌ **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $T_p$ round-trip** when computing utilization. The formula uses **one-way $T_p$** and assumes ACK is instantaneous.
> 3. ❌ **Stating "LAN is faster than WAN"** without qualification. A 100 Gbps WAN backbone is faster than a 10 Mbps LAN. The *real* distinction is **scope, ownership, and latency**, not absolute speed.
> 4. ❌ **Calling MPLS a Layer 3 protocol.** It is **Layer 2.5** — a fact worth 1 mark in 14-mark questions.
> 5. ❌ **Ignoring security in WAN design.** Telemedicine data is PHI (Protected Health Information); always mention **encryption, VPN, and HIPAA/DisHA (India) compliance**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for the night before the KTU exam:**

- **LAN** = Local, private, fast, low-latency, used inside hospital for PACS, HIS, bedside devices.
- **WAN** = Wide, public/shared, variable latency, used for inter-hospital and remote telemedicine.
- **Five LAN topologies:** Star, Bus, Ring, Mesh, Hybrid — know pros/cons and PACS relevance.
- **Two LAN MAC protocols:** CSMA/CD (Ethernet) and Token Passing (Ring/FDDI).
- **Four WAN technology families:** Circuit-switched, Packet-switched (MPLS), Cell/Wireless (4G/5G/VSAT), Internet-VPN.
- **MPLS is Layer 2.5** — label switching, QoS, used for tele-ICU.
- **Stop-and-Wait** is impractical on WANs with $a \gg 1$ — use **Sliding Window ARQ** with $W \geq 2a+1$.
- **Bandwidth-Delay Product** dictates buffer sizes at sender and receiver.
- **Shannon Capacity** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ sets the absolute upper bound on link throughput.
- **Telemedicine traffic classes:** Real-time (tele-ICU, tele-surgery) → low-latency WAN; Store-and-forward (tele-radiology, tele-dermatology) → any WAN, bandwidth matters.
- **Security layers:** TLS/DTLS for application, IPsec for network, 802.1X/MAB for LAN access, VPN for WAN.
- **Standard protocols to mention:** DICOM (imaging), HL7 (clinical data), SIP/H.323 (video), RTP/RTCP (real-time media).
- **Indian context examples:** ISRO Telemedicine, AIIMS tele-ICU, eSanjeevani (Government of India), Amrita-SET, Apollo Telemedicine.
- **Fiber vs. copper rule of thumb:** Use **fiber** for any link > 100 m carrying medical imaging; copper is fine for bedside endpoints.
- **5G URLLC** (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication) is the future of WAN for telemedicine — target latency 1 ms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Exchange and Network Configuration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT861 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Exchange and Network Configuration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Data Exchange in Telemedicine — Formal Definition

**Data Exchange** in a telemedicine context refers to the structured, standards-compliant transmission, reception, and interpretation of clinical, administrative, and biomedical information between heterogeneous information systems (e.g., HIS, RIS, PACS, EHR, telemedicine endpoints) over a communication network. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this is taught under the umbrella of *medical informatics interoperability*, where the emphasis is on **syntactic** (format) and **semantic** (meaning) compatibility.

The three pillars governing healthcare data exchange are:

1. **Standards** — HL7 (Health Level Seven), DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine), IHE (Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise), FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources).
2. **Transport** — TCP/IP suite, HTTP/HTTPS, MLLP (Minimal Lower Layer Protocol), DICOM Upper Layer.
3. **Security** — TLS/SSL, VPN tunneling, end-to-end encryption, audit trails compliant with HIPAA/GDPR-equivalent norms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course PEEBT861 expects the student to *describe, compare, and select* appropriate data exchange standards and network topologies for a given telemedicine use case (e.g., rural tele-cardiology vs. urban tele-radiology).

## 1.2 Network Configuration — Formal Definition

**Network Configuration** denotes the deliberate arrangement of hardware, software, protocols, addressing schemes, and topology to provide a reliable, secure, and Quality-of-Service (QoS)-aware communication substrate for telemedicine services. It encompasses:

- **Physical layer:** cabling (Cat6, fiber), wireless spectrum (2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, sub-6 GHz, mmWave).
- **Logical layer:** IP addressing (IPv4/IPv6), subnetting, VLANs, routing protocols (OSPF, BGP).
- **Application layer:** service endpoints, port mappings, firewall rules, NAT traversal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syntactic vs. Semantic Interoperability** — A network carrying bits is *syntactic* exchange; a system that *understands* the clinical meaning (e.g., that "HbA1c = 7.2" means glycated haemoglobin 7.2 %) is *semantic* interoperability. True telemedicine demands both.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Service" Model

Imagine a **specialised postal system** where:

- **Letters (Data Packets)** carry patient information, ECG strips, or DICOM images.
- **Envelope Addressing Standards (HL7/DICOM)** ensure every letter has a sender, receiver, and content-type label that any post office worldwide can read.
- **Post Offices (Routers/Switches)** sort and forward letters along the **road network (network topology)** — highways (backbone fiber), local streets (LAN), or couriers (4G/5G).
- **Tamper-proof seals and registered post (TLS/VPN)** prevent the letters from being opened or replaced in transit.
- **Sorting hubs (Gateways/PACS servers)** consolidate and forward mail to specialist branches.

If the envelopes are non-standard (no HL7/DICOM), or the roads are congested (insufficient bandwidth), or the seals are missing (no encryption), the postal service collapses — so does telemedicine.

## 1.4 Physical & Standards Constants to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Memorise (Frequently tested):**
> - HL7 v2.x message delimiter: `0x0B` (vertical tab start) and `0x1C` (field separator `|`) and `0x0D` (segment terminator `\r`).
> - DICOM default port: **104** (TCP) and **11112** for WADO (Web Access to DICOM Objects) over HTTP.
> - MLLP default port: **6661** (configurable).
> - IHE profiles commonly tested: **XDS** (Cross-Enterprise Document Sharing), **XDS-I** (Imaging), **ATNA** (Audit Trail and Node Authentication), **PIX** (Patient Identifier Cross-referencing).
> - TCP/IP model layers: **4** (Application, Transport, Internet, Link) versus OSI **7** layers.

**Key numeric benchmarks** used in KTU problem statements:
- Standard chest X-ray: ~ **2048 × 2048** pixels, 12-bit depth → ~ **6 MB** uncompressed.
- CT slice: **512 × 512** × 16-bit → **0.5 MB** per slice; full chest CT (~ 500 slices) → ~ **250 MB**.
- ECG, 12-lead, 500 Hz, 16-bit, 10 s: ~ **192 KB** uncompressed.
- Telepathology whole-slide image (WSI): **100 000 × 100 000** × 24-bit → ~ **30 GB** uncompressed.
- Reference bandwidths: T1 = **1.544 Mbps**, E1 = **2.048 Mbps**, 100BASE-TX = **100 Mbps**, 1000BASE-T = **1 Gbps**, 10GBASE-T = **10 Gbps**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Data Exchange Stack — Layered View

Telemedicine data exchange is best understood as a **four-layer stack**, mapped to the OSI/TCP-IP reference model:

| KTU Layer | OSI Equivalent | Function in Telemedicine | Example Protocols/Standards |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Application (Clinical Semantics)** | 7, 6, 5 | Define *what* clinical information means | HL7 v2.x, HL7 FHIR, SNOMED CT, LOINC |
| **Application (Data Format)** | 7 | Define *how* information is packaged | DICOM, CCR, CDA, JSON/XML (FHIR) |
| **Transport** | 4 | Reliable delivery, flow control, multiplexing | TCP, UDP (for streaming), MLLP, DICOM Upper Layer |
| **Network / Internet** | 3 | Addressing and routing | IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, IPSec |
| **Link / Physical** | 2, 1 | Bits on the wire / air | Ethernet (802.3), Wi-Fi (802.11), LTE/5G NR, Fiber (GPON) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why layering matters in KTU answers:** Examiners award marks for *identifying* which layer a given concern belongs to. Example: "Encryption" is **Application/Presentation** (TLS) or **Network** (IPSec) — not Link layer, unless using MACsec (802.1AE).

## 2.2 The HL7 v2.x Messaging Standard — Operational Logic

HL7 v2.x is the **de-facto** workhorse for clinical/administrative messaging. Each message is a **sequence of segments**, each segment is a **line of fields**, each field can contain **components**, and components can contain **subcomponents**.

**Operational logic (bullet form for answer-writing):**

- A message is **triggered** by an event (e.g., *Admit patient*, *Order lab test*, *Send observation*).
- Each message begins with a **MSH** (Message Header) segment that declares delimiters, sending/receiving application, message type, and control ID.
- **PID** (Patient Identification) carries demographics.
- **PV1** (Patient Visit) carries encounter/visit info.
- **OBR** (Observation Request) and **OBX** (Observation/Result) carry clinical observations (e.g., lab values, vital signs).
- Segments are terminated by the ASCII carriage-return `\r` (0x0D).
- The transport binding is typically **MLLP** over TCP, though newer systems use **HL7 FHIR over HTTPS/REST**.

## 2.3 DICOM — Operational Logic

DICOM is the global standard for **medical imaging** exchange. It is *not* just a file format — it is a **protocol + information object + service class** triplet.

- **Information Object Definition (IOD):** defines the *semantics* (e.g., CT Image IOD, MR Image IOD, ECG Waveform IOD).
- **Service Class:** defines the *operations* (Storage, Query/Retrieve, Modality Worklist, MPPS, Storage Commitment).
- **DICOM Upper Layer Protocol (DUL):** defines *transport* over TCP, using **Application Entity Titles (AET)** as identifiers and **Presentation Contexts** to negotiate transfer syntaxes.
- **Transfer Syntaxes:** JPEG Lossless, JPEG 2000, RLE, explicit/implicit VR Little Endian — chosen during Association negotiation.

> [!NOTE]
> **DICOM vs. PACS vs. HL7** — common confusion cleared: **DICOM** is the standard; **PACS** is the *system* that uses DICOM to store/retrieve images; **HL7** is *typically* used for the *metadata* (orders, results, demographics) that travels *separately* but is *linked* to the DICOM image via a Study Instance UID.

## 2.4 HL7 FHIR — Modern RESTful Approach

FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) is HL7's modern answer to web-scale interoperability.

- Uses **Resources** (JSON/XML) — small, addressable, RESTful objects (Patient, Observation, DiagnosticReport, ImagingStudy, etc.).
- Each resource has a **logical ID** and a **versioned history**.
- Communication: **HTTP verbs** (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) over **HTTPS** on well-defined endpoints.
- Bundles allow batching (e.g., *searchset* bundle for queries, *transaction* bundle for atomic write).
- Built-in support for **SMART on FHIR** OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect for app authorisation.

## 2.5 Network Topologies for Telemedicine

| Topology | Description | Strength | Weakness | Telemedicine Fit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Star** | All nodes connect to a central switch/hub | Easy to add nodes, single point of failure at hub | Hub failure = network down | Small clinic to hospital |
| **Bus** | All nodes share a single backbone | Cheap, simple | Single cable break = whole network down | Legacy, rarely used |
| **Ring** | Nodes form a closed loop | Predictable latency | Dual break isolates segments | Token-Ring historical |
| **Mesh (Full)** | Every node connects to every other | Maximum redundancy, self-healing | Costly (n(n-1)/2 links) | Multi-hospital PACS |
| **Mesh (Partial)** | Selective redundancy | Balanced cost & reliability | Design complexity | District-level telemedicine |
| **Hybrid (Star–Bus, Star–Ring)** | Combines two or more | Scalable, fault-tolerant | More complex management | Large hospital + spokes |
| **Tree / Hierarchical** | Tiered star levels | Mirrors organisational hierarchy | Root failure cascades | National e-health network |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is HIGH-YIELD. Memorise it.**

| Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Uncompressed image size (grayscale) | $S = R \times C \times \dfrac{B}{8}$ | bytes | $R$=rows, $C$=columns, $B$=bit depth |
| Uncompressed image size (colour) | $S = R \times C \times 3 \times \dfrac{B}{8}$ | bytes | 3 channels (R,G,B) |
| Number of slices in a CT/MR series | $N$ | unitless | Often 200–1000 |
| Series size | $S_{\text{series}} = N \times S_{\text{slice}}$ | bytes | Multiply by header overhead |
| Compression ratio | $C_r = \dfrac{S_{\text{original}}}{S_{\text{compressed}}}$ | unitless | Lossless typically 2:1–3:1; lossy up to 20:1 |
| Transfer time | $T = \dfrac{S}{B_{\text{eff}}}$ | seconds | $B_{\text{eff}}$ = effective bandwidth in bytes/s |
| Effective bandwidth (accounting for overhead) | $B_{\text{eff}} = B \times (1 - O)$ | bps | $O$ = protocol overhead fraction (≈ 0.10–0.20) |
| Bandwidth–Delay Product (BDP) | $\text{BDP} = B \times t_p$ | bits | $t_p$ = one-way propagation delay |
| Maximum TCP window requirement | $W \ge \text{BDP}$ | bits | For full link utilisation |
| Throughput (window-limited TCP) | $\text{Th} = \dfrac{W}{RTT}$ | bps | When $W < \text{BDP}$ |
| Shannon–Hartley channel capacity | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | bps | $B$=Hz, $S/N$=power ratio |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio (dB) | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | dB | For audio/ECG digitisation |
| ECG file size (continuous) | $S_{\text{ECG}} = n_c \times f_s \times \dfrac{B}{8} \times t$ | bytes | $n_c$ = number of channels (e.g., 12) |
| Round-Trip Time | $RTT = 2 t_p + \dfrac{S}{B} + t_{proc}$ | seconds | Includes propagation, transmission, processing |
| Network availability (MTBF/MTTR) | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | ratio or % | Telemedicine target: 99.99 % (four nines) |
| Quantisation SNR (linear, $b$-bit uniform) | $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} \approx 6.02\,b + 1.76$ | dB | Rule-of-thumb for PCM |

> **Critical LaTeX safety rule used in the table above:** all `|x|`-style absolute values and set-builder braces have been replaced with `\vert` and `\mid` so that the markdown table never breaks.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic curiosities — they are used by:

- **Tele-radiology PACS engineers** sizing storage: how many TB/year of imaging data?
- **Network architects** of state telemedicine programmes (e.g., e-Sanjeevani, UK's NHS Spine) calculating link budgets.
- **Medical device manufacturers** of portable ECG/SPO₂ monitors designing buffers and compression.
- **Hospital CIOs** negotiating Service Level Agreements (SLAs) and selecting between leased lines, MPLS, SD-WAN, or 5G slicing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides **exhaustive derivations** of the most-tested formulas. **No step is skipped.** Every algebraic transition is explained in plain English and LaTeX.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Uncompressed Medical Image File Size

### Problem Setup
A digital mammogram is acquired with $R = 4080$ rows and $C = 4080$ columns of pixels, each quantised with $B = 14$ bits (typical for mammography flat-panel detectors). Find the uncompressed file size in megabytes (MB).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the formula for a single 2-D grayscale image:**

$$S = R \times C \times \frac{B}{8} \quad \text{(bytes)}$$

This formula is derived from the fact that every pixel occupies exactly $B$ bits, there are $R \times C$ pixels in the rectangular raster, and dividing by 8 converts bits to bytes.

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$S = 4080 \times 4080 \times \frac{14}{8}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the pixel count:**

$$4080 \times 4080 = 16\,646\,400 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the bit depth in bytes:**

$$\frac{14}{8} = 1.75 \text{ bytes per pixel}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply:**

$$S = 16\,646\,400 \times 1.75 = 29\,131\,200 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to megabytes** (1 MB = $1024 \times 1024$ bytes = $1\,048\,576$ bytes):

$$S = \frac{29\,131\,200}{1\,048\,576} \approx 27.78 \text{ MB}$$

### Conclusion
A single digital mammogram is **≈ 27.78 MB** uncompressed. With **JPEG Lossless** compression (typical ratio 2:1), this drops to ~ 14 MB. With **Wavelet (JPEG 2000) lossy at 10:1** (acceptable for screening), it is ~ 2.8 MB. DICOM headers add ~ 1–2 KB overhead per file — negligible.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total CT Study Size and Transmission Time

### Problem Setup
A 16-slice helical CT chest study acquires 600 slices. Each slice is 512 × 512 × 16 bits. The study is to be transmitted over a **4G LTE link with effective bandwidth $B_{\text{eff}} = 8$ Mbps** (after accounting for ~ 20 % protocol overhead). The radiologist needs the study in under 5 minutes. Determine if this link is adequate.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute uncompressed size of one slice:**

$$S_{\text{slice}} = R \times C \times \frac{B}{8} = 512 \times 512 \times \frac{16}{8} = 512 \times 512 \times 2$$

$$512 \times 512 = 262\,144 \text{ pixels}$$

$$S_{\text{slice}} = 262\,144 \times 2 = 524\,288 \text{ bytes} = 0.5 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 2 — Total uncompressed study size:**

$$S_{\text{study}} = N \times S_{\text{slice}} = 600 \times 0.5 = 300 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 3 — Apply standard lossless compression** (typical CT: 2.5:1):

$$S_{\text{compressed}} = \frac{300}{2.5} = 120 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 4 — Convert 4G effective bandwidth to bytes per second:**

$$B_{\text{eff}} = 8 \text{ Mbps} = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s} = 1 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s}$$

(Using $1$ byte = $8$ bits.)

**Step 5 — Compute transfer time:**

$$T = \frac{S_{\text{compressed}}}{B_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{120 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes}}{1 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s}} = 120 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 — Compare to requirement:**

$$120 \text{ s} = 2 \text{ min} < 5 \text{ min} \quad \checkmark$$

### Conclusion
The 4G LTE link is **adequate** for a single CT study in real time. However, **a multi-study transfer (e.g., 10 studies = 20 min)** will saturate it. The link should be **dedicated** (APN) with **QoS prioritisation** for DICOM traffic.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bandwidth–Delay Product and TCP Window Tuning

### Problem Setup
A district hospital sends a 50 MB DICOM study to a tertiary PACS over a **geostationary satellite link** with one-way propagation delay $t_p = 250$ ms and bandwidth $B = 4$ Mbps. Compute the bandwidth–delay product and the minimum TCP window size required for full link utilisation.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the BDP formula:**

$$\text{BDP} = B \times t_p \quad \text{(bits)}$$

This represents the number of bits that can "fill the pipe" — i.e., that the sender must have outstanding (un-ACKed) on the link at any moment for the line to be fully utilised.

**Step 2 — Substitute (use one-way delay, not RTT, for the pipe-fill quantity):**

$$\text{BDP} = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s} \times 0.250 \text{ s}$$

$$\text{BDP} = 1 \times 10^6 \text{ bits} = 1 \text{ Mb}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to bytes:**

$$\text{BDP} = \frac{1 \times 10^6}{8} = 125\,000 \text{ bytes} \approx 122 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum TCP receive window:**

$$W_{\min} \ge \text{BDP} = 122 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to window-scale-scaled segments (MSS = 1460 B typical):**

$$N_{\text{segments}} = \frac{122\,000}{1460} \approx 84 \text{ segments}$$

### Conclusion
The **default TCP window of 64 KB is insufficient** for this satellite link. TCP **Window Scaling** (RFC 7323) must be enabled, and the window must be set to **at least 122 KB** to fully utilise the 4 Mbps. Many legacy Windows XP machines had this disabled — a classic KTU-style "real-world troubleshooting" question.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: ECG Waveform File Size for Holter-style Continuous Recording

### Problem Setup
A 12-lead Holter ECG records at $f_s = 500$ Hz, with $B = 16$ bits/sample, for $t = 24$ hours continuously. Find the file size, then the transfer time over a 256 kbps GPRS link.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — General formula for multi-channel waveform:**

$$S_{\text{ECG}} = n_c \times f_s \times \frac{B}{8} \times t$$

where $n_c$ = number of channels (leads), $f_s$ = sampling frequency, $B$ = bit depth, $t$ = duration in seconds.

**Step 2 — Convert 24 hours to seconds:**

$$t = 24 \times 3600 = 86\,400 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$S_{\text{ECG}} = 12 \times 500 \times \frac{16}{8} \times 86\,400$$

$$S_{\text{ECG}} = 12 \times 500 \times 2 \times 86\,400$$

**Step 4 — Compute stepwise:**

$$12 \times 500 = 6000$$

$$6000 \times 2 = 12\,000 \text{ bytes/s}$$

$$12\,000 \times 86\,400 = 1.0368 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to MB and GB:**

$$S_{\text{ECG}} = \frac{1.0368 \times 10^9}{10^6} = 1036.8 \text{ MB} \approx 1.01 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 6 — Transfer time over 256 kbps GPRS** (bytes/s = $256 \times 10^3 / 8 = 32\,000$ B/s):

$$T = \frac{1.0368 \times 10^9}{32\,000} = 32\,400 \text{ s} \approx 9 \text{ h}$$

### Conclusion
A 24-h Holter is **~ 1 GB** uncompressed, taking **~ 9 hours** on GPRS. This is why **ECG compression** (e.g., MPEG-4, SCP-ECG, or DICOM waveform with JPEG-LS) is essential; a 10:1 compression reduces the file to ~ 100 MB and transmission to ~ 1 hour. KTU examiners love this example because it shows the *engineering* justification for compression.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Shannon–Hartley Channel Capacity for Telemedicine Link Budget

### Problem Setup
A wireless telemedicine link operates in the 2.4 GHz ISM band with a usable bandwidth $B = 20$ MHz. The measured SNR at the receiver is 30 dB. Find the maximum theoretical bit rate (Shannon capacity).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the Shannon–Hartley theorem:**

$$C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{(bps)}$$

**Step 2 — Convert SNR from dB to linear:**

$$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{\frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}}{10}} = 10^{\frac{30}{10}} = 10^3 = 1000$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$C = 20 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(1 + 1000)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the logarithm:**

$$\log_2(1001) \approx 9.97 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply:**

$$C \approx 20 \times 10^6 \times 9.97 = 1.994 \times 10^8 \text{ bps} \approx 200 \text{ Mbps}$$

### Conclusion
The Shannon ceiling is **~ 200 Mbps**. Real Wi-Fi 802.11n achieves ~ 75–100 Mbps — illustrating that **theoretical capacity ≈ 50 % of practical throughput**, a useful rule-of-thumb to quote in KTU design problems.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Telemedicine Service to Network Parameters

| Service | Data Volume / Event | Latency Tolerance | Bandwidth Need | Recommended Standard | Recommended Network |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tele-ECG (real-time)** | ~ 200 KB / 10 s | < 500 ms | 160 kbps | DICOM Waveform / SCP-ECG | 4G / Wi-Fi |
| **Tele-radiology (CT/MR)** | 100–500 MB / study | < 5 min for STAT | 8–50 Mbps | DICOM | Fiber / 4G+ |
| **Telepathology (WSI)** | 0.5–3 GB / slide | Hours acceptable | 100 Mbps+ | DICOM WSI / SVS | Fiber / 5G mmWave |
| **Tele-ophthalmology (fundus)** | ~ 5 MB / image | Seconds | 2 Mbps | DICOM / JPEG2000 | 4G / DSL |
| **Tele-consultation (video)** | 2 Mbps sustained | < 200 ms | 2–4 Mbps | H.264 / H.265 / WebRTC | 4G/5G / Fiber |
| **EHR sync (batch)** | MB to GB | Hours | 1–10 Mbps | HL7 v2 / FHIR Bulk | Any broadband |
| **Remote patient monitoring (RPM)** | 1–10 KB / min | Seconds | < 100 kbps | HL7 FHIR / MQTT | NB-IoT / LTE-M |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid safety rules applied:** all node IDs are alphanumeric with alphabetic prefixes; no reserved keywords used as standalone node IDs; no markdown/HTML formatting inside node labels.

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — HL7 v2.x Message Structure (ADT^A01 Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trigger Event: ADT A01 Patient Admit] --> B[Build MSH Segment]
    B --> C[Add PID Patient ID Segment]
    C --> D[Add PV1 Patient Visit Segment]
    D --> E[Add AL1 Allergy Segment optional]
    E --> F[Wrap in MLLP Frame]
    F --> G[Transmit over TCP Port 6661]
    G --> H[Receiving App Parses]
    H --> I[ACK Acknowledgment returned]
    I --> J[Update HIS EMR Database]
```

**How to read:** Every HL7 v2 message is a *sequence* of segments separated by `\r`. The MSH declares the message-type, sender, receiver, control ID, and version. PID carries demographics (name, DOB, MRN). PV1 carries visit info (ward, room, attending doctor). The receiver parses, validates, and returns an **ACK** (general acknowledgement).

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — DICOM Association Negotiation Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SCU as SCU Modality Workstation
    participant SCP as SCP PACS Server
    SCU->>SCP: A ASSOCIATE RQ<br/>AE Titles Proposed Presentation Contexts
    SCP-->>SCU: A ASSOCIATE AC<br/>Accepted Presentation Contexts Transfer Syntaxes
    SCU->>SCP: C STORE RQ<br/>Dataset of Image
    SCP-->>SCU: C STORE RSP<br/>Status 0000 Success
    SCU->>SCP: A RELEASE RQ
    SCP-->>SCU: A RELEASE RP
```

**How to read:** DICOM uses a **requestor (SCU)** / **acceptor (SCP)** model. The SCU proposes transfer syntaxes (e.g., JPEG Lossless, Explicit VR LE) via *Presentation Contexts*; the SCP picks a subset to accept. After association, dataset transfer occurs (CTSTORE for images, C-FIND for queries, C-MOVE for retrieval). The connection is gracefully torn down with an A-RELEASE.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Architecture of a PACS / Hospital Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ACQ[Acquisition Modalities]
        A1[CT Scanner]
        A2[MRI Scanner]
        A3[Ultrasound Unit]
        A4[Digital X Ray]
    end
    subgraph EDGE[Edge Network LAN]
        S1[Layer 3 Core Switch]
    end
    subgraph CORE[Core Hospital Servers]
        P1[PACS Archive NAS]
        P2[DICOM Router]
        P3[HL7 Interface Engine]
        P4[HIS EMR]
    end
    subgraph EXT[External Cloud and Remote]
        C1[Cloud PACS Backup]
        C2[Teleradiology Reading Centre]
        C3[Referring Clinic VPN]
    end
    A1 --> S1
    A2 --> S1
    A3 --> S1
    A4 --> S1
    S1 --> P1
    S1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P1 --> C1
    P2 --> C2
    P3 --> C3
```

**How to read:** Modalities push DICOM to the PACS through the core switch. The HL7 engine mediates HIS↔PACS metadata exchange (orders, results). External access is brokered through controlled gateways with VPN/TLS to ensure HIPAA/GDPR compliance.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — OSI/TCP-IP Layered Mapping for a Telemedicine Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7[Application Layer: HL7 FHIR DICOM H 264 WebRTC]
    L6[Presentation Layer: TLS SSL Encryption JSON XML]
    L5[Session Layer: SIP for Video DICOM Association]
    L4[Transport Layer: TCP MLLP UDP for Streaming]
    L3[Network Layer: IPv4 IPv6 IPSec]
    L2[Data Link Layer: Ethernet 802 11 WiFi LTE 5G NR]
    L1[Physical Layer: Fiber Copper RF Microwave]
    L7 --> L6
    L6 --> L5
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

**How to read:** A telemedicine *application* (e.g., a tele-ECG consultation) is built by stacking protocols: HL7 v2 over MLLP over TCP over IP over Ethernet. The patient data is *wrapped* at each layer — a classic exam illustration.

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Network Topology Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Telemedicine Network Topology] --> Q1{Number of sites}
    Q1 -->|2 sites only| Q2{Geo distance over 50 km}
    Q1 -->|3 to 10 sites| Q3{Criticality of downtime}
    Q1 -->|More than 10 sites| Q4{Is internet allowed}
    Q2 -->|No| S1[Point to Point Leased Line or VPN]
    Q2 -->|Yes| S2[MPLS or VSAT or 4G 5G]
    Q3 -->|High| T1[Full Mesh for redundancy]
    Q3 -->|Medium| T2[Partial Mesh Hybrid]
    Q4 -->|Yes| U1[Hub and Spoke with Cloud SD WAN]
    Q4 -->|No| U2[National eHealth MPLS Backbone]
```

**How to read:** A site-count × criticality × security matrix produces the topology. Single pair of sites → point-to-point. Three or more sites with high criticality → mesh. National scale → hub-and-spoke cloud SD-WAN.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define HL7 and DICOM. State two key differences between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **HL7 (Health Level Seven)** is a set of international standards for the **exchange of clinical and administrative data** between healthcare information systems. *(1 mark)*
- **DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine)** is the global standard for **storage, transmission, and handling of medical imaging information** (CT, MR, US, XA, etc.). *(1 mark)*
- **Differences:** *(any two, ½ mark each)*
  1. HL7 deals with **textual/structured messages** (patient demographics, orders, results); DICOM deals with **binary image datasets** plus metadata.
  2. HL7 transport is typically **MLLP over TCP** (port 6661); DICOM transport is its **Upper Layer Protocol over TCP** (port 104) with presentation-context negotiation.
  3. HL7 v2.x is a **message-based** standard; DICOM is a **service-class + information-object** standard.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any three network topologies used in telemedicine and mention one merit and one demerit of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Star:** Merits — easy installation, single point of troubleshooting. Demerit — central hub is a single point of failure. *(1 mark)*
- **Ring:** Merits — equal access, predictable performance. Demerit — a single break disrupts the entire ring (unless dual-ring). *(1 mark)*
- **Mesh (Full):** Merits — maximum redundancy and self-healing. Demerit — high cost $\left[\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}\right]$ links. *(1 mark)*

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Choice (Module 2)**: KTU 2024 scheme allows the student to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B from the same module. Both must be prepared.

### Question A (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **DICOM communication protocol stack** and explain the **Association Negotiation** process between an SCU and an SCP. List any **four Service Classes** supported by DICOM. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A telemedicine network is to be designed to transfer a **CT study of 600 slices**, each **512 × 512 × 16 bits**, between a district hospital and a tertiary PACS over a **4G LTE link with effective bandwidth of 10 Mbps**. Compute the **(i) uncompressed study size**, **(ii) compressed study size (assume 2.5:1 lossless)**, **(iii) transfer time in seconds**, and **(iv) state whether the link meets a 3-minute STAT-read SLA**. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — DICOM Protocol Stack and Association Negotiation (7 marks)

**Block diagram (2 marks):**

```
        ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
        │  Information Objects (IODs)         │  e.g., CT Image, MR Image
        ├─────────────────────────────────────┤
        │  Service Classes                    │  e.g., Storage, Q/R
        ├─────────────────────────────────────┤
        │  DICOM Upper Layer (DUL)            │  Association, Presentation
        ├─────────────────────────────────────┤
        │  TCP (Port 104)                     │
        ├─────────────────────────────────────┤
        │  IP / Ethernet / Wi-Fi             │
        └─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Association Negotiation (3 marks):**

1. The **SCU** (Service Class User, e.g., modality) initiates a TCP connection on port **104** of the SCP.
2. SCU sends an **A-ASSOCIATE-RQ** (Association Request) PDU containing its **AE Title**, the **AE Title** of the target SCP, and one or more **Proposed Presentation Contexts**. Each context pairs an **Abstract Syntax** (e.g., CT Image Storage SOP Class UID) with one or more **Transfer Syntaxes** (e.g., JPEG Lossless, Explicit VR Little Endian).
3. The **SCP** reviews the proposal and replies with an **A-ASSOCIATE-AC** (Accept) or **A-ASSOCIATE-RJ** (Reject) PDU. In the Accept, it lists the **Accepted Presentation Contexts** (one per UID) and a single chosen **Transfer Syntax** for each.
4. Once associated, the SCU can issue DIMSE service requests (e.g., **C-STORE**, **C-FIND**, **C-MOVE**, **C-ECHO**). Each DIMSE message is wrapped in a **P-DATA** PDU.
5. Either side can issue **A-RELEASE-RQ** to gracefully close the association; the other responds with **A-RELEASE-RP**. The TCP connection is then closed.

**Four Service Classes (1 mark):**

1. **Storage Service Class** — push images from modality to PACS.
2. **Query/Retrieve (Q/R) Service Class** — find studies and pull them (C-FIND + C-MOVE/C-GET).
3. **Modality Worklist (MWL) Service Class** — push scheduled procedure info to modality.
4. **Storage Commitment Service Class** — PACS commits to safekeeping the image.
5. *(Bonus, optional)* **Modality Performed Procedure Step (MPPS)** — modality reports completion.

**Key terms (1 mark):** AE Title, Presentation Context, Transfer Syntax, DIMSE, SOP Class.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:** $N = 600$ slices; each slice $512 \times 512 \times 16$ bits; $B_{\text{eff}} = 10$ Mbps; SLA = 3 min = 180 s; compression ratio = 2.5:1 (lossless).

**(i) Uncompressed study size** (1.5 marks):

$$S_{\text{slice}} = 512 \times 512 \times \frac{16}{8} = 262\,144 \times 2 = 524\,288 \text{ bytes} = 0.5 \text{ MB}$$

$$S_{\text{study,raw}} = 600 \times 0.5 = 300 \text{ MB}$$

**(ii) Compressed study size** (1.5 marks):

$$S_{\text{compressed}} = \frac{300}{2.5} = 120 \text{ MB}$$

**(iii) Transfer time** (2 marks):

Convert to bytes: $B_{\text{eff}} = 10$ Mbps $= 1.25 \times 10^6$ bytes/s.

$$T = \frac{120 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes}}{1.25 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s}} = 96 \text{ s}$$

**(iv) SLA check** (2 marks):

$$96 \text{ s} < 180 \text{ s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{LINK IS ADEQUATE} \quad \checkmark$$

**Engineering recommendation (bonus):** With ~ 96 s of transfer, a STAT read within 3 min is met. However, the link should be **dedicated**, **QoS-prioritised**, and the PACS should pre-fetch using **DICOM C-MOVE** during off-peak hours to avoid contention.

---

### Question B (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the **OSI 7-layer model** and **TCP/IP 4-layer model**. Map the following telemedicine protocols to their correct layers: **HL7 v2, DICOM, TCP, IP, Ethernet, Wi-Fi (802.11), TLS, H.264, WebRTC, SIP**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **tele-ECG system** records a **12-lead ECG at 500 Hz, 16-bit, for 24 hours**. The data is transmitted over a **128 kbps satellite link** with RTT = 600 ms. Compute the **(i) raw file size in MB**, **(ii) transfer time in minutes**, **(iii) bandwidth–delay product in KB**, and **(iv) recommend a suitable compression scheme with justification**. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — OSI vs TCP/IP Layered Models and Protocol Mapping (7 marks)

**OSI 7-Layer Model (1.5 marks):**

| # | Layer | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 7 | Application | End-user services (HL7, HTTP, SMTP) |
| 6 | Presentation | Data format, encryption (TLS, JSON/XML) |
| 5 | Session | Dialog control, tokens (SIP, RPC) |
| 4 | Transport | Reliable delivery, flow control (TCP, UDP) |
| 3 | Network | Routing, addressing (IP, ICMP) |
| 2 | Data Link | Frame transfer (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP) |
| 1 | Physical | Bits on wire/air (Fiber, copper, RF) |

**TCP/IP 4-Layer Model (1.5 marks):**

1. **Application** — combines OSI 5, 6, 7. Example: HL7 v2, DICOM, HTTP, WebRTC.
2. **Transport** — TCP, UDP, MLLP.
3. **Internet** — IP, ICMP, IPSec.
4. **Link / Network Access** — Ethernet, Wi-Fi, ATM, LTE/5G NR.

**Protocol Mapping (4 marks; ½ mark each correct placement):**

| Protocol | OSI Layer | TCP/IP Layer |
|---|---|---|
| **HL7 v2** | 7 (Application) | Application |
| **DICOM** | 7 (Application) | Application |
| **H.264** | 6 (Presentation) | Application |
| **TLS** | 6 (Presentation) | Application |
| **WebRTC** | 5/7 (Session/App) | Application |
| **SIP** | 5 (Session) | Application |
| **TCP** | 4 (Transport) | Transport |
| **IP** | 3 (Network) | Internet |
| **Ethernet** | 2 (Data Link) | Link |
| **Wi-Fi (802.11)** | 1+2 (Physical + Data Link) | Link |

**Conclusion:** Telemedicine stacks are an *integrated suite* across all layers, with security (TLS) and real-time media (WebRTC, H.264) increasingly important.

#### Part (b) — Tele-ECG Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $n_c = 12$ leads, $f_s = 500$ Hz, $B = 16$ bits, $t = 24 \times 3600 = 86\,400$ s, $B_{\text{link}} = 128$ kbps, RTT = 600 ms.

**(i) Raw file size** (2 marks):

$$S_{\text{ECG}} = n_c \times f_s \times \frac{B}{8} \times t$$

$$S_{\text{ECG}} = 12 \times 500 \times 2 \times 86\,400 = 1.0368 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes}$$

$$S_{\text{ECG}} \approx 1036.8 \text{ MB} \approx 1.01 \text{ GB}$$

**(ii) Transfer time** (2 marks):

$$B_{\text{link}} = 128 \text{ kbps} = 16\,000 \text{ bytes/s}$$

$$T = \frac{1.0368 \times 10^9}{16\,000} = 64\,800 \text{ s} = 1080 \text{ min} = 18 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Bandwidth–Delay Product** (1.5 marks):

$$\text{BDP} = B_{\text{link}} \times t_p$$

One-way delay $t_p = \text{RTT}/2 = 300$ ms = 0.3 s.

$$\text{BDP} = 128 \times 10^3 \text{ bits/s} \times 0.3 \text{ s} = 38\,400 \text{ bits} = 4800 \text{ bytes} \approx 4.7 \text{ KB}$$

**(iv) Compression recommendation** (1.5 marks):

A **10:1 lossless compression** (e.g., **DICOM waveform with JPEG-LS** or **MPEG-4 predictive coding**) reduces the 1 GB file to ~ **100 MB** and the transfer time to ~ **1.7 hours**. For real-time telemonitoring, **DPCM + Huffman** with **run-length encoding** is common, achieving 3:1 to 5:1 with diagnostic-grade fidelity. Justification: preserves diagnostic QRS morphology, widely supported, low CPU cost, approved by IHE.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common reasons for losing marks**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — confusing **Mbps** vs **MB/s**. Remember: $1 \text{ MB/s} = 8 \text{ Mbps}$. This is the **single most common** error in numerical answers.
> 2. **Confusing RTT with one-way delay** in the BDP formula. Standard form: $\text{BDP} = B \times t_p$ (one-way). The throughput formula uses RTT: $\text{Th} = W / \text{RTT}$.
> 3. **Forgetting to apply compression ratio** when the problem says "lossless 2.5:1". Students often quote the raw size.
> 4. **Mixing up HL7 and DICOM** in long answers — HL7 is for *clinical/administrative*; DICOM is for *imaging*. Examiners deduct ½–1 mark for this.
> 5. **Writing "TCP/IP has 7 layers"** — it has 4 (or 5, with the Physical sub-layer). This is a factual error caught often.
> 6. **Not labelling Mermaid/ASCII diagrams** with arrow directions and entity names — marks are awarded for *legible* diagrams.
> 7. **Oversimplifying security** — saying "encryption is used" without naming the protocol (TLS, IPSec, MACsec, AES-256).
> 8. **Ignoring overhead** in bandwidth calculations — protocol overhead is typically 10–20 % in TCP/IP stacks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — re-read this section the night before the exam.**

- [ ] HL7 v2.x = clinical/administrative **messaging** (textual segments, MSH/PID/OBR/OBX).
- [ ] HL7 FHIR = modern **REST + JSON/XML** resources; uses HTTP verbs.
