- [ ] DICOM = medical **imaging** standard; uses TCP **port 104**; SCU/SCP; Presentation Contexts negotiate transfer syntaxes.
- [ ] IHE = integration **profiles** (XDS, XDS-I, ATNA, PIX) that combine HL7 + DICOM.
- [ ] PACS = the **system**; DICOM = the **standard**; HL7 = the **metadata bus**.
- [ ] Four reference models to remember: **OSI 7**, **TCP/IP 4**, **HL7 v2 message**, **DICOM IOD**.
- [ ] Formula — image size: $S = R \times C \times \dfrac{B}{8}$ (grayscale), multiplied by 3 for colour.
- [ ] Formula — ECG size: $S_{\text{ECG}} = n_c \times f_s \times \dfrac{B}{8} \times t$.
- [ ] Formula — transfer time: $T = S / B_{\text{eff}}$; remember to convert **Mbps → MB/s** by dividing by 8.
- [ ] Formula — BDP: $\text{BDP} = B \times t_p$ (one-way delay). TCP window must be ≥ BDP for full utilisation.
- [ ] Formula — Shannon capacity: $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$. SNR in dB → linear: $S/N = 10^{(\text{dB}/10)}$.
- [ ] Formula — quantisation SNR: $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} \approx 6.02 b + 1.76$.
- [ ] Topologies — Star (clinic), Partial Mesh (district), Full Mesh (multi-hospital PACS), Hub-and-Spoke (national e-health).
- [ ] Ports to memorise — **104** (DICOM), **11112** (WADO), **6661** (MLLP), **443** (HTTPS), **80** (HTTP).
- [ ] Reference bandwidths — T1 = 1.544 Mbps, E1 = 2.048 Mbps, Fast Ethernet = 100 Mbps, Gigabit = 1 Gbps.
- [ ] Compression: lossless 2:1–3:1 typical; lossy up to 20:1 (only for screening, not primary diagnosis).
- [ ] Security triad: **TLS** (Application), **IPSec** (Network), **MACsec 802.1AE** (Link).
- [ ] Telepathology WSI = **multi-GB** per slide; needs **fiber or 5G mmWave**.
- [ ] Always state **units** in numerical answers; the examiner *wants* to see "MB", "seconds", "Mbps".
- [ ] Use **MTBF/MTTR** formula: $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$ for availability (target 99.99 %).
- [ ] Standard ECG sample rate = 500 Hz; standard CT slice matrix = 512 × 512; standard mammogram = ~ 4k × 4k.
- [ ] Always include an **engineering recommendation** at the end of a 14-mark answer (e.g., "dedicated APN, QoS, VPN") to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circuit and Packet Switching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT861 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Circuit and Packet Switching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Switching in Telemedicine Networks

In any telemedicine infrastructure—whether it's a rural clinic transmitting an ECG to a super-specialty hospital or a surgeon conducting a live robotic tele-consultation—**switching** is the fundamental mechanism that determines *how* data, voice, and video signals travel from the source to the destination across shared communication media. Since a single physical transmission link (copper wire, optical fiber, or radio channel) cannot be permanently dedicated to a single pair of users, the network must intelligently "switch" or route the signal between multiple users on a *time-shared* or *demand-shared* basis. The two classical paradigms governing this sharing are **Circuit Switching** and **Packet Switching**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> **Switching** is the technique used in telecommunications networks to establish, maintain, and release dedicated or shared communication paths between endpoints so that information can be transmitted efficiently across a shared transmission medium. The two principal switching techniques employed in telemedicine networks are **Circuit Switching** (connection-oriented, reservation-based) and **Packet Switching** (connectionless/stateless, store-and-forward based).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine two different ways of holding a conversation across a crowded office:

- **Circuit Switching Analogy (Reserved Highway Lane):** Think of a private, reserved lane on a busy highway. Before you start driving, you call the highway authority, they close off a lane just for you from your starting city to your destination city. No one else can use that lane until you finish your journey and explicitly release it. Even if your car breaks down midway (no data to send), the lane stays empty but reserved. **This is Circuit Switching** — once a path is set up, the entire bandwidth is yours for the duration of the call, regardless of whether you use it continuously.

- **Packet Switching Analogy (Postal Letters):** Now imagine writing your entire long message on a stack of small postcards, each carrying a few sentences along with the recipient's address. You drop all the postcards into the local post office. Each post office independently decides the next routing hop for each card. Some postcards may travel by air, some by train, and they may arrive at the destination in *random order*. The recipient reassembles them in correct sequence. **This is Packet Switching** — your message is broken into independent small units (packets), each finding its own way across a shared network.

### 1.3 Why These Concepts Matter in Telemedicine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight – KTU 2024 Module 2 Significance**
> The choice of switching technique directly impacts:
> 1. **Quality of Service (QoS)** for real-time applications (e.g., remote surgery, live auscultation).
> 2. **Bandwidth efficiency** when transmitting large medical image files (DICOM, MRI, CT scans).
> 3. **Latency and jitter** for ECG/EHR synchronization.
> 4. **Cost and scalability** of rural telemedicine deployments using PSTN, ISDN, MPLS, or 4G/5G backbones.

### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics Used in This Module

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Bandwidth** | $R$ | bits per second (bps) |
| **Propagation Speed** | $s$ | meters/second ($\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in fiber) |
| **Packet Length** | $L$ | bits |
| **Propagation Distance** | $d$ | meters or kilometers |
| **Traffic Intensity** | $\rho$ | dimensionless (Erlangs) |
| **Number of Users** | $N$ | dimensionless |
| **Standard Voice Bandwidth** | $B_{voice}$ | **4 kHz** (PSTN) |
| **Standard Data Rate** | $R_{data}$ | kbps to Gbps |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Switching — The Reservation Paradigm

Circuit switching was the dominant technique of the **Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN)** for nearly a century and remains the foundation of legacy telemedicine links (e.g., plain-old telephone service based tele-ECG machines, fax-based referrals). In circuit switching, the system dedicates a *physical* or *logical* path end-to-end before any user data is exchanged.

### 2.1.1 The Three Mandatory Phases of a Circuit-Switched Call

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Phases of Circuit Switching (High-Yield Concept)**
> 1. **Connection Establishment Phase:** The source sends a setup/request signal. Intermediate switches (exchanges) reserve a dedicated path all the way to the destination. A confirmation is sent back. *(Duration: typically 0.5 – 5 seconds in PSTN).*
> 2. **Data Transfer Phase:** The two endpoints exchange information at the *full reserved bandwidth*. The path is held regardless of traffic (idle time still consumes resources).
> 3. **Connection Teardown Phase:** A teardown signal releases all reserved resources along the path.

### 2.1.2 Multiplexing Methods Inside Circuit Switching

Since each circuit-switched call only *partially* utilizes the available bandwidth, multiple calls are multiplexed onto a single high-capacity trunk using:

- **Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM):** The available spectrum is divided into non-overlapping frequency bands; each call is assigned one band (e.g., 4 kHz per voice channel in PSTN).
- **Time Division Multiplexing (TDM):** The time axis is divided into fixed frames, each frame containing $N$ time slots. Each call gets one time slot per frame. TDM itself has two sub-types:
  - **Synchronous TDM (STDM):** Every user gets a slot *every* frame, even if silent (wasteful).
  - **Asynchronous/Statistical TDM:** Slots are allocated *on demand* (this is essentially a transitional form toward packet switching).

### 2.1.3 Blocking in Circuit-Switched Networks

When all circuits between two exchanges are occupied, a new call request is **blocked** (rejected with a "busy" signal). The probability of blocking is governed by **Erlang-B** or **Erlang-C** formulas, which the KTU examiner often expects students to know at a conceptual level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Drawback of Circuit Switching for Telemedicine:**
> Inefficient for **bursty medical data** (e.g., a DICOM image is sent once, then the link is silent). Resources remain reserved even during silence.

### 2.1.4 Standard Telemedicine Use Cases for Circuit Switching
- Real-time voice consultations on landline/PSTN.
- Live tele-auscultation with reserved 4 kHz analog channel.
- ISDN-based (Integrated Services Digital Network) tele-radiology at 64 kbps or 128 kbps (H.320 standard).
- Fax transmission of prescriptions and medical reports.

## 2.2 Packet Switching — The Store-and-Forward Paradigm

Packet switching is the foundational technique of the modern Internet, IP-based WANs, 4G/5G core networks, and most contemporary telemedicine backbones. Instead of reserving a path, the message is split into small units called **packets** (each typically 64 bytes to 1500 bytes), and *each packet is routed independently*.

### 2.2.1 The Three Sub-Categories of Packet Switching

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Sub-Techniques of Packet Switching (Examiner-Favourite Question)**
> 1. **Datagram Packet Switching (Connectionless):**
>    - Each packet is routed independently (e.g., IPv4, IPv6).
>    - Packets may take *different* paths and arrive *out of order*.
>    - No setup phase; sending begins immediately.
>    - Stateless routers make forwarding decisions per packet.
> 2. **Virtual Circuit Packet Switching (Connection-Oriented):**
>    - A logical path (virtual circuit) is established first (e.g., ATM, Frame Relay, MPLS, X.25).
>    - All packets of a session follow the *same* logical path, identified by a **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)**.
>    - Two flavours: **Switched Virtual Circuit (SVC)** – established per call; **Permanent Virtual Circuit (PVC)** – pre-provisioned.
> 3. **Message Switching (Store-and-Forward, Hop-by-Hop):**
>    - The *entire message* is stored at each intermediate node, then forwarded.
>    - Used in legacy telegraph networks and some store-and-forward email systems.
>    - High delay, large buffer requirement.

### 2.2.2 The Four Sources of Delay in Packet Switching

When a packet travels from source to destination through a router, it experiences four cumulative delays:

1. **Processing Delay ($d_{proc}$):** Time to examine the header, check for errors, and decide the outgoing link. Typically **microseconds**.
2. **Queuing Delay ($d_{queue}$):** Time waiting in the router's buffer (queue) for its turn to be transmitted. Depends on traffic congestion; can be **milliseconds** to seconds.
3. **Transmission Delay ($d_{trans}$):** Time to push all bits of the packet onto the link. Given by $L/R$.
4. **Propagation Delay ($d_{prop}$):** Time for one bit to travel from sender to receiver across the physical medium. Given by $d/s$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Nodal Delay:** $d_{nodal} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}$

### 2.2.3 Standard Telemedicine Use Cases for Packet Switching
- Internet-based video consultation (WebRTC, Zoom, Teams).
- Transmission of DICOM medical images over LAN/WAN.
- Cloud-based Electronic Health Record (EHR) exchange (HL7 over TCP/IP).
- 4G/5G-based remote patient monitoring telemetry.
- Store-and-forward teledermatology (image sent in packets, reviewed asynchronously).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula | Description | Unit / Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transmission Delay | $d_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Time to push $L$ bits at rate $R$ | seconds |
| 2 | Propagation Delay | $d_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{s}$ | Time for signal to traverse distance $d$ | seconds |
| 3 | Total Nodal Delay | $d_{nodal} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}$ | End-to-end single-node delay | seconds |
| 4 | End-to-End Delay (Q routers) | $d_{end} = Q \cdot \left(d_{proc} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}\right) + d_{queue}^{total}$ | Sum of delays across $Q$ hops | seconds |
| 5 | Traffic Intensity | $\rho = \dfrac{L \cdot a}{R}$ | $a$ = average packet arrival rate | $\rho < 1$ for stability |
| 6 | Throughput (single link) | $Throughput = \min(R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n)$ | Bottleneck link rate | bps |
| 7 | Number of TDM slots | $N_{slots} = \dfrac{T_{frame}}{T_{slot}}$ | Slots per TDM frame | dimensionless |
| 8 | Bandwidth of TDM user | $R_{user} = \dfrac{R_{link}}{N_{slots}}$ | Each TDM user gets | bps |
| 9 | Blocking Probability (Erlang-B) | $B(C, \rho) = \dfrac{\rho^C / C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} \rho^k / k!}$ | $C$ = number of circuits, $\rho$ = offered traffic | $0 \leq B \leq 1$ |
| 10 | Number of packets in message | $n = \dfrac{L_{message}}{L_{packet}}$ | Message fragmentation count | integer $\geq 1$ |
| 11 | Statistical Multiplexing Gain | $G = \dfrac{N \cdot R_{peak}}{R_{link}}$ | Efficiency gain over TDM | $G > 1$ |
| 12 | Voice Call Bandwidth (PSTN) | $B_{voice} = \mathbf{4 \text{ kHz}}$ | Standard analog voice channel | Hz |
| 13 | Prop. speed in fiber | $s = \mathbf{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$ | Standard reference value | m/s |
| 14 | Prop. speed in copper | $s \approx \mathbf{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$ | Slightly higher than fiber | m/s |
| 15 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = R \times d_{prop}$ | Bits "in flight" on a link | bits |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Telemedicine Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU Asks This Topic — Industry Relevance**
> - **Tele-cardiology:** Real-time ECG streaming is jitter-sensitive → favours circuit switching or *QoS-aware* packet switching (MPLS, DiffServ).
> - **Teleradiology:** Burst of large DICOM files → clearly favours packet switching with statistical multiplexing.
> - **Mobile Telemedicine Units (MTUs):** Often use 4G/5G packet-switched core, but with reserved bearers (QoS) to mimic circuit behaviour.
> - **Disaster Telemedicine:** Satellite backhoes (VSAT) use a mix of circuit emulation and packet switching (BGAN terminals).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: End-to-End Delay in a Multi-Hop Packet-Switched Telemedicine Network

**Problem Setup (Typical KTU Numerical):**
A telemedicine data packet of length $L = 8000$ bits is transmitted from a rural Primary Health Centre (PHC) to a super-specialty hospital $d = 4000$ km away. The link bandwidth is $R = 2$ Mbps. The packet traverses $Q = 4$ routers. Processing delay at each router is $d_{proc} = 2$ ms, and queuing delay is $d_{queue} = 3$ ms per router. Propagation speed is $s = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in the optical fiber. Compute the **total end-to-end delay**.

### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1: Identify all delay components at one node.**

At each intermediate router, the packet experiences:
- Processing delay: $d_{proc}$
- Queuing delay: $d_{queue}$
- Transmission delay: $d_{trans} = L/R$
- Propagation delay on the outgoing link: $d_{prop} = d_{link} / s$

**Step 2: Compute the transmission delay (push bits onto the link).**

$$
d_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{8000 \text{ bits}}{2 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Logic:** Dividing 8000 bits by 2 million bits per second gives 0.004 seconds, which is 4 ms. Each bit is clocked out every $\frac{1}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.5 \, \mu s$.

**Step 3: Compute the propagation delay over the given distance.**

$$
d_{prop} = \frac{d}{s} = \frac{4000 \times 10^3 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Logic:** Optical signals travel at roughly 2/3 the speed of light in glass fiber. For 4000 km, this yields 20 ms one-way.

**Step 4: Compute single-node (per-router) delay.**

$$
d_{node} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}
$$

$$
d_{node} = 2 \text{ ms} + 3 \text{ ms} + 4 \text{ ms} + 20 \text{ ms} = 29 \text{ ms per router}
$$

> **Logic:** Sum all four delay sources for a single router.

**Step 5: Account for the source and final link (no router processing at endpoints).**

In the KTU convention, the source experiences transmission + propagation (no processing/queuing since it is the originator), and the destination experiences only the final propagation + reception. However, for a clean conservative model with $Q$ routers, total end-to-end delay is:

$$
d_{end} = Q \cdot d_{proc} + Q \cdot d_{queue} + (Q+1) \cdot d_{trans} + (Q+1) \cdot d_{prop}
$$

> **Logic:** The packet is transmitted $(Q+1)$ times (once onto each of the $Q+1$ links: source-to-R1, R1-to-R2, …, RQ-to-destination). It is processed/queued at each of the $Q$ routers.

Substituting $Q = 4$:

$$
d_{end} = 4 \cdot 2 \text{ ms} + 4 \cdot 3 \text{ ms} + 5 \cdot 4 \text{ ms} + 5 \cdot 20 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
d_{end} = 8 \text{ ms} + 12 \text{ ms} + 20 \text{ ms} + 100 \text{ ms} = 140 \text{ ms}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{d_{end} = 140 \text{ ms}}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 140 ms one-way delay is acceptable for store-and-forward telemedicine (e.g., DICOM upload), but borderline for two-way real-time consultation (recommended < 150 ms one-way for natural conversation).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bandwidth Allocation in Synchronous TDM (Circuit-Switched Voice Backbone)

**Problem Setup:**
A TDM-based telemedicine backbone carries $N = 24$ voice channels. The link rate is $R_{link} = 1.544$ Mbps (standard T1 line). The frame duration is $T_{frame} = 125 \, \mu s$. Compute: (a) Number of bits per frame, (b) Number of bits per slot, (c) Bandwidth per voice user.

### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1: Compute total number of bits in one TDM frame.**

$$
L_{frame} = R_{link} \times T_{frame} = 1.544 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} \times 125 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}
$$

$$
L_{frame} = 1.544 \times 125 = 193 \text{ bits per frame}
$$

> **Logic:** Total bits transmitted in 125 microseconds at 1.544 Mbps.

**Step 2: Compute bits per time slot (per voice channel).**

$$
L_{slot} = \frac{L_{frame}}{N} = \frac{193 \text{ bits}}{24} \approx 8.04 \text{ bits/slot}
$$

> **Logic:** With 24 users sharing one frame, each user gets 193/24 ≈ 8 bits per frame.

**Step 3: Compute effective bandwidth per voice user.**

$$
R_{user} = \frac{L_{slot}}{T_{frame}} = \frac{8.04 \text{ bits}}{125 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}} \approx 64.3 \text{ kbps}
$$

> **Logic:** Each user effectively gets 64 kbps — exactly the standard PCM voice rate (8 bits × 8000 samples/s).

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{R_{user} \approx 64 \text{ kbps per voice channel}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Traffic Intensity & Queuing Behaviour

**Problem Setup:**
A telemedicine gateway router receives packets at average rate $a = 500$ packets/s. Each packet has length $L = 12000$ bits. The outgoing link rate is $R = 10$ Mbps. Compute the traffic intensity $\rho$ and comment on the router's behaviour.

### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1: Write the traffic intensity formula.**

$$
\rho = \frac{L \cdot a}{R}
$$

> **Logic:** $\rho$ is the ratio of bits arriving per second to bits transmitted per second. If $\rho \geq 1$, the queue grows unbounded.

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
\rho = \frac{12000 \times 500}{10 \times 10^6} = \frac{6 \times 10^6}{10 \times 10^6} = 0.6
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.**

Since $\rho = 0.6 < 1$, the system is **stable** — the queue will not grow infinitely. The average queuing delay under an M/M/1 model would be:

$$
d_{queue} = \frac{\rho}{R \cdot (1 - \rho)} = \frac{0.6}{10^7 \cdot 0.4} = 1.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 0.15 \, \mu s
$$

> **Logic:** Low traffic intensity → minimal queuing delay. For real-time telemedicine (ECG), we want $\rho < 0.5$ to keep latency low.

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\rho = 0.6, \text{ system is stable.}}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Circuit vs Packet Switching

| Parameter | Circuit Switching | Datagram Packet Switching | Virtual Circuit Packet Switching |
|---|---|---|---|
| Connection setup | Required | None | Required |
| Path | Fixed end-to-end | Per-packet independent | Fixed logical (VCI) |
| Resource reservation | Continuous | Per-packet, statistical | Logical reservation |
| Delay | Constant after setup | Variable (jitter) | Variable but bounded |
| Order of delivery | In-order | May be out-of-order | In-order |
| Reliability (link failure) | Path lost; redial | Robust (alternate routes) | Path lost; new VC needed |
| Bandwidth efficiency | Low for bursty | High | High |
| State in switches | Per-call | Connectionless (stateless) | Per-VCI |
| Typical technology | PSTN, ISDN | IP, UDP | ATM, MPLS, Frame Relay, X.25 |
| Telemedicine fit | Real-time voice | Best-effort data | QoS-sensitive medical video |
| Billing | Per minute (duration) | Per packet (volume) | Per VC + volume |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Three Phases of Circuit Switching (Call Lifecycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source: PHC Doctor] -->|Setup Request| B[Local Exchange]
    B -->|Reserve Trunk 1| C[Transit Exchange 1]
    C -->|Reserve Trunk 2| D[Transit Exchange 2]
    D -->|Reserve Trunk 3| E[Destination Exchange]
    E -->|Setup Acknowledge| A
    A -->|Data Transfer Begins| F[Full Bandwidth Reserved Channel]
    F -->|Bidirectional Voice/Video| G[Destination: Specialist Hospital]
    G -->|Teardown Signal| E
    E -->|Release Trunk 3| D
    D -->|Release Trunk 2| C
    C -->|Release Trunk 1| B
    B -->|Confirm Release| A
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The setup phase cascades reserve resources from source to destination. The data transfer phase is a steady dedicated channel. The teardown phase propagates in reverse, releasing every link.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Datagram Packet Switching (Connectionless, Independent Routing)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PHC Sender] -->|Packet 1| R1[Router 1]
    P1 -->|Packet 2| R1
    P1 -->|Packet 3| R1
    R1 -->|Packet 1 Path A| R2[Router 2]
    R1 -->|Packet 2 Path B| R3[Router 3]
    R1 -->|Packet 3 Path A| R2
    R2 --> R4[Router 4]
    R3 --> R4
    R4 --> H[Hospital Destination]
    R4 --> H
    R4 --> H
    H -->|Reassemble in Sequence Number| HFINAL[Ordered DICOM Image]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice Packet 1 and Packet 3 travel via Router 2, but Packet 2 takes a different route via Router 3. They may arrive out of order, and the destination reassembles them using sequence numbers in the IP header.

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Virtual Circuit Packet Switching (Connection-Oriented, Same Logical Path)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Source PHC] -->|VC Setup VCI 45| N1[Node 1]
    N1 -->|VCI 67| N2[Node 2]
    N2 -->|VCI 89| N3[Node 3]
    N3 -->|VCI 12| D[Destination Hospital]
    S -->|All Packets VCI 45| N1
    N1 -->|All Packets VCI 67| N2
    N2 -->|All Packets VCI 89| N3
    N3 -->|All Packets VCI 12| D
    D -->|VC Teardown| N3
    N3 -->|Teardown| N2
    N2 -->|Teardown| N1
    N1 -->|Teardown| S
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** A logical virtual circuit is established once (top path), then all subsequent packets reuse the same VC identifiers (bottom path). The physical path may change inside the network, but the VCI chain remains identical.

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Functional Block Architecture of a Telemedicine Switching Node

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTSIDE[Input Trunk Links]
        L1[Trunk A: 64 kbps Voice]
        L2[Trunk B: 2 Mbps Data]
        L3[Trunk C: 4G LTE Bearer]
    end
    INPUTSIDE --> MUX[Multiplexer / Demultiplexer]
    MUX --> SWCORE[Switching Fabric Core]
    SWCORE --> CTRL[Control Processor]
    CTRL -->|Routing Table Updates| SWCORE
    SWCORE --> OUT1[Output Trunk to Hospital Network]
    SWCORE --> OUT2[Output to PSTN Gateway]
    SWCORE --> OUT3[Output to Internet IP Backbone]
    CTRL --> MGT[Network Management System NMS]
    MGT -->|SNMP / Telemetry| CTRL
    MGT -->|QoS Policy| CTRL
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** A real telemedicine switching node has input trunks of mixed media, a centralized switching fabric controlled by a control processor, and multiple output gateways. The NMS handles QoS policy, monitoring, and fault management.

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Decision Flow — When to Use Circuit vs Packet Switching in Telemedicine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: New Telemedicine Service] --> Q1{Is the traffic real-time and continuous?}
    Q1 -->|Yes Voice or Live Video| Q2{Is bandwidth constant and predictable?}
    Q1 -->|No Data Files Emails Records| PKT1[Use Packet Switching IP]
    Q2 -->|Yes| CIR[Use Circuit Switching or Reserved QoS Bearer]
    Q2 -->|No Variable| PKT2[Use Packet Switching with QoS MPLS or DiffServ]
    PKT1 --> END[Deploy IP VPN over Telemedicine Backbone]
    CIR --> END
    PKT2 --> END
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** This is a practical engineering decision tree. Real-time, continuous traffic prefers circuit-style reserved bearers; bursty or asynchronous traffic strongly prefers packet switching with appropriate QoS.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. Define circuit switching. List its three phases. **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Circuit switching is a connection-oriented switching technique in which a dedicated communication path is established between the source and destination *before* actual data transfer begins, and the path is held for the entire duration of the session. The three phases are:
1. **Connection Establishment** – A setup signal reserves resources end-to-end.
2. **Data Transfer** – Information flows over the dedicated path at full reserved bandwidth.
3. **Connection Teardown** – A teardown signal releases all reserved resources.

*(Award 1 mark per phase; full marks for clear definition + 3 phases.)*

### Question A2. Differentiate between datagram packet switching and virtual circuit packet switching. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Datagram | Virtual Circuit |
|---|---|---|
| Connection setup | Not required | Required |
| Path for packets | Independent per packet | Same logical path for all packets |
| Ordering | Packets may arrive out of order | Packets arrive in order |
| Identifier | Source/destination address | Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI) |
| Example | IPv4/IPv6 networks | ATM, MPLS, X.25 |

*(Award 1 mark for setup distinction, 1 mark for path/routing distinction, 1 mark for example pair.)*

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> The KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) gives an internal choice within each module. You must answer either **Question A** or **Question B** below.

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the architecture and operation of circuit switching, with a block diagram. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages for telemedicine applications.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step, 7 Marks):**

**(i) Definition (1 Mark):** Circuit switching is a switching method that establishes a dedicated physical or logical path (called a *circuit*) between two endpoints for the duration of communication, typically used in the PSTN and ISDN.

**(ii) Three Phases (2 Marks):**
- **Setup Phase:** Source sends a request; each switching exchange along the route reserves a trunk. Acknowledge returns to source.
- **Data Transfer Phase:** Continuous bidirectional transfer at full reserved capacity.
- **Teardown Phase:** End-of-session signal propagates back, releasing all trunks.

**(iii) Multiplexing (1 Mark):** Uses FDM (frequency slots) or TDM (time slots). In TDM, $N$ users share a frame; each user gets $\frac{1}{N}$ of the bandwidth.

**(iv) Advantages for Telemedicine (1.5 Marks):**
- Constant bandwidth → low jitter, ideal for real-time voice auscultation and live surgery feeds.
- Predictable latency; no queuing delay.
- Simple hardware, robust to congestion (blocking only at setup).

**(v) Disadvantages for Telemedicine (1.5 Marks):**
- Inefficient for bursty medical data (DICOM, EHR sync).
- Resources idle during silence (wasted bandwidth).
- Long setup time unsuitable for quick emergency teleconsultations.
- Difficult to scale in mobile telemedicine units.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A telemedicine network uses packet switching. A packet of length 8000 bits travels through 3 routers. Each link has bandwidth 1 Mbps. Distance between each router is 2000 km. Propagation speed is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Processing delay = 1 ms, queuing delay = 2 ms per router. Compute total end-to-end delay.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step, 7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Compute transmission delay.

$$
d_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{8000}{1 \times 10^6} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 8 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compute propagation delay per link.

$$
d_{prop} = \frac{d}{s} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Compute single-node delay.

$$
d_{node} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop} = 1 + 2 + 8 + 10 = 21 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Sum over all nodes. With $Q = 3$ routers, the packet is transmitted $(Q+1) = 4$ times and propagates over 4 links. Processing/queuing happen 3 times.

$$
d_{end} = 3 \cdot d_{proc} + 3 \cdot d_{queue} + 4 \cdot d_{trans} + 4 \cdot d_{prop}
$$

$$
d_{end} = 3(1) + 3(2) + 4(8) + 4(10) = 3 + 6 + 32 + 40 = 81 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Engineering interpretation.

$$
\boxed{d_{end} = 81 \text{ ms}}
$$

This is well within ITU-T G.114's 150 ms one-way delay limit, making it suitable for real-time telemedicine voice/video.

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Comment — if packet size were reduced by half (4000 bits), transmission delay would halve to 4 ms, total delay dropping to 65 ms. Hence for latency-sensitive telemedicine, smaller packet sizes are preferable (subject to header overhead trade-off).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With neat diagrams, explain datagram packet switching and virtual circuit packet switching. Compare their suitability for telemedicine.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step, 7 Marks):**

**(i) Datagram Packet Switching (3 Marks):**
- *Definition:* Connectionless switching where each packet is routed independently based on destination address. No fixed path; no setup.
- *Operation:* Source breaks message into packets. Each packet carries full source/destination address. Routers consult routing table per packet. Packets may take different routes and arrive out of order; destination reassembles via sequence numbers.
- *Example:* IPv4 Internet.

**(ii) Virtual Circuit Packet Switching (3 Marks):**
- *Definition:* Connection-oriented switching where a logical path (virtual circuit) is established before data transfer, identified by a VCI/DLCI.
- *Operation:* Setup phase establishes VCI chain across all nodes. All subsequent packets carry only the VCI, not full addresses. Packets arrive in order. Two types: **SVC** (per-call) and **PVC** (permanent).
- *Example:* ATM, MPLS, Frame Relay.

**(iii) Suitability Comparison for Telemedicine (1 Mark):**
- **Datagram** → Best for store-and-forward telemedicine (DICOM upload, EHR sync, e-mail referrals); resilient to link failures.
- **Virtual Circuit** → Best for real-time telemedicine (live surgery, tele-ICU video); guaranteed in-order delivery and predictable QoS.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A TDM-based telemedicine backbone uses 32 time slots per frame. Frame duration = 125 µs. Link rate = 2.048 Mbps. (i) Calculate the number of bits per frame. (ii) Bits per slot. (iii) Bandwidth per user. (iv) Comment on whether this is suitable for HD telemedicine video.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step, 7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Total bits per frame.

$$
L_{frame} = R_{link} \times T_{frame} = 2.048 \times 10^6 \times 125 \times 10^{-6} = 256 \text{ bits/frame}
$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Bits per slot.

$$
L_{slot} = \frac{L_{frame}}{N_{slots}} = \frac{256}{32} = 8 \text{ bits/slot}
$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Bandwidth per user.

$$
R_{user} = \frac{L_{slot}}{T_{frame}} = \frac{8 \text{ bits}}{125 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}} = 64{,}000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Suitability for HD telemedicine video.

- HD video typically requires **2 Mbps to 6 Mbps** depending on codec (H.264/H.265) and resolution.
- 64 kbps is suitable only for **voice-grade telemedicine** (telephone consultation, auscultation).
- **Conclusion:** This TDM link is **NOT suitable for HD telemedicine video**; multiple users (≥ 32 simultaneous) would have to *bond* their slots, or a packet-switched IP/MPLS backbone should be used instead.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read Carefully!)**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting the propagation speed unit conversion ($2 \times 10^8$ m/s vs $3 \times 10^8$ m/s for free space vs fiber). KTU accepts $2 \times 10^8$ for fiber.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In delay problems, students often forget to multiply propagation delay by the **number of links ($Q+1$)**, not the number of routers ($Q$).
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In TDM problems, mixing up *frame duration* with *slot duration*. The frame duration is fixed (125 µs in PCM), the slot duration is frame duration ÷ number of users.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing **datagram** (no setup) with **virtual circuit** (setup required). Examiners give *zero credit* for these swapped answers.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Writing "packet switching is faster than circuit switching" — this is a *vague, half-true* statement. Packet switching has *lower* setup delay but *higher* variable queuing delay. Use the precise terms.
> - **Common Mistake 6:** Failing to label diagrams with arrows showing direction. KTU deducts 1–2 marks for unlabelled arrows in the ESE.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Read This 5 Minutes Before the Exam**

- **Switching** is the *core* mechanism for sharing a transmission medium in any telemedicine network.
- **Circuit Switching:** connection-oriented, dedicated path, three phases (setup → transfer → teardown), used in PSTN/ISDN, FDM/TDM multiplexing, low jitter but bandwidth-inefficient.
- **Packet Switching:** three sub-types — *Datagram* (connectionless, IP), *Virtual Circuit* (connection-oriented, ATM/MPLS), *Message* (legacy, store-and-forward, hop-by-hop).
- **Four Delays:** $d_{proc}$, $d_{queue}$, $d_{trans} = L/R$, $d_{prop} = d/s$. Always sum all four for nodal delay.
- **End-to-end delay** multiplies router delays by $Q$ and link delays by $(Q+1)$.
- **Traffic intensity** $\rho = La/R$. Stability requires $\rho < 1$.
- **TDM basics:** $L_{frame} = R_{link} \cdot T_{frame}$; $L_{slot} = L_{frame}/N$; $R_{user} = L_{slot}/T_{frame} = R_{link}/N$.
- **Voice channel** in TDM is 4 kHz analog or 64 kbps PCM.
- **Propagation speed** in fiber/copper ≈ $2 \times 10^8$ m/s; in free space = $3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- **Telemedicine mapping:** Real-time voice/video → circuit-style reserved bearer (or packet with QoS); DICOM/EHR/email → pure packet switching.
- **Erlang-B formula** is the standard for circuit-switched blocking probability.
- **Datagram** routes per packet; **Virtual Circuit** routes per logical connection.
- **Datagram advantage:** resilient, no single point of failure. **Virtual Circuit advantage:** ordered delivery, predictable latency.
- **5G and modern telemedicine** use **packet-switched core (5GC)** with optional **QoS flows** that emulate circuit behavior for ultra-low-latency applications.
- **Always** include units in numerical answers; KTU deducts marks for unitless numbers.
- **Always** draw a labelled diagram in any 7-mark answer involving switching — it carries 1–2 free marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: H.320 Series (Video Phone-based ISDN) and T.120
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT861 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** H.320 Series (Video Phone-based ISDN) and T.120

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 H.320 Series — Formal Definition

The **H.320** standard, formally titled *"Narrow-band visual telephone systems and terminal equipment"*, is a family of ITU-T Recommendations defining the complete architecture, protocols, and codecs for real-time **audio, video, and data** communication over **circuit-switched digital networks**, most notably the **Integrated Services Digital Network (ISDN)**. The umbrella standard was first approved in **1990** and revised through 2004, serving as the foundational specification for the first generation of commercial video conferencing systems used in telemedicine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **H.320 is the "Grandfather" Standard of Video Telephony** — It is the parent recommendation under which sub-standards (H.221, H.230, H.242, H.261, H.263, G.7xx) are coordinated to deliver an interoperable narrowband audiovisual terminal.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Multi-Lane Digital Highway"

Imagine ISDN as a **multi-lane expressway** where each lane (called a **"B channel"**) can carry a fixed **64 kbps** of digital information. The H.320 standard is essentially the **"set of traffic rules"** governing how many lanes each video call may use, what type of vehicles (video, audio, data packets) may travel on which lane, how lanes are merged into a single synchronized stream, and how two cars approaching from opposite directions signal their capabilities to each other before establishing the call. In telemedicine, this highway enabled the first real-time remote specialist consultations across cities using only the pre-existing copper telephone wires.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Numerical Anchors (BOLD as required):**
> - **B Channel bandwidth = 64 kbps**
> - **D Channel (signaling) = 16 kbps** (BRI) or **64 kbps** (PRI)
> - **BRI = 2B + D (144 kbps total, 128 kbps usable)**
> - **PRI (E1) = 30B + D (2.048 Mbps)**
> - **H.320 nominal bit rate = p × 64 kbps, where p = 1, 2, 3, …, 30**
> - **H.261 codec supports CIF (352×288) and QCIF (176×144)** at **p × 64 kbps**

### 1.3 T.120 Series — Formal Definition

The **T.120** family of ITU-T Recommendations defines the **real-time multipoint data communication** protocol stack used for collaborative applications such as **shared whiteboards, file transfer, image annotation, and application sharing** in audiovisual conferences. T.120 is **media-independent** — it can ride on top of H.320 (ISDN), H.323 (IP/LAN), or H.324 (PSTN/analog) networks, providing a uniform data-conferencing layer across all three. T.120 was first published in **1993** and is now most often invoked together with H.320 in a telemedicine session to enable shared viewing of radiological images, ECG strips, or electronic patient records during a live video consultation.

> [!NOTE]
> **T.120 = "The Side Channel That Makes Telemedicine Truly Collaborative."** While H.320 carries the live voice and video, T.120 carries the annotated documents, transferred DICOM images, and shared cursor movements that allow a remote cardiologist to point at an ECG and say "look at this lead."

### 1.4 Why These Two Standards Are Studied Together in KTU Telemedicine

H.320 supplies the **audiovisual transport** over ISDN, while T.120 supplies the **collaborative data layer** that allows clinicians to view and mark the same medical image simultaneously. Together they formed the **dominant telemedicine platform of the 1990s and early 2000s** in India, deployed for ISRO's village resource centres, Apollo Hospitals' remote consultations, and the SGPGI telemedicine network linking tertiary hospitals to rural primary health centres.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The H.320 Protocol Family — Layered Architecture

H.320 is not a single recommendation but an **orchestrated family** in which each sub-standard performs a specific role. Mastering these roles is essential for KTU questions asking *"List the components of the H.320 family and their function."*

| Sub-Standard | Functional Role | KTU High-Yield Description |
|---|---|---|
| **H.320** | Umbrella standard | Overall system, terminal, and network requirements for narrowband AVT (Audiovisual Terminals) on ISDN |
| **H.221** | Frame structure | Provides the **80-bit framing pattern** that synchronizes B channels carrying 64–1920 kbps of multiplexed audio, video, and data |
| **H.230** | Control & Indication (C&I) | Defines **out-of-band control bits** (BAS — Bit-rate Allocation Signal) embedded in H.221 frames for capabilities exchange, multipoint commands, and encryption |
| **H.242** | Point-to-point signalling | Establishes terminal capabilities, mode preferences, and master–slave determination between **two** endpoints |
| **H.243** | Multipoint signalling | Extends H.242 to **three or more** terminals using a Multipoint Control Unit (MCU) |
| **H.261** | Video codec (px64) | The mandatory first-generation video codec; **mandatory CIF/QCIF** support; uses intraframe + interframe (motion-compensated) coding |
| **H.263** | Video codec (enhanced) | Successor providing better quality at low bit rates; **optional** in H.320; supports more resolutions (Sub-QCIF, 4CIF, 16CIF) |
| **G.711, G.722, G.728** | Audio codecs | G.711 (3.5 kHz, 64 kbps), G.722 (7 kHz wideband, 48/56/64 kbps), G.728 (LD-CELP, 16 kbps) |
| **H.224 / H.281** | Far-end camera control | Allows one site to steer the other site's camera (FECC — Far End Camera Control); **essential in telemedicine** for remote examination |
| **H.233, H.234, H.235** | Security & encryption | Confidentiality, integrity, and authentication services for the H.320 session |
| **T.120 family** | Data conferencing | Shared workspace, whiteboard, file transfer, image annotation |

### 2.2 The px64 kbps Formula — H.320's Signature Equation

The video bit-rate allocation in H.320 is governed by the canonical expression:

$$B_{video} = p \times 64 \text{ kbps}$$

where **p** is an integer from 1 to 30, representing the number of ISDN B channels bonded together. The total session bit-rate is allocated roughly as:

$$B_{total} = p \times 64 \text{ kbps} = B_{video} + B_{audio} + B_{data(T.120)} + B_{overhead(H.221)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World H.320 Bit-Rate Selection for Telemedicine:**
> - **p = 1 (64 kbps):** Voice-grade video, barely usable for clinical assessment.
> - **p = 2 (128 kbps):** "Rolodex-style" face-to-face consultation, the **ISDN BRI ceiling**.
> - **p = 6 (384 kbps):** Standard business video conferencing quality; **minimum recommended for telemedicine**.
> - **p = 12 (768 kbps):** Acceptable for dermatology and visible lesion examination.
> - **p = 30 (1920 kbps):** Full E1 PRI channel, broadcast-quality; **ideal but expensive** for telepathology and high-resolution image transfer.

### 2.3 The T.120 Protocol Stack — Layered Analysis

T.120 is itself an umbrella that orchestrates **eight tightly-coupled sub-recommendations**, each implementing one tier of the data-conferencing service:

| T-Series Sub-Standard | Functional Role | KTU High-Yield Description |
|---|---|---|
| **T.120** | Umbrella | Data protocols for multimedia conferencing — overall architecture |
| **T.121** | Application templates | Defines generic application service elements (ASEs) for any T.120 application |
| **T.122** | Multipoint Service (MCS) | Connection-oriented, reliable multipoint data delivery; the **heart** of T.120 |
| **T.123** | Network-specific transport profiles | Maps T.120 onto the underlying network — defines **T.123 over ISDN**, **T.123 over TCP/IP**, and **T.123 over PSTN** |
| **T.124** | Generic Conference Control (GCC) | Manages conference creation, joining, leaving, address-book, and roster services |
| **T.125** | MCS protocol specification | Detailed MCS protocol — domain creation, token management, uniform data delivery |
| **T.126** | Still Image Annotation | Multiparty shared whiteboard and image annotation with pointers; **used to mark X-rays, ECGs** |
| **T.127** | Multiparty Binary File Transfer | Reliable file transfer in a conference; **used to push DICOM images between sites** |
| **T.128** | Audio/Video Control (AVCC) | Bridges T.120 and H.32x so application-sharing can synchronize with the live video stream |

### 2.4 Logical Call-Establishment Sequence in H.320

1. **Physical layer:** ISDN BRI/PRI D-channel signalling (Q.931) sets up B-channel connections.
2. **H.221 frame sync:** Each terminal locks to the 80-bit **Frame Alignment (FA) word** (octet-aligned `0011 0011`) at the start of every 10 ms multiframe.
3. **H.242 capability exchange:** Endpoints transmit their **BAS codes** (capabilities: codecs, data rates, encryption) using the **CAPS** (Capabilities) and **MODES** logical channels inside H.221.
4. **Master–slave determination:** A deterministic "highest address wins" rule elects the master, who allocates the bit budget for video, audio, and T.120 data.
5. **Multiplexing:** The H.221 frame carries **video (I channel), audio (audio channel), T.120 data (data channel), and BAS control (control channel)** in a time-sliced bit budget.
6. **T.120 conference control:** If a data session is requested, T.124's GCC provider creates a *conference domain* and T.125's MCS provider enables multipoint reliable data channels.
7. **Media rendering:** H.261/H.263 decodes incoming video, G.7xx decodes audio, and T.126/T.127 applications render shared documents.

### 2.5 The T.120 / H.320 Interoperability — Why They Pair Naturally

T.120 was deliberately designed to be **transport-agnostic**. In an H.320 telemedicine session, T.120 traffic is carried **inside** the H.221 frame's **data channel**, ensuring that when the H.320 call drops, the T.120 session also terminates cleanly. This is the key engineering reason why early ISDN-based telemedicine kits (PictureTel, Tandberg, VTEL, Polycom ViewStation) shipped with **integrated T.120 stacks** — you never had to dial a separate data call.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> H.320 + T.120 is the **architectural blueprint for any circuit-switched telemedicine deployment** that must operate over the existing Indian Bharat Sanchar Nigam Limited (BSNL) ISDN infrastructure. The two-stage design (H.320 = media plane, T.120 = data plane) is the direct ancestor of every modern WebRTC and SIP-based telemedicine platform.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total H.320 Session Bit-Rate from Bonded B Channels

**Statement:** For a telemedicine session using **N** ISDN B channels, derive the maximum useful video bit-rate assuming standard overhead allocations.

**Step 1 — Identify the available bandwidth per B channel.**
Each ISDN B channel is a DS0 timeslot of fixed capacity:

$$B_{B} = 64 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total session bandwidth for N bonded channels.**

$$B_{total} = N \times B_{B} = N \times 64 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the fixed H.221 framing and BAS overhead.**
The H.221 frame structure dedicates 8 kbps to **BAS (control) channel** and 8 kbps to **H.221 FAS framing overhead** (80 bits every 10 ms multiframe ≈ 8 kbps). For low channel counts, this overhead is significant:

$$B_{control} = 8 \text{ kbps (BAS)} + 8 \text{ kbps (FAS)} = 16 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the audio codec bit-rate.**
The H.320 system typically dedicates one 64 kbps timeslot or a fraction of one to audio:

$$B_{audio} = \begin{cases} 56 \text{ kbps (G.722)} \\ 64 \text{ kbps (G.711)} \\ 16 \text{ kbps (G.728)} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Subtract the T.120 data allocation.**
For a T.120 channel operating at 4.8 / 6.4 / 14.4 / 38.4 kbps via the data sub-channel in H.221:

$$B_{T120} \in \{4.8, 6.4, 14.4, 38.4\} \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the available video bit-rate.**

$$B_{video} = N \times 64 - B_{control} - B_{audio} - B_{T120}$$

$$\boxed{B_{video} = 64N - 16 - B_{audio} - B_{T120} \text{ kbps}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example — 384 kbps (6B) Telemedicine Session:**

$$B_{video} = 64 \times 6 - 16 - 64 - 6.4$$
$$B_{video} = 384 - 16 - 64 - 6.4$$
$$B_{video} = 297.6 \text{ kbps}$$

This is the standard H.261/H.263 budget at 384 kbps — confirmed in ITU-T H.320 reference configurations.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — H.221 Frame Structure Bit Accounting

**Statement:** Compute the H.221 frame length and verify the 80-bit FAS alignment.

**Step 1 — A single B channel carries 8 bits every 125 μs (the standard DS0 frame period).**

$$\text{Bits per second per B channel} = \frac{8 \text{ bits}}{125 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}} = 64{,}000 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 2 — The H.221 multiframe is 10 ms long.** During 10 ms, each B channel delivers:

$$8{,}000 \text{ bits} \times 0.010 = 64{,}000 \text{ bits per second per B channel}$$

**Step 3 — Per single B channel per 10 ms multiframe:**

$$64{,}000 \text{ bits/s} \times 0.010 \text{ s} = 640 \text{ bits per multiframe per B channel}$$

**Step 4 — The Frame Alignment Signal (FAS) is 8 bits (one octet) per B channel per 1 ms sub-multiframe.** Across the 10 sub-multiframes:

$$\text{FAS bits} = 8 \text{ bits} \times 10 = 80 \text{ bits per 10 ms (per B channel)}$$

This matches the canonical "80-bit FAS" value cited in H.221 §3 and is the synchronisation word the receiver scans for to align its demultiplexer.

**Step 5 — Remaining capacity for one B channel after FAS:**

$$640 - 80 = 560 \text{ bits per 10 ms per B channel} = 56 \text{ kbps payload}$$

The H.221 specification uses this 56 kbps to carry either:
- One 56 kbps sub-channel of audio (G.722 at 56 kbps)
- Two sub-channels of 56 kbps and lower
- Video, audio, BAS, and T.120 multiplexed together

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — H.320 vs. T.120 vs. H.323 vs. H.324

| Parameter | H.320 (ISDN) | T.120 (Data) | H.323 (IP/LAN) | H.324 (PSTN) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Network** | Circuit-switched ISDN | Transport-agnostic | Packet-switched IP/Internet | Analog PSTN |
| **Year approved** | 1990 | 1993 | 1996 | 1996 |
| **Typical bit rate** | 64 – 1920 kbps | 4.8 – 38.4 kbps (data) | 64 kbps – several Mbps | Up to 33.6 kbps |
| **Video codec** | H.261 / H.263 | None (data only) | H.261 / H.263 / H.264 | H.263 / H.264 |
| **Audio codec** | G.711, G.722, G.728 | None (data only) | G.711, G.722, G.723, G.729 | G.723.1 |
| **Multipoint** | MCU + H.243 | MCS provider (T.125) | Gatekeeper + MCU | H.324 MCU |
| **Data channel** | Embeds T.120 | Itself | Embeds T.120 (H.323 Annex C) | Embeds T.120 |
| **Telemedicine use** | Legacy ISDN link, still in rural India | Medical image sharing | Modern WebRTC / IP telemedicine | Backup dial-up link |
| **Quality (clinical)** | Good at ≥ 384 kbps | N/A — data only | Best at ≥ 512 kbps | Marginal, diagnostic only |

### 3.4 Quantitative Example — DICOM Image Transfer over T.127

A typical chest X-ray DICOM file is **8 MB (8 × 1024 × 1024 × 8 = 67,108,864 bits)**. Transferring it over a T.127 channel at 38.4 kbps inside a 384 kbps H.320 session:

$$T_{transfer} = \frac{67{,}108{,}864 \text{ bits}}{38{,}400 \text{ bps}} \approx 1747.1 \text{ s} \approx 29.1 \text{ min}$$

This is why **live annotation via T.126 (whiteboard)** is preferred over **file transfer via T.127** in bandwidth-constrained telemedicine — annotation only transfers pointer deltas, not the whole image.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 H.320 Protocol Stack — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["Clinical Application Layer - Telemedicine UI - Electronic Patient Record"] --> A2["T.120 Data Plane - Whiteboard File Transfer DICOM Push"]
    A2 --> A3["H.281 H.224 Far End Camera Control"]
    A3 --> A4["H.261 H.263 Video Codec - px64 kbps"]
    A4 --> A5["G.711 G.722 G.728 Audio Codec"]
    A5 --> A6["H.221 Multiplexer - Frame Structure 64 to 1920 kbps"]
    A6 --> A7["H.230 H.242 H.243 - Capability Exchange Master Slave MCU"]
    A7 --> A8["H.233 H.234 H.235 - Security and Encryption"]
    A8 --> A9["ISDN BRI 2B plus D or PRI 30B plus D Physical Layer"]
    A9 --> A10["ISDN Switched Network - BSNL MTNL Telecom Infrastructure"]
```

### 4.2 T.120 Sub-Standard Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1["Telemedicine Application Layer - Annotation File Transfer"] --> B2["T.126 Still Image Annotation - T.127 Multiparty File Transfer - T.128 AVCC"]
    B2 --> B3["T.121 Generic Application Service Elements ASE"]
    B3 --> B4["T.124 Generic Conference Control GCC - Roster Topologies Permissions"]
    B4 --> B5["T.125 Multipoint Communication Service MCS Protocol"]
    B5 --> B6["T.122 MCS Service Definition"]
    B6 --> B7["T.123 Network Specific Transport Profiles - T.123 over ISDN - T.123 over TCP IP - T.123 over PSTN"]
    B7 --> B8["Underlying Network - H.320 ISDN - H.323 LAN IP - H.324 PSTN"]
```

### 4.3 H.320 Telemedicine Call Establishment Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1["ISDN Q.931 D channel signalling - SETUP - CALL PROCEEDING - ALERTING - CONNECT"] --> C2["B channels connected - N times 64 kbps"]
    C2 --> C3["H.221 Frame alignment - search for 80 bit FAS"]
    C3 --> C4["H.230 H.242 Capability exchange - BAS codes - video audio data rates"]
    C4 --> C5["Master slave determination - highest ISDN address wins"]
    C5 --> C6["Mode negotiation - H.261 CIF QCIF - G.722 56 kbps - T.120 at 6.4 kbps"]
    C6 --> C7["Multiplexed audiovisual session - live telemedicine consultation"]
    C7 --> C8["T.124 GCC creates conference - T.125 MCS joins all sites"]
    C8 --> C9["T.126 whiteboard enabled - shared X ray annotation"]
    C9 --> C10["T.127 file transfer - DICOM push to remote radiologist"]
```

### 4.4 Telemedicine Point-to-Point Link with Optional MCU

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D1["Patient Site A - District Hospital - ISDN BRI"] --> D2["ISDN Network BSNL Switch"]
    D2 --> D3["Specialist Site B - Tertiary Care - ISDN PRI"]
    D3 --> D4["Telemedicine MCU Optional - Multipoint Bridge H.243"]
    D4 --> D5["Site C - Super Specialist"]
    D4 --> D6["Site D - Reference Lab"]
    D1 -.-> D7["H.320 Audio Video Channel"]
    D3 -.-> D7
    D7 -.-> D8["T.120 Data Channel - Shared Workspace"]
    D7 -.-> D9["Far End Camera Control H.224 H.281"]
```

### 4.5 H.221 Frame Layout — Octet-by-Octet Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    E1["H.221 Multifram 10 ms - 640 bits per B channel"] --> E2["Subframe 1 to 10 - 64 bits each"]
    E2 --> E3["Octet 1 - Frame Alignment Signal FAS - 8 bits - 00110011 pattern"]
    E2 --> E4["Octet 2 - Bit rate Allocation Signal BAS - 8 bits - capabilities control"]
    E2 --> E5["Octets 3 to 8 - Payload 48 bits per subframe - 56 kbps usable"]
    E3 --> E6["Receiver scans all octets to find FAS then synchronises entire demultiplexer"]
    E4 --> E7["BAS codes carry H.242 capabilities H.243 MCU commands H.235 encryption flags"]
    E5 --> E8["Multiplexed stream - H.261 H.263 video - G.722 audio - T.120 data"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**  
*Explain the role of the H.221 recommendation within the H.320 family. Why is it essential for ISDN-based video conferencing?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
H.221 defines the **frame structure** that multiplexes audio, video, data, and control signals into a single bit stream at rates from **64 kbps to 1920 kbps**. It introduces the **80-bit Frame Alignment Signal (FAS)** that allows the receiver to synchronise to the transmitter's multiframe (10 ms) and recover the embedded control, audio, video, and T.120 data sub-channels. Without H.221, two H.320 terminals would have no common way to delimit which bits belong to which media stream on a bonded-ISDN link. The FAS pattern is the byte `0011 0011` repeated across the multiframe, and the BAS (Bit-rate Allocation Signal) channel riding on H.221 carries the H.242 capability exchange and H.243 MCU commands. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**  
*State the purpose of the T.120 recommendation family. List any three of its sub-standards and their roles.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
The T.120 family provides **real-time multipoint data communication services** for audiovisual conferences, enabling shared whiteboards, file transfer, image annotation, and application sharing during a telemedicine session. It is **media-independent** and can ride on H.320, H.323, or H.324.  
Three sub-standards:  
- **T.122 / T.125 (MCS):** Multipoint Communication Service — the reliable, connection-oriented data layer.  
- **T.124 (GCC):** Generic Conference Control — manages conference creation, joining, and roster.  
- **T.126:** Still image annotation and shared whiteboard — used by clinicians to mark X-rays. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — H.320 Deep-Dive

**(a) [7 marks]** *Describe the H.320 protocol family in detail, listing the role of at least six of its sub-standards and explaining the px64 kbps video bit-rate formula.*  
**(b) [7 marks]** *With a labelled block diagram, describe how an H.320 telemedicine session is established between two ISDN terminals. Indicate the role of H.221 framing and H.242 capability exchange.*

---

**(a) Model Solution:**

The H.320 standard, formally titled *"Narrow-band visual telephone systems and terminal equipment"*, is an umbrella ITU-T Recommendation for narrowband audiovisual communication over circuit-switched ISDN. The canonical video bit-rate formula is:

$$B_{video} = p \times 64 \text{ kbps}, \quad p \in \{1, 2, \ldots, 30\}$$

Each value of **p** corresponds to bonding that many 64 kbps ISDN B channels into a single H.320 session. Six key sub-standards are:

1. **H.221 — Frame Structure:** Multiplexes audio, video, data, and control into 80-bit-aligned 10 ms multiframes at 64–1920 kbps. Provides FAS for synchronisation and BAS for control.  
2. **H.230 — Control and Indication:** Defines out-of-band BAS codes (capabilities, MCU commands, encryption enable) embedded in H.221.  
3. **H.242 — Call Setup (Point-to-Point):** Establishes communication between two audiovisual terminals; performs master–slave arbitration, capability exchange, and mode switching.  
4. **H.243 — Multipoint Signalling:** Extends H.242 to three or more terminals through a Multipoint Control Unit (MCU).  
5. **H.261 — px64 Video Codec:** The mandatory first-generation codec using **CIF (352×288)** and **QCIF (176×144)** resolutions, intraframe DCT + interframe motion compensation.  
6. **H.263 — Enhanced Video Codec:** Optional, lower bit-rate codec with better motion compensation and additional resolutions (Sub-QCIF, 4CIF, 16CIF).  
7. **G.711 / G.722 / G.728 — Audio Codecs:** G.711 (3 kHz, 64 kbps), G.722 (7 kHz wideband, 48/56/64 kbps), G.728 (LD-CELP, 16 kbps).  
8. **H.224 / H.281 — Far End Camera Control (FECC):** Crucial for telemedicine — allows the remote specialist to steer the patient's site camera.  
9. **T.120 — Data Conferencing:** Carried inside H.221's data sub-channel for shared workspace. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

The call establishment proceeds in the following six steps, illustrated by the diagram below.

**Step 1 — ISDN D-channel signalling (Q.931):** The calling terminal sends `SETUP`, `CALL PROCEEDING`, `ALERTING`, and `CONNECT` messages on the 16 kbps D-channel; the network allocates **N** B channels at 64 kbps each. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — B-channel connection and H.221 frame alignment:** The receiver scans incoming bits for the H.221 **Frame Alignment Signal (FAS)** — an 8-bit pattern `00110011` appearing in octet 1 of every 1 ms subframe, totalling 80 bits per 10 ms multiframe. Locking to FAS demultiplexes the stream. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 3 — H.242 capability exchange (BAS codes):** Each terminal transmits its capabilities in the BAS channel — supported video codec (H.261, H.263), audio codec (G.711, G.722, G.728), maximum bit rate, and T.120 support. Common lowest-denominator mode is selected. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 4 — Master–slave determination:** Using the deterministic rule "highest ISDN address becomes master," the master takes responsibility for issuing mode commands and bit-rate allocation. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Bit-budget allocation:** The master allocates, for example, 56 kbps to G.722 audio, 6.4 kbps to T.120 data, 8 kbps to BAS, and the rest to H.261 video. **(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — T.120 conference activation:** If T.120 was negotiated, T.124's GCC provider creates a conference and T.125's MCS provider enables multipoint data channels for T.126 (whiteboard) and T.127 (file transfer). The full audiovisual telemedicine session is now live. **(1 mark)**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["ISDN Q.931 D channel SETUP ALERTING CONNECT"] --> F2["N B channels connected N x 64 kbps"]
    F2 --> F3["H.221 FAS detection 80 bit sync pattern"]
    F3 --> F4["H.242 capability exchange BAS codes"]
    F4 --> F5["Master slave arbitration"]
    F5 --> F6["Mode negotiation video audio data bit rates"]
    F6 --> F7["Live telemedicine session - clinical consultation begins"]
```

**(Total for sub-part (b) = 7 marks)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — T.120 Deep-Dive (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain the T.120 protocol family. List at least six of its sub-recommendations and their functions. Discuss why T.120 is called "transport-agnostic."*  
**(b) [7 marks]** *Compare H.320, H.323, and H.324 in terms of network type, video codec, audio codec, bit rate, and telemedicine suitability. Why did T.120 persist as the data layer across all three?*

---

**(a) Model Solution:**

T.120 is the ITU-T's **real-time multipoint data communication** standard for audiovisual conferences. First approved in **1993**, it is the data companion to the H.32x family. T.120 is called **transport-agnostic** because its **T.123** sub-recommendation defines network-specific transport profiles for **ISDN, IP/TCP, and PSTN**, allowing the same T.124/T.125/T.126/T.127 applications to run unchanged across H.320, H.323, and H.324 networks. **(1 mark)**

Six sub-recommendations:

1. **T.120 — Umbrella:** Overall data protocols for multimedia conferencing, architecture and terminology.  
2. **T.121 — Application Templates:** Generic Application Service Elements (ASEs) and protocol stacks for any T.120 application.  
3. **T.122 — Multipoint Service Definition:** Defines the MCS service primitives (Connect, Accept, Data, Token-Please, Token-Grant).  
4. **T.123 — Network-Specific Transport:** Profiles for T.122/T.125 over ISDN (X.224 over T.62), over TCP/IP, and over PSTN (V.120).  
5. **T.124 — Generic Conference Control (GCC):** Manages conference creation, joining, leaving, address-book, and conference roster.  
6. **T.125 — MCS Protocol Specification:** Detailed byte-level protocol for domains, channels, tokens, and uniform data delivery to all participants.  
7. **T.126 — Still Image Annotation:** Multipoint shared whiteboard with pointers, freehand drawing, image overlay — used in **telemedicine to mark radiological images, ECGs, and ultrasound frames in real time**.  
8. **T.127 — Multiparty Binary File Transfer:** Reliable file push among conference participants — used to **deliver DICOM images or pathology slides** to the remote specialist. **(6 marks: 0.75 mark per sub-standard with 0.25 mark each for T.121 and T.128 if asked)**

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

| Parameter | H.320 | H.323 | H.324 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Network** | Circuit-switched ISDN | Packet-switched IP/LAN/Internet | Analog PSTN / V.34 modem |
| **Approved year** | 1990 | 1996 | 1996 |
| **Typical bit rate** | 64 – 1920 kbps | 64 kbps – several Mbps | 33.6 kbps (V.34 modem) |
| **Video codec** | H.261 (mandatory), H.263 (optional) | H.261, H.263, **H.264/H.265** (modern) | H.263, H.264 (Annexes) |
| **Audio codec** | G.711, G.722, G.728 | G.711, G.722, **G.729, G.723.1** | G.723.1 |
| **Multipoint** | MCU + H.243 | Gatekeeper + MCU (H.460 series) | Limited MCU |
| **Telemedicine suitability** | Good for rural ISDN link | **Best — modern IP telemedicine** | Last-resort low-bandwidth |
| **Frame structure** | H.221 (synchronous) | RTP/RTCP over UDP | H.223 multiplexer |
| **T.120 data plane** | Embedded in H.221 | H.323 Annex C over T.125/TCP | Embedded in H.245 logical channel |

**(5 marks: 0.625 mark per row × 8 rows of substantive content)**

**Why T.120 persists across all three:** T.120 was deliberately **decoupled from the media transport** by design. T.123 maps the MCS service onto whatever transport the underlying network offers — X.224 over T.62 for ISDN, T.125 over TCP/IP for H.323, and T.125 over V.120 for H.324. This means a **single, common T.120 application suite (T.126 whiteboard, T.127 file transfer)** can be written once and deployed across any H.32x network. For a telemedicine vendor, this was a **massive development-cost saver**: the same shared-workspace UI ran on ISDN, IP, and PSTN kits. T.120's persistence is therefore a direct consequence of the **modular separation of data plane from media plane** that the ITU-T engineering committee enforced in the 1993 architecture. **(2 marks: 1 mark for the decoupling principle, 1 mark for the engineering-economic justification)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in H.320 / T.120 Answers:**
> 1. **Confusing H.221, H.230, and H.242.** Students frequently say *"H.221 is the call-setup protocol"* — it is NOT. H.221 is the **frame multiplexer**; H.230 supplies the **control bit definitions**; H.242 is the **call-setup and capability-exchange** protocol. This confusion alone costs 1–2 marks in most 7-mark sub-questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the 80-bit FAS.** When asked *"How does an H.320 receiver synchronise to the bit stream?"*, the answer must explicitly cite the **80-bit Frame Alignment Signal spread over 10 ms**. Writing just "H.221 handles framing" is incomplete and loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing T.122 and T.125.** T.122 is the **service definition** (what primitives exist); T.125 is the **protocol specification** (how the primitives are encoded on the wire). Examiners award 1 mark for distinguishing these.
> 4. **Forgetting that H.320 is for ISDN specifically.** Students often blur H.320 (ISDN) with H.323 (IP) and H.324 (PSTN). Always state the network type in the first sentence of any H.320 answer.
> 5. **Writing T.120 as if it were a video codec.** T.120 has **no video or audio**. It is **data only** (whiteboard, file transfer, conference control). Examiners routinely deduct marks for incorrectly listing video/audio under T.120.
> 6. **Skipping the px64 formula in the H.320 family answer.** Whenever an H.320 long-answer question appears, the canonical **B = p × 64 kbps** formula and the **p ∈ {1, …, 30}** range are expected. Missing them costs at least 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — H.320 & T.120**

- **H.320 = Narrowband audiovisual communication over circuit-switched ISDN.** First approved **1990**.  
- **H.320 is an umbrella, not a single protocol.** Master the sub-standard grid (H.221, H.230, H.242, H.243, H.261, H.263, G.7xx, H.224/H.281, T.120).  
- **Canonical video bit-rate formula:** $B_{video} = p \times 64$ kbps, $p \in \{1, \ldots, 30\}$.  
- **ISDN BRI = 2B + D (128 kbps usable); PRI (E1) = 30B + D (2.048 Mbps); PRI (T1) = 23B + D (1.544 Mbps).**  
- **H.221 is the FRAME STRUCTURE / MULTIPLEXER** that splices video, audio, data, and control into 64–1920 kbps.  
- **H.221 FAS = 80 bits / 10 ms multiframe;** the alignment pattern is `00110011` (octet value 0x33).  
- **H.230 = Control and Indication (BAS codes).**  
- **H.242 = Point-to-point call setup and capability exchange.**  
- **H.243 = Multipoint call setup via MCU.**  
- **H.261 = Mandatory video codec (CIF / QCIF, px64 kbps).**  
- **H.263 = Optional, enhanced video codec (Sub-QCIF to 16CIF).**  
- **H.224 / H.281 = Far-End Camera Control — vital for remote clinical examination.**  
- **T.120 = Real-time multipoint DATA conferencing family — NO video, NO audio.**  
- **T.120 is transport-agnostic:** rides on H.320 (ISDN), H.323 (IP), and H.324 (PSTN) via T.123.  
- **T.122 / T.125 = Multipoint Communication Service (MCS) —** the reliable data heart of T.120.  
- **T.124 = Generic Conference Control (GCC) —** rosters, joining, leaving.  
- **T.126 = Still Image Annotation / Whiteboard —** for marking X-rays and ECGs in real time.  
- **T.127 = Multiparty Binary File Transfer —** for DICOM push.  
- **T.128 = Audio/Video Control (AVCC) —** bridges T.120 with H.32x media streams.  
- **T.123 = Network-specific transport profiles** (ISDN / IP / PSTN) — the key to T.120's portability.  
- **Master–slave rule in H.320 = highest ISDN address wins.**  
- **Bit budget example:** At 384 kbps (6B), H.261 video gets ~297.6 kbps after subtracting 64 kbps G.711 audio, 16 kbps overhead, and 6.4 kbps T.120.  
- **Telemedicine historical context:** H.320 + T.120 powered the **first-generation Indian telemedicine networks** (ISRO, SGPGI, Apollo) over BSNL ISDN in the 1990s and 2000s; it is now being phased out by H.323/SIP/WebRTC IP telemedicine.  
- **Minimum recommended bit rate for clinical telemedicine over H.320 = 384 kbps (p = 6).**  
- **For diagnostic-quality dermatology / radiology, prefer p = 12 (768 kbps) or p = 30 (1920 kbps, full E1 PRI).**  
- **H.320 is one of the four "H.32x" video telephony families** — alongside **H.310** (ATM/B-ISDN), **H.321** (ATM), **H.322** (Iso-Ethernet), and the later **H.323** (IP) and **H.324** (PSTN).  
- **Examiners will award 1 mark for the statement that T.120 data is carried inside the H.221 frame's data sub-channel** during an H.320 session.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: H.324 (Video Phone-based PSTN)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT861 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** H.324 (Video Phone-based PSTN)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **H.324** is an **ITU-T Recommendation** that defines a terminal for low-bit-rate **multimedia communication** (voice, video, and data) over the analog **Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN)**. It is the umbrella standard under which a family of "H.32x" multimedia terminals operate, designed specifically for video telephony on plain old dial-up telephone lines.

The H.324 framework was first published in **1995**, revised in **1998**, and updated in **2009**. It allows simultaneous, real-time, full-duplex transmission of audio, video, and user data using a standard analog modem, and is the foundational architecture behind early commercial **video phones** used in telemedicine.

| Parameter | KTU Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Underlying Network | PSTN (Plain Old Telephone Service / POTS) |
| Modem Standard | V.34 (up to **33.6 kbps**) |
| Typical Aggregate Rate | **28.8 kbps** (after signaling overhead) |
| Video Codec | H.261 / H.263 / H.263+ |
| Audio Codec | G.723.1 (5.3 / 6.3 kbps) |
| Multiplexer | H.223 |
| Control Channel | H.245 |
| Modem Handshaking | V.8 / V.25 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** H.324 is the **only H.32x standard** designed for **analog (circuit-switched) PSTN lines**. Its siblings — H.320 (ISDN), H.321 (ATM), H.322 (LAN), H.323 (IP), and H.324M (mobile) — are all for *digital* or *packet* networks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Picture Telephone Line"

Imagine you have only one narrow copper wire connecting your house to the telephone exchange. Through this wire, in the 1990s, you could barely push a 56 kbps internet signal. Now imagine trying to send your face, your voice, AND a medical scan **all at the same time** through that same skinny wire.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** H.324 is like packing three different boxes (voice, video, medical data) into one tiny suitcase, putting a **shipping label** on each box (multiplexing), compressing the air out of the boxes (codecs), and squeezing the suitcase through a narrow pipe (V.34 modem over PSTN). The H.245 control channel acts as the **postal rulebook** that the sender and receiver agree upon before any packet leaves the suitcase.

For telemedicine, this meant a remote village doctor with a simple dial-up line could establish a **face-to-face video consultation** with a specialist hundreds of miles away — a revolutionary concept at the time.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **V.34 Modem Maximum Bit Rate:** **33.6 kbps** (with V.90/V.92 fallback to ~56 kbps in downstream-only mode)
- **H.223 Overhead:** Approximately **4.8 kbps** consumed by the multiplexer framing, leaving a net payload of **~28.8 kbps**
- **G.723.1 Audio Modes:** **5.3 kbps** (MP-MLQ) or **6.3 kbps** (ACELP)
- **H.263 Frame Rates:** Typically **10–15 fps** (frames per second) for QCIF resolution (176 × 144 pixels)
- **End-to-End Latency Target:** **< 400 ms** for natural conversation

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 H.324 System Architecture — The Five Building Blocks

An H.324 terminal consists of **five mandatory functional blocks**, each governed by a separate ITU-T Recommendation. The block-level architecture is as follows:

**Block 1 — Video Codec (H.261 / H.263)**
- Digitizes and compresses the camera's analog video signal.
- Supports resolutions: **CIF (352 × 288)**, **QCIF (176 × 144)**, and **Sub-QCIF (128 × 96)**.
- H.263 (the preferred codec) introduced **half-pixel motion estimation** and **unrestricted motion vectors**, giving roughly a **30–50% compression gain** over H.261.

**Block 2 — Audio Codec (G.723.1)**
- Samples speech at **8 kHz**, 16 bits/sample, producing a **64 kbps** PCM stream.
- Compresses this down to either **5.3 kbps** (algebraic code-excited linear prediction / ACELP variant) or **6.3 kbps** (multi-pulse maximum likelihood quantization / MP-MLQ).
- Designed for **low delay** (~37.5 ms frame size), critical for natural conversation.

**Block 3 — Data Channel (T.120 / V.14)**
- Carries non-real-time data such as **still medical images**, **ECG traces**, **patient records**, or **shared whiteboards**.
- Uses **LSD (Low-Speed Data)** channel of the V.34 modem.

**Block 4 — Multiplexer / Demultiplexer (H.223)**
- Combines the three streams (audio, video, data) into a **single serial bitstream** for transmission.
- Uses a **packet-oriented framing** with 16-bit CRC-protected headers.
- Has three operational **adaptation layers** (AL1, AL2, AL3) with different error-recovery trade-offs.

**Block 5 — Control Channel (H.245)**
- Manages **call setup, capability exchange, logical channel signaling, and termination**.
- It is a **reliable protocol** (uses AL3 of H.223) that establishes which codecs the two terminals have in common.

### 2.2 Why the Architecture is Layered — The "Why" Behind the Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Why:** Real-time video telephony is fundamentally a *resource-sharing* problem. You have only **~28.8 kbps** of usable bandwidth, but you have **three streams** that all demand different latencies:
> - **Audio** cannot tolerate any pause (needs **highest priority, lowest delay**).
> - **Video** can tolerate small jitter (needs **medium priority**).
> - **Data** can tolerate variable delay (needs **lowest priority, error-protected**).
>
> The H.223 multiplexer and the H.245 control channel exist *specifically* to negotiate and enforce these priorities dynamically.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Value | Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $R_{v34}$ | V.34 raw bit rate | $R_{v34} \leq 33.6$ | kbps | Theoretical modem ceiling |
| $R_{223}$ | H.223 overhead rate | $\approx 4.8$ | kbps | Fixed framing tax |
| $R_{net}$ | Net payload rate | $R_{net} = R_{v34} - R_{223}$ | kbps | Available to A/V/D |
| $R_{aud}$ | G.723.1 audio rate | $5.3$ or $6.3$ | kbps | Voice stream |
| $R_{vid}$ | Available video rate | $R_{vid} = R_{net} - R_{aud} - R_{data}$ | kbps | Allocated to video |
| $C_{vid}$ | Compression ratio | $C_{vid} = \dfrac{\text{Uncompressed}}{\text{Compressed}}$ | unitless | Codec efficiency |
| $f_{s,v}$ | H.263 video rate | $10$–$15$ | fps | Frame frequency |
| $N_{px}$ | QCIF pixel count | $176 \times 144 = 25{,}344$ | pixels | Frame size |
| $R_{ycrcb}$ | Raw YCrCb at 30 fps | $N_{px} \times 12 \times 30$ | bps | Pre-compression rate |
| $t_{frame}$ | Audio frame duration | $30$ or $37.5$ | ms | G.723.1 frame size |
| $L_{e2e}$ | End-to-end latency budget | $< 400$ | ms | ITU-T G.114 spec |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table-Pipe Safety:** In all formula rows above, every `|`, `|x|`, or `≤` is written as `\leq` or `\vert` so the markdown table parser does not break. Do **not** use raw `|` symbols inside cells.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pre-broadband telemedicine (1995–2005):** The only way a rural clinic could achieve video consultation.
- **Disaster recovery / low-infrastructure zones:** Works on any phone line — including a single-pair copper drop.
- **Embedded device baseline:** Conceptually similar to many modern low-power IoT telemedicine gateways.
- **Standards lineage:** H.324 directly influenced the design of **3G-324M** for circuit-switched mobile video calling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Net Available Bit Rate After Multiplexer Overhead

When an H.324 call is established, the V.34 modem negotiates the highest possible line rate. We must deduct the H.223 framing tax to find the bandwidth actually available for media.

**Step 1 — Define the V.34 negotiated line rate.**
Let $R_{v34}$ be the modem bit rate. For a clean line, the maximum is **33.6 kbps**.

$$
R_{v34} = 33.6 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the H.223 framing overhead.**
H.223 consumes approximately **4.8 kbps** for its packet headers, flags, and CRC fields. The overhead is essentially constant for a given physical line.

$$
R_{223} = 4.8 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the net available payload rate.**
Subtract the framing tax from the line rate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{net} &= R_{v34} - R_{223} \\
        &= 33.6 - 4.8 \\
        &= 28.8 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Out of every **33.6 kbps** offered by the modem, only **28.8 kbps** is left for voice, video, and data. This is why H.324 is classified as a *low-bit-rate* multimedia standard.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Allocating the Available Bandwidth to Streams

**Step 1 — Audio budget.**
