# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

### Question 1: Define Intellectual Property Rights. List any **four** categories of IPR with one example each from the telemedicine domain. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) refer to the legal rights granted by a state to creators and inventors over the products of their mental labor, enabling them to exclude others from using their creations for a specified period, in order to foster innovation and creativity.
> 
> **Four categories with telemedicine examples:**
> 1. **Patent** — An AI-based skin cancer detection algorithm deployed via a mobile app.
> 2. **Copyright** — Source code of a teleconsultation platform like *Practo*.
> 3. **Trademark** — Logo and brand name of *Apollo TeleHealth Services*.
> 4. **Trade Secret** — Confidential patient dataset used to train a deep-learning diagnostic model.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

### Question 2: What is the **term of patent protection** in India? Mention any **two** subject matters that are **not patentable** under the Indian Patents Act, 1970. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Term of Patent:** **20 years** from the date of filing the application. After expiry, the invention enters the public domain and can be freely used by anyone.
> 
> **Two non-patentable subject matters (Section 3):**
> 1. **Methods of medical treatment of humans or animals** [Section 3(i)] — e.g., a new surgical technique cannot be patented.
> 2. **Computer programs per se** [Section 3(k)] — e.g., a routine billing software without any technical contribution is not patentable.
> 
> **Additional example (if needed):** Frivolous inventions contrary to natural laws [Section 3(a)].

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]`**

### Question A: Discuss Intellectual Property Rights in the context of Telemedicine. **(14 Marks)**

**Syllabus Mapping:** Module 4 — Intellectual Property Rights

**Sub-Part (a):** Explain the concept of IPR. List and briefly describe the **major types of IPR** with **Indian statutory provisions** and their **duration of protection**. **(7 Marks)**

**Sub-Part (b):** Critically analyze the **IPR challenges specific to telemedicine** including patentability of medical methods, copyright in software, data privacy of EHR, and cross-border jurisdictional issues. Suggest **mitigation strategies**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Solution to Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Concept of IPR (2 marks):**

Intellectual Property Rights are statutory rights granted to individuals over their intangible intellectual creations. They incentivize innovation by providing creators with time-bound monopolistic control. In telemedicine, where innovation is largely software-driven, data-driven, and algorithm-driven, IPR serves as the backbone of investment protection and competitive differentiation.

**2. Major Types of IPR (5 marks):**

| S.No | Type | Statute | Duration | Telemedicine Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Patent | Patents Act, **1970** | **20 years** | AI-driven cardiac arrhythmia detection algorithm |
| 2 | Copyright | Copyright Act, **1957** | **Life + 60 years** | Source code of telehealth mobile application |
| 3 | Trademark | Trademarks Act, **1999** | **10 years** (renewable) | Brand logo of a telemedicine service provider |
| 4 | Industrial Design | Designs Act, **2000** | **10 years** | Aesthetic design of a wearable ECG patch |
| 5 | Trade Secret | Common law / Contract | **Indefinite** | Confidential patient database and AI model weights |
| 6 | GI of Goods | GI Act, **1999** | **10 years** (renewable) | Tag for telemedicine services from a specific state |

> [!NOTE]
> **Detailed Breakdown of Each Type:**
> 
> **Patent:** A patent is an exclusive statutory right granted for a new, useful, and non-obvious invention. In India, patent applications are filed at any of the four patent offices (Kolkata, Mumbai, Chennai, Delhi). Telemedicine startups increasingly file patents on AI algorithms provided they demonstrate a technical effect (e.g., processing real-time biosignal data).
> 
> **Copyright:** Protects the original expression of ideas. In India, copyright arises automatically upon creation in a tangible medium. Software code, medical e-learning videos, and patient education infographics are protected under this regime.
> 
> **Trademark:** A trademark is a distinctive mark used to identify goods/services. For telemedicine, both the word mark (e.g., "MFine") and the logo qualify. India follows the **first-to-use** principle with registration as prima facie evidence.
> 
> **Industrial Design:** Protects the visual/aesthetic appearance of an article. Relevant for the unique ergonomic shape of wearable health monitors.
> 
> **Trade Secret:** Information that derives commercial value from not being generally known. In telemedicine, proprietary AI training datasets and clinical triage protocols qualify. India currently has no dedicated trade secrets statute; protection is via **breach of confidence** under common law and NDAs.

---

#### Solution to Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Major IPR Challenges in Telemedicine:**

**Challenge 1 — Patentability of Medical Methods (2 marks):**

Under **Section 3(i)** of the Indian Patents Act, methods of treatment of humans are excluded from patentability. This creates ambiguity around **telemedicine-assisted diagnostic methods**. For instance, a novel tele-cardiology diagnostic protocol that involves remote ECG analysis may be argued as a "method of treatment," denying patent protection.

**Challenge 2 — Software Patents Ambiguity (2 marks):**

**Section 3(k)** excludes "computer programs per se." However, the Indian Patent Office has granted patents on AI-based telemedicine platforms (e.g., AI-driven X-ray interpretation) when integrated with novel hardware. The line between patentable and non-patentable software remains contested.

**Challenge 3 — Data Privacy of EHR (1.5 marks):**

Electronic Health Records contain sensitive personal data. The **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act)** imposes stringent obligations. Conflicts arise between trade secret claims of hospitals and patients' right to data portability under DPDP Act Section 12.

**Challenge 4 — Cross-Border Jurisdictional Issues (1.5 marks):**

Telemedicine inherently crosses borders. A doctor in India consulting a patient in the UAE faces conflicting IP regimes (e.g., Sharia-compliant data localization vs. Indian IT Act). Patent infringement suits face **forum shopping** issues.

**Mitigation Strategies:**

1. **Hybrid Protection Strategy:** Combine patent (for core algorithm) + copyright (for code) + trade secret (for data) + trademark (for brand).
2. **Strong NDAs and Employment Contracts:** All personnel handling proprietary data must sign NDAs with liquidated damages clauses.
3. **Cross-border IP filings:** Use the **PCT route** for patents and **Madrid Protocol** for trademarks to ensure international coverage.
4. **Compliance by Design:** Embed DPDP Act, IT Act 2000, and HIPAA (for US patients) compliance into the platform architecture.
5. **Cyber Insurance and IP Insurance:** Mitigate litigation and breach costs.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): Explain the **Indian patent system** with reference to **patentable and non-patentable subject matter**. Discuss the **step-by-step procedure for patent grant in India** with a neat flowchart. **(14 Marks)**

**Sub-Part (a):** Discuss **patentable subject matter** and the **three criteria of patentability** (novelty, inventive step, industrial application). Provide **five examples** of non-patentable inventions under Section 3 of the Indian Patents Act. **(7 Marks)**

**Sub-Part (b):** Describe the **complete patent prosecution procedure in India** step-by-step. Draw a **flowchart** and state the **timeline** for each stage. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Solution to Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Three Criteria of Patentability (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Most Tested Concept:**

1. **Novelty (Section 2(1)(j)):** The invention must not have been published or used anywhere in the world before the date of filing. Any disclosure (publication, sale, use) prior to filing destroys novelty. There is **no grace period** in India.

2. **Inventive Step / Non-Obviousness (Section 2(1)(ja)):** The invention must not be obvious to a **person skilled in the art (PSITA)**, having regard to the prior art.

3. **Industrial Application / Utility (Section 2(1)(ac)):** The invention must be capable of being made or used in an industry.

**Five Examples of Non-Patentable Inventions (4 marks):**

| Section | Non-Patentable Subject |
|---|---|
| **3(a)** | Inventions contrary to natural laws (e.g., perpetual motion machine) |
| **3(b)** | Inventions contrary to public order or morality (e.g., a cheating device for telemedicine exam) |
| **3(d)** | New form of known substance without enhanced efficacy |
| **3(i)** | Methods of treatment of humans and animals |
| **3(k)** | Computer programs per se (without technical contribution) |

---

#### Solution to Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step-by-Step Patent Prosecution Procedure in India:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Flowchart (text representation since it must be drawn in exam):**
> 
> **Invention → Prior Art Search → Draft Specification (Provisional/Complete) → File at IPO → Publication (18 months) → Request for Examination (within 48 months) → FER Issued → Applicant Response (within 6 months) → Hearing (if needed) → Grant or Refusal**

**Detailed Timeline (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Stage} \quad &\textbf{Timeline} \\
\text{Provisional Filing} \quad &\text{Day 0 (Priority Date Set)} \\
\text{Complete Specification} \quad &\text{Within 12 months} \\
\text{Publication} \quad &\text{18 months from priority date} \\
\text{Request for Examination} \quad &\text{Within 48 months of priority} \\
\text{Response to FER} \quad &\text{6 months (extendable by 3 months)} \\
\text{Grant and Sealing} \quad &\text{Total: 3 to 5 years (typical)} \\
\text{Patent Term} \quad &\text{20 years from priority date}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Examiner's Role (1.5 marks):**
The **Examiner of Patents** evaluates the application for novelty, inventive step, industrial applicability, and compliance with Section 3 exclusions. The **Controller of Patents** presides over hearings and final decisions.

**Post-Grant (1.5 marks):**
After grant, the patent must be renewed annually starting from the **3rd year** of the priority date. Failure to pay renewal fees results in patent lapse. Post-grant opposition can be filed within **12 months** of grant under **Section 25(2)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing priority date with filing date:** The 20-year term runs from the **priority date** (earliest filed application), NOT the complete specification date.
> 2. **Forgetting Section 3(k) caveat:** Software alone is NOT patentable. It must be tied to a **technical effect**.
> 3. **Mixing trademark duration with patent duration:** Patent = 20 years; Trademark = 10 years (renewable).
> 4. **Ignoring DPDP Act 2023:** Any answer on telemedicine data must mention the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023**.
> 5. **No mention of Berne Convention / TRIPS:** International dimension is mandatory for full marks.
> 6. **Wrong office for trademark:** Trademark is registered at **CGPDTM (Mumbai)**, not at any of the four patent offices.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4 (IPR in Telemedicine):**

- **IPR Definition:** Statutory rights over intangible intellectual creations.
- **Patent Term:** **20 years** from **priority date**.
- **Copyright Term (most works):** **Life of author + 60 years**.
- **Trademark Term:** **10 years** (renewable indefinitely).
- **Industrial Design Term:** **10 years** (extendable to **15 years**).
- **Trade Secret:** No statutory term; protected **indefinitely** if secrecy maintained.
- **Patentability Triad:** **Novelty + Inventive Step + Industrial Application**.
- **Section 3(i):** Excludes methods of medical treatment.
- **Section 3(k):** Excludes computer programs per se.
- **Section 3(d):** Excludes new forms of known substances without enhanced efficacy.
- **Patent Office Jurisdictions in India:** **Kolkata, Mumbai, Chennai, Delhi**.
- **Trademark Registration Authority:** **CGPDTM, Mumbai**.
- **First Examination Report (FER):** Issued by patent examiner after RFE.
- **Examination Request Deadline:** **48 months** from priority date.
- **Publication Period:** **18 months** from priority date (or earlier on request).
- **TRIPS Agreement:** WTO-administered IP harmonization (1994).
- **Berne Convention:** Automatic copyright protection across member states.
- **PCT:** Single international patent application via WIPO.
- **Madrid Protocol:** Single international trademark filing.
- **Doha Declaration (2001):** Public health flexibilities over IP rights.
- **DPDP Act 2023:** India's data protection law, critical for telemedicine EHR.
- **IT Act 2000:** India's cyber law covering digital signatures, e-records, and computer offenses.
- **Nice Classification:** Class 9 (software), 38 (telecom), 42 (SaaS), 44 (medical).
- **First-to-File Principle:** India follows **first-to-file** for patents (since 2003).
- **Grace Period:** India does **NOT** provide a grace period for novelty (unlike USA).
- **Bolar Exception:** Allowed in India — generic manufacturers can use patented inventions for regulatory approval before expiry.
- **Compulsory License:** Available under Section 84 for non-working of patents after 3 years.
- **IPAB:** Intellectual Property Appellate Board (now merged with High Courts).
- **Trade Secret Penalty:** Enforced via breach of confidence + NDAs + Specific Relief Act 1963.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Jurisdictional Issues in Telemedicine

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Jurisdictional Issues in Telemedicine** refer to the complex set of legal, ethical, and regulatory challenges that arise concerning **which legal authority (state, national, or international) has the power to govern, regulate, and adjudicate** a telemedicine consultation, the practice of medicine conducted across geographical boundaries, the licensing of healthcare professionals, the handling of electronic medical records (EMR), the resolution of malpractice claims, and the enforcement of patient rights when the patient, the physician, and the service provider are located in different jurisdictions.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEBT861)**, this topic sits at the intersection of **Health Law, Information Technology Law, Medical Ethics, and International Trade Law**, forming one of the most critical pillars for the safe, lawful, and ethical deployment of telemedicine services.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Driver's License Across States" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a doctor from **Kerala** provides a teleconsultation to a patient physically sitting in **Delhi**.
>
> - Whose medical council's rules apply — the Kerala State Medical Council (TCMC) or the Delhi Medical Council (DMC)?
> - If the prescription causes harm, which court hears the case — a consumer forum in Delhi, a civil court in Thiruvananthapuram, or a court in another country?
> - Whose data protection law governs the medical records — India's DPDP Act 2023, the State IT rules, or an EU GDPR framework if the cloud server is in Frankfurt?
>
> **This is precisely the "Jurisdictional Maze" of telemedicine.** Traditional medicine assumed both doctor and patient were in the same room, in the same town, governed by the same law. Telemedicine collapses geography — but the law has not fully collapsed with it.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Jurisdictional Law

- **Nexus / Minimum Contacts Doctrine** — A legal test (USA-origin) to determine whether a court has personal jurisdiction over an out-of-state defendant based on their purposeful contacts with the forum state.
- **Place of Practice Rule** — A traditional medical licensing principle stating that a physician must be licensed in the jurisdiction where the *patient is physically located at the time of consultation*.
- **Locus of the Patient (Lex loci patientis)** — The law of the place where the patient receives care governs the standard of care applied.
- **Locus of the Provider (Lex loci medici)** — The law of the place where the doctor is registered governs licensing.
- **Data Localization Threshold** — As per India's **Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA) 2023**, sensitive personal data including health data may be processed only under notified conditions; cross-border transfer requires specific safeguards.
- **Statute of Limitations (Malpractice)** — Typically **2 to 3 years** from the date of injury or discovery, varying by jurisdiction (India: **2 years** under the Consumer Protection Act 2019 for medical negligence claims; varies in USA state laws).

### 1.4 Primary Legal & Regulatory Stakeholders

| Stakeholder | Role in Telemedicine Jurisdiction |
|-------------|-----------------------------------|
| **National Medical Commission (NMC)** | Apex body regulating medical education and practice in India |
| **State Medical Councils (SMC)** | Issue registration; traditionally tied to physical practice |
| **Ministry of Health & Family Welfare (MoHFW)** | Issued Telemedicine Practice Guidelines (2020) |
| **Ministry of Electronics & IT (MeitY)** | Governs IT Act 2000, DPDPA 2023, data storage |
| **NITI Aayog** | Released National Digital Health Blueprint (2019) |
| **World Medical Association (WMA)** | Issues international ethical declarations |
| **Foreign Medical Regulators** | For cross-border telemedicine (e.g., GMC-UK, AMA-USA) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Telemedicine Jurisdiction

Telemedicine jurisdictional issues can be decomposed into **five interlocking pillars**. A student must internalize these as the "spinal column" of any answer on this topic.

### Pillar 1 — **Licensure Jurisdiction**
> The legal authority to practice medicine.

- A doctor must hold a valid registration with the **medical council of the jurisdiction where the patient is located** during consultation.
- This is called the **"Place of Practice Rule"** — universally accepted by NMC India (2020 Guidelines), USA (state medical boards), EU (mutual recognition under Directive 2005/36/EC for in-person; debated for tele-medicine), and UK (GMC).
- **India's Position**: The *Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020* (jointly by NMC and MoHFW) clarify that the **patient's location at the time of consultation** is treated as the place of practice for registration purposes.

### Pillar 2 — **Tort & Malpractice Jurisdiction**
> Where can the patient sue for negligence?

- **Lex loci delicti (law of the place of wrong)** — traditionally applied.
- **Cause of action arises** at the patient's location (where harm was suffered).
- **Consumer Protection Act 2019 (India)**: A patient can file at the consumer forum where they reside, where the service was rendered, or where the doctor operates — providing **multiple forum options**.
- **Conflict of Laws Doctrine**: Courts may apply either:
  1. **Lex loci delicti** — Law of the place where injury occurred.
  2. **Lex loci contractus** — Law of the place where the doctor-patient contract was formed (often the doctor's location).
  3. **Proper-law-of-the-tort doctrine** — A flexible, fact-based approach.

### Pillar 3 — **Data Protection & Privacy Jurisdiction**
> Whose data law governs the medical record?

- **India**: Governed by the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (DPDPA)**, supplemented by the **Information Technology (Reasonable Security Practices) Rules 2011** and the **Information Technology Act 2000 (Sections 43A, 72A)**.
- **Health data is classified as "Sensitive Personal Data or Information (SPDI)"** under the SPDI Rules 2011.
- **Cross-border data transfer**: Under DPDPA 2023, the Central Government may restrict transfer of personal data for processing to a country or territory outside India by notification.
- **HIPAA (USA)**: Federal law governing Protected Health Information (PHI).
- **GDPR (EU)**: Extraterritorial application — applies to any entity processing data of EU residents, regardless of the entity's location.

### Pillar 4 — **Prescription & Drug Regulation Jurisdiction**
> Which pharmacy law governs the e-prescription?

- **India**: The **Indian Medical Council (Professional Conduct, Etiquette and Ethics) Regulations 2002** and the **Drugs and Cosmetics Act 1940** govern prescriptions.
- A valid telemedicine prescription under Indian law must include the doctor's **registration number**, **state council name**, and the patient's **verified identity**.
- **Narcotic & Psychotropic Substances Act 1985** restricts telemedicine prescription of Schedule X drugs — only allowed in specific exceptional circumstances under the 2020 Guidelines.
- **Cross-border prescription** (e.g., Indian doctor prescribing to a patient in Germany) faces additional drug importation regulations.

### Pillar 5 — **Reimbursement & Insurance Jurisdiction**
> Who pays, and under whose insurance law?

- **Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI)** issued guidelines on telemedicine coverage.
- **CGHS (Central Government Health Scheme)** and **PMJAY (Ayushman Bharat)** now reimburse teleconsultations.
- **Cross-border reimbursement**: Most national insurance schemes do NOT cover telemedicine delivered from outside the country.
- **Consumer Fora jurisdiction**: For billing disputes, jurisdiction lies where the consumer resides.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive KTU-ready reference** for jurisdictional problem-solving. Memorize the framework, not just the rows.

| Framework Element | India (Statute / Rule) | USA | EU | Conflict Resolution Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Medical Practice Regulation** | NMC Act 2019; Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020 | State Medical Practice Acts; FSMB Interstate Medical Licensure Compact (IMLC) | Directive 2005/36/EC (professional qualifications) | Place-of-Practice Rule |
| **Data Protection** | DPDPA 2023; IT Act 2000 (Sec 43A, 72A); SPDI Rules 2011 | HIPAA (1996); HITECH Act | GDPR (2016/679) | Territoriality + Extraterritoriality |
| **Prescription Validity** | Drugs & Cosmetics Act 1940; NMC Reg 2002 | Ryan Haight Act 2008 (controlled substances) | EU Cross-Border Healthcare Directive 2011/24 | Doctor's licensing jurisdiction |
| **Malpractice Liability** | Consumer Protection Act 2019; IPC Sec 304A | State Tort Law; Medical Malpractice Insurance | National Tort Laws of Member States | Lex loci delicti + Lex loci contractus |
| **Consent Requirements** | Telemedicine Guidelines 2020 (explicit, informed) | HIPAA + State Laws | GDPR Article 6 & 9 (explicit consent) | Strictest applicable law |
| **Cross-Border Consultation** | NMC 2020 Guidelines (patient-side jurisdiction); MCI Ethics Reg 2002 Sec 1.1.3 | State-by-state; IMLC Compact (since 2017) | Directive 2011/24/EU | Mutual Recognition + Licensing |
| **Record Retention** | 3 years (adult); up to age of majority (minor) | 6 years (HIPAA general) | As long as necessary (GDPR) | National minimum |
| **Standard of Care** | Same as in-person (NMC 2020) | Same as in-person | Same as in-person | Lex loci patientis |
| **Statute of Limitations** | 2 years (CPA 2019) | 2 to 6 years (state dependent) | 1 to 10 years (state dependent) | Lex loci delicti |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Clinical Utility

- **Hospital Information System (HIS) Designers** must build jurisdiction-aware consent modules, multi-lingual privacy notices, and geolocation-tagging of consults.
- **Telehealth Platform Architects** must integrate cross-jurisdictional server redundancy, encryption-at-rest compliant with both HIPAA and DPDPA, and audit trails traceable to the doctor's licensing state.
- **Biomedical Engineers** designing wearable diagnostic devices must ensure data egress is routed through jurisdictional-compliant gateways, especially for **cross-border patient monitoring**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Jurisdictional Case Logic

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a **legal-regulatory topic** (not a mathematical one), the "derivations" below are **structured legal reasoning flows** — exactly the way a KTU examiner's model answer demands. Every step is shown to satisfy the *exhaustive content mandate*.

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Determining Applicable Jurisdiction (Step-by-Step)

When a telemedicine event occurs between two parties in different jurisdictions, the **determination of applicable law follows a five-step analytical cascade**.

### Step 1 — Identify the **Patient's Physical Location (P)** at the moment of consultation

- This is the **single most important fact**.
- Captured via **IP geolocation**, **documented self-declaration**, or **registered address**.
- **Why**: The patient's location fixes the **place-of-practice**, the **applicable medical council jurisdiction**, and the **consumer forum locus standi**.

### Step 2 — Identify the **Doctor's Registration Jurisdiction (D)**

- Look up the doctor's name in the **National Medical Commission (NMC) / State Medical Council registry** or **Foreign Medical Register**.
- Confirm **license validity** (active, not suspended) and **specialty endorsement**.

### Step 3 — Identify the **Service Provider's Jurisdiction (S)**

- The telemedicine platform (e.g., Practo, Apollo 24/7, Mfine, or a foreign platform) has its own corporate jurisdiction.
- Critical for **data server location**, **terms of service**, and **liability of the intermediary** under Section 79 of the IT Act 2000 (India).

### Step 4 — Identify the **Data Server / Cloud Storage Jurisdiction (C)**

- May be different from P, D, and S.
- **Example**: Patient in Kerala, doctor in Karnataka, platform headquartered in Singapore, cloud server in Mumbai.
- Each adds a layer of jurisdictional complexity.

### Step 5 — Apply the **Conflict-of-Laws Cascade**

Resolve overlapping laws using this priority:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Applicable Law} &= \text{Strictest Applicable Standard} \\
&\cup \text{Lex loci delicti (patient injury locus)} \\
&\cup \text{Lex loci contractus (where contract was formed)} \\
&\cup \text{Lex loci medici (doctor's registration jurisdiction)} \\
&\cup \text{Lex loci data (data subject's residence)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Jurisdictional Case Study 1 — *Indian Domestic Teleconsultation*

### Scenario
> **Patient**: Mr. Ravi, residing in Chennai, Tamil Nadu.
> **Doctor**: Dr. Priya, registered with the **Travancore-Cochin Medical Council (TCMC)**, Kerala.
> **Platform**: Practo (headquartered in Bengaluru, Karnataka).
> **Server**: AWS Mumbai Region (Maharashtra).
> **Event**: Dr. Priya prescribes an antibiotic. Mr. Ravi develops Stevens-Johnson Syndrome. He wants to sue.

### Step-by-Step Jurisdictional Analysis

**Step 1 — Patient's Location (P)**:
$$
P = \text{Chennai, Tamil Nadu}
$$

**Step 2 — Doctor's Registration (D)**:
$$
D = \text{Kerala (TCMC, Registration No. XXXXX)}
$$

**Step 3 — Platform's Jurisdiction (S)**:
$$
S = \text{Bengaluru, Karnataka}
$$

**Step 4 — Server Location (C)**:
$$
C = \text{Mumbai, Maharashtra}
$$

**Step 5 — Applicable Law Determination**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Licensure} &: \text{TCMC (Kerala)} \rightarrow \text{NMC Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020} \\
\text{Malpractice Forum} &: \text{Chennai District Consumer Forum OR State Commission Tamil Nadu} \\
&\text{ (per Section 35 of Consumer Protection Act 2019)} \\
\text{Standard of Care} &: \text{NMC Telemedicine Guidelines 2020 + IMC Ethics Reg 2002} \\
\text{Data Protection} &: \text{DPDPA 2023 + IT Act 2000 + SPDI Rules 2011} \\
\text{Prescription Validity} &: \text{Drugs \& Cosmetics Act 1940 + NMC Guidelines 2020}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Outcome**:
Mr. Ravi can file a complaint at:
1. The **District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission, Chennai** (place of residence + place where service was received).
2. Alternatively, at the **State Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission, Tamil Nadu**.

The **doctor's licensing jurisdiction** (Kerala) governs her authorization, but the **malpractice liability** is governed by the patient's location (Tamil Nadu) and the **Consumer Protection Act 2019**.

## 3.3 Worked Jurisdictional Case Study 2 — *Cross-Border Teleconsultation*

### Scenario
> **Patient**: Mr. John, a UK citizen temporarily in Bengaluru.
> **Doctor**: Dr. Mehta, an Indian cardiologist registered with Maharashtra Medical Council (MMC).
> **Event**: Dr. Mehta conducts a teleconsultation. Patient suffers a cardiac event and is hospitalized.
> **Data Storage**: Server in Singapore.

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1 — Patient's Location (P)**:
$$
P = \text{Bengaluru, India (at time of consultation)}
$$

**Step 2 — Doctor's Registration (D)**:
$$
D = \text{Maharashtra (MMC, Registration No. YYYYY)}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-Border Data Subject (UK)**:
- Although patient is in India at consultation, his **residency** is the UK.
- **GDPR Article 3** triggers if the data relates to an EU/UK resident AND the data is processed outside the EU/UK (here: Bengaluru, India) — but **note that GDPR protects "data subjects" located in the EU/UK, regardless of nationality**, when processing relates to offering them goods/services.
- However, since the consultation occurred while Mr. John was in India, **Indian law primarily applies**.
- If follow-up data processing occurs after Mr. John returns to the UK, **UK GDPR may apply**.

**Step 4 — Conflict Resolution Cascade**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Primary Law (consultation)} &: \text{Indian law (NMC Guidelines 2020, DPDPA 2023, IT Act 2000)} \\
\text{Secondary Law (data egress to UK)} &: \text{UK GDPR + Data Protection Act 2018 (UK)} \\
\text{Malpractice Jurisdiction} &: \text{Bengaluru Consumer Forum OR Karnataka State Commission}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Outcome**:
- Indian courts have **direct jurisdiction** because the act occurred within India.
- The UK may claim **residency-based data protection jurisdiction** for any data exported to UK.
- The **server in Singapore** introduces a third layer — Singapore's **Personal Data Protection Act 2012 (PDPA)** could apply to data at rest.

## 3.4 The Indian Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020 — Key Jurisdictional Provisions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Indian Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020**, released on **25 March 2020** by the **Board of Governors of the Medical Council of India (in supersession)** along with the **MoHFW**, are the **single most examinable document** on this topic. Here are the precise jurisdictional clauses:

| Clause | Provision |
|---|---|
| **Paragraph 1.4** | Telemedicine consultations must be initiated by the patient; the patient determines the location. |
| **Paragraph 2.1** | A Registered Medical Practitioner (RMP) must be registered with **any State Medical Council** OR the **NMC**. The place of practice for telemedicine is **deemed to be the patient's location**. |
| **Paragraph 3.1** | The RMP must display his/her **registration number**, **state council name**, and **contact details** prominently. |
| **Paragraph 4.2** | For first-time consultations, the RMP must verify the patient's identity (Aadhaar, PAN, etc.). |
| **Paragraph 5.1** | The RMP may prescribe medicines per the **specified list** — List O (OTC), List A (prescription but non-addictive), List B (addictive, restricted). |
| **Paragraph 7** | The RMP must **maintain records** for **3 years** (or longer if mandated by state law). |
| **Paragraph 9** | The RMP must **not solicit patients** through advertising. |
| **Section on Consent** | Explicit, informed consent is **mandatory** for every teleconsultation. |

## 3.5 International Comparative Jurisdictional Frameworks

### United States of America
- **State Medical Boards** govern licensing (50 states + DC + territories).
- **Interstate Medical Licensure Compact (IMLC)**: An agreement among participating states that streamlines multi-state licensing — but not all states participate.
- **Ryan Haight Online Pharmacy Consumer Protection Act 2008**: Federal law restricting telemedicine prescription of controlled substances unless a prior in-person examination has occurred (with limited exceptions during public health emergencies, e.g., COVID-19).
- **HIPAA**: Applies to "covered entities" and "business associates" — extraterritorial in the sense that any handling of US patient PHI must comply.

### European Union
- **Directive 2005/36/EC**: Recognition of professional qualifications across member states.
- **Cross-Border Healthcare Directive 2011/24/EU**: Establishes patients' rights to access healthcare across EU borders, including (limited) telemedicine.
- **GDPR (Regulation 2016/679)**: Applies extraterritorially to any processing of EU residents' personal data.

### United Kingdom
- **General Medical Council (GMC)** regulates doctors.
- **UK GDPR + Data Protection Act 2018** govern data processing.
- Post-Brexit, UK has its own adequacy regimes.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

### **Q1. Define 'jurisdictional issues' in the context of telemedicine. List any FOUR key legal challenges.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Jurisdictional issues in telemedicine refer to the legal challenges that arise in determining which legal authority — state, national, or international — has the power to govern a telemedicine consultation across geographical boundaries.

**Four key legal challenges are:**

1. **Licensure**: Which medical council has authority to license the doctor treating a patient in a different state/country?
2. **Malpractice liability**: Which court has jurisdiction to hear a negligence case?
3. **Data protection**: Which data privacy law governs the electronic medical record?
4. **Prescription validity**: Which pharmacy law governs the e-prescription issued?

---

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

### **Q2. What is the 'Place of Practice Rule' as applied to telemedicine in India?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Place of Practice Rule** states that a Registered Medical Practitioner (RMP) is considered to be practicing medicine in the jurisdiction **where the patient is physically located at the time of consultation**, and not merely where the doctor is physically situated.

Under the **Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020 (India)**, this rule mandates that the RMP must be registered with the **medical council of the state where the patient is located** during consultation. This ensures that the patient receives care under the regulatory framework of their own jurisdiction, protecting them from unlicensed practice.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern]**

### **Question A (14 Marks):**
> *(a)* Discuss in detail the **FIVE major jurisdictional issues** that arise in telemedicine practice, with specific reference to Indian law. *(7 Marks)*
>
> *(b)* Explain the **Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020** issued by the Government of India, highlighting their key provisions related to **registration, consent, prescription, and record-keeping**. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Five Major Jurisdictional Issues in Telemedicine — Indian Context (7 Marks)**

**Issue 1 — Licensure and Place of Practice:**
A medical practitioner must be registered with the medical council of the jurisdiction where the patient is located. Under the Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020, this is the patient's location at the time of consultation, irrespective of the doctor's physical location. Example: A doctor registered with the Travancore-Cochin Medical Council (TCMC, Kerala) can consult a patient in Tamil Nadu only if their NMC registration is valid in Tamil Nadu (typically via NMC's central register).

**Issue 2 — Malpractice and Tort Liability:**
Under the **Consumer Protection Act 2019**, a patient can file a complaint at the consumer forum of their own district, where the service was rendered, or where the opposite party resides. This gives patients multiple forums. Under IPC Section 304A, criminal negligence prosecution may be initiated where the patient resides.

**Issue 3 — Data Protection and Privacy:**
Health data is classified as **Sensitive Personal Data or Information (SPDI)** under the IT (Reasonable Security Practices) Rules 2011, and as **personal data** under the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (DPDPA)**. Cross-border transfer is regulated, and the **patient's consent** is mandatory.

**Issue 4 — Prescription and Drug Regulation:**
The **Drugs and Cosmetics Act 1940** and the **NMC Professional Conduct Regulations 2002** govern prescription validity. Schedule X and narcotic drugs face stricter rules under the **NDPS Act 1985**.

**Issue 5 — Reimbursement and Insurance:**
**IRDAI** has recognized telemedicine for insurance claims. However, cross-border telemedicine is generally not reimbursed under domestic insurance schemes, creating jurisdictional barriers for NRIs and medical tourists.

**(b) Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020 — Key Provisions (7 Marks)**

**Issuance**: Released on 25 March 2020 by the Board of Governors of MCI (in supersession) with the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (MoHFW).

**Key Provisions:**

1. **Registration (Para 2.1)**: Every RMP must be registered with the NMC or a State Medical Council. They must prominently display their **registration number, council name, and qualifications**.

2. **Consent (Section on Consent)**: **Explicit, informed consent** is mandatory for every teleconsultation. The consent must be recorded (text, audio, video).

3. **Prescription (Para 5)**: The RMP may prescribe drugs from three lists:
   - **List O**: Over-the-counter medications.
   - **List A**: Prescription medications that are non-addictive, safe for teleprescription.
   - **List B**: Addictive medications — restricted, only in exceptional circumstances.

4. **Record-Keeping (Para 7)**: The RMP must maintain consultation records for a minimum of **3 years**. Records must include patient identification, diagnosis, treatment plan, and prescription.

5. **Modes of Consultation**: Allowed modes include video, audio, text (chat), and asynchronous messaging, with specific restrictions on which modes are appropriate for first-time vs follow-up consultations.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**
> *(a)* Describe the **legal framework for cross-border telemedicine consultations**, comparing the Indian position with the **US Interstate Medical Licensure Compact (IMLC)** and the **EU Cross-Border Healthcare Directive**. *(7 Marks)*
>
> *(b)* Analyze the **conflict-of-laws doctrine** as applied to telemedicine malpractice cases, with reference to **lex loci delicti, lex loci contractus, and lex loci medici**. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Cross-Border Telemedicine — Comparative Legal Framework (7 Marks)**

**Indian Position:**
Under the Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020, an Indian RMP can consult a patient located abroad provided:
1. The RMP is registered with the NMC or a State Medical Council.
2. The patient has provided informed consent.
3. The prescription complies with the destination country's drug laws.
4. Cross-border data transfer complies with DPDPA 2023 and the destination country's data protection law (e.g., GDPR for EU patients).

**US Interstate Medical Licensure Compact (IMLC):**
Adopted in 2017, the IMLC is an agreement among participating US states (over 35 states as of 2024) that allows physicians licensed in one Compact state to obtain **expedited licensure** in other Compact states. This addresses the fragmented US state-based licensing system, enabling cross-state teleconsultation. Physicians must designate a **State of Principal License (SPL)**.

**EU Cross-Border Healthcare Directive 2011/24/EU:**
Establishes patients' rights to access healthcare in any EU member state, with reimbursement up to the level of their home country's tariff. Includes provisions for **eHealth interoperability** and **cross-border prescription recognition** via the **ePrescription** and **Patient Summary** services under the **MyHealth@EU** infrastructure.

**Comparative Summary**: India relies on a **centralized NMC registration** with place-of-practice flexibility; the US uses a **multi-state compact model**; the EU uses a **mutual recognition + interoperability** model.

**(b) Conflict-of-Laws Doctrine in Telemedicine Malpractice (7 Marks)**

When a telemedicine event spans jurisdictions, courts apply **conflict-of-laws principles** to determine the applicable substantive law.

**Lex loci delicti (Law of the Place of Wrong):**
This doctrine holds that the law of the place where the injury occurred governs the tort. In telemedicine, the injury is typically suffered at the **patient's location**, making it the default choice. Example: Patient in Chennai injured by negligent diagnosis from a doctor in Delhi — Madras High Court applies Tamil Nadu tort law.

**Lex loci contractus (Law of the Place of Contract):**
This doctrine applies the law of the place where the doctor-patient contract was formed. In telemedicine, the contract is often deemed to be formed at the **doctor's location** where the service originates, or at the **patient's location** where it is received — leading to ambiguity.

**Lex loci medici (Law of the Doctor's Location):**
This doctrine applies the law of the jurisdiction where the doctor is registered and licensed. Used for determining the **standard of care** expected from the practitioner.

**Practical Resolution**: Indian courts under the **Consumer Protection Act 2019** adopt a **multi-forum, plaintiff-friendly approach**, allowing the patient to choose among several jurisdictions. Internationally, the trend is toward **harmonization** through instruments like the **Hague Convention on Choice of Court Agreements**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Where students commonly lose marks on Jurisdictional Issues questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the "doctor's location" with the "place of practice."** The exam answer must clearly state that **the patient's location = place of practice**, not the doctor's. (Lose 1 mark if missed)
>
> 2. **Quoting the IT Act 2000 alone without mentioning DPDPA 2023.** Since 2023, DPDPA is the governing data protection law in India. Quoting only the old SPDI Rules 2011 or IT Act 2000 in 2024 exams will lose 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Omitting the Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020.** This is the **single most-cited document** in KTU answers. Any answer on Indian jurisdictional issues without a reference to these Guidelines is incomplete. (Lose 2 marks)
>
> 4. **Mixing up Ryan Haight Act (US) with HIPAA.** The Ryan Haight Act 2008 specifically governs **controlled substance prescriptions via telemedicine**, not general data protection. HIPAA governs **data privacy**. Examiners will deduct marks for confusing the two.
>
> 5. **Not distinguishing lex loci delicti from lex loci contractus.** Examiners specifically look for these Latin terms used correctly in context.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Jurisdictional Issues**

- **Definition**: Legal authority governing cross-border telemedicine practice.
- **Place of Practice Rule**: Patient's location at time of consultation = jurisdiction.
- **Five Pillars**: Licensure, Tort/Malpractice, Data Protection, Prescription/Drug, Reimbursement.
- **India's Apex Law**: Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020 (MoHFW + NMC).
- **Licensing Body**: National Medical Commission (NMC); State Medical Councils.
- **Malpractice Law**: Consumer Protection Act 2019 (2-year limitation period); IPC Sec 304A.
- **Data Protection**: Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (DPDPA); IT Act 2000 (Sec 43A, 72A); SPDI Rules 2011.
- **Prescription Law**: Drugs and Cosmetics Act 1940; Lists O, A, B.
- **Record Retention**: Minimum **3 years** under Indian Guidelines.
- **Cross-Border India**: NMC Guidelines 2020 + DPDPA 2023 + foreign data law.
- **US Model**: Interstate Medical Licensure Compact (IMLC) + HIPAA + Ryan Haight Act 2008.
- **EU Model**: Directive 2011/24/EU + GDPR 2016/679 + Directive 2005/36/EC.
- **Lex loci delicti**: Law of place where injury occurred (default in telemedicine).
- **Lex loci contractus**: Law of place where contract was formed.
- **Lex loci medici**: Law of doctor's registration jurisdiction.
- **Conflict Resolution**: Strictest applicable standard wins; consumer-friendly forum choice under CPA 2019.
- **Key International Bodies**: WMA (World Medical Association), FSMB (US), GMC (UK), EDPB (EU).
- **Recent Trend**: Move toward **harmonization** via international compacts (IMLC, Hague Convention) and **interoperability standards** (FHIR, HL7) to ease jurisdictional friction.
